22.04 falls within **18.5 ‚Äď 24.9** (ICMR "Normal" range) ‚ÄĒ confirms the BMR prediction is for a *healthy* individual, justifying the use of non-obese Mifflin‚ÄďSt Jeor.

## 3.5 Energy Adjustment for Weight Goals

| Goal | Caloric Rule of Thumb | Mechanism |
|------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Maintenance** | EER exactly as computed | Energy balance |
| **Weight Loss** | EER ‚ąí 500 kcal/day | ~0.5 kg/week loss (1 kg fat ‚Čą 7700 kcal) |
| **Weight Gain** | EER + 500 kcal/day | ~0.5 kg/week gain |
| **Endurance training** | EER + 300 to +600 kcal | Glycogen replenishment |

> [!WARNING]
> Never go below **1200 kcal/day (women)** or **1500 kcal/day (men)** ‚ÄĒ the KTU board considers this a clinical starvation threshold (1 mark deduction in valuation).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart ‚ÄĒ Computing Caloric Requirements (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Input Person Profile] --> varA[Age, Gender, Weight, Height]
    varA --> varB[Activity Level and Lifestyle]
    varB --> choiceBMR{Select BMR Equation}
    choiceBMR --> eq1[Harris-Benedict 1984]
    choiceBMR --> eq2[Mifflin-St Jeor 1990]
    choiceBMR --> eq3[Schofield WHO 1985]
    eq1 --> bmrOut[Compute BMR in kcal per day]
    eq2 --> bmrOut
    eq3 --> bmrOut
    bmrOut --> palStep[Lookup PAL from Activity Table]
    palStep --> eerCalc[Multiply BMR by PAL]
    eerCalc --> goal[Apply Goal Adjustment plus or minus 500 kcal]
    goal --> macroDist[Distribute Across Macronutrients AMDR]
    macroDist --> foodGroup[Map to Five Food Groups ICMR 2020]
    foodGroup --> finalOut[Final Daily Meal Plan and Menu]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map ‚ÄĒ Components of a Balanced Diet

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Balanced Diet] --> branchA[Macronutrients Energy Yielding]
    rootA --> branchB[Micronutrients Protective]
    rootA --> branchC[Water Solvent]
    rootA --> branchD[Fibre and Antioxidants]
    branchA --> nodeA1[Carbohydrates 4 kcal per g]
    branchA --> nodeA2[Proteins 4 kcal per g]
    branchA --> nodeA3[Fats 9 kcal per g]
    branchB --> nodeB1[Fat Soluble Vitamins A D E K]
    branchB --> nodeB2[Water Soluble Vitamins B and C]
    branchB --> nodeB3[Minerals Fe Ca Zn I]
    branchC --> nodeC1[2.0 to 2.7 L per day ICMR]
    branchD --> nodeD1[Soluble Fibre Oats Legumes]
    branchD --> nodeD2[Insoluble Fibre Whole Grains Vegetables]
```

## 4.3 Energy Expenditure Partitioning Diagram

```mermaid
pie showData
    title TDEE Composition in a Sedentary Adult
    "BMR (60 to 70 %)" : 65
    "Activity (20 to 30 %)" : 25
    "TEF (around 10 %)" : 10
```

## 4.4 Five Food Group Plate (Indian MyPlate ‚ÄĒ ICMR)

```mermaid
graph LR
    plate1[Cereals and Millets 45 %] --- plate2[Pulses and Legumes 17 %]
    plate2 --- plate3[Vegetables and Fruits 17 %]
    plate3 --- plate4[Milk and Animal Products 13 %]
    plate4 --- plate5[Fats Oils Nuts 8 %]
    plate1 --> note1[Main energy source]
    plate2 --> note2[Protein and fibre]
    plate3 --> note3[Vitamins and minerals]
    plate4 --> note4[Calcium and B12]
    plate5 --> note5[Concentrated energy]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8% allocated to fats **sounds small** but the volume on the plate is small *because* each gram yields **9 kcal** (more than 2√ó carbohydrate or protein per gram).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a balanced diet. List its essential components.
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **balanced diet**, as defined by the ICMR, is a diet which contains a variety of foods in appropriate quantities and proportions so as to provide all the essential nutrients ‚ÄĒ **carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, fibre, antioxidants and water** ‚ÄĒ required for maintenance, growth, physiological function, and a small safety reserve. The **five essential components** are: (1) Cereals & millets, (2) Pulses & legumes, (3) Milk & animal products, (4) Vegetables & fruits, and (5) Fats, oils & nuts, in the recommended proportions of 45%, 17%, 13%, 17%, and 8% of total food weight respectively.

---

### Q2. State the Atwater factors for carbohydrate, protein, and fat. Why is fat energy-dense?
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Atwater factors ‚ÄĒ the average physiological fuel values experimentally determined by Wilbur O. Atwater ‚ÄĒ are:

- **Carbohydrate = 4 kcal/g**
- **Protein = 4 kcal/g**
- **Fat = 9 kcal/g**

Fats are energy-dense because their hydrocarbon chains (**‚ÄďCH‚āā‚Äď and ‚ÄďCH‚āÉ** groups) contain more reduced carbon and hydrogen per gram than the highly oxidised C, H, O backbone of carbohydrates. Each gram of fat yields over **2√ó the energy of carbohydrate**, enabling compact energy storage in adipose tissue.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of BMR. Describe the Harris‚ÄďBenedict equation and compute the BMR of a 35-year-old male, 75 kg, 175 cm.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of BMR (3 marks):**
The **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)** is the minimum amount of energy expended by the body at rest in a thermoneutral environment, measured in the post-absorptive state (10‚Äď12 hours after the last meal). It represents the energy required to sustain vital functions such as **cellular metabolism, respiration, circulation, and neural activity**. BMR typically contributes **60‚Äď70%** of TDEE and is influenced by age, gender, body size, body composition, hormonal status, and genetics.

**Harris‚ÄďBenedict Equation (2 marks):**
The original Harris‚ÄďBenedict (1919) equation was revised by Roza and Shizgal in 1984. For adult males:

$$\text{BMR} = 88.362 + 13.397W + 4.799H - 5.677A$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 75 \text{ kg}, \quad H = 175 \text{ cm}, \quad A = 35 \text{ yr} \\
\text{BMR} &= 88.362 + 13.397(75) + 4.799(175) - 5.677(35) \\
&= 88.362 + 1004.775 + 839.825 - 198.695 \\
&= 1734.267 \text{ kcal/day} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1734 \text{ kcal/day}}
\end{aligned}$$

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compute the Total Energy Expenditure (TDEE) for the same person if he has a moderately active lifestyle. Distribute the energy across macronutrients as per AMDR.

**Model Solution:**

**TDEE Calculation (3 marks):**
For moderately active, ICMR 2020 PAL = **1.78** (range 1.70‚Äď1.99).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TDEE} &= \text{BMR} \times \text{PAL} \\
&= 1734.267 \times 1.78 \\
&= 3086.99 \text{ kcal/day} \\
&\approx \mathbf{3087 \text{ kcal/day}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Macronutrient Distribution using AMDR (4 marks):**
Select mid-AMDR: CHO 55%, Protein 15%, Fat 30%.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbohydrate (55 \%)} &: 0.55 \times 3087 = 1697.85 \text{ kcal} \div 4 = \mathbf{424.46 \text{ g}} \\
\text{Protein (15 \%)} &: 0.15 \times 3087 = 463.05 \text{ kcal} \div 4 = \mathbf{115.76 \text{ g}} \\
\text{Fat (30 \%)} &: 0.30 \times 3087 = 926.10 \text{ kcal} \div 9 = \mathbf{102.90 \text{ g}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sanity Check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= (424.46 \times 4) + (115.76 \times 4) + (102.90 \times 9) \\
&= 1697.85 + 463.05 + 926.10 \\
&= 3087.00 \text{ kcal} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice**

#### (a) [7 Marks] What is the Physical Activity Level (PAL)? List the PAL ranges for sedentary, moderate, and heavy activity as per FAO/WHO/UNU. Justify why the formula EER = BMR √ó PAL is acceptable even though TDEE technically contains a TEF component.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** PAL is defined as the ratio of total energy expenditure (TDEE) to basal metabolic rate (BMR), i.e., $\text{PAL} = \text{TDEE} / \text{BMR}$, and is a dimensionless index of habitual physical activity integrated over 24 hours.

**PAL Ranges (3 marks, as per FAO/WHO/UNU 2001):**

| Lifestyle | PAL Range | Examples |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Sedentary** | 1.40 ‚Äď 1.69 | Office worker, no exercise |
| **Moderately Active** | 1.70 ‚Äď 1.99 | Walking 2.5‚Äď5 km/day, light sport |
| **Heavy / Very Active** | 2.00 ‚Äď 2.40 | Agricultural labourer, athlete |

**Why EER = BMR √ó PAL is Valid (2 marks):**
Although the strict thermodynamic identity is $\text{TDEE} = \text{BMR} + \text{TEF} + \text{AEE}$, the TEF of a mixed diet averages **~10% of TDEE** and is correlated with BMR. Empirical derivation of the PAL bracket already **bakes TEF into the multiplier**. Thus the simpler composite form, $\text{TDEE} = \text{BMR} \times \text{PAL}$, gives the same numerical answer to within **¬Ī2%** ‚ÄĒ acceptable for KTU-level computation.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A 22-year-old female, 58 kg, 162 cm, is a sedentary software engineer wanting to lose 4 kg in 8 weeks. Design a calorie-controlled balanced diet plan.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Calculate BMR (Mifflin‚ÄďSt Jeor, Female) (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BMR} &= 10(58) + 6.25(162) - 5(22) - 161 \\
&= 580 + 1012.5 - 110 - 161 \\
&= 1321.5 \text{ kcal/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ TDEE at Sedentary (PAL = 1.55) (1 mark):**

$$\text{TDEE} = 1321.5 \times 1.55 = 2048.33 \text{ kcal/day}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Caloric Deficit Calculation (1 mark):**
Required loss = 4 kg = **30,800 kcal** in 8 weeks (8 √ó 7 = 56 days).

$$\text{Daily deficit} = \frac{30800}{56} = 550 \text{ kcal/day}$$

$$\text{Recommended intake} = 2048 - 550 = \mathbf{1498 \text{ kcal/day}}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Macronutrient Plan (AMDR: 55/15/30) (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CHO} &: 0.55 \times 1498 = 823.9 \text{ kcal} = \mathbf{206 \text{ g}} \\
\text{Protein (high, 1.2 g/kg)} &: 58 \times 1.2 = 69.6 \text{ g} \rightarrow \mathbf{70 g} = 280 \text{ kcal} \\
\text{Fat} &: 0.30 \times 1498 = 449.4 \text{ kcal} = \mathbf{50 \text{ g}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Menu (mapped to five food groups) (2 marks):**

| Meal | Food | Group | ~kcal |
|------|------|-------|-------|
| Breakfast | 2 roti + 1 cup curd + 1 banana | Cereals + Milk + Fruit | 450 |
| Mid-morning | 30 g almonds + green tea | Nuts + water | 180 |
| Lunch | 1 cup rice + dal + sabzi + salad | Cereals + Pulses + Veg | 550 |
| Evening | 1 glass buttermilk + 1 fruit | Milk + Fruit | 180 |
| Dinner | 1 roti + paneer sabzi + veg soup | Cereals + Milk + Veg | 250 |
| **Total** | | | **~1610 (round to 1500)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units** ‚ÄĒ always write **kcal/day**, not just "kcal". Losing 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Confusing PAL with TEF** ‚ÄĒ students write TDEE = BMR + TEF + AEE as the *practical* formula. It is theoretically correct but **practically impossible to use without empirical TEF & AEE data**. Always use **TDEE = BMR √ó PAL** in numerical answers. Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Rounding constants of Harris‚ÄďBenedict** (13.397 ‚Üí 13.4) ‚ÄĒ full-mark deduction if problem expects the exact equation form. Lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the 1498 vs 1500 round-off justification** ‚ÄĒ write a line such as "*rounded to nearest 50 kcal for practical meal planning*". Lose 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the AMDR mid-point chosen** (55/15/30) ‚ÄĒ the range is 45‚Äď65/10‚Äď35/20‚Äď35, so examiners look for your *justification*. Lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Mifflin vs Harris‚ÄďBenedict switch** ‚ÄĒ once chosen, **stick to it** within one answer. Mixing equations within a single question loses 1 mark for inconsistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin to Wall Before Exam):**

- ‚≠ź **Definition:** Balanced diet = all nutrients in right quantity & proportion + small reserve.
- ‚≠ź **Atwater Factors:** CHO = 4, Protein = 4, Fat = 9, Ethanol = 7 kcal/g.
- ‚≠ź **BMR Equations (memorise constants):** Harris‚ÄďBenedict Male constants 88.362, 13.397, 4.799, 5.677. Female constants 447.593, 9.247, 3.098, 4.330.
- ‚≠ź **Mifflin‚ÄďSt Jeor (easier, often used):** Male $10W + 6.25H - 5A + 5$; Female $10W + 6.25H - 5A - 161$.
- ‚≠ź **PAL values:** Sedentary 1.40‚Äď1.69, Moderate 1.70‚Äď1.99, Heavy 2.00‚Äď2.40.
- ‚≠ź **Working equation:** EER / TDEE = BMR √ó PAL.
- ‚≠ź **ICMR 2020 Reference Numbers (mnemonic ‚ÄĒ S/M/H, M/F, 60 kg / 55 kg):** Sedentary Man 2320, Woman 1900; Moderate Man 2730, Woman 2230; Heavy Man 3490, Woman 2850.
- ‚≠ź **AMDR ranges:** CHO 45‚Äď65%, Protein 10‚Äď35%, Fat 20‚Äď35%, Sat-fat < 10%, Trans < 1%, Free sugars < 10%.
- ‚≠ź **Five Food Groups % (by weight):** Cereals 45, Pulses 17, Milk 13, Veg+Fruit 17, Fats/Nuts 8.
- ‚≠ź **Energy balance equation:** $\Delta\text{Stores} = E_{\text{in}} - E_{\text{out}}$; balance = weight stable.
- ‚≠ź **Thermodynamic rule:** 1 kg of body fat ‚Čą **7700 kcal**.
- ‚≠ź **Sanity check:** Always verify $4 \cdot \text{CHO} + 4 \cdot \text{Protein} + 9 \cdot \text{Fat} = \text{TDEE}$.
- ‚≠ź **Safety floor:** Minimum intake ‚Č• 1200 kcal (F) or 1500 kcal (M) for clinical safety.
- ‚≠ź **Indian context reference:** Use Schofield (WHO) or ICMR PAL, not Western Harris‚ÄďBenedict, for Indian subjects ‚ÄĒ examiners appreciate this distinction.
- ‚≠ź **Units rule:** Always kg (W), cm (H), years (A), kcal/day (EER). Lose 0.5 mark per missing unit.
- ‚≠ź **5-Mark trick in long questions:** "Justify the choice of AMDR split" ‚ÄĒ state "55/15/30 chosen as central tendency of ICMR 2020 recommendation for an adult with weight-loss goal (high protein preserves lean mass)".

> **End of KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Notes for Module 1 ‚ÄĒ Balanced Diet & Computing Caloric Requirements.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemistry of Carbohydrates - Definition, Classification, Structure of Major Mono-, Di- and Polysaccharides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemistry of Carbohydrates - Definition, Classification, Structure of Major Mono-, Di- and Polysaccharides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Carbohydrates

**Carbohydrates** are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or compounds that upon hydrolysis yield these functional groups. Chemically, they are organic compounds composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, typically following the empirical formula:

$$\text{C}_x(\text{H}_2\text{O})_y$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCFTT205 - Module 2):**
> Carbohydrates are the most abundant class of biomolecules on Earth. They are defined as *polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones* and their derivatives. They serve as primary energy sources (4 kcal/g), structural components, and recognition molecules in biological systems.

### 1.2 Classification Overview

Carbohydrates are classified based on the number of sugar units (saccharide residues) produced upon hydrolysis:

| Class | Hydrolysis Behavior | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Monosaccharides** | Cannot be hydrolyzed further | Glucose, Fructose, Galactose |
| **Disaccharides** | Yield 2 monosaccharides | Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose |
| **Oligosaccharides** | Yield 3‚Äď10 monosaccharides | Raffinose, Stachyose |
| **Polysaccharides** | Yield many monosaccharides | Starch, Cellulose, Glycogen |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Building Block" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: LEGO Blocks of Nutrition**
> Imagine carbohydrates as **LEGO blocks of life**:
> - **Monosaccharides** = Individual single LEGO bricks (the basic building unit)
> - **Disaccharides** = Two bricks stuck together with glue
> - **Polysaccharides** = Hundreds or thousands of bricks assembled into massive structures (walls, towers, castles)
> - The "glue" that joins them is called a **glycosidic bond**, formed by a dehydration (condensation) reaction that releases **one molecule of water per bond**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Equivalence Constant:** The average caloric value of carbohydrates is **4 kcal/g (or 16.7 kJ/g)** ‚ÄĒ this is a KTU-graded standard fact you must memorize for Module 2 numerical problems.

### 1.4 Functional Group Classification

Monosaccharides are further divided based on their carbonyl group:

- **Aldoses** ‚Üí contain an **aldehyde** (‚ÄďCHO) group (e.g., Glucose, Galactose, Mannose, Ribose)
- **Ketoses** ‚Üí contain a **ketone** (C=O) group (e.g., Fructose, Ribulose)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Trick:**
> **"ALDOses have ALDOhyde"** and **"KETOses have a KETOne."**
> The suffix "-ose" denotes a sugar; the prefix "aldo-/keto-" denotes the carbonyl type.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Monosaccharides

Monosaccharides are classified by:
1. **Number of carbon atoms** (trios, tetroses, pentoses, hexoses, heptoses)
2. **Functional group** (aldose vs. ketose)

| Carbon Count | Aldose | Ketose |
|---|---|---|
| 3 (Triose) | Glyceraldehyde | Dihydroxyacetone |
| 4 (Tetrose) | Erythrose | Erythrulose |
| 5 (Pentose) | Ribose, Xylose | Ribulose |
| 6 (Hexose) | **Glucose**, Galactose, Mannose | **Fructose** |
| 7 (Heptose) | Sedoheptulose | ‚ÄĒ |

### 2.2 Stereochemistry: D- and L- Isomers

The **D-/L- configuration** is determined by the orientation of the ‚ÄďOH group on the **highest-numbered chiral carbon** (the penultimate carbon):

- **D-sugar**: ‚ÄďOH on the **right** in Fischer projection (most natural sugars)
- **L-sugar**: ‚ÄďOH on the **left** in Fischer projection (mirror image)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Point:** Glucose has **four (4) chiral carbons (C2, C3, C4, C5)**, giving **2‚Āī = 16 stereoisomers** (8 D-forms + 8 L-forms). Of these, only **D-glucose** is the biologically active form in human metabolism.

### 2.3 Ring Structures: Mutarotation & Anomers

In aqueous solution, pentoses and hexoses cyclize via **intramolecular hemiacetal/hemiketal formation**:

- **Aldohexoses (e.g., Glucose)** form **6-membered pyranose** rings
- **Ketohexoses (e.g., Fructose)** form **5-membered furanose** rings (or pyranose form)

**Anomers (őĪ and ő≤):**
- **őĪ-anomer**: ‚ÄďOH on C1 (anomeric carbon) is **trans** to the ‚ÄďCH‚āāOH group (axial, below ring plane in Haworth)
- **ő≤-anomer**: ‚ÄďOH on C1 is **cis** to the ‚ÄďCH‚āāOH group (equatorial, above ring plane)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mutarotation Definition:** The change in optical rotation (őĪ) of a freshly prepared sugar solution until it reaches an equilibrium value, caused by interconversion of őĪ- and ő≤- anomers through the open-chain form.
> **Equilibrium composition of D-glucose:** őĪ-D-glucopyranose (36%) ‚áĆ ő≤-D-glucopyranose (64%) ‚áĆ open-chain (<1%)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Empirical formula of carbohydrate | $\text{C}_x(\text{H}_2\text{O})_y$ | Dimensionless | Identification |
| Number of stereoisomers | $2^n$ where $n$ = chiral carbons | ‚ÄĒ | Glucose = $2^4 = 16$ |
| Energy from carbohydrate | $E = 4 \text{ kcal/g}$ | 4 kcal/g ‚Čą 16.7 kJ/g | Dietetics / Nutrition |
| Molar mass of glucose | $M_{\text{glucose}}$ | **180 g/mol** | Calculations |
| Condensation reaction | $\text{Mono}_1 + \text{Mono}_2 \rightarrow \text{Di} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Releases 18 g/mol H‚āāO | Disaccharide formation |
| Hydrolysis (reverse) | $\text{Di} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Mono}$ | Adds 18 g/mol H‚āāO | Digestion |
| Optical rotation (specific) | $[\alpha] = \dfrac{\alpha_{\text{obs}}}{l \cdot c}$ | deg¬∑mL¬∑g‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑dm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | Mutarotation |
| Glycosidic bond energy | Bond dissociation | $\approx 1000$ kJ/mol (enzymatic) | Digestion kinetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In **Food Engineering**, the energy density of carbohydrates dictates the calorific value of packaged foods, dietary formulations, sports nutrition, and diabetic food design. The molar mass of glucose (180 g/mol) is essential for calculating **% hydrolysis, sweetness index, and glycemic load** in food chemistry laboratories.

### 2.5 Glycosidic Bond ‚ÄĒ The Molecular "Glue"

When the **anomeric carbon (C1)** of one sugar reacts with a hydroxyl group of another sugar, water is eliminated and a **glycosidic bond** is formed.

$$\text{C}_1\text{‚ÄďOH (Sugar A)} + \text{HO‚ÄďC}_x \text{ (Sugar B)} \xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{C}_1\text{‚ÄďO‚ÄďC}_x \text{ (Glycosidic bond)}$$

The two key configurations are:
- **őĪ-glycosidic bond** ‚Üí bond lies **below** the ring plane (axial)
- **ő≤-glycosidic bond** ‚Üí bond lies **above** the ring plane (equatorial)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Common Mistake:** Many students incorrectly state that ALL disaccharides contain an őĪ-1,4 bond. **Lactose contains a ő≤-1,4 bond**, and **sucrose contains an őĪ-1,ő≤-2 bond** ‚ÄĒ these are high-value distinctions tested in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Stereoisomers of a Monosaccharide

The number of stereoisomers for a monosaccharide with $n$ chiral carbons follows:

$$
N_{\text{isomers}} = 2^{n}
$$

**For D-Glucose (aldohexose):**
- Step 1: Identify chiral carbons. The carbons C2, C3, C4, and C5 each bear four different substituents ‚Üí all are **chiral**.
- Step 2: Therefore, $n = 4$.
- Step 3: Apply formula:

$$
N_{\text{isomers}} = 2^{4} = 16
$$

- Step 4: Of these 16 stereoisomers, 8 are D-series and 8 are L-series (mirror images). Only **D-glucose** is metabolically active in humans.

**For D-Fructose (ketohexose):**
- Chiral carbons: C3, C4, C5 ‚Üí $n = 3$
- Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{isomers}} = 2^{3} = 8
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Calculation During Disaccharide Formation

When two hexose monomers (each 180 g/mol) join to form a disaccharide:

$$
\text{Hexose}_1 + \text{Hexose}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Disaccharide} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Molar mass balance:**

$$
M_{\text{disaccharide}} = (2 \times M_{\text{hexose}}) - M_{\text{water}}
$$

$$
M_{\text{disaccharide}} = (2 \times 180) - 18 = 360 - 18 = 342 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Generalized formula for a polysaccharide of $n$ glucose units:**

$$
M_{\text{polysaccharide}} = (n \times 180) - [(n-1) \times 18] = 162n + 18
$$

For example, a tetrasaccharide (4 glucose units) formed by 3 condensation reactions:

$$
M = (4 \times 180) - (3 \times 18) = 720 - 54 = 666 \text{ g/mol}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Mutarotation of D-Glucose

The specific rotation of an optically active sugar is given by:

$$
[\alpha]_{\lambda}^{T} = \frac{\alpha_{\text{observed}}}{l \cdot c}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha_{\text{observed}}$ = observed rotation in degrees
- $l$ = path length of the polarimeter tube (in dm)
- $c$ = concentration in g/mL
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (typically sodium D-line, 589 nm)
- $T$ = temperature (typically 20¬įC)

**Mutarotation sequence:**

$$
\alpha\text{-D-glucopyranose } ([\alpha] = +112¬į) \rightleftharpoons \text{Open chain } (<1\%) \rightleftharpoons \beta\text{-D-glucopyranose } ([\alpha] = +18.7¬į)
$$

**Equilibrium specific rotation at 20¬įC:**

$$
[\alpha]_{D}^{20} = +52.7¬į
$$

The equilibrium value is reached when the interconversion between őĪ- and ő≤- anomers stabilizes at the open-chain-mediated ratio (36% : 64%).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Structure of Major Mono-, Di-, and Polysaccharides

| Sugar | Type | Molecular Formula | Molar Mass (g/mol) | Key Structural Feature | Glycosidic Linkage (if polymeric) | Sweetness (relative to sucrose = 1) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **D-Glucose** | Aldohexose (monosaccharide) | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āāO‚āÜ | 180 | Pyranose ring; ‚ÄďOH on C5 (right) ‚Üí D-form | N/A (monomer) | 0.7 |
| **D-Fructose** | Ketohexose (monosaccharide) | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āāO‚āÜ | 180 | Furanose ring; C=O at C2 | N/A (monomer) | 1.7 (sweetest natural sugar) |
| **D-Galactose** | Aldohexose (monosaccharide) | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āāO‚āÜ | 180 | Epimer of glucose at C4 | N/A (monomer) | 0.3 |
| **D-Mannose** | Aldohexose (monosaccharide) | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āāO‚āÜ | 180 | Epimer of glucose at C2 | N/A (monomer) | 0.5 |
| **D-Ribose** | Aldopentose (monosaccharide) | C‚āÖH‚āĀ‚āÄO‚āÖ | 150 | Furanose form in RNA/ATP | N/A (monomer) | 0.35 |
| **Maltose** | Disaccharide (Glc + Glc) | C‚āĀ‚āāH‚āā‚āāO‚āĀ‚āĀ | 342 | Reducing sugar (free anomeric ‚ÄďOH) | őĪ-1,4 | 0.4 |
| **Lactose** | Disaccharide (Glc + Gal) | C‚āĀ‚āāH‚āā‚āāO‚āĀ‚āĀ | 342 | Reducing sugar; "milk sugar" | ő≤-1,4 | 0.16 |
| **Sucrose** | Disaccharide (Glc + Fru) | C‚āĀ‚āāH‚āā‚āāO‚āĀ‚āĀ | 342 | **Non-reducing** (both anomeric C's bonded) | őĪ-1,ő≤-2 | 1.0 (reference standard) |
| **Starch (Amylose)** | Polysaccharide | (C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āÄO‚āÖ)‚āô | ~10‚ĀĶ‚Äď10‚Ā∂ | Linear chain; blue color with I‚āā | őĪ-1,4 | None (tasteless) |
| **Starch (Amylopectin)** | Polysaccharide | (C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āÄO‚āÖ)‚āô | ~10‚Ā∂‚Äď10‚Āł | Branched; purple/violet with I‚āā | őĪ-1,4 (main); őĪ-1,6 (branches every 24‚Äď30 units) | None |
| **Glycogen** | Polysaccharide | (C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āÄO‚āÖ)‚āô | ~10‚Ā∂‚Äď10‚ĀĻ | Highly branched (every 8‚Äď12 units); red-brown with I‚āā | őĪ-1,4 (main); őĪ-1,6 (branches) | None |
| **Cellulose** | Polysaccharide | (C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āÄO‚āÖ)‚āô | ~10‚ĀĶ‚Äď10‚Ā∂ | Linear; **ő≤-1,4** bond ‚Üí indigestible by humans | ő≤-1,4 | None |

### 3.5 Reaction Derivations

**Reaction 1: Hydrolysis of Sucrose (Inversion of Sugar)**

$$
\underset{\text{Sucrose}}{\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ or invertase}} \underset{\text{Glucose}}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6} + \underset{\text{Fructose}}{\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6}
$$

- Step 1: The őĪ-1,ő≤-2 bond is cleaved by acid hydrolysis or by the enzyme **invertase**.
- Step 2: Sucrose (dextrorotatory, $[\alpha] = +66.5¬į$) yields an equimolar mixture of glucose ($+52.7¬į$) and fructose ($-92¬į$).
- Step 3: The net rotation of the mixture is **negative (levorotatory)** because fructose is strongly levorotatory.
- Step 4: This sign reversal is called **inversion**, and the resulting mixture is called **invert sugar** (used in candy making, honey).

**Reaction 2: Hydrolysis of Starch to Maltose to Glucose**

$$
\underset{\text{Starch}}{(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n} \xrightarrow{\text{Amylase}} \underset{\text{Maltose}}{\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}} \xrightarrow{\text{Maltase}} \underset{\text{Glucose}}{2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6}
$$

- Step 1: őĪ-amylase randomly cleaves internal őĪ-1,4 glycosidic bonds of starch.
- Step 2: Maltase hydrolyzes maltose's őĪ-1,4 bond to release two glucose units.
- Step 3: In the human body, salivary and pancreatic amylases perform Step 1; intestinal maltase performs Step 2.

**Reaction 3: Cellulose Hydrolysis (Microbial Only)**

$$
\underset{\text{Cellulose}}{(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n} \xrightarrow{\text{Cellulase}} \underset{\text{Cellobiose}}{\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-glucosidase}} \underset{\text{Glucose}}{2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6}
$$

- Step 1: Only microorganisms (e.g., *Trichoderma reesei*, *Ruminococcus flavefaciens*) and fungi/termites produce cellulase.
- Step 2: The ő≤-1,4 bond in cellulose is **NOT hydrolyzed by human digestive enzymes** ‚ÄĒ this is why cellulose is classified as **dietary fiber**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Insight:** The **őĪ- vs. ő≤- configuration** of the glycosidic bond determines whether the polymer is digestible. **őĪ-1,4 (starch, glycogen)** ‚Üí digestible; **ő≤-1,4 (cellulose)** ‚Üí indigestible to humans. This is a recurring 14-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Carbohydrate Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CARBOHYDRATES] --> B[MONOSACCHARIDES]
    A --> C[DISACCHARIDES]
    A --> D[OLIGOSACCHARIDES]
    A --> E[POLYSACCHARIDES]

    B --> B1[Aldoses: Glucose, Galactose, Mannose, Ribose]
    B --> B2[Ketoses: Fructose, Ribulose, Dihydroxyacetone]

    C --> C1[Maltose = Glc alpha-1,4 Glc]
    C --> C2[Lactose = Gal beta-1,4 Glc]
    C --> C3[Sucrose = Glc alpha-1,beta-2 Fru]

    E --> E1[Homopolysaccharides: Starch, Glycogen, Cellulose]
    E --> E2[Heteropolysaccharides: Hyaluronic acid, Chitin, Pectin]

    E1 --> F1[Starch: Amylose linear alpha-1,4 and Amylopectin branched alpha-1,4 with alpha-1,6]
    E1 --> F2[Glycogen: Highly branched alpha-1,4 and alpha-1,6]
    E1 --> F3[Cellulose: Linear beta-1,4 unbranched]
```

### 4.2 D-Glucose: Fischer and Haworth Projection Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FISCHER PROJECTION OF D-GLUCOSE] -->|Cyclization via C1 and C5| B[HAWORTH PROJECTION]

    A --> A1[C1: CHO at top]
    A --> A2[C2: OH on RIGHT]
    A --> A3[C3: OH on LEFT]
    A --> A4[C4: OH on RIGHT]
    A --> A5[C5: OH on RIGHT - D-config]
    A --> A6[C6: CH2OH at bottom]

    B --> B1[C1 anomeric carbon: alpha-OH DOWN or beta-OH UP]
    B --> B2[Ring oxygen between C1 and C5]
    B --> B3[Six-membered pyranose ring]
```

### 4.3 Disaccharide Bond Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DISACCHARIDE FORMATION MECHANISM] --> B[Step 1: Identify Anomeric Carbon of Sugar A]
    A --> C[Step 2: Identify Hydroxyl Group of Sugar B]
    A --> D[Step 3: Dehydration Condensation -H2O]

    B --> B1[Anomeric C1 of aldose or C2 of ketose]
    C --> C1[May be C2, C4, or C6 of second sugar]
    D --> D1[Forms C-O-C glycosidic linkage]

    D1 --> E1[alpha-1,4 bond: MALTOSE]
    D1 --> E2[beta-1,4 bond: LACTOSE]
    D1 --> E3[alpha-1,beta-2 bond: SUCROSE non-reducing]
```

### 4.4 Polysaccharide Architecture: Starch vs. Glycogen vs. Cellulose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POLYSACCHARIDES OF GLUCOSE] --> B[STARCH plant]
    A --> C[GLYCOGEN animal]
    A --> D[CELLULOSE plant cell wall]

    B --> B1[AMYLOSE: linear chain, alpha-1,4 only, helical structure]
    B --> B2[AMYLOPECTIN: alpha-1,4 main chain with alpha-1,6 branches every 24 to 30 units]

    C --> C1[Highly branched structure]
    C --> C2[alpha-1,4 main chain with alpha-1,6 branches every 8 to 12 units]
    C --> C3[Storage form of glucose in liver and muscle]

    D --> D1[Linear unbranched chain]
    D --> D2[beta-1,4 glycosidic bonds only]
    D --> D3[Straight chain forms microfibrils via hydrogen bonding]
    D --> D4[Indigestible dietary fiber for humans]
```

### 4.5 Reducing vs. Non-Reducing Sugar Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Is the sugar reducing?] --> B{Is at least one anomeric carbon FREE?}
    B -->|YES| C[REDUCING SUGAR: Glucose, Fructose, Galactose, Maltose, Lactose]
    B -->|NO| D[NON-REDUCING SUGAR: Sucrose, Trehalose]

    C --> C1[Benedicts test or Fehlings test POSITIVE: forms brick-red Cu2O precipitate]
    D --> D1[Benedicts test or Fehlings test NEGATIVE: no color change]
```

### 4.6 Sweetness Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Relative Sweetness Scale] --> B[Fructose 1.7 - sweetest natural sugar]
    A --> C[Sucrose 1.0 - reference standard]
    A --> D[Glucose 0.7]
    A --> E[Maltose 0.4]
    A --> F[Galactose 0.3]
    A --> G[Lactose 0.16 - least sweet]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define carbohydrates. Classify them with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or substances that yield these on hydrolysis. They have the general formula $\text{C}_x(\text{H}_2\text{O})_y$.
- **Monosaccharides** (e.g., Glucose, Fructose) ‚ÄĒ single units
- **Disaccharides** (e.g., Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose) ‚ÄĒ two units
- **Polysaccharides** (e.g., Starch, Cellulose, Glycogen) ‚ÄĒ many units

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark classification + 1 mark examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between reducing and non-reducing sugars with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reducing sugars** have a free anomeric (‚ÄďOH) carbon that can open up to form a free aldehyde, which reduces Cu¬≤‚Āļ to Cu‚Āļ in Fehling's/Benedict's test. *Example: Glucose, Maltose, Lactose.*
- **Non-reducing sugars** have both anomeric carbons involved in the glycosidic bond, hence no free ‚ÄďOH at C1. *Example: Sucrose.* (1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for examples)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define carbohydrates. Classify them in detail with examples, giving the general formula and characteristics of each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the Fischer projection of D-glucose and explain how it cyclizes to form őĪ- and ő≤-D-glucopyranose. Discuss the phenomenon of mutarotation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Classification (7 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, with the empirical formula $\text{C}_x(\text{H}_2\text{O})_y$. They serve as primary energy sources (4 kcal/g).
- **Classification (5 marks):**
  1. **Monosaccharides** ‚ÄĒ single units, cannot be hydrolyzed. Classified by carbonyl type (aldose/ketose) and carbon number. Examples: Glucose (aldohexose), Fructose (ketohexose), Ribose (aldopentose).
  2. **Disaccharides** ‚ÄĒ yield 2 monosaccharides on hydrolysis. Examples: Sucrose (Glc + Fru), Maltose (Glc + Glc), Lactose (Glc + Gal). General formula C‚āĀ‚āāH‚āā‚āāO‚āĀ‚āĀ.
  3. **Oligosaccharides** ‚ÄĒ yield 3‚Äď10 monosaccharides. Example: Raffinose (Gal + Glc + Fru).
  4. **Polysaccharides** ‚ÄĒ yield many monosaccharides. Examples: Starch (C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āÄO‚āÖ)‚āô, Glycogen, Cellulose. May be homopolymers (one sugar) or heteropolymers (multiple sugars).

**(b) Fischer Projection & Mutarotation (7 marks):**

**Fischer Projection of D-Glucose (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C1:} \quad & \text{CHO} \\
\text{C2:} \quad & \text{H‚ÄďC‚ÄďOH} \quad (\text{OH on right}) \\
\text{C3:} \quad & \text{HO‚ÄďC‚ÄďH} \quad (\text{OH on left}) \\
\text{C4:} \quad & \text{H‚ÄďC‚ÄďOH} \quad (\text{OH on right}) \\
\text{C5:} \quad & \text{H‚ÄďC‚ÄďOH} \quad (\text{OH on right ‚Üí D-config}) \\
\text{C6:} \quad & \text{CH}_2\text{OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cyclization (3 marks):**
- The C1 aldehyde (CHO) reacts with the C5 hydroxyl (‚ÄďOH) to form a **hemiacetal**.
- The result is a 6-membered **pyranose ring** (named after pyran).
- The C1 becomes the **anomeric carbon** with a new ‚ÄďOH group.
- In Haworth projection:
  - **őĪ-D-glucopyranose**: ‚ÄďOH on C1 points **down** (trans to CH‚āāOH at C5)
  - **ő≤-D-glucopyranose**: ‚ÄďOH on C1 points **up** (cis to CH‚āāOH at C5)

**Mutarotation (2 marks):**
- When őĪ-D-glucose is dissolved in water, $[\alpha] = +112¬į$ initially.
- The őĪ-form interconverts via the open-chain form to the ő≤-form ($[\alpha] = +18.7¬į$).
- At equilibrium, $[\alpha]_{D}^{20} = +52.7¬į$ with composition őĪ (36%) : ő≤ (64%) : open-chain (<1%).
- This change in optical rotation is called **mutarotation**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure and properties of the following disaccharides: (i) Sucrose, (ii) Maltose, (iii) Lactose. Highlight the glycosidic linkages in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the structures of amylose, amylopectin, glycogen, and cellulose. Explain why cellulose is indigestible to humans. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Disaccharide Structures (7 marks):**

**(i) Sucrose (2 marks):**
- Composition: **őĪ-D-Glucose + ő≤-D-Fructose**
- Glycosidic bond: **őĪ-1,ő≤-2 (both anomeric carbons involved)**
- Molar mass: 342 g/mol
- **Non-reducing** sugar ‚ÄĒ both C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose are linked, so no free anomeric ‚ÄďOH.
- Hydrolysis (acid or invertase) yields equimolar glucose + fructose = **invert sugar** (used in confectionery, honey).
- Found in sugarcane and sugar beet (~16‚Äď20% sucrose).

**(ii) Maltose (2 marks):**
- Composition: **őĪ-D-Glucose + D-Glucose** (two őĪ-glucose units)
- Glycosidic bond: **őĪ-1,4**
- Molar mass: 342 g/mol
- **Reducing** sugar ‚ÄĒ free anomeric ‚ÄďOH on the second glucose unit's C1.
- Produced during starch hydrolysis by amylase.
- Found in germinating grains (malt).

**(iii) Lactose (2 marks):**
- Composition: **ő≤-D-Galactose + D-Glucose**
- Glycosidic bond: **ő≤-1,4**
- Molar mass: 342 g/mol
- **Reducing** sugar.
- Known as **"milk sugar"** (~5% in cow's milk, ~7% in human milk).
- Hydrolyzed by **lactase** enzyme; lactase deficiency causes **lactose intolerance**.
- Sweetness is very low (0.16 relative to sucrose).

**(1 mark for general comparison / summary table.)**

**(b) Polysaccharide Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | Amylose | Amylopectin | Glycogen | Cellulose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source | Plant starch | Plant starch | Animal (liver/muscle) | Plant cell wall |
| Monomer | őĪ-D-Glucose | őĪ-D-Glucose | őĪ-D-Glucose | ő≤-D-Glucose |
| Linkage | őĪ-1,4 only | őĪ-1,4 + őĪ-1,6 (every 24‚Äď30 units) | őĪ-1,4 + őĪ-1,6 (every 8‚Äď12 units) | ő≤-1,4 only |
| Structure | Linear, helical | Branched | Highly branched | Linear, unbranched |
| Iodine test | Deep blue | Purple/violet | Red-brown | None |
| Digestibility (human) | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No (fiber)** |

**Why Cellulose is Indigestible (3 marks):**
1. Cellulose contains **ő≤-1,4 glycosidic bonds**, while human digestive enzymes (őĪ-amylase, maltase) can only hydrolyze **őĪ-bonds**.
2. The linear chains form **tight hydrogen-bonded microfibrils**, making them physically inaccessible to enzymes.
3. Only **microorganisms** (e.g., *Ruminococcus* in cattle, *Trichoderma* fungi) produce **cellulase** that cleaves ő≤-1,4 bonds.
4. Humans lack cellulase ‚Üí cellulose passes through as **dietary fiber**, adding bulk to feces and aiding peristalsis.
5. Ruminants (cattle, sheep, goats) host symbiotic gut bacteria that ferment cellulose into volatile fatty acids ‚ÄĒ a key reason for multi-chambered stomachs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PCFTT205 Carbohydrate Questions:**
> 1. **Mutarotation vs. Optical Activity confusion** ‚ÄĒ Many students describe mutarotation as "rotation of molecules" rather than "change in optical rotation due to anomeric interconversion." **Loses 1‚Äď2 marks.**
> 2. **Glycosidic bond notation** ‚ÄĒ Writing "őĪ-1,4" for lactose or "ő≤-1,4" for maltose will be **marked wrong**. Always double-check the linkage by counting carbons in the bond.
> 3. **Sucrose classification** ‚ÄĒ Sucrose is **non-reducing**, NOT reducing. Forgetting that both anomeric carbons are bonded loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting C2 in fructose** ‚ÄĒ Fructose's anomeric carbon is **C2 (not C1)**. In sucrose, the bond is between **C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose** ‚Üí őĪ-1,ő≤-2.
> 5. **Cellulose is NOT made of őĪ-glucose** ‚ÄĒ It is ő≤-D-glucose. This is the single most-tested misconception.
> 6. **Forgetting the water molecule** in condensation reactions ‚ÄĒ always show "‚ÄďH‚āāO" for accurate stoichiometry in 14-mark derivations.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block ‚ÄĒ PCFTT205 Module 2 Carbohydrates**

- ‚úÖ **Definition:** Polyhydroxy aldehydes/ketones; formula $\text{C}_x(\text{H}_2\text{O})_y$
- ‚úÖ **Energy value:** **4 kcal/g (16.7 kJ/g)** ‚ÄĒ must memorize
- ‚úÖ **Molar masses:** Glucose = 180 g/mol; Disaccharides = 342 g/mol; Water loss per bond = 18 g/mol
- ‚úÖ **Glucose chiral carbons = 4** ‚Üí $2^4 = 16$ stereoisomers
- ‚úÖ **Fructose chiral carbons = 3** ‚Üí $2^3 = 8$ stereoisomers
- ‚úÖ **Anomeric carbon:** C1 in aldoses; **C2** in ketoses
- ‚úÖ **őĪ vs ő≤:** őĪ-OH is **down/below** ring plane (axial); ő≤-OH is **up/above** (equatorial)
- ‚úÖ **Mutarotation equilibrium:** őĪ (36%) : ő≤ (64%) : open-chain (<1%); $[\alpha]_{D}^{20} = +52.7¬į$
- ‚úÖ **Sucrose:** őĪ-1,ő≤-2 bond ‚Üí **non-reducing**
- ‚úÖ **Maltose:** őĪ-1,4 bond ‚Üí **reducing** (glucose + glucose)
- ‚úÖ **Lactose:** ő≤-1,4 bond ‚Üí **reducing** (galactose + glucose)
- ‚úÖ **Amylose:** linear őĪ-1,4 ‚Üí blue with I‚āā
- ‚úÖ **Amylopectin:** branched őĪ-1,4 + őĪ-1,6 ‚Üí purple with I‚āā
- ‚úÖ **Glycogen:** highly branched őĪ-1,4 + őĪ-1,6 ‚Üí red-brown with I‚āā
- ‚úÖ **Cellulose:** linear ő≤-1,4 ‚Üí **indigestible to humans** (no cellulase enzyme)
- ‚úÖ **Invertase** hydrolyzes sucrose ‚Üí **invert sugar** (used in candy/honey)
- ‚úÖ **D-/L-** configuration determined by **penultimate carbon's** ‚ÄďOH orientation in Fischer projection
- ‚úÖ **őĪ-linkage ‚Üí digestible; ő≤-linkage ‚Üí indigestible** (humans)
- ‚úÖ **Lactose intolerance** = lactase deficiency; treat with lactase supplements or lactose-free dairy
- ‚úÖ **Sweetness order (high to low):** Fructose (1.7) > Sucrose (1.0) > Glucose (0.7) > Maltose (0.4) > Galactose (0.3) > Lactose (0.16)
- ‚úÖ **Iodine test specificity:** Starch-iodine complex colors depend on chain length and branching ‚ÄĒ amylose (blue), amylopectin (purple), glycogen (red-brown), cellulose (no color)

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes ‚ÄĒ PCFTT205 Food Chemistry | KTU 2024 Scheme**
> *This concludes the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 study sheet for "Chemistry of Carbohydrates ‚ÄĒ Definition, Classification, Structure of Major Mono-, Di- and Polysaccharides." For best exam results, redraw all Haworth projections, memorize the glycosidic linkages, and practice at least two 14-mark structural comparison questions per week.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties and Reactions of Carbohydrates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Properties and Reactions of Carbohydrates # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carbohydrates** are polyhydroxy aldehydes (aldoses) or polyhydroxy ketones (ketoses) and their derivatives, or substances that yield these compounds on hydrolysis. They are classified as **monosaccharides** (single unit, e.g., glucose, fructose), **oligosaccharides** (2‚Äď10 units, e.g., sucrose, maltose), and **polysaccharides** (long polymers, e.g., starch, cellulose). The empirical formula of most carbohydrates follows **C‚āô(H‚āāO)‚āô**, which historically gave rise to the term "hydrates of carbon." Carbohydrates serve as the primary source of metabolic energy (**‚Čą 4 kcal/g**), structural components (cellulose), and storage reserves (glycogen, starch) in biological systems. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Reactions of carbohydrates are foundational for understanding food chemistry phenomena such as **non-enzymatic browning (Maillard reaction)**, **caramelization**, and **polysaccharide gel formation** ‚ÄĒ all of which are directly assessed in Part B questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of carbohydrates as a **versatile Lego¬ģ brick set for the food industry**: - The **smallest bricks (monosaccharides like glucose)** are reactive, soluble, and sweet ‚ÄĒ they can be snapped, twisted, or oxidized easily. - When you **glue these bricks together (glycosidic bonds)**, you build medium-sized structures (disaccharides like sucrose) that have new properties. - When you build **massive walls (polysaccharides like starch)**, the bricks lose their sweetness and solubility, but gain strength (structure) and storage capacity. The "reactions" we study in this module are essentially the **ways food chemists and biochemists break, rebuild, or transform these Lego¬ģ bricks** during cooking, baking, preservation, and digestion. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Property | Standard Metric / Value | |---|---| | Calorific value of carbohydrates | **4.0 kcal/g (‚Čą 16.7 kJ/g)** | | Optical rotation (D-glucose, őĪ) | **+112.2¬į** | | Optical rotation (D-glucose, ő≤) | **+18.7¬į** | | Equilibrium specific rotation (D-glucose) | **+52.7¬į** | | Sweetness (relative to sucrose = 1) | Fructose **1.7**, Glucose **0.7**, Maltose **0.4** | | Solubility of sucrose in water at 20 ¬įC | **‚Čą 211 g/100 mL** | | Glycogen energy storage | **Liver:** ~100 g; **Muscle:** ~400 g | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Properties of Carbohydrates ### 2.1.1 Solubility - **Monosaccharides** (glucose, fructose, galactose): freely soluble in water due to multiple ‚ÄďOH groups capable of **hydrogen bonding** with water. - **Disaccharides** (sucrose, maltose, lactose): soluble, but less so than monosaccharides. - **Polysaccharides** (starch, cellulose): generally **insoluble** in cold water; some form **colloidal dispersions** (e.g., starch in hot water). > [!NOTE] > Cellulose is insoluble because of extensive **inter-chain hydrogen bonding** that forms rigid microfibrils ‚ÄĒ humans lack the enzyme **cellulase** to hydrolyze ő≤-1,4 linkages. ### 2.1.2 Optical Activity - All carbohydrates (except dihydroxyacetone and sucrose's őĪ-anomer) possess **asymmetric (chiral) carbon atoms**. - They rotate the plane of **plane-polarized light**. - **Dextrorotatory (+)** ‚Üí rotates light clockwise (e.g., D-glucose). - **Levorotatory (‚ąí)** ‚Üí rotates light counter-clockwise (e.g., D-fructose, "levulose"). **Specific rotation formula (Bi Biot's Law):** $$[\alpha]_{D}^{T} = \frac{\alpha_{obs}}{l \cdot c}$$ Where: - $[\alpha]_{D}^{T}$ = specific rotation at temperature T using sodium D-line - $\alpha_{obs}$ = observed rotation in degrees - $l$ = path length in **decimeters (dm)** - $c$ = concentration in **g/mL** ### 2.1.3 Mutarotation - When a crystalline sugar (e.g., őĪ-D-glucose) is dissolved in water, the specific rotation **gradually changes** until reaching an equilibrium value. - This is due to interconversion between **őĪ-anomer** and **ő≤-anomer** via the open-chain form. $$\alpha\text{-D-glucose} ([\alpha] = +112.2¬į) \rightleftharpoons \text{Open-chain aldehyde} \rightleftharpoons \beta\text{-D-glucose} ([\alpha] = +18.7¬į)$$ At equilibrium, the specific rotation stabilizes at **+52.7¬į**, with the mixture containing **‚Čą 36 % őĪ-anomer** and **‚Čą 64 % ő≤-anomer**. ### 2.1.4 Sweetness - Sweetness is **structure-dependent**: carbonyl group, hydroxyl groups at C-2, C-3, C-4, C-5, and the C-1 anomeric configuration all play a role. - **Invert sugar** (glucose + fructose, formed by hydrolysis of sucrose) is sweeter than sucrose itself. ## 2.2 Chemical Reactions of Carbohydrates ### 2.2.1 Reactions Based on the Carbonyl Group **A. Oxidation Reactions (Reducing Sugar Tests):** 1. **Benedict's Test:** - Reagent: Alkaline copper sulfate + sodium citrate. - Reducing sugars reduce **Cu¬≤‚Āļ (blue) ‚Üí Cu‚Āļ (brick-red Cu‚āāO precipitate)**. - More stable than Fehling's; standard test for urine glucose. 2. **Fehling's Test:** - Reagent: Fehling A (CuSO‚āĄ) + Fehling B (K-Na tartrate + NaOH). - Tartrate complexes Cu¬≤‚Āļ to keep it in solution; heating yields **red Cu‚āāO**. 3. **Tollens' Test (Silver Mirror Test):** - Reagent: Ammoniacal silver nitrate [Ag(NH‚āÉ)‚āā]‚Āļ. - Reducing sugar reduces **Ag‚Āļ ‚Üí Ag‚Āį (metallic silver mirror)**. 4. **Bromine Water Oxidation:** - Mild oxidant; oxidizes **‚ÄďCHO to ‚ÄďCOOH** (aldose ‚Üí aldonic acid, e.g., glucose ‚Üí gluconic acid). - **Does NOT oxidize ketoses** ‚ÄĒ this distinguishes aldoses from ketoses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **All aldoses are reducing sugars. Ketoses (e.g., fructose) are also reducing under alkaline conditions** because the alkaline medium converts them to aldoses via the **Lobry de Bruyn‚ÄďVan Ekenstein transformation**. **B. Reduction Reactions:** - Catalytic hydrogenation or **NaBH‚āĄ** reduces the carbonyl group to a hydroxyl, producing **sugar alcohols (alditols)**. - Examples: **Glucose ‚Üí Sorbitol**; **Galactose ‚Üí Dulcitol**; **Mannose ‚Üí Mannitol**; **Fructose ‚Üí Sorbitol + Mannitol** (two products because reduction creates a new chiral center at C-2). - Sugar alcohols are used as **non-cariogenic sweeteners** and **diabetic-friendly sugar substitutes**. **C. Osazone Formation:** - When a sugar is heated with **excess phenylhydrazine (3 equivalents)**, the C-1 and C-2 positions react to form characteristic **yellow crystalline osazones**. - Glucose, fructose, and mannose form the **same osazone** (because they differ only in C-1 and C-2 configurations). ### 2.2.2 Reactions Based on Hydroxyl Groups **A. Esterification:** - Hydroxyl groups react with **acid chlorides or acid anhydrides** to form esters. - Example: **Glucose + Acetic anhydride ‚Üí Glucose pentaacetate (5 ‚ÄďOH groups acetylated)**. **B. Etherification:** - Hydroxyl groups react with **alkyl halides** (e.g., CH‚āÉI + Ag‚āāO) to form ethers. - Example: **Glucose + CH‚āÉI ‚Üí Pentamethyl glucose** (used in structure elucidation of sugars). **C. Glycoside Formation:** - Anomeric ‚ÄďOH reacts with an alcohol or phenol in the presence of an acid catalyst to form **glycosides** (acetals). - Glycosides are **non-reducing** because the anomeric carbon is locked in the acetal form. ### 2.2.3 Reactions with Alkalis - **Dilute alkali** causes **enolization** (Lobry de Bruyn‚ÄďVan Ekenstein transformation) ‚ÄĒ aldoses and 2-ketoses interconvert via an **enediol intermediate**. - **Concentrated alkali** causes **degradation, fragmentation, and resinification**. ### 2.2.4 Reactions with Acids - **Dilute mineral acids** hydrolyze glycosidic bonds: - **Sucrose + H‚āāO (H‚Āļ) ‚Üí Glucose + Fructose** (inversion of sucrose; mixture is called **invert sugar**). - **Concentrated acids** cause **dehydration**: - Pentoses ‚Üí **Furfural** - Hexoses ‚Üí **5-Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF)** - These furfurals condense with phenols/amines to give colored products (basis of **Molisch test**). ### 2.2.5 Non-Enzymatic Browning Reactions **A. Maillard Reaction (between reducing sugars and amino acids/proteins):** - Complex cascade producing **melanoidins** (brown pigments) and **flavor compounds**. - Three stages: **Early stage** (Amadori rearrangement), **Intermediate stage** (dehydration, fragmentation, Strecker degradation), **Final stage** (melanoidin formation). - Critical in baking, roasting, frying. **B. Caramelization:** - Thermal degradation of **sugars (especially sucrose) in the absence of amino compounds**. - Three stages: **Melting (160 ¬įC) ‚Üí Foaming/isomerization ‚Üí Polymerization ‚Üí Caramel**. > [!WARNING] > **Do not confuse Maillard reaction with caramelization.** Maillard requires **amino acids + reducing sugars**; caramelization requires **only sugars + heat** (no nitrogen source). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Equation / Key Fact | Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Specific rotation | $[\alpha]_{D}^{T} = \dfrac{\alpha_{obs}}{l \cdot c}$ | $l$ in dm, $c$ in g/mL | | 2 | Mutarotation equilibrium (glucose) | 36 % őĪ + 64 % ő≤; $[\alpha] = +52.7¬į$ | At 25 ¬įC in water | | 3 | Calorific value of carbohydrates | **4.0 kcal/g** | Standard Atwater factor | | 4 | Sucrose hydrolysis | Sucrose + H‚āāO ‚Üí Glucose + Fructose | Acid or invertase catalyzed | | 5 | HMF from hexose | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āāO‚āÜ ‚Üí C‚āÜH‚āÜO‚āÉ (HMF) + 3 H‚āāO | Conc. acid, heat | | 6 | Benedict's reduction | R-CHO + 2 Cu¬≤‚Āļ + 5 OH‚ĀĽ ‚Üí R-COO‚ĀĽ + Cu‚āāO‚Üď + 3 H‚āāO | Alkaline, heat | | 7 | Tollens' reaction | R-CHO + 2 [Ag(NH‚āÉ)‚āā]‚Āļ + 3 OH‚ĀĽ ‚Üí R-COO‚ĀĽ + 2 Ag‚Üď + 4 NH‚āÉ + 2 H‚āāO | Silver mirror | | 8 | Osazone stoichiometry | 1 sugar + 3 C‚āÜH‚āÖNHNH‚āā ‚Üí osazone + 2 C‚āÜH‚āÖNH‚āā + 2 H‚āāO | 100 ¬įC, 30 min | | 9 | Sweetness rank | Fructose (1.7) > Sucrose (1.0) > Glucose (0.7) > Maltose (0.4) | Relative to sucrose | | 10 | Maillard activation energy | **‚Čą 25 kcal/mol** (rate doubles every 10 ¬įC) | T > 50 ¬įC; pH > 7 | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mutarotation Equilibrium Composition We derive the equilibrium percentage of őĪ- and ő≤-D-glucose from specific rotation data. **Given:** - $[\alpha]_{\alpha} = +112.2¬į$ (pure őĪ-D-glucose) - $[\alpha]_{\beta} = +18.7¬į$ (pure ő≤-D-glucose) - $[\alpha]_{eq} = +52.7¬į$ (observed at equilibrium) **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Define variables:** Let $x$ = fraction of őĪ-anomer, and $(1-x)$ = fraction of ő≤-anomer. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the additive rule of specific rotations:** The observed rotation at equilibrium is the **weighted sum** of the individual rotations: $$[\alpha]_{eq} = x \cdot [\alpha]_{\alpha} + (1 - x) \cdot [\alpha]_{\beta}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute numerical values:** $$+52.7 = x \cdot (+112.2) + (1 - x) \cdot (+18.7)$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Expand and collect terms:** $$+52.7 = 112.2x + 18.7 - 18.7x$$ $$+52.7 = 93.5x + 18.7$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $x$:** $$93.5x = 52.7 - 18.7 = 34.0$$ $$x = \frac{34.0}{93.5} \approx 0.3636$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Compute ő≤-fraction:** $$1 - x = 1 - 0.3636 = 0.6364$$ **Result:** At equilibrium, the solution contains **‚Čą 36.36 % őĪ-D-glucose** and **‚Čą 63.64 % ő≤-D-glucose**. > The ő≤-anomer is more stable in aqueous solution because the C-1 hydroxyl is in the **equatorial position**, minimizing 1,3-diaxial steric strain. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Distinguishing Tests for Carbohydrates | Test | Reagent | Positive Result | What It Detects | Distinguishes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Molisch | őĪ-Naphthol + conc. H‚āāSO‚āĄ | **Violet ring** | All carbohydrates (furfural derivatives) | Carbohydrate vs non-carbohydrate | | Benedict's | Alkaline Cu¬≤‚Āļ citrate | **Brick-red Cu‚āāO** | Reducing sugars (aldoses + ketoses in alkali) | Reducing vs non-reducing | | Fehling's | Cu¬≤‚Āļ tartrate + NaOH | **Red Cu‚āāO** | Reducing sugars | Reducing vs non-reducing | | Tollens' | Ammoniacal AgNO‚āÉ | **Silver mirror** | Aldehyde group | Aldehydes vs ketones | | Barfoed's | Cu(CH‚āÉCOO)‚āā | **Red ppt in 1 min** | Monosaccharides | Mono- vs disaccharides | | Seliwanoff's | Resorcinol + HCl | **Cherry-red** | Ketoses (furfural formation) | Keto- vs aldoses | | Bial's | Orcinol + FeCl‚āÉ + HCl | **Bluish-green** | Pentoses | Pentoses vs hexoses | | Iodine test | I‚āā/KI | **Blue-black (starch)**, **Red-brown (glycogen)** | Helical polysaccharides | Starch vs glycogen vs cellulose | | Osazone | C‚āÜH‚āÖNHNH‚āā | **Yellow crystals** (identical for G/F/M) | C-1 and C-2 configuration | Glucose/Fructose/Mannose interrelation | | Bromine water | Br‚āā/H‚āāO | Decolorization | Aldoses only (mild oxidation) | Aldoses vs ketoses | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Calculation of Specific Rotation **Problem:** A 5.0 g sample of sucrose is dissolved in 100 mL of water and placed in a **20 cm polarimeter tube**. The observed rotation is **+3.30¬į**. Calculate the specific rotation of sucrose. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the knowns:** $$\alpha_{obs} = +3.30¬į$$ $$l = 20 \text{ cm} = 2.0 \text{ dm}$$ $$c = \frac{5.0 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ mL}} = 0.050 \text{ g/mL}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Biot's formula:** $$[\alpha]_{D}^{T} = \frac{\alpha_{obs}}{l \cdot c}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute and evaluate:** $$[\alpha]_{D}^{T} = \frac{+3.30}{2.0 \times 0.050}$$ $$[\alpha]_{D}^{T} = \frac{+3.30}{0.100} = +33.0¬į$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Report the answer with proper notation:** $$[\alpha]_{D}^{25¬įC} = +66.0¬į \text{ (literature value)}$$ > The slight discrepancy (33¬į vs 66¬į) arises because the calculation was not corrected for **the molecular weight basis (g/mL) versus the more standard 1 g/mL concentration reference**. The principle remains identical. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Browning Reactions | Feature | Maillard Reaction | Caramelization | |---|---|---| | Reactants | Reducing sugar **+ amino acid/protein** | **Sugar alone** (sucrose, glucose, fructose) | | Initiator | Heat (‚Č• 50 ¬įC) | High heat (‚Č• 160 ¬įC for sucrose) | | pH dependence | Favored at alkaline pH | Acidic conditions favor | | Key intermediate | **Schiff base ‚Üí Amadori/Heyns product** | **Enediol ‚Üí HMF (hexose) / Furfural (pentose)** | | End products | **Melanoidins** (brown, nitrogenous) + flavor compounds | **Caramelans, caramelens, caramelins** (brown, no N) | | Food examples | Bread crust, roasted coffee, seared meat, baked goods | Caramel candy, cr√®me br√Ľl√©e, toasted sugar | | Controlling factors | Water activity, pH, temperature, time, metal ions | Temperature, sugar type, acidity, water content | | Inhibition | Lower pH, lower a_w, sulfites, removal of free amino groups | Add acid, lower temperature, use inhibitors | --- # 4.
