topics.title: Dextrose Equivalent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dextrose Equivalent # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Dextrose Equivalent (DE)** is a standard industrial and analytical measure that quantifies the total amount of **reducing sugars** present in a carbohydrate syrup, expressed as a percentage of the dry weight of the syrup, calculated on the basis of anhydrous D-glucose (dextrose), which is assigned a reference DE value of **100**. In the context of KTU 2024 Food Chemistry syllabus, DE is specifically discussed in relation to **starch hydrolysis products** (also called glucose syrups, corn syrups, or maltodextrins), where it serves as the principal quality-control parameter to express the **degree of hydrolysis** of starch into shorter saccharide chains. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** > *"Dextrose Equivalent (DE) is defined as the percentage of reducing sugars, calculated as dextrose, in the dry substance of a syrup or hydrolysate, expressed relative to pure anhydrous D-glucose (DE = 100)."* Mathematically, the definition can be stated as: $$DE = \frac{\text{Reducing Sugar Content (expressed as D-glucose)}}{\text{Total Dry Substance Content}} \times 100$$ A reducing sugar is any sugar that possesses a **free anomeric carbon** (a carbon bearing a hemiacetal or hemiketal hydroxyl group in its open-chain form), which allows it to act as a **reducing agent** in redox titration reactions ‚ÄĒ for example, reducing Fehling's solution, Benedict's reagent, or 3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid (DNS) from blue to brick-red copper(I) oxide. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a long pearl necklace representing an **unhydrolyzed starch molecule** (a polymer of hundreds of glucose units joined by őĪ-1,4 and őĪ-1,6 glycosidic bonds). Every pearl is firmly wired to its neighbour, so very few free ends are exposed. This corresponds to starch with **DE ‚Čą 0** (almost no free reducing ends). Now, take scissors and cut the necklace at random points: - A few cuts produce **long chains** ‚Üí many pearls still tied ‚Üí few free ends ‚Üí **low DE**. - Many cuts produce **short chains** ‚Üí more free ends ‚Üí **higher DE**. - Cutting every bond yields **individual pearls** (pure glucose) ‚Üí maximum free ends ‚Üí **DE = 100**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** *DE is essentially a "count of free chain-ends" inside a syrup, normalized against what pure glucose would show. The shorter the average chain length, the higher the DE.* This is why **maltodextrins** (DE 3‚Äď20) behave like long-chain polymers (low sweetness, high viscosity), while **high-DE glucose syrups** (DE 60‚Äď95) are sweet, low-viscosity, and highly reactive in browning reactions. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Standards | Parameter | Standard Value | Use | |---|---|---| | DE of pure anhydrous D-glucose | **100** | Reference standard | | DE of pure maltose (theoretical) | **52.6** | Disaccharide reference | | DE of unhydrolyzed starch | **‚Čą 0** | Polymer end-member | | Molar mass of anhydrous glucose (C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āāO‚āÜ) | **180.16 g/mol** | Stoichiometric reference | | Standard titration reagent | **Fehling's solution** (Cu¬≤‚Āļ-tartrate complex) | Classical DE determination | > [!TIP] > **Why pure glucose = 100?** Because all 100% of its dry mass is itself a reducing sugar. So the "reducing sugar content / dry mass" ratio equals unity, scaled to 100. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why DE Exists When starch is hydrolyzed (by acid, by enzymes like őĪ-amylase and glucoamylase, or by a combination), the products are **mixtures** of glucose (DP-1), maltose (DP-2), maltotriose (DP-3), and longer oligosaccharides and dextrins. Because the mixture composition varies with hydrolysis time, pH, temperature, and enzyme dose, food chemists need **a single number** to characterize the product. DE was introduced (around 1930s‚Äď1940s in the corn refining industry) as this **single-number quality index**. It correlates strongly with: - **Average Degree of Polymerization (DP‚āźŠĶ•)** - **Sweetness intensity** - **Viscosity** - **Hygroscopicity** - **Browning tendency (Maillard reaction)** - **Freezing-point depression** - **Fermentability** ## 2.2 Relationship Between DE and Average Degree of Polymerization Since each starch chain has only **one reducing end** (the anomeric carbon of the terminal glucose unit) and the rest are non-reducing, the **inverse proportionality** is fundamental: $$DE \approx \frac{100}{\overline{DP}}$$ where $\overline{DP}$ is the number-average degree of polymerization. ### Derivation Logic: - A single glucose molecule (DP = 1) has 1 reducing end out of 1 glucose unit ‚Üí ratio = 1.0 ‚Üí DE = 100. - A maltose molecule (DP = 2) has 1 reducing end out of 2 glucose units ‚Üí ratio = 0.5 ‚Üí DE ‚Čą 50 (theoretical 52.6 because of the small free-energy penalty in the glycosidic bond). - A maltotriose (DP = 3) ‚Üí DE ‚Čą 33.3. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Linkage:** Students are often asked: *"If DE of a syrup is 40, what is the average DP?"* ‚ÄĒ Answer: $\overline{DP} \approx 100/40 = 2.5$. ## 2.3 Classification of Starch Hydrolysates by DE | DE Range | Common Name | Typical Properties | Applications | |---|---|---|---| | **0 ‚Äď 3** | Native / unconverted starch | Insoluble, no sweetness | Thickening, encapsulation | | **3 ‚Äď 20** | Maltodextrins | Low sweetness, high viscosity, bland taste | Infant formula, sports drinks, spray-drying carrier | | **20 ‚Äď 38** | Low-DE glucose syrup / "low-sweet" syrup | Mild sweetness, bodying agent | Confectionery, ice cream | | **38 ‚Äď 58** | Intermediate / "regular" corn syrup | Moderate sweetness, prevents sucrose crystallization | Candies, jams, jellies | | **58 ‚Äď 75** | High-DE / "high-conversion" syrup | Pronounced sweetness, Maillard-active | Baking, soft drinks, brewing | | **75 ‚Äď 95** | Very-high-DE syrup | Strong sweetness, hygroscopic, fermentable | Frozen desserts, brewing adjuncts | | **‚Čą 100** | Dextrose / glucose syrup | Crystalline, strongly hygroscopic | Sweetener, fermentation substrate | > [!NOTE] > **Sweetness vs. DE:** DE correlates with sweetness **only up to a point** ‚ÄĒ pure glucose is only ~70‚Äď75% as sweet as sucrose. So doubling DE does not double sweetness. ## 2.4 Methods of DE Determination (KTU 2024 Important) ### A. Lane-Eynon Titration (Classical, Copper Reduction) Uses **Fehling's A + Fehling's B** (alkaline copper-tartrate). The reducing sugar reduces **Cu¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Cu‚Āļ** (blue ‚Üí brick-red Cu‚āāO). The volume of sample required to reduce a fixed amount of copper(II) is the titration end-point (detected by methylene blue indicator). ### B. 3,5-Dinitrosalicylic Acid (DNS) Method (Modern Colorimetric) The reducing sugar reduces yellow DNS to a reddish-brown **3-amino-5-nitrosalicylic acid**, measured at **őĽ = 540 nm** in a spectrophotometer. Cheaper, faster, and safer than Fehling's. ### C. Munson-Walker Method A gravimetric variant ‚ÄĒ the precipitated **Cu‚āāO is filtered, dried, and weighed**, then looked up in a sugar table. ### D. Enzymatic / HPLC Methods Used for research-grade precision; directly measure glucose content. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $DE = \dfrac{m_{RS}}{m_{DS}} \times 100$ | Master DE definition | dimensionless % | | 2 | $\overline{DP} \approx \dfrac{100}{DE}$ | Inverse relationship | dimensionless | | 3 | $m_{RS} = V_{titrant} \times C_{std} \times M_{glucose}$ | Reducing sugar from titration | g | | 4 | $DE_{mixture} = \dfrac{\sum_i (w_i \times DE_i)}{\sum_i w_i}$ | Mass-weighted DE of a blend | % | | 5 | $W_{glucose} = \dfrac{DE \times m_{sample}}{100}$ | Grams of reducing sugar in a syrup | g | | 6 | $m_{DS} = m_{sample} - m_{moisture}$ | Dry substance after moisture correction | g | | 7 | $C_{Cu^{2+}} = \dfrac{n_{Cu^{2+}}^{reduced}}{V_{sample}}$ | Titration stoichiometry (Lane-Eynon) | mol/L | > [!CAUTION] > **Critical Pitfall in KTU Answers:** Students often confuse **"as-is basis"** and **"dry basis."** Always express reducing sugar on the **dry substance** of the syrup. If a syrup is 80% solids, the DE on as-is basis will appear lower than its true DE. Multiply by 100/(100 ‚ąí %moisture) to convert. ## 2.6 Engineering / Food-Industry Utility - **Confectionery:** DE 38‚Äď55 syrups are blended with sucrose to inhibit crystallization in hard candies. - **Brewing:** High-DE syrups (DE 60‚Äď70) serve as fermentable adjuncts replacing malt. - **Dairy & Ice Cream:** Maltodextrins (DE 10‚Äď20) improve body and prevent lactose crystallization. - **Baked Goods:** High-DE syrups accelerate Maillard browning and yeast fermentation. - **Pharmaceutical Encapsulation:** Maltodextrins (DE 18‚Äď20) are ideal wall materials for spray-dried flavors. - **Biorefinery:** DE is the master control variable in enzymatic saccharification plants. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Master DE Formula from First Principles **Starting Premise:** Pure anhydrous D-glucose is defined as the *reference* reducing sugar with DE ‚Č° 100. This is an arbitrary but universally accepted convention. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Define the reducing sugar content of pure glucose:** For a sample of pure anhydrous glucose of mass $m_g$, the entire mass is itself a reducing sugar: $$m_{RS,\;pure\;glucose} = m_g$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Define the dry substance of pure glucose:** Since pure glucose contains no water, ash, or impurities: $$m_{DS,\;pure\;glucose} = m_g$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute the ratio for pure glucose:** $$\left(\frac{m_{RS}}{m_{DS}}\right)_{pure\;glucose} = \frac{m_g}{m_g} = 1.000$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Scale to percentage and assign reference DE = 100:** $$DE_{pure\;glucose} = 1.000 \times 100 = 100 \quad \blacksquare$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Generalization to any syrup:** For an arbitrary syrup of mass $m_{sample}$ containing a moisture fraction $W$, the dry substance is: $$m_{DS} = m_{sample} \times (1 - W)$$ Let $m_{RS}$ be the mass of reducing sugar (as glucose) in the sample, measured by titration/colorimetry. The DE is then: $$\boxed{DE = \frac{m_{RS}}{m_{sample} \times (1 - W)} \times 100}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Worked numerical example:** - Sample mass: 5.00 g - Moisture: 18.0% (so dry substance = 5.00 √ó 0.82 = 4.10 g) - Reducing sugar (as glucose) found by Lane-Eynon: 1.64 g $$DE = \frac{1.64}{4.10} \times 100 = 40.0$$ Therefore, the syrup is a **regular-conversion corn syrup (DE 40)**, average DP ‚Čą 2.5. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Degree of Polymerization (DPŐĄ) from DE **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reductionist assumption:** Consider a syrup as a collection of linear glucan chains, each of length $n_i$ glucose units. For every chain there is exactly **one reducing end** and $(n_i - 1)$ non-reducing internal units. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total reducing ends in the mixture:** $$N_{RS} = \sum_i N_i$$ where $N_i$ is the number of chains of length $n_i$. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Total glucose units in the mixture:** $$N_{GU} = \sum_i n_i \cdot N_i$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ By definition, the *number-average* degree of polymerization is:** $$\overline{DP} = \frac{N_{GU}}{N_{RS}} = \frac{\sum_i n_i N_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Identify the reducing sugar fraction.** The mass of reducing sugar is proportional to $N_{RS}$ and the dry substance mass is proportional to $N_{GU}$. Therefore: $$DE = \frac{N_{RS}}{N_{GU}} \times 100 = \frac{100}{\overline{DP}}$$ $$\boxed{\overline{DP} = \frac{100}{DE}}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Verification with maltose (DP = 2):** $$DE_{maltose,\;theoretical} = \frac{100}{2} = 50$$ The accepted literature value is **52.6**, the small deviation being due to the free-energy cost of the glycosidic bond and the slightly higher reducing power per gram of free anomeric carbon in the disaccharide. The 50/52.6 difference is an "anomeric correction factor" that KTU questions sometimes probe. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Titration Stoichiometry for Lane-Eynon Determination **Fehling's solution** contains Cu¬≤‚Āļ complexed with tartrate in alkaline medium. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Balanced redox reaction (simplified):** $$\text{R-CHO} + 2\,Cu^{2+} + 5\,OH^{-} \longrightarrow \text{R-COO}^{-} + Cu_2O \downarrow + 3\,H_2O$$ One mole of reducing sugar (glucose) reduces **2 moles of Cu¬≤‚Āļ**. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ In 10 mL of mixed Fehling's solution (A + B):** Standard Fehling's contains 0.05 mol/L Cu¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí 10 mL contains $5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol Cu¬≤‚Āļ. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Moles of glucose required for complete reduction:** $$n_{glucose} = \frac{n_{Cu^{2+}}}{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equivalent mass of glucose:** $$m_{glucose,\,eq} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 180.16 = 0.04504\ \text{g}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Lane-Eynon titre tables are calibrated to give DE directly.** If 0.04504 g glucose neutralizes 10 mL Fehling's, and a syrup requires $V$ mL to do the same: $$\text{Glucose in sample} = \frac{0.04504}{V} \times V_{aliquot}$$ This $m_{RS}$ is then substituted into the master DE formula. --- ## 3.4 KTU Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix | Problem Type | Given | Required Approach | Governing Equation | |---|---|---|---| | **Type I ‚ÄĒ DE from titration data** | Mass of sample, % moisture, titre volume | Compute $m_{RS}$ via standard table ‚Üí divide by $m_{DS}$ ‚Üí √ó100 | $DE = \frac{m_{RS}}{m_{DS}} \times 100$ | | **Type II ‚ÄĒ Average DP from DE** | Reported DE | Take reciprocal √ó 100 | $\overline{DP} = 100/DE$ | | **Type III ‚ÄĒ Blend DE** | Masses and DEs of two syrups | Weighted arithmetic mean | $DE_{blend} = \frac{m_1 DE_1 + m_2 DE_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | | **Type IV ‚ÄĒ Theoretical DE from composition** | Mole fractions of DP-1, DP-2, DP-n | Weight by reducing-end count | $DE = \frac{100}{\sum x_i \cdot DP_i}$ | | **Type V ‚ÄĒ Reverse: glucose content** | DE, total dry mass | Multiply | $m_{glucose} = DE \times m_{DS} / 100$ | ### Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question Type): > A corn syrup sample weighs 10.0 g with 20% moisture. Lane-Eynon titration consumes 15.2 mL of the syrup solution (prepared by dissolving 1.0 g in 100 mL water) to reduce 10 mL of standard Fehling's solution. Calculate: (a) the Dextrose Equivalent; (b) the average degree of polymerization; (c) the grams of maltose equivalent present in 1 kg of this syrup. **Solution:** **(a) DE Calculation** - Mass of syrup in 15.2 mL of prepared solution = (1.0 g / 100 mL) √ó 15.2 mL = 0.152 g - This 0.152 g contains $m_{RS}$ = 0.04504 g of reducing sugar (glucose equivalent) - $m_{DS} = 0.152 \times (1 - 0.20) = 0.1216$ g $$DE = \frac{0.04504}{0.1216} \times 100 = 37.04 \approx 37$$ **(b) Average DP** $$\overline{DP} = \frac{100}{37.04} = 2.70$$ **(c) Glucose in 1 kg of syrup** - Mass of dry substance in 1 kg = 1000 √ó 0.80 = 800 g - Reducing sugar (as glucose) = 800 √ó 37.04/100 = 296.3 g **Answer:** **DE = 37**, **$\overline{DP}$ = 2.70**, **296.3 g of reducing-sugar-as-glucose per kg syrup**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow: From Starch to DE-Classified Syrup ```mermaid flowchart LR rawStarch["Raw Starch Granule
(Long Glucan Chains)"] slurry["Starch Slurry
(30 to 40 percent solids)"] liquefaction["Liquefaction
Heat 105 C with alpha amylase
pH 6.0 to 6.5"] saccharification["Saccharification
Glucoamylase at 60 C
pH 4.5 for 24 to 72 hours"] deBranch["Optional Debranching
Pullulanase or Isoamylase"] refine["Refining
Carbon Decolorization
Ion Exchange"] concentrate["Vacuum Evaporation
to 70 to 85 percent solids"] classify["DE Classification Step
Quality Control Lab Lane Eynon or DNS"] maltodex["Maltodextrins
DE 3 to 20"] lowDE["Low DE Syrups
DE 20 to 38"] medDE["Regular Syrups
DE 38 to 58"] highDE["High Conversion
DE 58 to 75"] vhighDE["Very High DE
DE 75 to 95"] dextrose["Dextrose Syrup
DE greater than 95"] rawStarch --> slurry slurry --> liquefaction liquefaction --> saccharification saccharification --> deBranch deBranch --> refine refine --> concentrate concentrate --> classify classify --> maltodex classify --> lowDE classify --> medDE classify --> highDE classify --> vhighDE classify --> dextrose ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Map: How DE Drives Functional Properties ```mermaid flowchart TD deCore["Dextrose Equivalent DE
Master Quality Index"] p1["Polymer Length
DP average = 100 / DE"] p2["Reducing End Concentration
More chain ends at high DE"] s1["Viscosity
High DE means low viscosity"] s2["Sweetness
High DE means sweeter"] s3["Hygroscopicity
High DE binds more water"] s4["Browning Maillard
High DE means stronger browning"] s5["Freezing Point Depression
High DE means lower freezing point"] s6["Fermentability
High DE is more easily fermented"] app1["Confectionery anti crystallisation"] app2["Brewing adjunct"] app3["Infant formula and sports drink"] app4["Bakery browning"] app5["Frozen dessert texture"] app6["Spray drying carrier"] deCore --> p1 deCore --> p2 p1 --> s1 p1 --> s2 p1 --> s3 p2 --> s4 p2 --> s5 p1 --> s6 s1 --> app5 s1 --> app1 s2 --> app1 s2 --> app2 s3 --> app5 s3 --> app6 s4 --> app4 s5 --> app5 s6 --> app2 s3 --> app3 ``` ## 4.3 DE Measurement Workflow (Lane-Eynon Classical) ```mermaid flowchart LR a1["Weigh 5 to 10 g Syrup"] a2["Determine Moisture
Oven or Refractometer"] a3["Prepare Solution
1 g per 100 mL water"] b1["Pipette 10 mL Fehling A and B
into a conical flask"] b2["Heat to boil"] b3["Add sample from burette
until blue fades"] b4["Add methylene blue indicator
2 to 3 drops"] b5["Titrate to brick red endpoint"] c1["Look up titre volume V mL
in Lane Eynon table"] c2["Compute mass of reducing sugar m RS"] c3["Apply DE = m RS divided by m DS times 100"] a1 --> a2 a2 --> a3 a3 --> b1 b1 --> b2 b2 --> b3 b3 --> b4 b4 --> b5 b5 --> c1 c1 --> c2 c2 --> c3 ``` ## 4.4 DE-Sweetness-Viscosity Triangular Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR v1["DE equals 10
Maltodextrin"] v2["DE equals 40
Regular Syrup"] v3["DE equals 70
High Conversion"] v4["DE equals 100
Pure Dextrose"] v1 --- visc1["High Viscosity
Bland Taste
Bodying Agent"] v2 --- visc2["Medium Viscosity
Mild Sweetness
Anti Crystalliser"] v3 --- visc3["Low Viscosity
Strong Sweetness
Maillard Reactive"] v4 --- visc4["Water Thin
Strongly Sweet
Highly Hygroscopic"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Set A]** **Define Dextrose Equivalent (DE). Why is pure anhydrous glucose assigned a DE of 100?** **Model Answer:** Dextrose Equivalent (DE) is a measure of the reducing sugar content of a syrup, expressed as a percentage of its dry substance, with pure anhydrous D-glucose (dextrose) taken as the reference standard. Mathematically, $$DE = \frac{\text{Reducing sugar (as glucose)}}{\text{Dry substance}} \times 100$$ Pure glucose is assigned DE = 100 because 100% of its dry mass is itself a reducing sugar; thus the ratio $m_{RS}/m_{DS}$ equals 1.0, scaled to 100. This reference anchors the DE scale. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]** **A glucose syrup has DE = 45. Calculate (i) the average degree of polymerization and (ii) the expected sweetness class of this syrup.** **Model Answer:** (i) $\overline{DP} = 100/DE = 100/45 = 2.22$. This is the average chain length. (ii) The syrup belongs to the "regular conversion" class (DE 38‚Äď58), with moderate sweetness (‚Čą 35‚Äď50% of sucrose), and is widely used in confectionery to inhibit sucrose crystallization. --- ## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks) ### Question A ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the principle and procedure of the **Lane-Eynon titration method** for the determination of Dextrose Equivalent. Highlight the chemistry of the reaction involved. **(b) [7 Marks]** A corn syrup sample of mass **6.00 g** contains **20% moisture**. Lane-Eynon titration of a 100 mL solution prepared by dissolving **1.00 g** of this syrup consumed **18.4 mL** to reduce **10 mL of standard Fehling's solution** (calibrated to 0.04504 g glucose equivalent). Calculate the **DE** of the syrup, its **average degree of polymerization**, and the **grams of reducing sugar (as glucose) present in 1 kg of the syrup**. --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Principle and Procedure (7 Marks):** **Principle:** The Lane-Eynon method relies on the **reducing power** of the carbohydrate. In an alkaline medium, the **free aldehyde group** of glucose (or the open-chain form of any reducing sugar) reduces **Cu¬≤‚Āļ (blue, complexed with tartrate)** in Fehling's solution to **Cu‚Āļ**, which precipitates as **brick-red cuprous oxide (Cu‚āāO)**. The volume of sample required to completely reduce a known amount of Cu¬≤‚Āļ is proportional to the reducing sugar present. **Chemistry:** $$R-CHO + 2\,Cu^{2+} + 5\,OH^{-} \longrightarrow R-COO^{-} + Cu_2O \downarrow + 3\,H_2O$$ **Procedure:** 1. Standardize Fehling's A (CuSO‚āĄ¬∑5H‚āāO) and Fehling's B (K-Na-tartrate + KOH) and mix equal volumes immediately before use. 2. Pipette 10 mL of mixed Fehling's solution into a 250 mL conical flask. 3. Heat to gentle boil over a wire gauze. 4. Add the syrup solution from a burette in 1 mL increments at ~15 s intervals, swirling. 5. When the blue colour becomes faint, add 2‚Äď3 drops of **methylene blue indicator**. 6. Continue titration drop-by-drop until the indicator is decolorized (brick-red Cu‚āāO appears at the bottom) ‚ÄĒ this is the end-point. 7. Record titre volume $V$ and look up the corresponding glucose mass in the **Lane-Eynon table**. 8. Apply $DE = (m_{RS}/m_{DS}) \times 100$. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reducing sugar in 18.4 mL of sample solution:** - 1.00 g syrup in 100 mL ‚Üí 18.4 mL contains $(1.00/100) \times 18.4 = 0.184$ g syrup - 10 mL Fehling's = 0.04504 g glucose equivalent - $m_{RS} = 0.04504$ g of reducing sugar in the 0.184 g of syrup **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute dry substance of the 0.184 g sample:** - $m_{DS} = 0.184 \times (1 - 0.20) = 0.184 \times 0.80 = 0.1472$ g **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Calculate DE:** $$DE = \frac{0.04504}{0.1472} \times 100 = 30.60 \approx 30.6$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Average DP:** $$\overline{DP} = \frac{100}{30.6} = 3.27$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Reducing sugar in 1 kg of syrup:** - Dry substance in 1 kg = 1000 √ó 0.80 = 800 g - Reducing sugar as glucose = 800 √ó 30.6/100 = **244.8 g** **Final Answers:** - **DE = 30.6** (Low-DE syrup, maltodextrin-class borderline) - **$\overline{DP}$ = 3.27** - **244.8 g of reducing sugar per kg of syrup** --- ### Question B ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **relationship between Dextrose Equivalent and average degree of polymerization**. Discuss how DE influences (i) sweetness, (ii) viscosity, and (iii) Maillard browning of starch hydrolysates. **(b) [7 Marks]** Two syrups, A and B, of DE **40** and **65** respectively, are blended in the mass ratio **3 : 2** to obtain syrup C. A second blend D is made by mixing **2 kg of syrup C** with **1 kg of pure dextrose (DE 100)**. Compute the **DE of blends C and D**, and the **average DP of blend D**. --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) DE‚ÄďDP Relationship and Functional Correlations (7 Marks):** **DE‚ÄďDP Inverse Relationship:** In any glucan chain, only the **terminal anomeric carbon** is reducing. If the average chain has $\overline{DP}$ glucose units, the fraction of "free reducing ends" is $1/\overline{DP}$. Therefore: $$DE = \frac{100}{\overline{DP}} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \overline{DP} = \frac{100}{DE}$$ A high DE means short chains (many reducing ends); a low DE means long chains (few reducing ends). **Functional Correlations:** | Property | Trend with Increasing DE | Reason | |---|---|---| | (i) Sweetness | **Increases** (up to ~75% sucrose level for DE = 100) | More free glucose/maltose molecules enhance sweet taste | | (ii) Viscosity | **Decreases** | Shorter chains mean less polymer entanglement; water-thin at DE > 90 | | (iii) Maillard Browning | **Increases** | Reducing ends react with amino groups; longer chain ends in maltodextrins are sterically hindered | > [!NOTE] > A practical implication: maltodextrins (DE 3‚Äď20) are virtually non-browning, non-sweet, and highly viscous ‚ÄĒ ideal as fat replacers. Conversely, a DE-95 syrup browns rapidly when heated with proteins. **(b) Blend DE Calculations (7 Marks):** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ DE of Blend C (3 parts A : 2 parts B):** Mass-weighted average: $$DE_C = \frac{3 \times 40 + 2 \times 65}{3 + 2} = \frac{120 + 130}{5} = \frac{250}{5} = 50$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ DE of Blend D (2 kg C + 1 kg pure dextrose):** $$DE_D = \frac{2 \times 50 + 1 \times 100}{2 + 1} = \frac{100 + 100}{3} = \frac{200}{3} = 66.67$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Average DP of Blend D:** $$\overline{DP}_D = \frac{100}{DE_D} = \frac{100}{66.67} = 1.50$$ **Final Answers:** - **DE of C = 50** - **DE of D = 66.67** - **$\overline{DP}$ of D = 1.50** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ‚ÄĒ Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the dry-basis correction** ‚ÄĒ Always express DE on dry solids, not as-is. If a syrup has 20% moisture, you must divide by 0.80. > 2. **Confusing the inverse DP formula with 100/DE** ‚ÄĒ Some students write DE/100 instead of 100/DE. Memorize the anchor: pure glucose (DP = 1) gives DE = 100, so DE √ó DP = 100. > 3. **Blending calculation without mass-weighting** ‚ÄĒ DE does NOT average by mole or volume, only by mass. > 4. **Lane-Eynon endpoint errors** ‚ÄĒ Over-titration gives falsely high DE; under-titration gives falsely low DE. Methylene blue must be added *only* near the end-point, never at the start. > 5. **Ignoring the difference between DE 100 and "100% glucose syrup"** ‚ÄĒ Commercial "glucose syrup" sold as DE 100 is *pure* dextrose monohydrate equivalent; commercial "high-DE syrup" is rarely 100 because complete hydrolysis is uneconomic. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **DE = (Reducing sugar as glucose √∑ Dry substance) √ó 100** ‚ÄĒ the master formula. - [x] Pure anhydrous D-glucose = **DE 100**; native unhydrolyzed starch = **DE ‚Čą 0**. - [x] **DE and $\overline{DP}$ are inversely related:** $\overline{DP} = 100/DE$. - [x] DE classifies starch hydrolysates: **maltodextrins (3‚Äď20)**, **low (20‚Äď38)**, **regular (38‚Äď58)**, **high (58‚Äď75)**, **very high (75‚Äď95)**, **dextrose (‚Čą100)**. - [x] Higher DE ‚Üí higher sweetness, lower viscosity, higher browning, higher hygroscopicity, higher fermentability. - [x] **Lane-Eynon titration** uses Fehling's solution (Cu¬≤‚Āļ-tartrate); 1 mol reducing sugar reduces 2 mol Cu¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí Cu‚āāO. - [x] DNS (3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid) method is the modern colorimetric alternative (őĽ = 540 nm). - [x] **Always compute DE on a dry basis** ‚ÄĒ divide by $(1 - W)$ where $W$ is moisture fraction. - [x] **Blending DE is a mass-weighted average:** $DE_{blend} = \sum m_i DE_i / \sum m_i$. - [x] Maillard browning and fermentability both increase with DE because of more available reducing-end carbonyls. - [x] Industrial applications: confectionery (anti-crystallization), brewing (adjunct), baking (browning), infant formula (maltodextrin carrier), spray-drying (encapsulation wall). - [x] Anomalies: maltose theoretical DE = 50, but reported value 52.6 (anomeric correction); pure glucose is only 70‚Äď75% as sweet as sucrose despite DE = 100. ---

topics.title: Sugar Alcohols - Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sugar Alcohols - Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sugar Alcohols (Polyols / Polyhydric Alcohols)** are defined as hydrogenated derivatives of carbohydrates (monosaccharides, disaccharides, and oligosaccharides) in which the **carbonyl group (C=O)** of the reducing sugar has been chemically **reduced to a hydroxyl group (‚ÄďOH)**, producing a polyhydric alcohol with the general empirical framework:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}\text{O}_n \quad \text{(where } n \geq 4\text{)}$$

They retain the polyhydroxy backbone of the parent sugar but **lack a free anomeric carbon**, which makes them chemically **non-reducing**, **non-cariogenic**, and metabolically **inert to oral microflora**.

The IUPAC-recommended nomenclature term is **"sugar alcohol"**; however, the WHO/FAO Codex Alimentarius prefers **"polyol"**. Under KTU 2024 Scheme, the term "sugar alcohols" is the prescribed terminology for the PCFTT205 syllabus.

### Common Sugar Alcohols in the Food Industry

| Common Name | Chemical Name | Parent Sugar | Molecular Formula |
|-------------|---------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Erythritol | meso-Erythritol | Erythrose | C‚āĄH‚āĀ‚āÄO‚āĄ |
| Xylitol | Xylitol | Xylose | C‚āÖH‚āĀ‚āāO‚āÖ |
| Sorbitol | D-Glucitol | Glucose | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āĄO‚āÜ |
| Mannitol | D-Mannitol | Mannose | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āĄO‚āÜ |
| Galactitol | Dulcitol | Galactose | C‚āÜH‚āĀ‚āĄO‚āÜ |
| Maltitol | 4-O-őĪ-D-Glucopyranosyl-D-sorbitol | Maltose | C‚āĀ‚āāH‚āā‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āĀ |
| Lactitol | 4-O-ő≤-D-Galactopyranosyl-D-glucitol | Lactose | C‚āĀ‚āāH‚āā‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āĀ |
| Isomalt | 6-O-őĪ-D-Glucopyranosyl-D-sorbitol + mannitol | Isomaltulose | C‚āĀ‚āāH‚āā‚āĄO‚āĀ‚āĀ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCFTT205 ‚Äď Module 2):** Sugar alcohols are examined under the topic of "Carbohydrate Derivatives and Food Applications." The Board typically frames 14-mark questions around (a) their distinguishing properties vs. parent sugars, and (b) their role as **low-calorie, non-cariogenic sweeteners and bulking agents**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a regular sugar molecule (say, glucose) as a **tiny, sparkly crystal that loves to react with everything** ‚ÄĒ it browns your toast (Maillard reaction), feeds the bacteria on your teeth (cariogenic), and spikes your blood sugar fast. Now imagine a **"pacified twin"** of that molecule: same shape, same number of carbons, same sweet taste, but the most reactive part (the aldehyde or ketone door at the end) has been **chemically welded shut by adding two hydrogen atoms** and converting it to a hydroxyl group.

That pacified twin is a **sugar alcohol** ‚ÄĒ a "mellowed-out" sugar that:
- **Doesn't brown** when heated (no Maillard, no caramelization to the same extent).
- **Doesn't feed tooth bacteria** (the "numbing tooth" of xylitol is famous).
- **Doesn't spike insulin** as sharply (lower glycemic index).
- **Still tastes sweet and looks like sugar** because the rest of the molecule (the polyhydroxy chain) is intact.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy (Simplified):** Think of a sugar as a **gasoline** (highly reactive, energetic, flammable). A sugar alcohol is like **diesel** ‚ÄĒ it still burns and provides energy, but it burns **slower, cooler, and cleaner** with less dramatic side effects. That's why sugar alcohols are the **"diesel of sweeteners"** in diabetic, dental-care, and reduced-calorie food products.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Syllabus Mandate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants/metrics are **must-know** for KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions.

- **Caloric value of sucrose (reference):** **4.0 kcal/g (16.7 kJ/g)**
- **Caloric value of typical sugar alcohols:** **1.5 ‚Äď 3.0 kcal/g (6.3 ‚Äď 12.5 kJ/g)**
- **Relative sweetness (vs. sucrose = 1.0):**
  - **Xylitol: 1.0** (isocaloric sweetness ‚ÄĒ unique!)
  - Sorbitol: 0.6
  - Maltitol: 0.9
  - Mannitol: 0.5‚Äď0.7
  - Erythritol: 0.7
  - Lactitol: 0.4
- **Glycemic Index (GI) of sucrose:** 65
- **GI of sugar alcohols:** typically **0 ‚Äď 35** (Erythritol = 0; Xylitol = 7; Sorbitol = 9; Maltitol = 35)
- **Heat of solution (Xylitol):** ‚Äď36.6 kJ/kg (highly negative ‚Üí produces a strong **cooling effect** on the tongue)
- **Hygroscopicity threshold (Sorbitol):** 65% RH (begins to absorb moisture above this)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic of Sugar Alcohols

Sugar alcohols are classified along three orthogonal axes that KTU examiners love to test:

1. **By degree of polymerization (DP) of the parent sugar:**
   - **Monomeric polyols** (from monosaccharides): Erythritol, Xylitol, Sorbitol, Mannitol, Galactitol.
   - **Dimeric polyols** (from disaccharides): Maltitol, Lactitol, Isomalt.

2. **By molecular weight / osmotic load:**
   - **Low-MW polyols (MW < 200):** Erythritol, Xylitol, Sorbitol, Mannitol ‚ÄĒ high osmotic effect, used in dental & sugar-free products.
   - **High-MW polyols (MW > 300):** Maltitol, Lactitol, Isomalt ‚ÄĒ lower osmotic effect, used as bulk sweeteners.

3. **By caloric density (EU regulatory classification):**
   - **"Zero-calorie" polyols** (‚Č§ 0.2 kcal/g under FDA ruling): **Erythritol**.
   - **"Reduced-calorie" polyols** (1.5‚Äď3 kcal/g): All others.

## 2.2 Why and How: The Reduction Chemistry

The industrial and conceptual basis of a sugar alcohol is the **catalytic hydrogenation** of the carbonyl carbon:

$$\text{R‚ÄďCHO} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Raney Ni, } 100\!-\!150\,¬įC, \, 50\!-\!150\,\text{atm}} \text{R‚ÄďCH}_2\text{OH}$$

- **Why:** The aldehyde (aldose) or ketone (ketose) is responsible for all "reactive-sugar" behavior (Maillard, caramelization, reducing power). Hydrogenating it **eliminates the open-chain form** and locks the molecule into the cyclic **acetal/hemiketal** form, which has no free anomeric OH to act as a reducing terminus.
- **How:** The reaction is carried out in **aqueous solution** over a **Raney nickel catalyst** at high temperature and pressure. The product is then purified by **ion-exchange chromatography and crystallization**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** The reducing end is "dead" ‚Üí this is why sugar alcohols give a **negative Benedict's / Fehling's test** and are classified as **non-reducing sugars (NRS)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Equation | Formula or Value | Units / Remarks |
|---|---------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | General empirical formula of polyols | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}\text{O}_n$ | $n \geq 4$ |
| 2 | Caloric reduction (vs. sucrose) | $\% \text{Reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{E_{\text{polyol}}}{4.0}\right)\!\times\!100$ | $E$ in kcal/g |
| 3 | Relative Sweetness | $RS = \dfrac{C_{\text{polyol}}}{C_{\text{sucrose}}}$ at iso-sweet concentration | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Cooling effect (heat of solution) | $\Delta H_{\text{soln}} < 0$ (exothermic dissolution) | kJ/kg, negative |
| 5 | Hygroscopicity onset | $RH_{\text{crit}}$ at which $a_w$ changes rapidly | Expressed in %RH |
| 6 | Laxative threshold (single dose) | Typically **> 20 g/day** for sorbitol; **> 50 g/day** for erythritol | g/day adult |
| 7 | Freezing point depression (in ice cream) | $\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$ | K (cryoscopy constant) |
| 8 | Water activity depression | $a_w = \dfrac{p_{\text{solution}}}{p_{\text{water}}^*}$ | Dimensionless, 0 ‚Äď 1 |
| 9 | GI of typical polyol | $GI_{\text{polyol}} \approx 0\!-\!35$ | Sucrose = 65, Glucose = 100 |
| 10 | Osmotic load (single dose tolerance) | Inversely proportional to MW | $MW \uparrow \Rightarrow$ tolerance $\uparrow$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** When writing "general formula" do **NOT** confuse sugar alcohols with **sugar acids** (where ‚ÄďCHO is oxidized to ‚ÄďCOOH, e.g., gluconic acid) or with **deoxy sugars** (where ‚ÄďOH is replaced by ‚ÄďH, e.g., rhamnose). The KTU key specifically penalizes the use of the wrong functional group.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In food process engineering, sugar alcohols are exploited for:

- **Bulking agent functionality:** Replace sucrose's mass without the glycemic shock.
- **Hygroscopicity control:** Polyols like sorbitol bind water ‚Üí inhibit microbial growth, extend shelf life of baked goods, prevent crystallization of jellies.
- **Cryoprotection:** In frozen desserts (ice cream, sorbet), polyols depress the freezing point without the harsh sweetness spike.
- **Dental health products:** Chewing gums (Xylitol) ‚ÄĒ proven to reduce *Streptococcus mutans* colonization.
- **Diabetic & ketogenic foods:** Erythritol & xylitol for low-GI formulations.
- **Tabletting aid:** Mannitol as a directly compressible excipient in pharmaceutical tablets.
- **Plastics/bio-based polymers:** Sorbitol as a monomer for **isosorbide**, used in bio-PET.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Percent Caloric Reduction of a Sugar Alcohol

**Problem (KTU Typical 3-Mark):** Sorbitol has a caloric value of 2.6 kcal/g. Calculate the percent reduction in calories compared to sucrose (4.0 kcal/g). Comment on the regulatory classification.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the reference value (sucrose):**

$$E_{\text{sucrose}} = 4.0 \ \text{kcal/g}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Identify the polyol value (sorbitol):**

$$E_{\text{sorbitol}} = 2.6 \ \text{kcal/g}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the caloric-reduction formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Reduction} &= \left(1 - \frac{E_{\text{polyol}}}{E_{\text{sucrose}}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{2.6}{4.0}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - 0.65\right) \times 100 \\
&= 0.35 \times 100 \\
&= 35\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Regulatory interpretation (KTU 2024 expected answer):**

Since the reduction is **> 25 %** and the final value is **< 3 kcal/g**, sorbitol qualifies under the **EU Regulation (EC) No 1924/2006** as a **"reduced-calorie" sweetener** and may be labelled accordingly. *(Erythritol, with E = 0.2 kcal/g, qualifies as "zero-calorie" under the same regulation.)*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooling Effect (Heat of Solution) ‚ÄĒ Quantitative

**Problem:** Xylitol has a standard enthalpy of solution $\Delta H_{\text{soln}} = -36.6 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ in water. Calculate the temperature drop when **5.0 g** of xylitol is dissolved in **50 mL of water** at 25 ¬įC. Assume specific heat of solution $\approx 4.18 \ \text{J g}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$ and density $\approx 1.0 \ \text{g/mL}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Total mass of solution:**

$$m_{\text{solution}} = m_{\text{water}} + m_{\text{xylitol}} = 50 \ \text{g} + 5.0 \ \text{g} = 55 \ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total heat released (exothermic, sign convention: heat lost by solid = negative; absorbed by solution):**

$$q = \Delta H_{\text{soln}} \times m_{\text{xylitol}} = -36.6 \ \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}} \times 0.005 \ \text{kg} = -0.183 \ \text{kJ} = -183 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply $q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ for the solution warming up from the heat released:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= \frac{-q}{m_{\text{solution}} \cdot c} \\
&= \frac{-(-183 \ \text{J})}{55 \ \text{g} \times 4.18 \ \text{J g}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{183}{229.9} \\
&\approx 0.80 \ \text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Final temperature of the solution:**

$$T_{\text{final}} = 25\,¬įC - 0.80\,¬įC = 24.20\,¬įC$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The ~0.8 ¬įC drop is **not large in absolute terms**, but on the tongue the effect is amplified because the heat is extracted **locally and rapidly** at the taste-bud‚Äďsolution interface. This is the perceptual basis of the famous "Xylitol cool" sensation. In sugar-free mints and chewing gums, this cooling is a **sensory signature** of xylitol.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relative Sweetness Calculation (Iso-Sweet Concentration)

**Problem:** A taste panel found that a 10 % (w/v) sorbitol solution matches the sweetness of a 6 % (w/v) sucrose solution. Calculate the **Relative Sweetness (RS)** of sorbitol.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ At iso-sweetness, perceived sweetness is equal:**

$$C_{\text{polyol}} \times RS = C_{\text{sucrose}}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solve for RS:**

$$\begin{aligned}
RS &= \frac{C_{\text{sucrose}}}{C_{\text{polyol}}} \\
&= \frac{6\,\%}{10\,\%} \\
&= 0.60
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Interpretation:**

Sorbitol is **60 % as sweet as sucrose**. Therefore, to achieve equivalent sweetness, a manufacturer must use **~1.67√ó the mass of sorbitol** compared to sucrose ‚ÄĒ an important mass-balance and cost consideration in product reformulation.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]
**Define sugar alcohols. Why are they classified as "non-reducing" derivatives of sugars?**

**Model Answer:**

Sugar alcohols (polyols) are polyhydric alcohols obtained by the **catalytic hydrogenation** of the carbonyl group (C=O) of the reducing sugar, converting it into a hydroxyl group (‚ÄďOH). The general formula is $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}\text{O}_n$ with $n \geq 4$. Examples: sorbitol, mannitol, xylitol, maltitol.

They are **non-reducing** because:
1. The **free anomeric ‚ÄďOH is destroyed** during hydrogenation ‚Üí no open-chain aldehyde form.
2. They give a **negative Benedict's/Fehling's test**.
3. They **cannot participate in Maillard browning** (no carbonyl-amine condensation).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024]
**Mention the relative sweetness of xylitol, sorbitol, and erythritol with respect to sucrose. State the unique property of xylitol related to enthalpy of solution.**

**Model Answer:**

| Polyol | Relative Sweetness (Sucrose = 1.0) |
|--------|-------------------------------------|
| Xylitol | **1.0** (isocaloric-equivalent sweetness) |
| Sorbitol | **0.6** |
| Erythritol | **0.7** |

**Unique property of xylitol:** Its enthalpy of solution in water is **strongly negative** ($\Delta H_{\text{soln}} \approx -36.6 \ \text{kJ/kg}$), producing a pronounced **endothermic (cooling) sensation** on the tongue when it dissolves. This makes xylitol the preferred sweetener in **sugar-free mints and chewing gums**, where the cooling effect complements the fresh mouthfeel.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks ‚Äď Choice Option A)

### **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the **physicochemical and physiological properties of sugar alcohols**. Compare them with parent sugars and explain their advantages as food additives. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Write the **industrial production process of sorbitol** from glucose with a flow diagram. What are the key parameters that determine product purity? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**1. Physicochemical Properties:**

- **Crystalline nature:** Mostly white, odourless, crystalline solids; some exist as syrups (e.g., maltitol syrup).
- **Solubility:** Variable ‚ÄĒ sorbitol is highly water-soluble (235 g/100g), mannitol poorly so (22 g/100g).
- **Melting point:** Generally higher than parent sugars (e.g., mannitol 165 ¬įC vs. mannose 132 ¬įC).
- **Hygroscopicity:** Sorbitol, maltitol absorb moisture at high RH ‚Üí function as **humectants** in baked goods.
- **Heat of solution:** Negative (exothermic on dissolution) ‚Üí cooling effect (xylitol strongest).
- **Stability:** Thermally stable; do not undergo Maillard or caramelization.
- **Reducing power:** Zero (negative Fehling's, Benedict's, Tollens' tests).
- **Sweetness:** Lower than sucrose for most, except xylitol (= sucrose).

**2. Physiological Properties:**

- **Caloric value:** 1.5‚Äď3.0 kcal/g (vs. 4.0 kcal/g for sucrose).
- **Glycemic Index:** 0‚Äď35 (much lower than sucrose's 65).
- **Cariogenicity:** None (non-fermentable by *S. mutans*).
- **Digestion:** Slowly and incompletely absorbed in the small intestine; fermented in the colon.
- **Laxative effect:** Osmotic diarrhea at high doses (> 20 g/day for sorbitol).

**3. Advantages as Food Additives:**

- Low-calorie bulking agent
- Non-cariogenic ‚Üí dental health products
- Suitable for **diabetic & ketogenic** foods
- Heat-stable ‚Üí suitable for baked & retorted products
- Multi-functional: sweetener + humectant + cryoprotectant
- Prebiotic potential (some polyols selectively feed beneficial gut flora)

**4. Comparison Table (must include in answer):**

| Property | Sucrose (Parent Sugar) | Sugar Alcohol |
|----------|------------------------|---------------|
| Reducing power | Yes | **No** |
| Maillard browning | Yes | **No** |
| Caloric value | 4.0 kcal/g | 1.5‚Äď3.0 kcal/g |
| Glycemic Index | 65 | 0‚Äď35 |
| Cariogenicity | Yes | **None** |
| Hygroscopicity | Low | Variable (often higher) |
| Cooling on dissolution | No | **Yes (negative őĒH)** |

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Industrial Production of Sorbitol from Glucose:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Feedstock Preparation:** High-Dextrose Equivalent (DE ‚Č• 95) corn syrup is purified by **ion-exchange resins** to remove salts, proteins, and color bodies.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Catalytic Hydrogenation:**
The purified glucose solution (40‚Äď50 % w/w) is fed into a **stirred-tank or trickle-bed reactor** containing **Raney nickel catalyst** (5‚Äď10 % Ni on support). Operating conditions:
- **Temperature:** 100‚Äď150 ¬įC
- **Pressure:** 50‚Äď150 atm H‚āā
- **pH:** 5.5‚Äď6.5 (slightly acidic)
- **Residence time:** 1‚Äď3 h

$$\text{D-Glucose (CHO)} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni, }\Delta, P} \text{D-Sorbitol (CH}_2\text{OH)}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Catalyst Removal:** Filtration through **pressure leaf filters** to recover the spent Ni catalyst for recycling.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Purification:**
- **Decolorization** with activated carbon.
- **Ion-exchange polishing** (cation + anion exchangers) to remove residual acids, nickel ions, and color.

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Concentration & Crystallization:** Evaporated under vacuum to 70 % solids (liquid sorbitol syrup) or further crystallized to crystalline powder.

**Key Parameters Determining Purity:**
1. **DE of feed syrup** (higher DE ‚Üí higher yield).
2. **Hydrogen pressure & temperature** (must remain within catalyst tolerance).
3. **pH** (excess acidity can form side products like sorbitan).
4. **Catalyst loading & age** (deactivated Ni ‚Üí incomplete reduction).
5. **Post-hydrogenation purification** (residual Ni, color, off-flavors must be removed).
6. **Microbial load** (HPLC must show ‚Č• 98 % sorbitol, < 0.1 % reducing sugars).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks ‚Äď Choice Option B)

### **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Differentiate between **monomeric and dimeric sugar alcohols** with examples. Explain why dimeric polyols have a higher laxative threshold than monomeric ones. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **functional roles of sugar alcohols** in food formulations (bakery, confectionery, frozen desserts, and pharmaceuticals). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**1. Definition of Monomeric Polyols:**

Derived from the **hydrogenation of a single monosaccharide unit**. The product retains 4‚Äď6 hydroxyl groups on a single carbon chain.
- **Examples:** Erythritol (C4), Xylitol (C5), Sorbitol, Mannitol, Galactitol (all C6).

**2. Definition of Dimeric Polyols:**

Derived from the **hydrogenation of a disaccharide** ‚ÄĒ only the **reducing-end** monosaccharide unit is reduced; the glycosidic bond remains intact.
- **Examples:** Maltitol (őĪ-1,4 linked), Lactitol (ő≤-1,4 linked), Isomalt (őĪ-1,6 linked).

**3. Key Differences Table:**

| Aspect | Monomeric | Dimeric |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Source | Mono-saccharide | Di-saccharide |
| Molecular weight | 122‚Äď182 g/mol | ~344 g/mol |
| Hydroxyl groups | 4‚Äď6 | 9‚Äď12 |
| Caloric value (avg) | 1.5‚Äď2.6 kcal/g | 2.0‚Äď2.1 kcal/g |
| Sweetness (avg) | 0.5‚Äď1.0 | 0.4‚Äď0.9 |
| Osmotic load | Higher | Lower |
| Tolerance (laxative threshold) | Lower (20‚Äď30 g/day) | Higher (40‚Äď50 g/day) |

**4. Why Dimerics Have a Higher Laxative Threshold (key concept):**

The laxative effect is **osmotic in nature** ‚ÄĒ unabsorbed polyols draw water into the intestinal lumen, causing loose stools. The **osmotic load per molecule** is governed by the **number of moles ingested**, not the mass.

- For a given **mass** of polyol, **monomeric polyols produce ~2√ó the number of moles** as dimeric polyols (since MW is half).
- Hence, dimeric polyols (e.g., maltitol, MW 344) produce **fewer moles per gram**, exerting **lower osmotic pressure per gram ingested**.
- This shifts the **laxative dose-response curve** to higher intakes ‚Üí **higher tolerance** before diarrhea occurs.

**Mathematical Justification:**

$$\pi = i \cdot c \cdot R \cdot T \quad \text{where} \quad c = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{m/MW}{V}$$

For fixed $m$ and $V$: $c \propto \frac{1}{MW}$. Doubling MW halves $c$ and therefore halves $\pi$. Thus, dimeric polyols ($\pi_{\text{dimer}} \approx 0.5 \pi_{\text{monomer}}$ per gram).

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Functional Roles of Sugar Alcohols in Food & Pharma:**

**1. Bakery Products:**
- **Sorbitol & maltitol** as humectants ‚Üí retain freshness, prevent staling.
- Provide **browning-free sweetness** in cookies and cakes (no Maillard, hence light-colored "angel cake"-style products).
- Improve **plasticity and shelf life** of doughs.

**2. Confectionery (sugar-free chocolates, mints, chewing gums):**
- **Xylitol** ‚Üí cooling effect + non-cariogenic.
- **Maltitol** ‚Üí closest taste profile to sucrose; used in sugar-free chocolate.
- **Isomalt** ‚Üí low hygroscopicity, suitable for **hard-boiled candies** (does not crystallize or absorb water).

**3. Frozen Desserts (ice cream, sorbet):**
- **Sorbitol & maltitol** ‚Üí freezing point depression without excessive sweetness.
- **Cryoprotective effect** ‚Üí smaller ice crystals, smoother texture.
- Calorie reduction of 30‚Äď50 % vs. full-sugar formulations.

**4. Pharmaceuticals:**
- **Mannitol** ‚Üí directly compressible tablet excipient, diuretic (osmotic).
- **Sorbitol** ‚Üí syrup base, laxative, sweetening agent in liquid formulations.
- **Xylitol** ‚Üí in chewable tablets, prevents dental caries in pediatric syrups.
- **Isomalt** ‚Üí bulk sweetener in lozenges, dissolution control.

**5. Additional Roles (board marks):**
- **Prebiotic effect** ‚ÄĒ selectively feeds *Bifidobacteria*.
- **Moisture control** ‚ÄĒ sorbitol in surimi, processed meats.
- **Plastics/polymers** ‚ÄĒ sorbitol-derived isosorbide for bio-PET.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (per KTU 2024 valuation key):**
> 1. **Confusing "sugar alcohol" with "sugar acid" or "amino sugar"** ‚ÄĒ Read the reducing end carefully; sugar alcohol = **‚ÄďCH‚āāOH** at C1, NOT ‚ÄďCOOH.
> 2. **Stating the wrong relative sweetness** ‚ÄĒ Students often write sorbitol = 1.0 (it is xylitol!). Memorize the table in ¬ß3.4.
> 3. **Forgetting the cooling-effect mechanism** ‚ÄĒ Many students state "xylitol feels cool" without the *heat-of-solution* explanation. Always quantify: $\Delta H_{\text{soln}} \approx -36.6 \ \text{kJ/kg}$.
> 4. **Ignoring the laxative threshold** ‚ÄĒ Examiners *will* award a mark for the dose-response (osmotic) basis of GI tolerance. Skipping this costs 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 5. **Hand-drawn flow diagrams in part (b) production questions** ‚ÄĒ A **labelled block flow diagram with T, P, and catalyst** is worth 2‚Äď3 marks on its own. Skipping it = large mark loss.
> 6. **Wrong units for calories** ‚ÄĒ Use **kcal/g** consistently (not kJ/g in one line, kcal/g in another).
> 7. **Writing "sugar alcohol is a sugar"** ‚ÄĒ Strictly, it is a **sugar derivative**; examiners deduct for sloppy terminology.

---
