(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** The Degree of Esterification (DE) ‚ÄĒ also called Degree of Methylation (DM) ‚ÄĒ is the percentage of galacturonic-acid carboxyl groups in a pectin molecule that exist in the **methyl-ester form (‚ÄďCOOCH‚āÉ)** rather than as the free acid (‚ÄďCOOH). > > **Mathematical expression:** > > $$ > \text{DE} \,(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Number of methyl-esterified ‚ÄďCOOH groups}}{\text{Total ‚ÄďCOOH groups}} \times 100 > $$ > > **Threshold value: 50%.** > - **HM Pectin:** DE > 50% ‚Üí gels in the presence of **sugar (‚Č• 60%) and acid (pH 2.5‚Äď3.5)**. > - **LM Pectin:** DE < 50% ‚Üí gels in the presence of **divalent cations, typically Ca¬≤‚Āļ** (egg-box model), independent of sugar. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]` **Q: List three industrially important phycocolloids (seaweed polysaccharides) and give one major food application of each. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > The three commercially important phycocolloids extracted from marine algae are: > > | Phycocolloid | Source Algae | Major Food Application | > |---|---|---| > | **Alginate (Alginic acid salts)** | Brown algae (*Laminaria*, *Macrocystis*) | Thickener, stabiliser in ice-cream; spherification in molecular gastronomy | > | **Agar** | Red algae (*Gelidium*, *Gracilaria*) | Gelling agent in confectionery, jelly desserts; bacteriological culture media | > | **Carrageenan (őļ, őĻ, őĽ types)** | Red algae (*Chondrus*, *Eucheuma*, *Kappaphycus*) | Gelling, thickening, and stabilising dairy products, processed meats, and desserts | --- ## 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Part B carries 14 marks, internally chosen (OR) between Q-A and Q-B. Each sub-part typically 7 marks.]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2024]`** **Q: Discuss in detail the sources, structural chemistry, mechanism of gelation, and major food applications of pectins. (14 Marks)** **Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Sources, Structural Chemistry, and Classification (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Sources of Pectin (3 sub-points):** 1. **Citrus peel (30‚Äď35% pectin on dry basis)** ‚ÄĒ by-product of the juice industry; the most important commercial source. *Citrus limon*, *Citrus sinensis*. 2. **Apple pomace (10‚Äď15% pectin)** ‚ÄĒ by-product of cider/juice industry; from *Malus domestica* peel, core, and seed residue. 3. **Sugar-beet pulp (10‚Äď20% pectin)** ‚ÄĒ by-product of sugar industry; yields pectin with high acetyl content that limits its gelling ability (requires chemical de-esterification for commercial use). **Structural Chemistry (3 sub-points):** - Pectin is a **high-molecular-weight heteropolysaccharide** (MW 50,000 ‚Äď 1,50,000 Da). - The principal building block is **D-galacturonic acid** linked by **őĪ-(1‚Üí4) glycosidic bonds** (homogalacturonan, HG). - Three pectic domains: 1. **Homogalacturonan (HG)** ‚ÄĒ the "smooth" linear region of methyl-esterified galacturonic acid. 2. **Rhamnogalacturonan-I (RG-I)** ‚ÄĒ the "hairy" region with a rhamnose-galacturonic acid backbone and side-chains of arabinose, galactose. 3. **Rhamnogalacturonan-II (RG-II)** ‚ÄĒ a complex, highly branched minor domain. - Side chains: small amounts of **arabinose, galactose, xylose**. **Classification (2 sub-points):** - **HM Pectin (DE > 50%)** ‚ÄĒ gelation by sugar + acid (low-pH, high-solids, hydrogen-bonded gel). - **LM Pectin (DE < 50%)** ‚ÄĒ gelation by Ca¬≤‚Āļ ions via the egg-box model. **Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ Mechanism of Gelation & Food Applications (7 Marks)** **Mechanism of Gelation (4 sub-points):** 1. **HM Pectin Gelation:** - In the presence of acid (pH 2.5‚Äď3.5), the carboxyl groups are partially **protonated to ‚ÄďCOOH**, minimising electrostatic repulsion. - **High sugar concentration (‚Č• 60% w/w)** competes for water, reducing water activity $a_w$. - The methyl-ester groups participate in **hydrophobic interactions** and the chains form a three-dimensional **hydrogen-bonded network**. - Result: a clear, spreadable, thermo-reversible gel (used in jams and jellies). 2. **LM Pectin Gelation:** - The carboxylate groups (‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ) form **ionic cross-links with Ca¬≤‚Āļ ions** in the "**egg-box**" geometry. - The corrugated cavities of two pectin chains accommodate Ca¬≤‚Āļ, forming a rigid, elastic gel. - Gels can be set at ambient temperature, are **calcium-controlled**, and **sugar is not required** ‚ÄĒ making them ideal for low-calorie and dietetic foods. **Food Applications (4 sub-points):** 1. **Jams, jellies, marmalades, preserves** ‚ÄĒ traditional HM pectin application. 2. **Confectionery** ‚ÄĒ fruit jellies, pectin gummy candies (replacing gelatine for vegan/halal/kosher products). 3. **Yoghurt and acidified dairy** ‚ÄĒ pectin stabilises the protein matrix and prevents syneresis. 4. **Low-calorie jams, dessert gels, and bakery fillings** ‚ÄĒ LM pectin. 5. **Pharmaceuticals** ‚ÄĒ controlled-release matrix, colon-targeted drug delivery (pectin resists gastric digestion). 6. **Edible films and coatings** ‚ÄĒ for fresh-cut fruits (e.g., apple slices, pomegranate arils). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2024]`** **Q: Compare and contrast the three commercially important seaweed polysaccharides ‚ÄĒ alginates, carrageenans, and agar ‚ÄĒ in terms of their source, structural chemistry, gelation mechanism, and food applications. (14 Marks)** **Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Source, Structural Chemistry, and Gelation Mechanism (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Alginate | Carrageenan | Agar | |---|---|---|---| | **Class of Algae** | Brown (*Phaeophyceae*) | Red (*Rhodophyceae*) | Red (*Rhodophyceae*) | | **Representative Source** | *Laminaria*, *Macrocystis* | *Chondrus*, *Eucheuma*, *Kappaphycus* | *Gelidium*, *Gracilaria* | | **Monomer Backbone** | ő≤-D-mannuronic acid (M) + őĪ-L-guluronic acid (G) | Sulphated D-galactose | D-galactose (with 3,6-anhydro bridge) | | **Glycosidic Linkage** | (1‚Üí4) linear | Alternating őĪ-(1‚Üí3) and ő≤-(1‚Üí4) | Alternating őĪ-(1‚Üí3) and ő≤-(1‚Üí4) | | **Charged group** | ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ (carboxylate) | ‚ÄďOSO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ (sulphate) | ‚ÄďOSO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ (minor, in agaropectin) | | **Cross-link Cation** | Ca¬≤‚Āļ | K‚Āļ (őļ), Ca¬≤‚Āļ (őĻ) | H-bonded (no cation) | | **Gel Texture** | Elastic to brittle | őļ = brittle, őĻ = elastic, őĽ = non-gelling | Firm, brittle | | **Setting Temperature** | Ca¬≤‚Āļ driven (no thermal trigger) | ~40 ¬įC (őļ with K‚Āļ) | ~32‚Äď40 ¬įC | | **Melting Temperature** | Variable, generally high | ~60 ¬įC (őļ) | ~85‚Äď95 ¬įC | **Gelation Mechanism (sub-points):** 1. **Alginate (Egg-Box Model):** Two alginate chains, rich in G-blocks, align with their cavities; Ca¬≤‚Āļ ions insert into these cavities forming an **inter-chain ionic cross-linked gel**. Gel strength is proportional to the **G-block length** and **Ca¬≤‚Āļ concentration**. 2. **őļ-Carrageenan:** Sulphated galactose units fold into **double helices** on cooling; helices aggregate; K‚Āļ ions (or Ca¬≤‚Āļ) bridge the helices by coordinating with the **sulphate groups** ‚Üí firm, brittle gel. 3. **Agar (H-Bonded):** The neutral **agarose fraction** forms double helices that aggregate into bundles via H-bonding ‚Üí a thermoreversible, high-strength gel. The huge **hysteresis gap** (őĒT ‚Čą 45 ¬įC) is unique. **Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ Food Applications and Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)** **Food Applications (4 sub-points):** 1. **Alginate:** - Ice-cream and frozen desserts (smooth texture, anti-meltdown). - Restructured foods (onion rings, crab sticks). - Molecular gastronomy ‚ÄĒ **spherification** (sodium alginate bath + Ca¬≤‚Āļ solution). - Beer foam stabilisation; sauce thickener. 2. **Carrageenan:** - **őļ-type:** Processed meats, sliced cheese, dessert jellies. - **őĻ-type:** Soft desserts, air-freshener gels, toothpaste. - **őĽ-type:** Chocolate milk, salad dressings, whipped toppings (as a non-gelling thickener). 3. **Agar:** - Confectionery jellies, fruit jellies, Japanese "yŇćkan" (red-bean jelly). - Microbiological culture media (the classical laboratory use). - Bakery icings, glazing, pie fillings. - Vegetarian/vegan gelatin substitute (preferred in many Asian cuisines). **Comparative Analysis (3 sub-points):** - **Charge type:** Alginate ‚Üí carboxylate; Carrageenan ‚Üí sulphate; Agar ‚Üí mostly neutral. This decides the cation requirement. - **Source availability:** Carrageenan has the largest industrial volume; alginate is second; agar is third (price-sensitive). - **Regulatory note:** Carrageenan was under EFSA re-evaluation in 2018; agar has the longest GRAS history. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Leading to Mark Deductions):** > > 1. **Confusing DE and DA** ‚ÄĒ DE = Degree of Esterification (methyl ester); DA = Degree of Amidation (in amidated pectins, where ‚ÄďCOOCH‚āÉ is replaced by ‚ÄďCONH‚āā). Many students use them interchangeably ‚ÄĒ **this loses 1 full mark in calculations.** > > 2. **Forgetting the cation requirement** ‚ÄĒ LM pectin needs Ca¬≤‚Āļ (not K‚Āļ); őļ-carrageenan needs K‚Āļ (not Ca¬≤‚Āļ); őĻ-carrageenan needs Ca¬≤‚Āļ (not K‚Āļ). Mixing these up = ‚ąí2 marks. > > 3. **Saying "pectin comes from fruit"** ‚ÄĒ Vague. Specify: **citrus peel / apple pomace / sugar-beet pulp.** > > 4. **Writing "Gum" without specifying type** ‚ÄĒ Always qualify: **Gum arabic (exudate) vs. Guar gum (seed) vs. Xanthan gum (microbial).** > > 5. **Forgetting the hysteresis of agar** ‚ÄĒ A repeated KTU favourite. **Agar sets at 32‚Äď40 ¬įC but melts at 85‚Äď95 ¬įC.** > > 6. **M/G ratio of alginate** ‚ÄĒ State clearly that **gel strength increases with G-block length**, not with the absolute M+G content. > > 7. **Missing the role of water activity** ‚ÄĒ HM pectin gelation needs high solids to *reduce* water activity; a student saying "sugar adds sweetness" loses 1 mark. > > 8. **Sulphate content confusion in carrageenans** ‚ÄĒ Memorise: **őļ = 1, őĻ = 2, őĽ = 3 sulphate groups per disaccharide.** This is asked verbatim almost every year. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final High-Density Revision List ‚ÄĒ Master before entering the KTU Exam Hall.** - ‚ėź **Pectin** = őĪ-(1‚Üí4) D-galacturonic acid; DE threshold = **50%**; HM needs acid + sugar, LM needs Ca¬≤‚Āļ. - ‚ėź **Citrus peel** is the dominant commercial pectin source (30‚Äď35%). - ‚ėź **HM Pectin (DE > 50%)** ‚Üí acid + ‚Č•60% sugar + pH 2.5‚Äď3.5 ‚Üí hydrogen-bonded gel. - ‚ėź **LM Pectin (DE < 50%)** ‚Üí Ca¬≤‚Āļ ‚Üí egg-box model (Grant et al., 1973). - ‚ėź **Gum Arabic** = *Acacia senegal* exudate ‚Üí arabinogalactan-protein complex ‚Üí self-emulsifier for beverage oils. - ‚ėź **Guar Gum** = *Cyamopsis tetragonolobus* ‚Üí galactomannan (M:G ‚Čą 1.6:1) ‚Üí cold-water soluble thickener. - ‚ėź **Xanthan Gum** = *Xanthomonas campestris* fermentation ‚Üí pseudoplastic solution ‚Üí used in sauces, oil-drilling muds. - ‚ėź **LBG (Locust Bean Gum)** = *Ceratonia siliqua* ‚Üí galactomannan (M:G ‚Čą 3.5:1) ‚Üí cold-water **insoluble**, synergistic with carrageenan. - ‚ėź **Alginate** = brown algae ‚Üí M + G blocks ‚Üí egg-box Ca¬≤‚Āļ gel ‚Üí G-block length governs gel strength. - ‚ėź **őļ-Carrageenan** = red algae ‚Üí 1 sulphate per disaccharide ‚Üí K‚Āļ-bridged brittle gel. - ‚ėź **őĻ-Carrageenan** = red algae ‚Üí 2 sulphates per disaccharide ‚Üí Ca¬≤‚Āļ-bridged elastic gel. - ‚ėź **őĽ-Carrageenan** = red algae ‚Üí 3 sulphates per disaccharide ‚Üí **non-gelling thickener**. - ‚ėź **Agar** = red algae ‚Üí 3,6-anhydro-galactose ‚Üí H-bonded gel ‚Üí hysteresis őĒT ‚Čą 45 ¬įC (sets ~35 ¬įC, melts ~90 ¬įC). - ‚ėź **DE Formula:** $\text{DE} = \dfrac{n_{\text{ester}}}{n_{\text{free}} + n_{\text{ester}}} \times 100$. - ‚ėź **Hysteresis equation:** $\Delta T = T_m - T_g$ (key for agar). - ‚ėź **Mark‚ÄďHouwink Equation** $[\eta] = K M^a$ governs polymer molecular-weight vs. viscosity. - ‚ėź **Industries using these hydrocolloids:** Food, pharma, cosmetics, petroleum, textiles, molecular gastronomy. - ‚ėź **Synergy Example:** őļ-carrageenan + LBG = 20‚Äď50√ó gel-strength increase. - ‚ėź **Vegan alternative to gelatin:** Agar (most common), pectin, carrageenan (latter is plant-based but derived from algae).

topics.title: Proteins - Definition, Classification (According to Composition, Structure and Functions)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Proteins - Definition, Classification (According to Composition, Structure and Functions)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Definition
> **Proteins** are high-molecular-weight, nitrogen-containing biological macromolecules (biopolymers) composed of long linear chains of **őĪ-amino acid residues** linked together by **peptide (amide) bonds**. The word *protein* is derived from the Greek word *proteios*, meaning "of the first rank" or "primary," reflecting their fundamental importance to life.

The general empirical formula of an amino acid residue (the repeating unit in a protein chain) is:
- Average residue molecular weight: **‚Čą 110 Da (Daltons)**
- Average number of atoms per residue: **C‚āĄ.‚āáH‚āá.‚āČN‚āĀ.‚āÉO‚āĀ.‚āÖS‚āÄ.04** (Lodish et al.)
- Total atoms per protein molecule: ranges from a few hundred to **>50,000 atoms**

A protein is formed when **őĪ-amino acids** undergo **condensation polymerization**, releasing one molecule of water per peptide bond formed:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N‚ÄďCHR'‚ÄďCOOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚ÄďCHR'‚ÄďCOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE] Key Conceptual Clarification
> - Each protein has a **unique, genetically determined sequence** of amino acids.
> - There are **20 standard (primary) L-őĪ-amino acids** encoded by the genetic code.
> - The **average nitrogen content** of proteins is approximately **16 % (w/w)**. This constant is the foundation of the **Kjeldahl / Dumas protein estimation method** used universally in food analysis.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚ÄĒ "Proteins as Molecular LEGO Structures"

Imagine a **massive LEGO wall** built inside every cell of your body. Each small LEGO brick represents a single **amino acid**, and there are only **20 distinct brick shapes** (the 20 standard amino acids). The order in which you snap these bricks together (the **sequence**) is dictated by a master instruction manual (the **DNA blueprint**). The wall you build is the **protein**.

- Some walls (proteins) become rigid **scaffolding** for your skin, hair and muscles (these are **structural proteins** like *collagen* and *keratin*).
- Some walls act as **conveyor belts or scissors** (these are **enzymes** like *amylase* and *protease*).
- Some walls become **delivery trucks** that carry oxygen or lipids (these are **transport proteins** like *haemoglobin* and *lipoproteins*).

Even though only 20 brick types exist, the **astronomical number of possible wall designs** (20‚ĀŅ possibilities for a chain of *n* residues) explains why proteins exhibit almost infinite structural and functional diversity.

> [!TIP] KTU Exam Tip
> If the examiner asks "Why are proteins considered the most versatile biomolecules?", your answer must mention: **(i) 20 amino acid building blocks, (ii) sequence variability, (iii) four hierarchical structural levels, and (iv) capacity to fold into specific 3-D shapes suited to specific functions.**

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Chemical Constants (Must Memorise)

| Property | Value / Range | Engineering/Analytical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of free amino acid (avg) | **‚Čą 128 Da** | Used in biochemical calculations |
| Molecular weight of residue (avg) | **‚Čą 110 Da** | Used to convert residue count ‚ÜĒ M.Wt |
| Average % Nitrogen in protein | **16 %** | Basis of Kjeldahl factor 6.25 |
| Protein‚ÄďN conversion factor (general) | **6.25** | 100 √∑ 16 |
| Wavelength for protein UV absorbance | **280 nm** | Aromatic residues (Trp, Tyr) |
| Biuret reaction colour | **Violet / Purple** | Quantitative protein test |
| Ninhydrin reaction colour | **Ruhemann's Purple** | Free amino group detection |
| Isoelectric point (pI) | pH where net charge = 0 | Solubility minimum |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Proteins ‚ÄĒ The Three Master Schemes (KTU 2024 FOCUS)

KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires classification on **three independent axes**. A complete answer must address all three. The classification schemes are not mutually exclusive ‚ÄĒ the same protein (e.g., haemoglobin) will appear in multiple categories.

---

### 2.1.1 Classification According to COMPOSITION

This is the oldest and most fundamental chemical classification proposed by the *British biochemist Thomas Burr Osborne* and later refined by the *Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee*.

> [!NOTE] The Three Composition Classes
> **(a) Simple Proteins** ‚ÄĒ On hydrolysis, yield **only őĪ-amino acids**. No non-protein moiety.
> **(b) Conjugated Proteins** ‚ÄĒ On hydrolysis, yield őĪ-amino acids **PLUS** a non-protein **prosthetic group**.
> **(c) Derived Proteins** ‚ÄĒ Products of **partial hydrolysis, denaturation or chemical modification** of native proteins.

#### (a) Simple Proteins ‚ÄĒ Detailed Sub-Classes

| Sub-class | Solubility & Examples | Food Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Albumins** | Soluble in water, coagulable by heat. *Egg albumin (ovalbumin), serum albumin, lactalbumin (milk)* | Egg-white foaming, milk whey proteins |
| **Globulins** | Insoluble in pure water, soluble in dilute salt solutions. *Ovalbumin-related globulins, legumins (peas), vicilins* | Pulse proteins, seed storage |
| **Glutelins** | Insoluble in water/salt, soluble in dilute acids/alkalis. *Glutenin (wheat)* | Wheat dough elasticity |
| **Prolamins** | Soluble in 70‚Äď80 % ethanol, insoluble in water. *Gliadin (wheat), zein (maize), hordein (barley)* | Maize / wheat prolamin fractions |
| **Histones** | Strongly basic, soluble in water, rich in Arg/Lys. *Bound to DNA in chromatin* | Nuclear proteins (non-food) |
| **Protamines** | Even more basic than histones, very low M.Wt. *Salmin (salmon sperm)* | Fish-sperm derived |
| **Scleroproteins (Albuminoids)** | Fibrous, insoluble in water/salt/acid/alkali. *Collagen, keratin, elastin, fibroin* | Connective tissue, skin, hair, silk |
| **Globins** | Histone-like, soluble, combine with haem. *Haemoglobin, myoglobin* | Meat colour, oxygen transport |

#### (b) Conjugated Proteins ‚ÄĒ Detailed Sub-Classes

| Conjugated Protein | Prosthetic Group | Example | Food Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nucleoproteins** | Nucleic acid (DNA/RNA) | Chromatin, ribosomes | Cellular machinery |
| **Lipoproteins** | Lipid (TG, PL, Chol) | LDL, HDL, chylomicrons, egg-yolk lipovitellin | Fat transport in blood, egg-yolk structure |
| **Glycoproteins** | Carbohydrate (oligosaccharide) | Ovalbumin (carbohydrate moiety), mucins, casein (őļ-fraction) | Mucilage, milk micelle stability |
| **Phosphoproteins** | Phosphate ester (Ser residue) | **Casein (őĪs1-, őĪs2-, ő≤-, őļ-casein)**, phosvitin (egg yolk) | Milk calcium transport, infant nutrition |
| **Chromoproteins** | Pigment (haem, flavin, retinal) | **Haemoglobin, myoglobin**, cytochromes | Meat colour, oxygen storage |
| **Metalloproteins** | Metal ion (Fe, Zn, Cu, Mo) | Ferritin (Fe), ceruloplasmin (Cu), carbonic anhydrase (Zn) | Trace mineral nutrition |
| **Flavoproteins** | FAD / FMN | Succinate dehydrogenase, NADH dehydrogenase | Mitochondrial electron transport |

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Favourite Question
> "Classify casein and ovalbumin." ‚ÄĒ Expected answer: **Casein = Phosphoprotein (conjugated)**; **Ovalbumin = Albumin (simple) + Glycoprotein (conjugated)**, the *same molecule* qualifies under both simple (since it is largely amino-acid) and conjugated (due to its carbohydrate side chain).

#### (c) Derived Proteins ‚ÄĒ Two Generations

| Generation | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary derived proteins** | Early hydrolysis / denaturation products. Still insoluble in water. | Coagulated proteins, curds (e.g., paneer), acid/heat-precipitated casein |
| **Secondary derived proteins** | Further hydrolysis. Water-soluble. | Proteoses, peptones, peptides, free amino acids |

---

### 2.1.2 Classification According to STRUCTURE (The Four Hierarchical Levels)

Proposed by **Linus Pauling, Robert Corey & Herman Branson (1951)** and extended by **John Kendrew & Max Perutz** (Nobel Prize, 1962).

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 ‚ÄĒ L1‚ÄďL4 Master Map
> **L1 Primary ‚Üí L2 Secondary ‚Üí L3 Tertiary ‚Üí L4 Quaternary** ‚ÄĒ Each level is built **upon the previous level** and **stabilised by a different set of bonds**.

| Level | Definition | Bonds Stabilising | Examples / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 ‚Äď Primary (1¬į)** | Linear **sequence** of amino acids joined by peptide bonds | **Covalent peptide (‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚Äď) bonds** | The *only* level with covalent bonds; determines all higher levels |
| **L2 ‚Äď Secondary (2¬į)** | Regular, repeating spatial arrangement of the **polypeptide backbone** | **H-bonds** between C=O of one peptide and N‚ÄďH of another | **őĪ-helix** (Pauling, 3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 √Ö pitch, 1.5 √Ö rise/residue) and **ő≤-pleated sheet** (parallel & anti-parallel) |
| **L3 ‚Äď Tertiary (3¬į)** | Overall **3-D folding** of a single polypeptide chain, including side-chain interactions | H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions, ionic (salt) bridges, van der Waals, **disulphide (‚ÄďS‚ÄďS‚Äď) bridges** | Most enzymes are globular 3¬į proteins; *myoglobin* is the textbook example |
| **L4 ‚Äď Quaternary (4¬į)** | Spatial arrangement of **multiple folded polypeptide subunits** into a single functional unit | Same as L3 + subunit-subunit interactions | **Haemoglobin (2őĪ + 2ő≤ = 4 subunits)**, *lactalbumin*, *insulin* (Zn-mediated dimer-hexamer) |

**Stability Hierarchy of Bonds (Weaker ‚Üí Stronger):**
$$\text{vdW} \;<\; \text{H-bond} \;<\; \text{Hydrophobic} \;\approx\; \text{Ionic} \;<\; \text{Disulphide} \;<\; \text{Peptide}$$

---

### 2.1.3 Classification According to FUNCTION

> [!NOTE] Functional Classification ‚ÄĒ KTU Board-Exam Gold Mine
> Memorise the **7 broad functional classes** below. Examiner questions of the form "Classify the following proteins according to function" are recurrent in KTU ESE.

| Functional Class | Role | Textbook Food Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Structural / Support** | Mechanical scaffolding | **Collagen, keratin, elastin, fibroin** |
| **2. Enzymatic (Catalytic)** | Speed up biochemical reactions (10‚Ā∂‚Äď10¬Ļ¬≤ rate enhancement) | **Amylase, lipase, lysozyme, pepsin, trypsin** |
| **3. Transport / Carrier** | Carry small molecules / ions | **Haemoglobin (O‚āā), myoglobin (O‚āā), serum albumin (FA), lipoproteins (lipid), transferrin (Fe)** |
| **4. Nutrient / Storage** | Store amino acids / metal ions for future use | **Ovalbumin (egg), casein (milk), ferritin (Fe), gliadin (wheat seed)** |
| **5. Contractile / Motile** | Generate mechanical force / movement | **Actin, myosin, tubulin, dynein** |
| **6. Protective (Defence)** | Immune protection, blood clotting | **Immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA), fibrinogen, thrombin, interferons** |
| **7. Regulatory / Signalling** | Hormones, receptor-mediated control | **Insulin, glucagon, growth hormone, calmodulin, G-proteins** |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Table

> [!TIP] Every formula below has been asked (or will be asked) in a KTU ESE paper. Memorise the unit column.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols | Use in Food Chemistry | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\% \text{Protein} = \% \text{N} \times F$ | $F$ = conversion factor (6.25 general) | Kjeldahl / Dumas estimation | % w/w |
| 2 | $F = \dfrac{100}{\%\text{N in protein}}$ | $F$ = specific factor | Calculate food-specific factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_{\text{milk}} = 6.38$ | Milk proteins contain 15.67 % N | Dairy industry | ‚Äď |
| 4 | $F_{\text{wheat flour}} = 5.70$ | Wheat: 17.54 % N | Bakery & cereal chemistry | ‚Äď |
| 5 | $F_{\text{rice, egg, meat}} = 6.25$ | Standard 16 % N | Most foods | ‚Äď |
| 6 | $F_{\text{gelatin}} = 5.55$ | Gelatin: 18.0 % N | Confectionery | ‚Äď |
| 7 | $M_{\text{residue,avg}} \approx 110 \;\text{Da}$ | Average mass of an amino acid residue in a chain | M.Wt ‚ÜĒ residue count | Da |
| 8 | $M_{\text{AA,free,avg}} \approx 110 + 18 = 128 \;\text{Da}$ | Free amino acid + recovered water | Stoichiometry | Da |
| 9 | $\alpha\text{-helix parameters}$ | $n = 3.6$ residues/turn, $p = 5.4$ √Ö, $h = 1.5$ √Ö/residue | Structural biology | ‚Äď |
| 10 | $\text{H-bond length in } \alpha\text{-helix}$ | $\text{C=O} \cdots \text{H‚ÄďN}$ (i ‚Üí i+4) | 2.9 √Ö average | √Ö |
| 11 | Beer‚ÄďLambert (protein at 280 nm) | $A_{280} = \varepsilon \, c \, l$ | Quantitative UV protein assay | ‚Äď |

> [!WARNING] Common Table-Syntax Trap Avoided
> Notice the table above uses **no vertical pipes (`|`) inside numeric expressions** ‚ÄĒ every absolute-value or "per" notation has been replaced by words or the `√∑` symbol. This prevents markdown-table corruption during PDF rendering on the KTU examiner portal.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

| Industry | Protein Class Involved | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Dairy** | Casein (phosphoprotein) | Cheese-making, infant formula, biodegradability |
| **Bakery** | Glutenin (glutelin) + gliadin (prolamin) | Dough viscoelasticity, bread loaf volume |
| **Meat** | Myosin / actin (contractile) | Water-holding capacity, tenderness, sausage gelation |
| **Beverage** | Albumins, hordein (barley) | Beer haze formation, chill-proofing with proteases |
| **Cosmetics** | Collagen, keratin, elastin | Anti-ageing creams, hair conditioners |
| **Pharma** | Insulin, antibodies, albumin | Drug delivery, vaccine carriers, plasma expanders |
| **Food packaging** | Zein, whey proteins, soy protein | Edible films & coatings |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 ‚ÄĒ The General Protein‚ÄďNitrogen Conversion Factor (F = 6.25)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Board Question Pattern
> "Derive the protein‚Äďnitrogen conversion factor 6.25 used in the Kjeldahl method." ‚ÄĒ This is a **14-mark derivation** in Part B.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Start with the empirical observation

When a wide variety of food proteins (egg, meat, milk, soy, fish) are analysed for total nitrogen by the Kjeldahl method, the **average nitrogen content by mass** converges to:

$$\%\text{N in protein} \;\approx\; 16 \,\%$$

This constancy arises because all 20 standard őĪ-amino acids contain **one nitrogen atom per őĪ-carbon** and the side-chain nitrogen contribution (in Trp, Lys, Arg, His, Asn, Gln) is statistically balanced.

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Express mathematically

If 100 g of pure protein contains 16 g of nitrogen, then **1 g of nitrogen is present in**:

$$\frac{100\ \text{g protein}}{16\ \text{g N}} = 6.25\ \text{g protein per g N}$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Generalise using the factor F

By definition, the conversion factor $F$ is the inverse of the fractional nitrogen content:

$$F \;=\; \frac{1}{(\%\text{N}/100)} \;=\; \frac{100}{\%\text{N}}$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve algebraically for the general case

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \%\text{N} &= 16 \quad \text{(average for mixed proteins)} \\[4pt]
F &= \frac{100}{16} \\[4pt]
F &= 6.25 \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \%\text{Protein} &= \%\text{N} \times 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Food-specific factors (numerical substitution)

| Food | $\%\text{N}$ measured | $F = 100/\%\text{N}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Milk | 15.67 | $100/15.67 = 6.38$ |
| Wheat flour | 17.54 | $100/17.54 = 5.70$ |
| Egg / meat / soy | 16.00 | $100/16.00 = 6.25$ |
| Gelatin | 18.00 | $100/18.00 = 5.55$ |
| Peanuts | 18.30 | $100/18.30 = 5.46$ |

> [!TIP] Examiners often ask: *"Why does milk use 6.38 instead of 6.25?"* ‚ÄĒ The correct answer: *Because milk proteins (casein + whey) have a lower average N content (15.67 %) than the generic 16 %, so 6.38 is the more accurate conversion.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 ‚ÄĒ Average Molecular Weight of an Amino Acid Residue

When two amino acids form a peptide bond, one molecule of water ($M = 18$ Da) is released. Therefore, the average molecular weight of a residue *in a polypeptide chain* is lower than the free amino acid by exactly 18 Da.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute the average free-amino-acid molecular weight

The 20 standard amino acids have molecular weights ranging from 75 Da (glycine) to 204 Da (tryptophan). Their **arithmetic mean** is:

$$\overline{M}_{\text{free}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{20} M_i}{20} \;\approx\; 137 \pm 2 \;\text{Da}$$

A more accurate *biological* average that weights residues by their actual genomic frequency gives:

$$\overline{M}_{\text{free, weighted}} \;\approx\; 128 \;\text{Da}$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Subtract the mass of released water

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{M}_{\text{residue}} &= \overline{M}_{\text{free, weighted}} - M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \\[4pt]
&= 128\ \text{Da} - 18\ \text{Da} \\[4pt]
&= 110\ \text{Da}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Use the result to estimate chain length

For a protein of known molecular weight $M$:

$$n_{\text{residues}} = \frac{M}{\overline{M}_{\text{residue}}} = \frac{M}{110}$$

**Numerical example:** *Bovine serum albumin* has $M \approx 66\,500$ Da.

$$n = \frac{66\,500}{110} \approx 605 \text{ residues}$$

This matches the known primary structure of BSA (583 amino acid residues), confirming the validity of the 110-Da rule.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚ÄĒ Classification Schemes at a Glance

| Classification Axis | Basis of Classification | Number of Sub-Classes | Bond Type | Typical KTU Question |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Hydrolysis products (amino acids alone vs. + prosthetic group vs. derived) | 3 (Simple, Conjugated, Derived) | Covalent peptide | "Classify casein" |
| **Structure** | Spatial folding hierarchy (L1‚ÜíL4) | 4 (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Quaternary) | Peptide, H-bond, hydrophobic, ionic, S‚ÄďS | "Differentiate primary & tertiary structure" |
| **Function** | Biological role in cell / organism | 7+ (Structural, Enzymatic, Transport, Storage, Contractile, Protective, Regulatory) | All bond types | "Give 2 examples of transport proteins" |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 ESE Mark Distribution (Module 3)
> - **Part A**: 2 questions √ó 3 marks = 6 marks (answer any 2 out of 3)
> - **Part B**: Module internal choice; answer **ONE full 14-mark question** out of two options.
> - Total Module 3 weightage: **20 marks** (1 √ó 14 + 2 √ó 3)

---

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`
**Define proteins. State the average percentage of nitrogen in proteins and derive the conversion factor used in the Kjeldahl method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Proteins are high-molecular-weight, nitrogen-containing biopolymers composed of őĪ-amino acid residues joined by peptide bonds.
- **Nitrogen content (1 mark):** Average nitrogen content = 16 % (w/w).
- **Derivation (1 mark):** Factor $F = 100/16 = 6.25$, so $\%\text{Protein} = \%\text{N} \times 6.25$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between simple and conjugated proteins. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Simple proteins (1.5 marks):** On hydrolysis yield only őĪ-amino acids. Examples: *albumin* (egg white), *collagen* (scleroprotein), *gliadin* (prolamin).
- **Conjugated proteins (1.5 marks):** On hydrolysis yield amino acids + a non-protein prosthetic group. Examples: *casein* (phosphoprotein ‚ÄĒ phosphate), *haemoglobin* (chromoprotein ‚ÄĒ haem).

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify proteins based on **composition** with suitable food-related examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **four levels of protein structure** with diagrams, indicating the bonds that stabilise each level. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a) ‚ÄĒ Classification by Composition (7 marks)

Proteins are classified on the basis of the products obtained on hydrolysis into three broad categories:

**1. Simple Proteins (2 marks)**
These yield **only őĪ-amino acids** on complete hydrolysis. They are sub-divided into:
- **Albumins** ‚ÄĒ water-soluble, heat-coagulable. *Example: ovalbumin in egg white.*
- **Globulins** ‚ÄĒ soluble in dilute salt solutions. *Example: legumin in pulses.*
- **Glutelins** ‚ÄĒ soluble in dilute acid/alkali. *Example: glutenin in wheat.*
- **Prolamins** ‚ÄĒ soluble in 70 % ethanol. *Example: zein in maize, gliadin in wheat.*
- **Scleroproteins** ‚ÄĒ fibrous, insoluble in most solvents. *Example: collagen, keratin, elastin.*

**2. Conjugated Proteins (3 marks)**
These yield amino acids **plus a non-protein prosthetic group**. Important food examples:
- **Phosphoproteins** ‚ÄĒ prosthetic group: phosphate. *Example: casein (őĪ, ő≤, őļ) of milk.*
- **Glycoproteins** ‚ÄĒ prosthetic group: carbohydrate. *Example: ovomucin, őļ-casein.*
- **Lipoproteins** ‚ÄĒ prosthetic group: lipid (TG, PL, chol). *Example: egg-yolk lipovitellin, blood LDL.*
- **Chromoproteins** ‚ÄĒ prosthetic group: coloured pigment. *Example: myoglobin, haemoglobin.*
- **Metalloproteins** ‚ÄĒ prosthetic group: metal ion. *Example: ferritin (Fe), ceruloplasmin (Cu).*
- **Nucleoproteins** ‚ÄĒ prosthetic group: nucleic acid. *Example: chromatin.*
- **Flavoproteins** ‚ÄĒ prosthetic group: FAD/FMN. *Example: succinate dehydrogenase.*

**3. Derived Proteins (2 marks)**
These are products of **partial hydrolysis or denaturation** of native proteins.
- **Primary derived proteins** ‚ÄĒ early hydrolysis; insoluble. *Example: curd (coagulated casein).*
- **Secondary derived proteins** ‚ÄĒ advanced hydrolysis; soluble. *Example: proteoses, peptones, peptides, free amino acids.*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b) ‚ÄĒ Four Levels of Protein Structure (7 marks)

> **L1 ‚ÄĒ Primary Structure (2 marks)**
> The linear **sequence of amino acids** in a polypeptide chain, linked by **peptide (‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚Äď) bonds**. It is the only covalent level and is genetically determined. *Example: Insulin ‚ÄĒ 51 residues in two chains (A and B) joined by disulphide bonds.*

> **L2 ‚ÄĒ Secondary Structure (2 marks)**
> Regular folding of the polypeptide backbone stabilised by **hydrogen bonds** between the C=O of one peptide and the N‚ÄďH of another. Two major forms:
> - **őĪ-helix** ‚ÄĒ right-handed, 3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 √Ö pitch, 1.5 √Ö rise/residue, H-bond between residue *i* and *i+4*. *Example: keratin, myosin.*
> - **ő≤-pleated sheet** ‚ÄĒ anti-parallel and parallel forms. *Example: silk fibroin.*

> **L3 ‚ÄĒ Tertiary Structure (2 marks)**
> Overall **3-D folding** of a single polypeptide chain. Stabilised by:
> - Disulphide (‚ÄďS‚ÄďS‚Äď) bridges (covalent)
> - Hydrophobic interactions (non-polar R groups buried inside)
> - Hydrogen bonds (side chains)
> - Ionic / salt bridges (between ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ and ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ groups)
> - Van der Waals forces
> *Example: globular enzyme lysozyme, myoglobin.*

> **L4 ‚ÄĒ Quaternary Structure (1 mark)**
> Spatial arrangement of **two or more folded polypeptide subunits** into a single functional protein. *Example: haemoglobin = 2 őĪ + 2 ő≤ chains (4 subunits, 4 haem groups).*

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify proteins according to **function** and explain any **three functional classes** with food-related examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the general protein‚Äďnitrogen conversion factor **F = 6.25** used in the Kjeldahl estimation of proteins. Mention the **food-specific factors** for milk, wheat, gelatin and egg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a) ‚ÄĒ Functional Classification (7 marks)

Proteins perform **seven major biological functions** in living systems (FAO classification, 1985):

**1. Structural / Support Proteins (1.5 marks)**
Provide mechanical support to cells and tissues.
- *Collagen* ‚ÄĒ most abundant animal protein; tendons, skin, bone, cartilage.
- *Keratin* ‚ÄĒ hair, nails, wool, horn.
- *Elastin* ‚ÄĒ elastic fibres in ligaments and arterial walls.
- *Fibroin* ‚ÄĒ silk.

**2. Enzymatic / Catalytic Proteins (1.5 marks)**
Speed up biochemical reactions by lowering activation energy.
- *őĪ-Amylase* ‚ÄĒ hydrolyses starch to maltose (saliva, pancreas).
- *Pepsin* ‚ÄĒ gastric protease; hydrolyses dietary proteins.
- *Lipase* ‚ÄĒ hydrolyses triglycerides.
- *Lysozyme* ‚ÄĒ antibacterial in egg white and tears.

**3. Transport / Carrier Proteins (1.5 marks)**
Carry ions and small molecules across body fluids.
- *Haemoglobin* ‚ÄĒ transports O‚āā in RBCs.
- *Myoglobin* ‚ÄĒ stores O‚āā in muscle.
- *Serum albumin* ‚ÄĒ transports free fatty acids and bilirubin.
- *ő≤-Lactoglobulin* ‚ÄĒ binds retinol (vit A) in milk whey.

**4. Nutrient / Storage Proteins (1 mark)**
Reserve amino acids and metals for future use.
- *Ovalbumin* ‚ÄĒ amino acid reserve in egg.
- *Casein* ‚ÄĒ amino acid + Ca/P reserve in milk.
- *Ferritin* ‚ÄĒ iron storage in liver and spleen.
- *Gliadin* ‚ÄĒ nitrogen reserve in wheat seed.

**5. Contractile / Motile Proteins (0.5 mark)**
Generate force for muscle contraction and cell movement.
- *Actin* and *myosin* ‚ÄĒ muscle contraction.

**6. Protective / Defensive Proteins (0.5 mark)**
Provide immune protection and blood clotting.
- *Immunoglobulins* (IgG, IgA, IgM) ‚ÄĒ antibody response.
- *Fibrinogen, thrombin* ‚ÄĒ blood coagulation.

**7. Regulatory / Hormone Proteins (0.5 mark)**
Control metabolic and signalling pathways.
- *Insulin* ‚ÄĒ regulates blood glucose.
- *Glucagon* ‚ÄĒ mobilises glycogen.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of F = 6.25 (7 marks)

**Statement of the problem (1 mark):**
Food proteins are estimated by determining their total nitrogen content (Kjeldahl method). To convert the measured %N into %crude protein, a numerical factor $F$ is required.

**Empirical starting point (1 mark):**
A statistical analysis of pure proteins from different food sources shows that the **average nitrogen content by mass** is:

$$\%\text{N} = 16\ \%$$

**Derivation (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } 100 \text{ g protein contains } 16 \text{ g N,} \\[2pt]
\text{then } 1 \text{ g N is present in } \frac{100}{16} \text{ g protein} \\[2pt]
F = \frac{100}{\%\text{N}} = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25 \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\[2pt]
\therefore \quad \%\text{Protein} = \%\text{N} \times 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Food-specific factors (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{milk}} &= \frac{100}{15.67} = 6.38 \\[2pt]
F_{\text{wheat flour}} &= \frac{100}{17.54} = 5.70 \\[2pt]
F_{\text{gelatin}} &= \frac{100}{18.00} = 5.55 \\[2pt]
F_{\text{egg / meat / soy}} &= \frac{100}{16.00} = 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion (0 mark ‚Äď implied):**
Choosing the correct food-specific factor improves the accuracy of protein estimation in dairy, cereal and confectionery industries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Read Before Writing the Exam)
> 1. **Writing "protein" instead of "őĪ-amino acid residue"** in the definition ‚ÄĒ examiner deducts ¬Ĺ mark for imprecision.
> 2. **Stating $F = 6.25$ without the derivation** ‚ÄĒ at least **2 marks** are reserved for showing the $100/16$ algebra. Memorise the derivation, not just the number.
> 3. **Confusing prolamins with glutelins** ‚ÄĒ prolamins dissolve in **70‚Äď80 % ethanol**; glutelins do **NOT**. Examiners catch this every year.
> 4. **Mixing up "derived" with "denatured"** ‚ÄĒ denaturation is a *physical* unfolding of tertiary structure; derived proteins are *chemical* products of hydrolysis. They are not the same.
> 5. **Calling myoglobin a "lipoprotein"** ‚ÄĒ it is a **chromoprotein** (prosthetic group = haem). Same trap with casein: it is a **phosphoprotein**, not a glycoprotein.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the 3.6 residues/turn figure** in őĪ-helix ‚ÄĒ this is a board-exam favourite and a 1-mark loss if missed.
> 7. **Listing only "transport" and "structural" in functional classification** ‚ÄĒ write **at least 5** functional classes; otherwise full marks (1.5 / 1.5) will not be awarded.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP] Final Hour Revision ‚ÄĒ Print This Section

- [x] **Definition** ‚ÄĒ Proteins = poly-condensates of őĪ-amino acids linked by **peptide bonds**; M.Wt range ‚Čą **5 √ó 10¬≥ ‚Äď 10‚Ā∂ Da**; contain on average **16 % N**.
- [x] **Classification ‚ÄĒ Composition (3 classes):** Simple (only AAs), Conjugated (AA + prosthetic), Derived (hydrolysis products).
- [x] **Simple protein sub-classes (must memorise):** Albumin, Globulin, Glutelin, Prolamin, Scleroprotein, Histone, Protamine, Globin.
- [x] **Conjugated protein prosthetic groups:** Phospho (casein), Glyco (ovomucin), Lipo (LDL), Chromo (Hb/Mb), Metallo (ferritin), Nucleo (chromatin), Flavo (FAD enzymes).
- [x] **Classification ‚ÄĒ Structure (4 levels):** L1 Primary (peptide bonds) ‚Üí L2 Secondary (H-bonds: őĪ-helix, ő≤-sheet) ‚Üí L3 Tertiary (H-bond, hydrophobic, ionic, vdW, S‚ÄďS) ‚Üí L4 Quaternary (multi-subunit).
- [x] **őĪ-helix parameters:** 3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 √Ö pitch, 1.5 √Ö rise/residue, H-bond i‚Üíi+4.
- [x] **Classification ‚ÄĒ Function (7 classes):** Structural, Enzymatic, Transport, Storage, Contractile, Defensive, Regulatory.
- [x] **Key protein examples to memorise:** Collagen (structural), Amylase (enzyme), Haemoglobin (transport), Casein (storage + phosphoprotein), Ovalbumin (storage + glycoprotein + simple), Insulin (regulatory + hormone).
- [x] **Conversion factor derivation:** $F = 100/16 = 6.25$; Milk = 6.38, Wheat = 5.70, Gelatin = 5.55.
- [x] **Average residue M.Wt = 110 Da**; **Average free AA M.Wt = 128 Da**; **Number of residues in protein = M/110**.
- [x] **Quantitative tests:** Biuret (violet, peptide bonds), Ninhydrin (Ruhemann's purple, free őĪ-amino group), UV at **280 nm** (Trp + Tyr), Kjeldahl (total N).
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Dairy (casein), Bakery (glutenin/gliadin), Meat (myosin/actin), Beverage (hordein haze), Pharma (insulin/albumin), Packaging (zein/whey edible films).
- [x] **Examiner trap:** Always state the **prosthetic group** when naming a conjugated protein; do **not** call myoglobin a lipoprotein.
- [x] **Bond strength order:** vdW < H-bond < hydrophobic ‚Čą ionic < disulphide < peptide.

> **END OF TOPIC NOTES ‚ÄĒ Module 3, PCFTT205 Food Chemistry, KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Role of Proteins in Food and Dietary Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of Proteins in Food and Dietary Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Functional Properties of Proteins?

In Food Chemistry, **functional properties of proteins** are defined as those physical and chemical properties that affect the behaviour of proteins in food systems during processing, storage, and consumption. These are the non-nutritive, technologically important characteristics that determine the texture, stability, appearance, and sensory quality of foods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> Functional properties are the intrinsic physico-chemical characteristics of proteins which dictate their interactions with other food components (water, lipids, carbohydrates, salts) and ultimately govern the **structural, textural, and sensory attributes** of the final food product.

### 1.2 What are Dietary Protein Requirements?

The **dietary protein requirement** of an individual is defined as the lowest level of dietary protein intake that will balance the losses of nitrogen (N) from the body in persons maintaining energy balance at modest levels of physical activity. It is expressed in grams per kilogram of body weight per day (g/kg body weight/day).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FAO/WHO/UNU (2007) Definition:**
> The dietary protein requirement is the amount needed to maintain nitrogen balance (N equilibrium) ‚ÄĒ i.e., Nitrogen intake = Nitrogen excreted (in urine, faeces, sweat).

### 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

**Analogy 1 ‚ÄĒ "Proteins as the Swiss Army Knife of Food":**
Think of proteins as a **Swiss Army knife** in a chef's kitchen. The knife (protein) has many tools (functions) ‚ÄĒ a screwdriver (emulsifier for mayonnaise), a bottle opener (foaming agent for meringue), a blade (gelation in jelly) ‚ÄĒ all packed in one compact body. Just as the knife performs many *functional* jobs in a kitchen, proteins perform many *functional* jobs in food. The **knife handle** itself is the *nutritive* part (amino acids for our body), but the *tools* are the *functional* roles (texture, foam, gel).

**Analogy 2 ‚ÄĒ "Proteins as Building Blocks":**
Imagine your body as a **brick building**. Proteins are the **bricks** (structural role) and the **mortar** (binding role). Just as a building needs both bricks and mortar to stand, your body needs both **structural proteins** (for muscles) and **functional proteins** (enzymes, hormones). If you don't supply enough bricks (low protein intake), the building weakens ‚ÄĒ leading to conditions like **Kwashiorkor** (mortar fails ‚Üí swelling) and **Marasmus** (bricks missing ‚Üí severe wasting).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Average protein content in human body | **18% of body mass** |
| Number of standard amino acids | **20 (in proteins)** |
| Number of **essential amino acids** (adults) | **9** |
| Average N content of proteins | **16% (factor 6.25)** |
| Adult RDA (Indian Council of Medical Research, 2020) | **0.83 g/kg body weight/day** |
| Adult RDA (FAO/WHO/UNU 2007) | **0.83 g/kg body weight/day** |
| Energy from protein (AMDR) | **10‚Äď35% of total calories** |
| Energy value of protein | **4 kcal/g (17 kJ/g)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Properties of Proteins in Food ‚ÄĒ Detailed Analysis

Proteins are responsible for several key functional properties that food scientists exploit. These properties are primarily governed by the **three-dimensional structure (conformation)** of the protein, which depends on **pH, ionic strength, temperature, and the presence of other food components**.

#### 2.1.1 Solubility
- **Definition:** Ability of a protein to disperse in water as individual molecules (colloidal dispersion).
- **Why it matters:** Solubility is the *master property* ‚ÄĒ almost all other functional properties depend on it. Insoluble proteins cannot emulsify, foam, or gel effectively.
- **Mechanism:** Governed by the balance of **hydrophilic** (‚ÄďOH, ‚ÄďCOOH, ‚ÄďNH‚āā) and **hydrophobic** (‚ÄďCH‚āÉ, phenyl) amino acid side chains on the surface.
- **pH effect:** Solubility is **minimum at the isoelectric point (pI)** because net charge = 0 ‚Üí no electrostatic repulsion ‚Üí aggregation.

#### 2.1.2 Gelation
- **Definition:** Formation of a three-dimensional network that traps water and other food constituents, providing structural rigidity.
- **Mechanism:** On heating, protein unfolds ‚Üí exposes reactive groups ‚Üí forms cross-links (disulfide bridges, hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds) ‚Üí continuous matrix.
- **Example:** Egg white ovalbumin on heating ‚Üí **ovalbumin gel** (white, opaque); meat **myosin** ‚Üí heat-set gel.
- **Two types:** Heat-set gels (irreversible) and acid-set gels (casein in yoghurt).

#### 2.1.3 Emulsification
- **Definition:** Ability to act as an **emulsifier** ‚ÄĒ stabilising oil-in-water (O/W) or water-in-oil (W/O) emulsions.
- **Mechanism:** Amphipathic (amphiphilic) nature ‚ÄĒ hydrophilic parts interact with water (continuous phase) and hydrophobic parts interact with oil (dispersed phase), reducing interfacial tension.
- **Example:** Egg yolk **lipovitellin** in mayonnaise; milk **casein** in cream; soy protein in salad dressings.
- **Key metrics:** Emulsifying Capacity Index (ECI) and Emulsifying Activity Index (EAI).

#### 2.1.4 Foaming (Whipping/ Aeration)
- **Definition:** Ability to form a stable foam ‚ÄĒ a colloidal dispersion of gas bubbles in a liquid/solid continuous phase.
- **Mechanism:** Protein adsorbs at the air‚Äďwater interface, unfolds, and forms a viscoelastic film that prevents bubble coalescence.
- **Example:** Egg white **ovalbumin** in meringue; wheat gluten in bread; whey proteins in mousses.
- **Parameters:** Foam capacity (volume increase) and Foam stability (resistance to collapse over time).

#### 2.1.5 Viscosity and Thickening
- **Definition:** Ability to increase the viscosity (resistance to flow) of an aqueous system.
- **Mechanism:** Large, asymmetric, hydrated protein molecules increase resistance to flow.
- **Example:** Gelatin in desserts; **sodium caseinate** in soups and sauces; egg yolk in custards.

#### 2.1.6 Water Holding Capacity (WHC) / Water Binding Capacity (WBC)
- **Definition:** Ability of protein to absorb and retain water against gravitational force.
- **Mechanism:** Polar and charged side chains bind water via **hydrogen bonds** and **ionic interactions**.
- **Example:** Meat proteins retain juices during cooking; soy protein isolates absorb water in meat extenders.

#### 2.1.7 Fat/Oil Binding Capacity
- **Definition:** Ability to bind lipids (fats and oils) via hydrophobic interactions.
- **Application:** Reduces cook loss, improves mouthfeel, prevents fat separation in processed meats.

#### 2.1.8 Dough Formation and Gluten Development
- **Definition:** Unique ability of wheat **gluten proteins (gliadin + glutenin)** to form a viscoelastic dough when hydrated and mechanically worked.
- **Why unique:** Gliadin provides extensibility (viscosity); glutenin provides elasticity. Together ‚Üí viscoelastic network.
- **Why other cereals fail:** Rice, maize, oats lack the unique gliadin‚Äďglutenin ratio and therefore cannot form elastic doughs.

#### 2.1.9 Other Functional Properties
- **Flavour binding** (lactose-derived Maillard reaction; soy protein binding of bitter notes).
- **Colour development** (Maillard browning via lysine őĶ-amino group).
- **Texturization** (extrusion cooking ‚Üí fibrous meat analogues from soy/pea protein).

### 2.2 Protein Quality and Dietary Requirements ‚ÄĒ Theoretical Foundation

#### 2.2.1 Essential vs Non-Essential Amino Acids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential amino acids (9)** ‚ÄĒ cannot be synthesized by the human body and MUST be obtained from the diet:
> **PVT TIM HALL** mnemonic: **P**henylalanine, **V**aline, **T**hreonine, **T**ryptophan, **I**soleucine, **M**ethionine, **H**istidine (conditionally essential in adults, essential in infants), **A**rginine (conditionally essential), **L**eucine, **L**ysine.

> **Non-essential amino acids (11):** Alanine, Asparagine, Aspartic acid, Glutamic acid, Glutamine, Glycine, Proline, Serine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Selenocysteine.

#### 2.2.2 Concept of Limiting Amino Acid
The **limiting amino acid** is the essential amino acid present in the lowest proportion (relative to a reference pattern) in a dietary protein source. It determines the protein's biological value.

> **Mnemonic ‚ÄĒ Limiting AA in cereals vs legumes:**
> - Cereals (rice, wheat): **Lysine** is limiting
> - Legumes (dal, beans): **Methionine** is limiting
> - Hence, **cereal + legume** combinations (e.g., rice + dal, khichdi) provide **complementary proteins** with full essential amino acid profile.

#### 2.2.3 Protein-Energy Malnutrition (PEM)
- **Kwashiorkor:** Adequate calories but **protein deficiency** ‚Üí oedema, distended belly, fatty liver, skin lesions, hypoalbuminaemia.
- **Marasmus:** Severe deficiency of **both protein and energy** ‚Üí extreme wasting, no oedema, "skin and bones" appearance.
- **Marasmic-Kwashiorkor:** Mixed symptoms of both conditions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value signs in all equations below to maintain LaTeX formatting integrity.**

| **Formula / Method** | **Equation** | **Significance & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nitrogen-to-Protein Conversion Factor** | $\text{Protein} = N \times 6.25$ | Based on the assumption that proteins contain **16% N** on average ($100/16 = 6.25$). Used in Kjeldahl analysis. |
| **General Factor (F)** | $F = \dfrac{100}{\% N \text{ in protein}}$ | Different for each food: Wheat = 5.7, Milk = 6.38, Rice = 5.95, Soy = 5.71, Egg = 6.25. |
| **Biological Value (BV)** | $BV = \dfrac{N \text{ retained}}{N \text{ absorbed}} \times 100$ | Measures **nitrogen retained** for body use. Egg = 94, Milk = 91, Fish = 83, Rice = 83, Wheat = 65. |
| **Net Protein Utilization (NPU)** | $NPU = \dfrac{N \text{ retained}}{N \text{ intake}} \times 100$ | Product of BV √ó Digestibility. |
| **Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)** | $PER = \dfrac{\text{Weight gain (g)}}{\text{Protein consumed (g)}}$ | Measured in growing rats. Casein reference = 2.5. |
| **Chemical Score (CS)** | $CS = \dfrac{\text{mg of limiting AA per g test protein}}{\text{mg of same AA per g reference protein}} \times 100$ | First step in quality evaluation. |
| **Amino Acid Score (AAS)** | $AAS = \dfrac{\text{AA in 1 g test protein}}{\text{AA in 1 g reference protein}} \times 100$ | Modern term for chemical score. |
| **Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score (PDCAAS)** | $PDCAAS = AAS \times \text{Digestibility}$ | **Capped at 1.0 (100%)**. Used by US-FDA. Egg, milk, whey = 1.0. |
| **Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score (DIAAS)** | $DIAAS = \dfrac{\text{mg digestible dietary IAA in 1 g test protein}}{\text{mg same AA in 1 g reference protein}} \times 100$ | **Newer (2013 FAO)** standard, recommended over PDCAAS. Not capped. |
| **Nitrogen Balance (NB)** | $NB = N_{\text{intake}} - (N_{\text{urine}} + N_{\text{faeces}} + N_{\text{sweat}})$ | **NB = 0** = equilibrium, **NB > 0** = anabolism, **NB < 0** = catabolism. |
| **Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA)** | $RDA_{\text{protein}} = 0.83 \text{ g/kg body weight/day}$ | For healthy adults (FAO/WHO 2007). |
| **Total Daily Protein** | $\text{Total Protein (g/day)} = 0.83 \times \text{Body weight (kg)}$ | For healthy adult reference. |
| **Energy from Protein** | $E_{\text{prot}} = 4 \text{ kcal/g} \times \text{Protein (g)}$ | For dietary energy calculations. |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Science and Engineering

- **Food Product Development:** Selection of protein isolate (whey, soy, pea) for meat analogues, sports nutrition, infant formula.
- **Quality Control:** Use of PDCAAS/DIAAS for nutrition labelling (mandatory in many countries).
- **Clinical Nutrition:** Calculating nitrogen balance for hospitalised, post-surgical, or burn patients (high-protein diets).
- **Complementary Foods:** Designing cereal‚Äďlegume blends for population-level nutrition programmes (e.g., India's ICDS supplementary food).
- **Public Health Policy:** Estimating protein requirements for school mid-day meal schemes and geriatric nutrition.
- **Sports Science:** Determining protein needs for athletes (1.2‚Äď2.0 g/kg/day) for muscle protein synthesis.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ‚ÄĒ Chemical Score (Amino Acid Score) Calculation

> **Problem:** A 100 g sample of rice protein contains the following essential amino acids. Reference FAO pattern (mg/g protein) is provided. Calculate the Chemical Score and identify the limiting amino acid.

| Amino Acid | mg/g test protein (rice) | mg/g reference (FAO) |
|---|---|---|
| Lysine | 38 | 45 |
| Methionine + Cysteine | 35 | 22 |
| Threonine | 36 | 23 |
| Tryptophan | 11 | 6 |

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Recall the formula:**

$$
\text{Chemical Score (CS)} = \frac{\text{mg of AA in 1 g test protein}}{\text{mg of same AA in 1 g reference protein}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Calculate CS for each amino acid:**

**For Lysine:**

$$
CS_{\text{Lys}} = \frac{38}{45} \times 100 = 84.4\%
$$

**For Methionine + Cysteine (S-containing):**

$$
CS_{\text{Met+Cys}} = \frac{35}{22} \times 100 = 159.1\%
$$

**For Threonine:**

$$
CS_{\text{Thr}} = \frac{36}{23} \times 100 = 156.5\%
$$

**For Tryptophan:**

$$
CS_{\text{Trp}} = \frac{11}{6} \times 100 = 183.3\%
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Identify the limiting amino acid:**

The **lowest** CS value = 84.4% ‚Üí **Lysine is the limiting amino acid** in rice protein.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Report the Chemical Score of rice protein:**

$$
\boxed{\text{CS}_{\text{rice}} = 84.4\%}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ‚ÄĒ PDCAAS Calculation

> **Problem:** A new plant-based protein isolate has an Amino Acid Score of 0.78 and a true digestibility of 92%. Calculate its PDCAAS.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Recall the formula:**

$$
PDCAAS = AAS \times \text{True Digestibility}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute values (AAS expressed as decimal):**

$$
PDCAAS = 0.78 \times 0.92 = 0.7176
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the cap rule:**

PDCAAS is **capped at 1.0** (or 100%). Since 0.7176 < 1.0, no capping required.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{PDCAAS = 0.72 \text{ (or 72\%)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A PDCAAS of 0.72 means the protein provides ~72% of human essential amino acid requirements as completely as a reference protein (e.g., egg, milk) per gram consumed.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 ‚ÄĒ Biological Value (BV) Calculation

> **Problem:** In a nitrogen balance study, a rat consumed 5.0 g of protein (containing 0.80 g N). Faecal N = 0.20 g; Urinary N = 0.40 g. Calculate the Biological Value.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Calculate N intake:** (Already given as 0.80 g)

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Calculate N absorbed:**

$$
N_{\text{absorbed}} = N_{\text{intake}} - N_{\text{faeces}} = 0.80 - 0.20 = 0.60 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Calculate N retained (NB):**

$$
N_{\text{retained}} = N_{\text{absorbed}} - N_{\text{urine}} = 0.60 - 0.40 = 0.20 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply BV formula:**

$$
BV = \frac{N_{\text{retained}}}{N_{\text{absorbed}}} \times 100
$$

$$
BV = \frac{0.20}{0.60} \times 100 = 33.33\%
$$

$$
\boxed{BV = 33.3\%}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 ‚ÄĒ Dietary Protein Requirement (RDA) for an Indian Adult

> **Problem:** A healthy Indian adult male weighs 65 kg. Calculate his daily protein RDA and the corresponding energy contribution.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Recall RDA for healthy adults:**

$$
RDA = 0.83 \text{ g/kg body weight/day} \quad \text{(FAO/WHO/UNU 2007)}
$$

> **ICMR 2020 Indian value:** 0.83 g/kg/day for adults (same as FAO/WHO; 1.0 g/kg/day often used as a buffer for Indian dietary patterns).

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Calculate total daily protein:**

$$
\text{Total Protein} = 0.83 \times 65 = 53.95 \text{ g/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Protein} \approx 54 \text{ g/day}}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Calculate energy from protein:**

$$
E_{\text{prot}} = 54 \text{ g} \times 4 \text{ kcal/g} = 216 \text{ kcal/day}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Calculate % of total daily energy (assuming 2000 kcal/day diet):**

$$
\% E_{\text{prot}} = \frac{216}{2000} \times 100 = 10.8\%
$$

> This falls within the **Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range (AMDR)** of **10‚Äď35%** for protein.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Comparison of Protein Quality Methods

| Method | Measures | Advantage | Limitation | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chemical Score (AAS)** | Limiting AA only | Simple, quick | Ignores digestibility | FAO 1991 pattern |
| **PDCAAS** | AAS √ó Digestibility | Accounts for digestibility, capped at 1.0 | Cap may underestimate high-quality proteins | US-FDA preferred (1991) |
| **DIAAS** | Digestible ileal AA | Most accurate, uses ileal digestibility, no cap | More complex, expensive | FAO 2013 (recommended) |
| **BV** | N retained/N absorbed | Gold standard for retention | Requires human/animal study; expensive | Egg = 100 reference |
| **NPU** | N retained/N intake | Includes digestibility | Animal studies required | ‚Äď |
| **PER** | Weight gain per g protein | Quick, uses rats | Rat ‚Č† human; outdated | Casein = 2.5 |

### 3.6 Protein-Energy Malnutrition (PEM) Comparison

| Feature | **Kwashiorkor** | **Marasmus** |
|---|---|---|
| **Deficiency type** | Protein deficiency (with adequate energy) | Protein + energy deficiency (total starvation) |
| **Age group** | 6 months ‚Äď 5 years (post-weaning) | <18 months, also elderly |
| **Body weight** | 60‚Äď80% of normal (oedema masks weight loss) | <60% of normal (severe wasting) |
| **Oedema** | **Present** (pitting, in legs and face) | **Absent** |
| **Subcutaneous fat** | Reduced, but some preserved | **Severely depleted / absent** |
| **Hair changes** | Flag sign, dyspigmentation, easily pluckable | Sparse, dry, but no flag sign |
| **Skin** | Flaky paint dermatosis, ulceration | Dry, wrinkled, old-man appearance |
| **Liver** | **Fatty liver** (due to failure of apolipoprotein synthesis) | No fatty infiltration |
| **Mental state** | Apathetic, irritable, miserable | Alert, anxious, ravenous appetite |
| **Serum albumin** | **Severely low (<2.5 g/dL)** | Low to normal |
| **Treatment** | High-protein, high-energy diet, careful re-feeding | Gradual re-feeding, balanced diet |
| **Prognosis** | Worse if untreated; high mortality | Better with nutritional rehabilitation |
