## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]`
**Define functional properties of proteins. List any four important functional properties of food proteins with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Functional properties of proteins are the physico-chemical characteristics that influence the behaviour of proteins in food systems during processing, storage and consumption, affecting texture, stability and sensory attributes (1 mark).

Four important functional properties (¬Ĺ mark each = 2 marks):
1. **Emulsification** ‚ÄĒ Egg yolk lipovitellin stabilises oil-in-water emulsion in mayonnaise.
2. **Foaming** ‚ÄĒ Egg white ovalbumin forms a stable foam in meringues.
3. **Gelation** ‚ÄĒ Gelatin forms a three-dimensional gel network in desserts.
4. **Water holding capacity** ‚ÄĒ Meat proteins retain water during cooking, preventing drip loss.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2023]`
**What is a limiting amino acid? Explain with suitable examples how complementary protein combinations overcome this limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **limiting amino acid** is the essential amino acid that is present in the lowest proportion (relative to a reference pattern) in a dietary protein, thereby limiting its biological utilisation (1 mark).

In **cereals** (rice, wheat, maize), **lysine** is the limiting amino acid; in **legumes** (dal, beans, soy), **methionine** is the limiting amino acid (1 mark).

When cereals and legumes are **consumed together** (e.g., rice + dal, khichdi; wheat + chana dal; idli = rice + urad dal), the excess lysine from legumes compensates for the lysine deficiency of cereals, and the excess methionine from cereals compensates for the methionine deficiency of legumes. This **complementary protein** combination provides a **complete essential amino acid profile** with improved biological value and PDCAAS (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various functional properties of proteins that are important in food systems. Discuss emulsification and gelation mechanisms with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define Biological Value (BV), Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER) and Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score (PDCAAS). Explain how PDCAAS is calculated with a suitable example.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**
Functional properties are the physico-chemical and sensory attributes of proteins that determine their behaviour and interactions in food systems (1 mark). They govern texture, stability, appearance and consumer acceptance of foods.

**Important functional properties (with examples):**

1. **Solubility** (1 mark): Ability of protein to disperse in water. Affected by pH, ionic strength, temperature. Minimum at isoelectric point (pI). Example: Whey proteins are highly soluble; gluten is sparingly soluble.

2. **Gelation** (1 mark): Formation of 3-D network trapping water ‚Üí structural rigidity. Heat-induced unfolding ‚Üí cross-linking via disulfide bonds, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions. Example: Egg white ovalbumin gels on boiling (irreversible heat-set gel); casein gels under acidic conditions in yoghurt (acid-induced gel).

3. **Emulsification** (1 mark): Amphipathic proteins reduce interfacial tension at oil‚Äďwater interface ‚Üí stable emulsions. Hydrophobic regions interact with oil; hydrophilic regions with water. Example: Egg yolk lipoproteins in mayonnaise; sodium caseinate in coffee whiteners; soy protein in salad dressings.

4. **Foaming** (1 mark): Proteins adsorb at air‚Äďwater interface ‚Üí form viscoelastic film ‚Üí stable foam. Example: Egg white ovalbumin in meringue; whey proteins in mousses.

5. **Viscosity / Thickening** (¬Ĺ mark): Large hydrated protein molecules increase resistance to flow. Example: Gelatin in desserts; xanthan-proteins in sauces.

6. **Water Holding Capacity** (¬Ĺ mark): Polar/charged side chains bind water. Example: Meat proteins retain juices; soy protein in meat extenders.

7. **Dough Formation** (¬Ĺ mark): Unique to wheat gluten (gliadin + glutenin) ‚Üí viscoelastic dough. Example: Bread, chapati, pasta.

8. **Fat binding, Flavour binding, Colour (Maillard)** (¬Ĺ mark): Affect sensory and nutritional quality of processed foods.

**Summary:** The functional behaviour depends on protein conformation, which is governed by intrinsic factors (amino acid composition, sequence) and extrinsic factors (pH, temperature, ionic strength, processing conditions).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**
**1. Biological Value (BV)** (1 mark):
- Definition: Percentage of absorbed nitrogen that is retained by the body.
- Formula: $BV = \dfrac{N_{\text{retained}}}{N_{\text{absorbed}}} \times 100$
- Egg = 94, Milk = 91, Wheat = 65.

**2. Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)** (1 mark):
- Definition: Gain in body weight (in grams) per gram of protein consumed.
- Formula: $PER = \dfrac{\text{Weight gain (g)}}{\text{Protein consumed (g)}}$
- Measured in growing rats. Casein = 2.5. Beef = 2.3, Egg = 3.8.

**3. PDCAAS** (1 mark):
- Definition: Amino acid score corrected for true faecal digestibility of protein.
- Formula: $PDCAAS = AAS \times \text{True Digestibility}$ (Capped at 1.0).
- Reference: FAO 1991 amino acid pattern for ages 1‚Äď2 years.

**Example calculation (3 marks):**
A new protein source has the following composition:
- Lysine content = 42 mg/g protein
- FAO reference for lysine = 45 mg/g protein
- True digestibility = 90%

Step 1: $AAS_{\text{Lys}} = \dfrac{42}{45} \times 100 = 93.3\%$
Step 2: $PDCAAS = 0.933 \times 0.90 = 0.84$ (less than 1.0, so no capping needed)
Step 3: Interpretation: PDCAAS = 0.84 means this protein meets ~84% of human essential amino acid needs as completely as the reference (egg/milk) per gram consumed.

**Comparison of methods (1 mark):** PDCAAS is preferred by US-FDA for food labelling; DIAAS is the newer (2013) FAO-recommended method based on ileal digestibility.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the concept of protein quality in human nutrition. Explain essential amino acids, limiting amino acid, and the principle of complementary proteins with two examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is Protein-Energy Malnutrition (PEM)? Compare Kwashiorkor and Marasmus with respect to cause, clinical features, and treatment.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**
**1. Protein Quality** (1 mark):
Protein quality refers to the ability of a dietary protein to satisfy the body's needs for essential amino acids and nitrogen for growth and maintenance. It depends on amino acid composition, digestibility, and bioavailability. A "high-quality" or "complete" protein provides all nine essential amino acids in adequate proportions and is well digested and absorbed.

**2. Essential Amino Acids (EAAs)** (1 mark):
The 9 EAAs that the human body cannot synthesize and must be supplied through diet: **Histidine, Isoleucine, Leucine, Lysine, Methionine (+ Cysteine), Phenylalanine (+ Tyrosine), Threonine, Tryptophan, Valine**. Conditionally essential (under specific physiological conditions): Arginine, Glutamine.

**3. Limiting Amino Acid** (2 marks):
The EAA that is present in the lowest proportion (relative to the reference FAO pattern) in a food protein. It becomes the bottleneck for protein synthesis in the body, lowering the overall biological value of the protein.
- Cereals: **Lysine** is limiting
- Legumes: **Methionine (and threonine)** is limiting
- Egg, milk, fish, meat: well-balanced (no limiting AA; complete proteins)

**4. Complementary Proteins** (3 marks):
Two or more plant protein sources, each deficient in a different EAA, are combined such that the amino acid deficiencies of one are compensated by the surplus of the other. The combination yields a mixture with a complete and balanced EAA profile, often with a PDCAAS approaching 1.0.

**Examples:**
- **Rice + Dal (Khichdi):** Lysine-rich dal complements lysine-deficient rice; methionine-rich rice complements methionine-deficient dal.
- **Wheat + Chickpea (Bengal gram) chapati:** Chickpea (Bengal gram) is rich in lysine; wheat supplies methionine.
- **Idli:** Fermented combination of rice (urad dal) ‚ÄĒ rice provides methionine; urad dal provides lysine.

This principle is the foundation of traditional Indian vegetarian diets, which provide adequate protein quality despite being plant-based.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**
**1. Definition of PEM** (1 mark):
Protein-Energy Malnutrition is a state of deficient energy and/or protein intake, resulting in measurable adverse effects on body composition, function, and clinical outcome. It is the most common form of malnutrition in developing countries, especially affecting infants, young children, the elderly, and hospitalised patients.

**2. Kwashiorkor** (3 marks):
- **Cause:** Primarily protein deficiency (with relatively adequate or even normal energy intake from carbohydrates). Common after weaning, especially when diet shifts to starch-based weaning foods.
- **Clinical features:** Pitting oedema (in legs, feet, face), distended abdomen (due to hepatomegaly from fatty liver and ascites), skin lesions (flaky-paint dermatosis, ulceration), hair changes (flag sign, dyspigmentation, easy pluckability), apathy, irritability, severe hypoalbuminaemia (<2.5 g/dL), growth retardation, fatty liver (due to failure of apolipoprotein synthesis, impairing VLDL export).
- **Treatment:** Gradual re-feeding with high-quality protein (1‚Äď1.5 g/kg/day), supplementation with potassium and magnesium, treatment of infections, careful fluid and electrolyte management.

**3. Marasmus** (2 marks):
- **Cause:** Combined deficiency of protein AND energy (chronic semi-starvation). Common in famine, severe neglect, or chronic illness.
- **Clinical features:** Severe wasting of muscle and subcutaneous fat ("skin and bones" appearance), old-man facies, no oedema, sparse dry hair (no flag sign), alert mental state (sometimes anxious), growth retardation, no fatty liver. Weight is often <60% of standard for age.
- **Treatment:** Gradual re-feeding with balanced macro- and micronutrients, F-75 and F-100 therapeutic milks (WHO protocol), family education.

**4. Comparison summary** (1 mark):
The key distinguishing features are **oedema (present in kwashiorkor, absent in marasmus)**, **fatty liver (in kwashiorkor)**, and **age of onset** (kwashiorkor: 6 months‚Äď5 years; marasmus: <18 months and elderly).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ūüĒī KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing BV and NPU:** Many students interchange the formulas. **BV** uses *absorbed* nitrogen in the denominator; **NPU** uses *intake* nitrogen. Memorise: $NPU = BV \times Digestibility$.
> 2. **Forgetting the PDCAAS cap:** PDCAAS is **capped at 1.0 (100%)**. If AAS √ó Digestibility > 1.0, the value is reported as 1.0, not the higher number. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Missing the limiting AA in cereals vs legumes:** A very common trap ‚ÄĒ students write "methionine is limiting in cereals" or "lysine is limiting in legumes" ‚ÄĒ both are **wrong**. Memorise: **cereals ‚Üí lysine**; **legumes ‚Üí methionine**.
> 4. **Kwashiorkor oedema:** Many students write "kwashiorkor is caused by energy deficiency". This is **incorrect** ‚ÄĒ it is **protein deficiency** (with adequate energy). Marasmus is the total protein + energy deficiency.
> 5. **N-factor in Kjeldahl:** Don't blindly use 6.25. Use the **specific factor** for the food (milk = 6.38, wheat = 5.7, etc.). Examiners love this trick.
> 6. **DIAAS vs PDCAAS confusion:** DIAAS uses **ileal** (not faecal) digestibility and is **not capped**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Functional properties** = non-nutritive, technological roles of proteins (solubility, gelation, emulsification, foaming, viscosity, WHC, fat binding, dough formation).
- [x] **Solubility is the master property** ‚ÄĒ affects almost all other functional behaviours.
- [x] **Gelation** = 3-D protein network trapping water (heat-set ovalbumin; acid-set casein in yoghurt).
- [x] **Emulsification** requires amphipathic proteins (egg yolk lipovitellin, casein, soy protein).
- [x] **Foaming** ‚ÄĒ protein film at air-water interface (egg white, whey).
- [x] **Dough formation** is unique to wheat gluten (gliadin + glutenin).
- [x] **9 Essential Amino Acids** (adults): Histidine, Isoleucine, Leucine, Lysine, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Valine.
- [x] **Limiting AA**: Lysine in cereals; Methionine in legumes.
- [x] **Complementary proteins**: Cereal + legume = complete AA profile.
- [x] **Protein quality methods**: Chemical Score, BV, NPU, PER, PDCAAS, DIAAS.
- [x] **PDCAAS** = AAS √ó True Digestibility, **capped at 1.0**.
- [x] **DIAAS** uses ileal digestibility, **not capped** (FAO 2013 standard).
- [x] **Nitrogen-to-protein factor** = 6.25 (general); specific factors: milk 6.38, wheat 5.7, rice 5.95.
- [x] **RDA of protein** = 0.83 g/kg body weight/day (FAO/WHO 2007, ICMR 2020).
- [x] **Energy from protein** = 4 kcal/g.
- [x] **AMDR for protein** = 10‚Äď35% of total daily energy.
- [x] **Kwashiorkor** = protein deficiency ‚Üí oedema, fatty liver, hypoalbuminaemia.
- [x] **Marasmus** = protein + energy deficiency ‚Üí severe wasting, no oedema, no fatty liver.
- [x] **Nitrogen balance** = N intake ‚ąí (N urine + N faeces + N sweat); NB = 0 at equilibrium.
- [x] **Complementary food examples**: Rice + Dal (khichdi); Wheat + Chana dal; Idli (rice + urad dal).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amino Acids - Definition and Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Amino Acids - Definition and Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Amino Acids (KTU 2024 Official Definition)**
> Amino acids are the fundamental **monomeric building blocks (subunits) of proteins**, defined as organic molecules that contain at least one **amino group (‚ÄďNH‚āā)** and at least one **carboxyl group (‚ÄďCOOH)** attached to the same **őĪ-carbon atom** (the carbon adjacent to the carboxyl group). They are therefore classified as **őĪ-amino acids** in biochemistry.

The general structural formula of an őĪ-amino acid can be written as:

$$
\text{H}_{2}\text{N} - \overset{\overset{\displaystyle R}{|}}{\underset{\underset{\displaystyle H}{|}}{C}} - \text{COOH}
$$

Where:
- **‚ÄďCOOH** = Carboxyl group (acidic, proton donor)
- **‚ÄďNH‚āā** = Amino group (basic, proton acceptor)
- **R** = Variable side chain (R-group) ‚ÄĒ distinguishes the 20 standard amino acids
- **H** = Hydrogen atom
- **C** = őĪ-carbon (central carbon)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A total of **20 standard (proteinogenic) őĪ-amino acids** are encoded by the human genetic code and are directly incorporated into proteins via translation. Additionally, there are over **250 non-standard amino acids** found in nature (e.g., Ornithine, Citrulline, GABA) that are not encoded by DNA but perform specialized biological roles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Lego Brick" of Life

Imagine you are building a **giant LEGO castle** representing a protein molecule. Each LEGO brick is an **amino acid**. The bricks come in **20 different shapes, sizes, and colors** ‚ÄĒ this represents the **20 standard amino acids** with their different R-groups (side chains).

Just as every LEGO brick has:
- **Two universal studs on top** (analogous to the ‚ÄďNH‚āā and ‚ÄďCOOH groups) ‚ÄĒ these are the *connecting* parts
- **A unique bottom pattern** (analogous to the R-group) ‚ÄĒ this gives each brick its *unique identity*

When LEGO bricks click together, they form long chains (proteins/polypeptides). Similarly, amino acids link via **peptide bonds** to form proteins.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** Think **"Amino = NH‚āā (Ammonia-like)"** and **"Acid = COOH"**. So an amino acid has BOTH. Simple! ‚úď

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Metrics

| Metric | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Number of standard őĪ-amino acids** | **20** (encoded by the genetic code) |
| **őĪ-Carbon chirality (except Glycine)** | **L-configuration** in natural proteins |
| **Average molecular weight of an amino acid residue** | **~110 Da (Daltons)** |
| **Isoelectric point (pI) range** | **pH 3.0 ‚Äď 10.0** (varies by side chain) |
| **Biological pH (~7.4)** | Amino acids exist as **zwitterions** (net charge = 0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for Food Chemistry (PCFTT205)?**
> Amino acids determine **protein quality** (e.g., lysine in cereals is limiting), **flavor development** (Maillard browning uses the free ‚ÄďNH‚āā group), **nutritional value** (essential vs. non-essential), and **functional properties** of foods (emulsification, gelation, foaming).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an őĪ-Amino Acid ‚ÄĒ Four Functional Substituents

Every őĪ-amino acid is built around a central **őĪ-carbon** that carries **four** distinct substituents (tetrahedral geometry, sp¬≥ hybridized):

- **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ The Carboxyl Group (‚ÄďCOOH):**
  This is the **acidic** functional group. At physiological pH (~7.4), it loses a proton (H‚Āļ) and becomes **‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ** (carboxylate). This is why amino acids are weak acids. The pKa of the őĪ-COOH group is typically in the range of **1.8 ‚Äď 2.5**.

- **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ The Amino Group (‚ÄďNH‚āā):**
  This is the **basic** functional group. At physiological pH, it accepts a proton and becomes **‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ** (ammonium). The pKa of the őĪ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ group is typically in the range of **8.8 ‚Äď 10.6**.

- **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ The R-group (Side Chain):**
  This is the **unique fingerprint** of each amino acid. It can be:
  - A simple **H** (Glycine ‚ÄĒ only non-chiral amino acid)
  - A hydrocarbon chain (Alanine, Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine)
  - An aromatic ring (Phenylalanine, Tyrosine, Tryptophan)
  - A hydroxyl group (Serine, Threonine, Tyrosine)
  - A sulfur-containing group (Cysteine, Methionine)
  - A basic group (Lysine, Arginine, Histidine)
  - An acidic group (Aspartate, Glutamate)
  - An amide group (Asparagine, Glutamine)

- **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ The őĪ-Hydrogen (H):**
  A single hydrogen atom completes the tetrahedral arrangement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" ‚ÄĒ The Genius of Having Both Groups:**
> The simultaneous presence of ‚ÄďCOOH (acid) and ‚ÄďNH‚āā (base) makes amino acids **amphoteric** ‚ÄĒ they can act as BOTH an acid AND a base. This is the molecular basis for **buffering action**, **protein folding**, and **enzyme catalysis**.

## 2.2 Classification of Amino Acids ‚ÄĒ Multiple Schemes

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **three** primary classification frameworks:

### A. Classification Based on Polarity of the R-group (Most Common Biochemical Scheme)

- **Nonpolar (Hydrophobic) R-groups ‚ÄĒ 9 amino acids:**
  Glycine, Alanine, Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine, Proline, Phenylalanine, Tryptophan, Methionine
  - *Why hydrophobic?* R-groups are mostly hydrocarbons (C and H only).
  - *Location in proteins:* Usually buried in the **interior core** of globular proteins.

- **Polar Uncharged R-groups ‚ÄĒ 6 amino acids:**
  Serine, Threonine, Tyrosine, Cysteine, Asparagine, Glutamine
  - *Why polar?* They contain ‚ÄďOH, ‚ÄďSH, or ‚ÄďCONH‚āā groups that form **hydrogen bonds** with water.
  - *Location:* Found on the **surface** of proteins or in **active sites**.

- **Positively Charged (Basic) R-groups at pH 7.0 ‚ÄĒ 3 amino acids:**
  Lysine (őĶ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ), Arginine (guanidinium), Histidine (imidazolium)
  - *pKa of side chain:* Lysine ‚Čą **10.5**, Arginine ‚Čą **12.5**, Histidine ‚Čą **6.0**
  - *Functional role:* Form **salt bridges** with negatively charged groups; provide positive charges for **DNA-binding proteins** (e.g., histones).

- **Negatively Charged (Acidic) R-groups at pH 7.0 ‚ÄĒ 2 amino acids:**
  Aspartate (‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ), Glutamate (‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ)
  - *pKa of side chain:* Aspartate ‚Čą **3.9**, Glutamate ‚Čą **4.1**
  - *Functional role:* Form **salt bridges**; participate in **enzymatic catalysis** (e.g., catalytic triad).

### B. Nutritional Classification (Critical for Food Chemistry)

- **Essential Amino Acids (9) ‚ÄĒ MUST be obtained from diet:**
  Histidine, Isoleucine, Leucine, Lysine, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Valine
  - *Mnemonic:* **"PVT TIM HALL"** (Phenylalanine, Valine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Isoleucine, Methionine, Histidine, All, Leucine, Lysine)

- **Non-Essential Amino Acids (11) ‚ÄĒ Synthesized by the body:**
  Alanine, Arginine, Asparagine, Aspartate, Cysteine, Glutamate, Glutamine, Glycine, Proline, Serine, Tyrosine

- **Conditionally Essential Amino Acids:**
  Become essential under specific physiological stress (illness, prematurity): Glutamine, Arginine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Proline, Glycine

### C. Metabolic Classification (Based on Degradation Products)

- **Glucogenic Amino Acids (15):** Their carbon skeletons are converted to **pyruvate or TCA cycle intermediates** ‚Üí can synthesize **glucose** via gluconeogenesis.
  - *Examples:* Alanine, Aspartate, Glutamate, Glycine, Serine, etc.

- **Ketogenic Amino Acids (2):** Their carbon skeletons are converted to **acetyl-CoA or acetoacetyl-CoA** ‚Üí can synthesize **ketone bodies** and **fatty acids**.
  - *Examples:* Leucine, Lysine

- **Both Glucogenic and Ketogenic (5):**
  Isoleucine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Tyrosine

## 2.3 The Zwitterion Concept (The "True" Form of Amino Acids)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept ‚ÄĒ Zwitterion:**
> In aqueous solution at physiological pH (~7.4), amino acids do **NOT** exist as neutral H‚āāN‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOOH molecules. Instead, the acidic ‚ÄďCOOH donates its proton to the basic ‚ÄďNH‚āā, forming a **dipolar ion (zwitterion)** that has **zero net charge** but carries both a positive (‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ) and a negative (‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ) charge.

The zwitterion structure:

$$
\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+} - \overset{\overset{\displaystyle R}{|}}{\underset{\underset{\displaystyle H}{|}}{C}} - \text{COO}^{-}
$$

**Why does this matter?**
- Explains the **high melting points** of amino acids (ionic crystal lattice)
- Explains **high water solubility** (zwitterions interact strongly with water)
- Explains **minimal migration in electric field at physiological pH**
- The foundation for understanding the **Isoelectric Point (pI)** ‚ÄĒ see Section 3.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following formulas for ESE (End Semester Examination).** They are the most-tested equations in the Amino Acid unit.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables Explained |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch Equation** | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^{-}]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$ | pK‚āź = acid dissociation constant; [A‚ĀĽ] = conjugate base; [HA] = weak acid |
| 2 | **pI for Neutral Amino Acid** (no ionizable R-group) | $\text{p}I = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1}(\text{COOH}) + \text{p}K_{a2}(\text{NH}_{3}^{+})}{2}$ | Average of the two pK‚āź values flanking the zwitterion |
| 3 | **pI for Acidic Amino Acid** (Asp, Glu) | $\text{p}I = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1}(\alpha\text{-COOH}) + \text{p}K_{aR}(\text{side chain COOH})}{2}$ | Average of the two acidic pK‚āź values |
| 4 | **pI for Basic Amino Acid** (Lys, Arg, His) | $\text{p}I = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{aR}(\text{side chain NH}_{3}^{+}) + \text{p}K_{a2}(\alpha\text{-NH}_{3}^{+})}{2}$ | Average of the two basic pK‚āź values |
| 5 | **Number of Standard Amino Acids** | $\text{N} = \mathbf{20}$ | Encoded by **61 sense codons** of the genetic code |
| 6 | **Mean Residue Weight** | $\text{M}_{residue} = \dfrac{\text{M}_{protein}}{\text{N}_{residues}} \approx \mathbf{110 \text{ Da}}$ | Used in protein quantification |
| 7 | **Titration Curve Midpoint** | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ | When [HA] = [A‚ĀĽ], buffering capacity is maximum |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** In the formula table above, **do NOT confuse pI formulas for acidic vs. basic amino acids**. Many students wrongly apply the neutral formula to ALL amino acids. The correct rule: **pI = average of the two pK‚āź values on either side of the zwitterionic (zero net charge) species.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Food Industry Applications

- **Food Fortification:** Lysine and threonine are the **limiting amino acids** in rice and wheat-based diets. Industrial fortification (e.g., in India, "Quality Protein Maize") addresses this.
- **Flavor Chemistry:** Free amino acids participate in **Maillard browning** (with reducing sugars) ‚Üí produces aromas in baked bread, roasted coffee, seared meat.
- **Artificial Sweeteners:** Aspartame is a **dipeptide** of Aspartate + Phenylalanine (methyl ester) ‚ÄĒ 200√ó sweeter than sucrose.
- **MSG (Monosodium Glutamate):** The sodium salt of **Glutamate** ‚ÄĒ the famous "umami" (savory) taste enhancer.
- **Sports Nutrition:** **Branched-Chain Amino Acids (BCAAs)** = Leucine + Isoleucine + Valine ‚ÄĒ marketed for muscle recovery.
- **Gelatin & Collagen:** Rich in **Glycine, Proline, Hydroxyproline** ‚ÄĒ used in food gelling, pharma capsules, cosmetics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch Equation (Foundation for All pI Calculations)

**Starting Point:** The dissociation of a weak acid in water:

$$
\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^{+} + \text{A}^{-}
$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Write the acid dissociation constant:**

$$
K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^{+}][\text{A}^{-}]}{[\text{HA}]}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Rearrange to isolate [H‚Āļ]:**

$$
[\text{H}^{+}] = K_a \cdot \dfrac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^{-}]}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Take the negative logarithm of both sides:**

$$
-\log[\text{H}^{+}] = -\log K_a - \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^{-}]}\right)
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Use the definitions pH = ‚Äďlog[H‚Āļ] and pK‚āź = ‚Äďlog K‚āź:**

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a - \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^{-}]}\right)
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Apply the logarithmic identity ‚Äďlog(x/y) = log(y/x):**

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^{-}]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)
$$

**Final Result ‚ÄĒ Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^{-}]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)\;}
$$

> **Logic:** This equation links measurable pH to the ratio of protonated/deprotonated forms. **At the pKa**, the acid is 50% protonated and 50% deprotonated ‚ÄĒ this is the point of **maximum buffering capacity**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: pI Formula for a Neutral Amino Acid (e.g., Glycine, Alanine)

**Starting Assumption:** Glycine has two ionizable groups ‚ÄĒ őĪ-COOH (pK‚āź‚āĀ ‚Čą 2.34) and őĪ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ (pK‚āź‚āā ‚Čą 9.60). No ionizable side chain.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the three protonation states of Glycine:**

At very low pH (fully protonated cation): $\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOOH}$ (charge = +1)

At intermediate pH (zwitterion): $\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO}^{-}$ (charge = 0)

At very high pH (fully deprotonated anion): $\text{H}_{2}\text{N‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO}^{-}$ (charge = ‚Äď1)

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Define the Isoelectric Point (pI):**
pI is the pH at which the **net charge of the amino acid is zero**, i.e., it exists predominantly as the zwitterion.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch to each transition:**

For the equilibrium $\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOOH} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO}^{-} + \text{H}^{+}$:

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{Zwitterion}]}{[\text{Cation}]}\right)
$$

For the equilibrium $\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{2}\text{N‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO}^{-} + \text{H}^{+}$:

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a2} + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{Anion}]}{[\text{Zwitterion}]}\right)
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ At the pI, [Cation] = [Anion] (charge balance).** Therefore, both numerator and denominator reduce so that **pH = (pK‚āź‚āĀ + pK‚āź‚āā) / 2**.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{p}I_{\text{neutral}} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}\;}
$$

**Numerical Example for Glycine:**

$$
\text{p}I_{\text{Glycine}} = \dfrac{2.34 + 9.60}{2} = \dfrac{11.94}{2} = 5.97
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: pI Formula for an Acidic Amino Acid (e.g., Aspartate)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Aspartate has THREE ionizable groups** with approximate pK‚āź values:
- őĪ-COOH: pK‚āź‚āĀ ‚Čą **1.99**
- Side chain ő≤-COOH: pK‚āźR ‚Čą **3.90**
- őĪ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ: pK‚āź‚āā ‚Čą **9.90**

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Identify the species that carries net charge = 0 (the zwitterionic form):**

The zwitterion of aspartate is: $\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO}^{-}\text{ with ‚ÄďCOO}^{-}\text{ on side chain}$ (net charge = 0)

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ The pI is the average of the two pK‚āź values that bracket this zwitterion:**
- Going from the **first ‚ÄďCOOH** (pK‚āź‚āĀ = 1.99) ‚Üí zwitterion
- Going from the **zwitterion** ‚Üí **second ‚ÄďCOOH** (pK‚āźR = 3.90)

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{p}I_{\text{acidic}} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}}{2}\;}
$$

**Numerical Example for Aspartate:**

$$
\text{p}I_{\text{Asp}} = \dfrac{1.99 + 3.90}{2} = \dfrac{5.89}{2} = 2.95
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: pI Formula for a Basic Amino Acid (e.g., Lysine)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Lysine has THREE ionizable groups** with approximate pK‚āź values:
- őĪ-COOH: pK‚āź‚āĀ ‚Čą **2.18**
- őĪ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ: pK‚āź‚āā ‚Čą **8.95**
- Side chain őĶ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ: pK‚āźR ‚Čą **10.53**

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Identify the zwitterionic species (net charge = 0):**
The relevant form has all three groups deprotonated except the two that are charged: $\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}(\alpha)\text{‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO}^{-}\text{ with ‚ÄďNH}_{3}^{+}(\varepsilon)$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ The pI is the average of the two pK‚āź values flanking this zwitterion:**

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{p}I_{\text{basic}} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{aR} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}\;}
$$

**Numerical Example for Lysine:**

$$
\text{p}I_{\text{Lys}} = \dfrac{10.53 + 8.95}{2} = \dfrac{19.48}{2} = 9.74
$$

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ The 20 Standard Amino Acids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table for ESE (End Semester Exam).** This is the **most-tested chart** in the Amino Acid unit. Memorize the categorization and key pK‚āź values.

| Amino Acid | 3-Letter | 1-Letter | R-group Character | Polar Class | pK‚āź‚āĀ (COOH) | pK‚āź‚āā (NH‚āÉ‚Āļ) | pK‚āźR (side chain) | pI | Essential? | Gluco/Keto |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glycine | Gly | G | ‚ÄďH | Nonpolar | 2.34 | 9.60 | ‚ÄĒ | 5.97 | No | Glucogenic |
| Alanine | Ala | A | ‚ÄďCH‚āÉ | Nonpolar | 2.34 | 9.69 | ‚ÄĒ | 6.01 | No | Glucogenic |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]
**Define the term "essential amino acids" and list any six examples with their dietary sources.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Essential amino acids are those őĪ-amino acids that **cannot be synthesized by the human body** in sufficient quantities and **must be supplied through the diet** to maintain proper nitrogen balance and support protein biosynthesis.

**Six Examples with Dietary Sources (2 marks):**

| Essential Amino Acid | Major Dietary Sources |
|---|---|
| Lysine | Rice bran, pulses (lentils, chickpeas), soybean, quinoa |
| Tryptophan | Milk, cheese, turkey, chicken, oats, sesame seeds |
| Methionine | Eggs, fish, Brazil nuts, sesame seeds, whole grains |
| Phenylalanine | Meat, fish, dairy, soy products, nuts |
| Threonine | Cottage cheese, poultry, fish, lentils, sesame |
| Leucine | Dairy products, soy, beef, peanuts, eggs (highest in BCAAs) |

> *Note: The remaining three essential amino acids are Valine, Isoleucine, and Histidine.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]
**What is a zwitterion? Explain with reference to an amino acid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **zwitterion** (from German *zwitter* = "hybrid") is a molecule that carries **both a positive and a negative electrical charge on different atoms** but has an **overall net charge of zero** (1 mark).

In an amino acid such as glycine, at physiological pH (~7.4), the **carboxyl group (‚ÄďCOOH) loses a proton** to become ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ, and the **amino group (‚ÄďNH‚āā) accepts a proton** to become ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ (1 mark).

The resulting zwitterion can be represented as:

$$
\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+} - \text{CH}_{2} - \text{COO}^{-}
$$

This **dipolar ionic form** is the predominant species of amino acids in aqueous solution at neutral pH (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into two sub-parts: **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ‚≠ź QUESTION A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ CHOICE 1 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Jul 2024]

**Q. (a)** Define amino acids. Classify the 20 standard amino acids based on the polarity of their side chains (R-groups). List the amino acids under each category. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Amino Acid (2 marks):**
Amino acids are **organic compounds** that contain:
- At least one **amino group (‚ÄďNH‚āā)** and
- At least one **carboxyl group (‚ÄďCOOH)**
- Both attached to the **same őĪ-carbon atom**

The general formula is $\text{H}_{2}\text{N‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOOH}$. They are the **monomeric building blocks of proteins**. There are 20 standard amino acids encoded by the genetic code (excluding selenocysteine and pyrrolysine).

**Classification Based on R-group Polarity (5 marks):**

**Category 1 ‚ÄĒ Nonpolar (Hydrophobic) R-groups [9 AAs]:**
The R-groups contain only hydrocarbons (C and H), making them water-repellent. They are typically buried in the **protein core**.

1. Glycine (Gly, G) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďH
2. Alanine (Ala, A) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āÉ
3. Valine (Val, V) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH(CH‚āÉ)‚āā
4. Leucine (Leu, L) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāCH(CH‚āÉ)‚āā
5. Isoleucine (Ile, I) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH(CH‚āÉ)CH‚āāCH‚āÉ
6. Proline (Pro, P) ‚ÄĒ cyclic secondary amine
7. Phenylalanine (Phe, F) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďC‚āÜH‚āÖ
8. Tryptophan (Trp, W) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āā‚Äďindole
9. Methionine (Met, M) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāCH‚āāSCH‚āÉ

**Category 2 ‚ÄĒ Polar Uncharged R-groups [6 AAs]:**
The R-groups contain electronegative atoms (O, N, S) that can form **hydrogen bonds** with water. They are found on **protein surfaces**.

1. Serine (Ser, S) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāOH
2. Threonine (Thr, T) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH(OH)CH‚āÉ
3. Tyrosine (Tyr, Y) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďC‚āÜH‚āĄ‚ÄďOH
4. Cysteine (Cys, C) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāSH
5. Asparagine (Asn, N) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāCONH‚āā
6. Glutamine (Gln, Q) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāCH‚āāCONH‚āā

**Category 3 ‚ÄĒ Positively Charged (Basic) R-groups at pH 7 [3 AAs]:**
These amino acids have basic groups that are **protonated at pH 7**, giving a net positive charge. They are critical in DNA-binding proteins and enzyme active sites.

1. Lysine (Lys, K) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚Äď(CH‚āā)‚āĄNH‚āÉ‚Āļ (pK‚āźR ‚Čą 10.53)
2. Arginine (Arg, R) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚Äď(CH‚āā)‚āÉ‚ÄďNH‚ÄďC(NH‚āā)‚āā‚Āļ (pK‚āźR ‚Čą 12.48)
3. Histidine (His, H) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āā‚Äďimidazolium (pK‚āźR ‚Čą 6.00)

**Category 4 ‚ÄĒ Negatively Charged (Acidic) R-groups at pH 7 [2 AAs]:**
These have carboxyl groups that are **deprotonated at pH 7**, giving a net negative charge.

1. Aspartate / Aspartic acid (Asp, D) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāCOO‚ĀĽ (pK‚āźR ‚Čą 3.90)
2. Glutamate / Glutamic acid (Glu, E) ‚ÄĒ R = ‚ÄďCH‚āāCH‚āāCOO‚ĀĽ (pK‚āźR ‚Čą 4.25)

---

**Q. (b)** What is the isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid? Derive the formula for pI of a neutral amino acid and calculate the pI of glycine. Given: pK‚āź‚āĀ (őĪ-COOH) = 2.34, pK‚āź‚āā (őĪ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ) = 9.60. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Isoelectric Point (2 marks):**
The **isoelectric point (pI)** is defined as the **pH at which a given amino acid (or protein) carries no net electrical charge** in solution. At this pH, the amino acid exists predominantly as a **zwitterion** and therefore shows **no net migration** in an electric field.

**Mathematically, pI is the average of the pK‚āź values that flank the zwitterionic (zero net charge) species on a titration curve.**

**Derivation of pI Formula for a Neutral Amino Acid (3 marks):**

Consider glycine, which has two ionizable groups: őĪ-COOH and őĪ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ. The titration curve passes through three key regions:

**Region 1 ‚ÄĒ Low pH (Cation, charge = +1):**

$$
\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCH}_{2}\text{‚ÄďCOOH}
$$

**Region 2 ‚ÄĒ Intermediate pH (Zwitterion, charge = 0):**

$$
\text{H}_{3}\text{N}^{+}\text{‚ÄďCH}_{2}\text{‚ÄďCOO}^{-}
$$

**Region 3 ‚ÄĒ High pH (Anion, charge = ‚Äď1):**

$$
\text{H}_{2}\text{N‚ÄďCH}_{2}\text{‚ÄďCOO}^{-}
$$

Applying Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch to Region 1 ‚Üí Region 2 transition:

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a1} + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{Zwitterion}]}{[\text{Cation}]}\right)
$$

Applying Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch to Region 2 ‚Üí Region 3 transition:

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{a2} + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{Anion}]}{[\text{Zwitterion}]}\right)
$$

**At the pI (charge = 0), [Cation] = [Anion]** (charge balance principle).

Therefore, the zwitterion concentration is geometrically between the two equal extremes, and:

$$
\text{p}I = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}
$$

**Calculation of pI for Glycine (2 marks):**

$$
\text{p}I_{\text{Glycine}} = \dfrac{2.34 + 9.60}{2} = \dfrac{11.94}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{p}I_{\text{Glycine}} = 5.97\;}
$$

This means that in a solution buffered at **pH 5.97**, glycine carries **no net charge** and will not migrate in an electric field. Below pH 5.97, it is positively charged (cation); above pH 5.97, it is negatively charged (anion).

---

### ‚≠ź QUESTION B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ CHOICE 2 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Classify amino acids based on their **nutritional requirement** and **metabolic fate**. Give at least two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**A. Nutritional Classification (3.5 marks):**

| Category | Definition | Examples (2 required) |
|---|---|---|
| **Essential Amino Acids (9)** | Cannot be synthesized by the human body; must be obtained from the diet | (1) **Lysine** ‚ÄĒ limiting AA in cereals; (2) **Tryptophan** ‚ÄĒ precursor of serotonin & niacin |
| **Non-Essential Amino Acids (11)** | Can be synthesized by the body in adequate amounts | (1) **Glycine** ‚ÄĒ simplest AA; (2) **Glutamate** ‚ÄĒ key nitrogen donor |
| **Conditionally Essential (6)** | Become essential under physiological stress (illness, prematurity, trauma) | (1) **Glutamine** ‚ÄĒ critical for immune cells & gut; (2) **Arginine** ‚ÄĒ needed during wound healing & sepsis |

*Mnemonic for Essential AAs: "PVT TIM HALL" = Phe, Val, Thr, Trp, Ile, Met, His, (All), Leu, Lys*

**B. Metabolic Classification (3.5 marks):**

| Category | Definition | Examples (2 required) |
|---|---|---|
| **Glucogenic (15)** | Carbon skeleton catabolized to pyruvate or TCA cycle intermediates ‚Üí can be converted to **glucose** via gluconeogenesis | (1) **Alanine** ‚ÄĒ converted to pyruvate via transamination; (2) **Aspartate** ‚ÄĒ converted to oxaloacetate |
| **Ketogenic (2)** | Carbon skeleton catabolized to acetyl-CoA or acetoacetyl-CoA ‚Üí can be converted to **ketone bodies** and fatty acids | (1) **Leucine** ‚ÄĒ exclusively ketogenic; (2) **Lysine** ‚ÄĒ exclusively ketogenic |
| **Both Glucogenic & Ketogenic (5)** | Have dual metabolic fate ‚ÄĒ part of the carbon skeleton enters glucogenic pathway, part enters ketogenic pathway | (1) **Isoleucine** ‚ÄĒ yields both acetyl-CoA and succinyl-CoA; (2) **Phenylalanine** ‚ÄĒ yields fumarate + acetoacetate |

---

**Q. (b)** What is a peptide bond? Explain the formation of a dipeptide with a suitable example and diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Peptide Bond (1 mark):**
A **peptide bond** is an **amide linkage (‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚Äď)** formed between the **őĪ-carboxyl group of one amino acid** and the **őĪ-amino group of another amino acid**, with the elimination of **one molecule of water (H‚āāO)**. It is a **covalent, planar bond** with partial double-bond character due to resonance.

**General Equation of Peptide Bond Formation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{H}_{2}\text{N‚ÄďCHR}_{1}\text{‚ÄďCOOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{N‚ÄďCHR}_{2}\text{‚ÄďCOOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_{2}\text{N‚ÄďCHR}_{1}\text{‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚ÄďCHR}_{2}\text{‚ÄďCOOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O}
$$

This is a **condensation (dehydration) reaction**, typically catalyzed by the **ribosome** (in vivo) or by chemical coupling agents (in vitro).

**Detailed Example: Formation of Aspartame (Dipeptide) (3 marks):**

Aspartame is a synthetic dipeptide of **L-Aspartate** and **L-Phenylalanine methyl ester**.

**Step 1:** Identify the functional groups participating.

- L-Aspartate provides the **őĪ-COOH** group.
- L-Phenylalanine methyl ester provides the **őĪ-NH‚āā** group.

**Step 2:** Condensation reaction with loss of H‚āāO.

The amino group (‚ÄďNH‚āā) of phenylalanine attacks the carboxyl group (‚ÄďCOOH) of aspartate. One molecule of water is eliminated.

**Step 3:** Final Aspartame Structure:

$$
\text{HOOC‚ÄďCH}_{2}\text{‚ÄďCH(NH}_{2}\text{)‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚ÄďCH(CH}_{2}\text{‚ÄďC}_{6}\text{H}_{5}\text{)‚ÄďCOOCH}_{3}
$$

**Functional Significance:** Aspartame is approximately **200 times sweeter than sucrose** and is widely used as an artificial sweetener in diet beverages and sugar-free products. However, it is **unstable at high temperatures** (cooking/baking) and is **contraindicated for phenylketonuria (PKU) patients**, who cannot metabolize phenylalanine.

**Mermaid Diagram of Peptide Bond Formation:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    AA1["Amino Acid 1: H2N-CHR1-COOH Carboxyl group will react"]:::reactant
    AA2["Amino Acid 2: H2N-CHR2-COOH Amino group will react"]:::reactant
    WATER["Water Molecule: H2O eliminated"]:::water
    PEPTIDE["Dipeptide: H2N-CHR1-CO-NH-CHR2-COOH New Peptide Bond CO-NH"]:::product

    AA1 -->|"condensation dehydration"| PEPTIDE
    AA2 -->|"condensation dehydration"| PEPTIDE
    AA1 -->|"releases OH"| WATER
    AA2 -->|"releases H"| WATER

    classDef reactant fill:#F39C12,stroke:#7F4F00,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#27AE60,stroke:#0E4D1E,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef water fill:#3498DB,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF
```
