## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students Marks (Avoid These!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "essential" with "necessary for life"** ‚ÄĒ All 20 amino acids are necessary for life, but only 9 are "essential" in the nutritional sense (must come from diet). Do not list all 20 as essential.
>
> 2. **Wrong pI Formula Selection** ‚ÄĒ Many students apply the **neutral** pI formula (pK‚āź‚āĀ + pK‚āź‚āā)/2 to **acidic or basic** amino acids. **Examiner's rule:** The pI is always the average of the **two pK‚āź values that flank the zwitterion** (zero net charge species). For acidic AAs, use the two carboxyl pK‚āź values; for basic AAs, use the two amino pK‚āź values.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "Zwitterion" form** ‚ÄĒ When defining amino acid structure, students often draw the neutral form (H‚āāN‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOOH). Examiners expect you to explicitly state that at **physiological pH**, the molecule exists as a **zwitterion** (H‚āÉN‚Āļ‚ÄďCHR‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ).
>
> 4. **Mixing up the pK‚āź numbering** ‚ÄĒ Convention: pK‚āź‚āĀ = lowest pK‚āź value (őĪ-COOH), pK‚āź‚āā = second lowest (őĪ-NH‚āÉ‚Āļ or side chain COOH), pK‚āźR = R-group ionization. Reversing this is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 5. **Not citing the 20 standard amino acids by name AND single-letter code** ‚ÄĒ Examiners reward students who use both conventions (e.g., "Lysine, Lys, K").
>
> 6. **In metabolic classification, marking Leucine or Lysine as "both"** ‚ÄĒ These are **purely ketogenic**. "Both" category = Isoleucine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Tyrosine. Memorize this!
>
> 7. **Skipping units in calculations** ‚ÄĒ If you write pI = 5.97, mention it's a **dimensionless pH value**. Trivial, but examiners notice.
>
> 8. **Not drawing peptide bond formation** ‚ÄĒ In "explain peptide bond" questions, an **unlabeled diagram** gets partial credit. A **labeled diagram with H‚āāO elimination** gets full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Memorize Before the ESE**

### ūüďč Core Definitions
- [ ] **Amino Acid** = Organic compound with ‚ÄďNH‚āā and ‚ÄďCOOH on the **same őĪ-carbon**
- [ ] **Zwitterion** = Dipolar ion with both ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ and ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ but net charge = 0
- [ ] **Essential AA** = Cannot be synthesized by humans; 9 total
- [ ] **Isoelectric Point (pI)** = pH at which an amino acid carries zero net charge
- [ ] **Peptide Bond** = ‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚Äď linkage formed by condensation with loss of H‚āāO
- [ ] **pK‚āź** = Negative log of acid dissociation constant; midpoint of buffering region

### ūüďč Classification Schemes (Memorize All 4)
- [ ] **By Polarity:** Nonpolar (9), Polar uncharged (6), Positive (3), Negative (2)
- [ ] **By Nutrition:** Essential (9), Non-essential (11), Conditionally essential (6)
- [ ] **By Metabolism:** Glucogenic (15), Ketogenic (2), Both (5)
- [ ] **By R-group Structure:** Aliphatic, Aromatic, Sulfur, Hydroxyl, Acidic/Amide, Basic

### ūüďč The 20 Standard Amino Acids (Memorize Names, Codes, and Categories)
- [ ] Essential: **PVT TIM HALL** = Phe, Val, Thr, Trp, Ile, Met, His, Leu, Lys
- [ ] Purely Ketogenic: **Leu, Lys**
- [ ] Both Gluco/Keto: **Ile, Phe, Thr, Trp, Tyr**
- [ ] Positively Charged: **His, Lys, Arg**
- [ ] Negatively Charged: **Asp, Glu**
- [ ] Sulfur-containing: **Cys, Met**
- [ ] Aromatic: **Phe, Tyr, Trp**
- [ ] Non-chiral: **Glycine only** (R-group = H)

### ūüďč The 3 Critical pI Formulas
- [ ] **Neutral AA** (Gly, Ala, Val, etc.): $\text{p}I = (\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}) / 2$
- [ ] **Acidic AA** (Asp, Glu): $\text{p}I = (\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}) / 2$
- [ ] **Basic AA** (Lys, Arg, His): $\text{p}I = (\text{p}K_{aR} + \text{p}K_{a2}) / 2$

### ūüďč Standard pI Values (Frequently Tested)
- [ ] Glycine: **5.97** | Alanine: **6.01** | Valine: **5.96**
- [ ] Aspartate: **2.95** | Glutamate: **3.22**
- [ ] Histidine: **7.59** | Lysine: **9.74** | Arginine: **10.76**

### ūüďč Industrial / Food Chemistry Applications (High-Yield for PCFTT205)
- [ ] **MSG** (Monosodium Glutamate) = umami flavor enhancer
- [ ] **Aspartame** = dipeptide (Asp + Phe-OMe), 200√ó sweeter than sucrose
- [ ] **Maillard Reaction** = free ‚ÄďNH‚āā groups react with reducing sugars ‚Üí browning & aroma
- [ ] **BCAAs** (Leu, Ile, Val) = branched-chain AAs for sports nutrition
- [ ] **Lysine fortification** = improves protein quality of cereals
- [ ] **Gelatin** = rich in Gly, Pro, Hyp (collagen-derived)

### ūüďč Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch Equation (Must Derive from Scratch)
- [ ] $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\left(\dfrac{[\text{A}^{-}]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$
- [ ] At pH = pK‚āź ‚Üí [HA] = [A‚ĀĽ] ‚Üí maximum buffer capacity

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For the **14-mark long answer**, always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition (2 marks) ‚Üí (2) Classification table (4 marks) ‚Üí (3) Diagram or derivation (1 mark)**. This guarantees 7 marks per sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Properties of Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical and Chemical Properties of Proteins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Proteins** are high-molecular-weight, nitrogen-containing biological macromopolymers (biopolymers) composed of one or more long chains of **őĪ-amino acid residues** linked together by **peptide (amide) bonds** formed between the őĪ-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the őĪ-amino group of the next. The average nitrogen content of proteins is **16 % (w/w)**, a foundational constant used in the classical Kjeldahl and Dumas methods for crude protein estimation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Highlight)**
> *"Physical and chemical properties of proteins govern their functional behavior in food systems ‚ÄĒ including solubility, hydration, gelation, foaming, emulsification, denaturation, and their characteristic color/precipitation reactions."*

The **Physical Properties** of proteins describe their behavior in bulk (solubility, viscosity, optical activity, sedimentation, electrophoretic mobility) without altering covalent bonding. The **Chemical Properties** describe covalent and ionic reactivity: amphoteric nature, hydrolysis, and the specific qualitative color reactions used as analytical fingerprints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **"The Origami Paper Analogy"** ūüß¨
>
> Imagine a long printed newspaper strip (the polypeptide chain). The *print* on the paper represents the amino acid sequence (primary structure). Now:
> - **Folding the strip into a crane** = secondary & tertiary structure (held by weak H-bonds, hydrophobic forces, and disulfide bridges).
> - **Putting the crane inside a transparent glass display case** = the protein's physical behavior in a solution (how it dissolves, scatters light, moves in an electric field).
> - **Burning the paper, cutting the print, or bleaching it with chemicals** = the *chemical* properties ‚ÄĒ hydrolysis, color reactions, denaturation.
>
> The "shape" (physical) and the "stuff it is made of" (chemical) together determine whether a protein behaves like egg-white (gels), milk casein (curdles), or wheat gluten (gives dough elasticity).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Recap (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Average N content | **16 % (w/w)** | Kjeldahl factor: Protein % = N % √ó **6.25** |
| Peptide bond length | **0.133 nm** (‚Čą 1.33 √Ö) | Partial double-bond character |
| Peptide bond energy | **~88 kJ/mol** | Stabilizes backbone |
| Average residue mass | **110 Da** | Basis of UV absorbance calculations |
| Isoelectric point (pI) of a neutral AA | **pI = (pKa‚āĀ + pKa‚āā) / 2** | Henderson-Hasselbalch derived |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Properties

### 2.1.1 Physical Properties of Proteins

**1. Solubility**
- Governed by the balance of hydrophilic (‚ÄďOH, ‚ÄďCOOH, ‚ÄďNH‚āā) and hydrophobic (‚ÄďR alkyl, aromatic) side chains.
- **Globular proteins** (albumin, globulin) ‚Üí water-soluble.
- **Fibrous proteins** (collagen, keratin) ‚Üí insoluble.
- **Minimum solubility occurs at the isoelectric point (pI)** because the net charge = 0 ‚Üí no electrostatic repulsion ‚Üí aggregation.

**2. Hydration & Water Binding**
- Hydrophilic groups bind water via H-bonds.
- Expressed as **g water bound per g protein** (hygroscopicity).
- Critical in baking, meat hydration, and powder flow.

**3. Viscosity**
- Intrinsic viscosity [ő∑] reflects hydrodynamic volume of the molecule.
- Increases with molecular asymmetry (e.g., myosin > serum albumin).
- Given by the **Mark‚ÄďHouwink equation** (see formula sheet).

**4. Gelation**
- Partial unfolding followed by ordered aggregation via H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and disulfide cross-links.
- Examples: ovalbumin (egg-white), gelatin (collagen hydrolysate), gluten (wheat).

**5. Foaming & Emulsification**
- Proteins adsorb at air‚Äďwater (foam) or oil‚Äďwater (emulsion) interfaces, lowering interfacial tension.
- Stabilized by the **film elasticity** of the unfolded protein layer.
- Functionality index: **Foam Capacity (FC)** and **Foam Stability (FS)**.

**6. Denaturation**
- Loss of native 3-D structure **without cleavage of peptide bonds**.
- Caused by heat, pH extremes, organic solvents, heavy metals (Hg¬≤‚Āļ, Pb¬≤‚Āļ), urea, guanidine HCl, detergents, radiation.
- Denaturation is often **reversible** (renaturation, e.g., ribonuclease) but in foods it is **irreversible** (egg-white coagulation).

**7. Optical Properties**
- **UV absorption at 280 nm** ‚Üí due to aromatic residues Tyr, Trp (and to a lesser extent Phe, Cys disulfides).
- Governed by the **Beer‚ÄďLambert Law**: $A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$
- Proteins are **optically active** due to chiral őĪ-carbons; rotate plane-polarized light (specific rotation [őĪ]).

**8. Sedimentation & Electrophoresis**
- Sedimentation coefficient *s* measured in **Svedbergs (S)**: 1 S = 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≥ s.
- At pH ‚Č† pI, proteins migrate in an electric field (anode at low pH, cathode at high pH).

### 2.1.2 Chemical Properties of Proteins

**1. Amphoteric Nature & Zwitterion**
- Proteins carry both ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ and ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ groups ‚Üí act as **buffers** and have a characteristic **pI**.

**2. Hydrolysis**
- **Acid hydrolysis** (6 M HCl, 110 ¬įC, 24 h) ‚Üí complete hydrolysis to free AAs; destroys Trp, partially destroys Ser, Thr, Tyr.
- **Alkaline hydrolysis** (4 M NaOH) ‚Üí destroys Cys, Ser, Thr, Arg; used for Trp estimation.
- **Enzymatic hydrolysis** (trypsin, chymotrypsin, pepsin, papain, bromelain) ‚Üí specific peptide cleavage; preserves all AAs.

**3. Color Reactions (Analytical Fingerprints)**

| Reaction | Reagent | Detects | Color |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biuret** | CuSO‚āĄ + NaOH | Peptide bonds (‚Č• 2) | Violet / Purple |
| **Ninhydrin** | Ninhydrin (0.2 %) | Free őĪ-amino groups | Ruhemann's Purple (570 nm); Pro ‚Üí Yellow (440 nm) |
| **Xanthoproteic** | Conc. HNO‚āÉ | Aromatic AAs (Tyr, Trp, Phe) | Yellow ‚Üí Orange (with NH‚āÉ) |
| **Millon's** | Hg(NO‚āÉ)‚āā / HNO‚āÉ + heat | Phenolic OH (Tyr) | Red / Brick-red |
| **Hopkins‚ÄďCole** | Glyoxalic acid + H‚āāSO‚āĄ | Indole ring (Trp) | Violet |
| **Sakaguchi** | őĪ-Naphthol + NaOBr | Guanidino group (Arg) | Red |
| **Nitroprusside** | Sodium nitroprusside + NH‚āÉ | ‚ÄďSH group (Cys) | Red ‚Üí Purple (on adding acetic acid) |
| **Pauly's Diazo** | Diazonium salt | Imidazole / Phenol (His, Tyr) | Orange-red |

**4. Precipitation Reactions**
- **Salting-out** (e.g., (NH‚āĄ)‚āāSO‚āĄ) ‚ÄĒ preferential hydration; preserves native structure.
- **Salting-in** ‚ÄĒ at low salt concentration, increases solubility.
- **Isoelectric precipitation** ‚ÄĒ at pI (e.g., casein at pH 4.6).
- **Heavy metal precipitation** ‚ÄĒ Pb¬≤‚Āļ, Hg¬≤‚Āļ, Ag‚Āļ form insoluble proteinates (used in detoxification, e.g., "egg-white for mercury poisoning").
- **Organic solvent precipitation** ‚ÄĒ ethanol, acetone (dehydrates and disrupts hydrophobic core).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations ‚ÄĒ they are repeated every KTU examination cycle.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Protein \%} = N\% \times 6.25$ | Kjeldahl conversion factor (1/0.16) | % w/w |
| 2 | $A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$ | Beer‚ÄďLambert Law (UV at 280 nm) | A dimensionless, $\varepsilon$ L¬∑g‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, c g/L, ‚Ąď cm |
| 3 | $c = \dfrac{A_{280}}{\varepsilon_{280} \, \ell}$ | Concentration from UV absorbance | g/L |
| 4 | $\mathrm{pI} = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}$ | pI of neutral amino acid | pH units |
| 5 | $\mathrm{pH} = pK_{a} + \log \dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch | dimensionless |
| 6 | $[\eta] = K \cdot M^{a}$ | Mark‚ÄďHouwink intrinsic viscosity vs. M | dL/g |
| 7 | $s = \dfrac{v}{\omega^{2} r}$ | Sedimentation coefficient | Svedberg (1 S = 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≥ s) |
| 8 | $\text{DH \%} = \dfrac{h}{h_{\text{tot}}} \times 100$ | Degree of hydrolysis (h = cleaved bonds) | % |
| 9 | $\text{FC \%} = \dfrac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{liquid}}} \times 100$ | Foam capacity | % |
| 10 | $E\% = \dfrac{A_{280}}{c \, \ell} \times 100$ | Extinction coefficient (1 %) | mL¬∑g‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Dairy**: Casein isoelectric precipitation at pH 4.6 ‚Üí paneer, cheese, caseinates.
- **Baking**: Gluten viscoelasticity ‚Üí dough strength; Maillard browning of egg proteins ‚Üí crust color.
- **Meat processing**: Salt-induced protein extraction ‚Üí myosin ‚Üí actomyosin ‚Üí heat-set gel.
- **Beverages**: Foam stability of beer (hordein) and cappuccino milk foam (casein/ő≤-lactoglobulin).
- **Pharma & Nutrition**: Protein hydrolysates with low molecular weight for hypoallergenic infant formula.
- **Detoxification**: Raw egg-white (albumin) historically used to bind Hg¬≤‚Āļ before gastric lavage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ The Kjeldahl Factor 6.25

The Kjeldahl method determines the nitrogen content of a food. Because proteins contain (on average) **16 % nitrogen by mass**, the protein mass can be back-calculated.

### Step-by-Step Logic

Let the total mass of the food sample be $m_{\text{sample}}$ (g). Let the experimentally measured nitrogen mass be $m_N$ (g).

**Step 1:** The mass fraction of nitrogen in pure protein is empirically established as:
$$w_N = \frac{\text{mass of N in 1 mol of average AA residue}}{\text{molar mass of 1 mol of average AA residue}} = \frac{14}{110} \approx 0.16$$

**Step 2:** The mass of protein in the sample is therefore the nitrogen mass divided by 0.16:
$$m_{\text{protein}} = \frac{m_N}{0.16} = 6.25 \, m_N$$

**Step 3:** Expressed as a percentage:
$$\% \text{ Protein} = \frac{m_{\text{protein}}}{m_{\text{sample}}} \times 100 = \frac{6.25 \, m_N}{m_{\text{sample}}} \times 100 = 6.25 \times \%N$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor 6.25 is universal for "average" protein. For specific foods with known AA composition, more accurate factors are used: **5.70 (rice), 5.83 (maize), 6.38 (milk), 5.46 (almond).**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Isoelectric Point from pKa Values

The **Henderson‚ÄďHasselbalch equation** describes the dissociation of a weak acid:
$$\mathrm{pH} = pK_a + \log \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

For a neutral amino acid with three dissociable groups (e.g., Alanine: COOH, NH‚āÉ‚Āļ, neutral R), the pI is the pH at which the zwitterion concentration is maximum and the net charge = 0.

**Step 1:** At very low pH, the species is fully protonated $\text{H}_2\text{A}^+$.
**Step 2:** As pH rises, the carboxyl deprotonates first: $\text{H}_2\text{A}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{HA} + \text{H}^+$ with $K_{a1}$.
**Step 3:** At intermediate pH, the neutral zwitterion HA dominates.
**Step 4:** As pH rises further, the amino group deprotonates: $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{A}^- + \text{H}^+$ with $K_{a2}$.

The pI is the pH at which $[\text{HA}]$ is maximum. Setting $d[\text{HA}]/d\mathrm{pH} = 0$ (or, equivalently, the average of the two pKa values flanking the zero-charge species):

$$\mathrm{pI} = \frac{pK_{a1}(\text{COOH}) + pK_{a2}(\text{NH}_3^+)}{2}$$

### Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Glycine
$pK_{a1} = 2.34$ (COOH), $pK_{a2} = 9.60$ (NH‚āÉ‚Āļ).

$$\mathrm{pI}_{\text{gly}} = \frac{2.34 + 9.60}{2} = \frac{11.94}{2} = 5.97$$

**Verification**: at pH 5.97, Gly exists predominantly as the zwitterion ‚ĀļH‚āÉN‚ÄďCH‚āā‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ; net charge = 0. ‚úď

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Protein Concentration from UV Absorbance (Beer‚ÄďLambert Law)

The Beer‚ÄďLambert Law states that the absorbance $A$ of a solution is proportional to the molar absorptivity $\varepsilon$, the concentration $c$, and the optical path length $\ell$:
$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange for concentration:
$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \, \ell}$$

**Step 2:** For proteins, $\varepsilon_{280}$ is expressed per gram (not per mole) because proteins are polydisperse. Common value for BSA: $\varepsilon_{280}^{1\%} = 6.7$ (dimensionless when $c$ is in g/100 mL and $\ell$ in cm).

**Step 3:** Therefore, in practical food-chemistry units (g/L with ‚Ąď in cm):
$$c \, (\text{g/L}) = \frac{A_{280}}{6.7 / 10} \times \frac{1}{\ell \, (\text{cm})} = \frac{A_{280}}{0.67 \, \ell}$$

### Worked Example
A whey protein isolate solution shows $A_{280} = 0.402$ in a 1 cm cuvette. Calculate the concentration in g/L.

**Given**: $A = 0.402$, $\varepsilon_{280}^{1\%} = 6.7$ for whey proteins, $\ell = 1$ cm.

**Step 1:** Express $\varepsilon$ in L¬∑g‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ: $\varepsilon = 6.7 \div 10 = 0.67$ L¬∑g‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑cm‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
**Step 2:** Apply Beer‚ÄďLambert:
$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \, \ell} = \frac{0.402}{0.67 \times 1} = 0.60 \, \text{g/L}$$

**Answer:** $c = 0.60$ g/L of whey protein.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚ÄĒ Degree of Hydrolysis (DH) from OPA Method

The OPA (ortho-phthalaldehyde) method quantifies free őĪ-amino groups released during hydrolysis. The DH is:
$$\mathrm{DH \%} = \frac{h}{h_{\text{tot}}} \times 100$$

where $h$ = number of peptide bonds cleaved (per mole of protein) and $h_{\text{tot}}$ = total peptide bonds per mole of native protein (‚Čą 7.7 meq/g for casein, 8.8 for whey).

### Worked Example
A 10 g/L casein hydrolysate is reacted with OPA. The absorbance at 340 nm gives a free őĪ-amino content of 0.77 meq/g protein. Compute DH.

**Step 1:** Identify $h$ from OPA calibration = 0.77 mmol of new N-termini per g of protein.
**Step 2:** $h_{\text{tot}}$ for casein = 7.7 meq/g.
**Step 3:**
$$\mathrm{DH \%} = \frac{0.77}{7.7} \times 100 = 10 \,\%$$

This corresponds to a "limited" hydrolysate ‚ÄĒ useful for moderately flavored, hypoallergenic formulations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Protein Structure & Functional Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRIMARY STRUCTURE\nLinear AA sequence\nheld by PEPTIDE BONDS]
    B[SECONDARY STRUCTURE\nalpha-helix / beta-sheet\nheld by H-BONDS]
    C[TERTIARY STRUCTURE\n3D folding\nheld by Hydrophobic, Ionic,\nDisulfide, H-bond]
    D[QUATERNARY STRUCTURE\nMulti-subunit assembly\nheld by non-covalent forces]
    E[DENATURED STATE\nLoss of 2, 3, 4 structure\nPeptide bonds INTACT]
    F[FUNCTIONAL FOOD PROPERTIES]
    G[CHEMICAL REACTIONS\nHydrolysis, Biuret, Ninhydrin]
    H[PHYSICAL PROPERTIES\nSolubility, Viscosity, Gelation,\nFoaming, Emulsification]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    A -.cleavage of peptide bonds.-> G
    C -.heat, pH, salt, solvents.-> E
    D -.heat, pH, salt, solvents.-> E
    E --> H
    C --> F
    D --> F
    A --> G
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Protein Denaturation in Food Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Native Protein] -->|Heat\n70-90 deg C| B[Partial Unfolding]
    A -->|Acid pH 1-3\nor Alkali pH 11-13| B
    A -->|Heavy Metals\nHg2+, Pb2+| B
    A -->|Organic Solvents\nEthanol, Acetone| B
    A -->|Urea / Guanidine HCl| B
    A -->|Mechanical Shear\nHigh-pressure| B
    B --> C[Aggregation\nvia exposed SH, H-bonds,\nHydrophobic patches]
    C --> D[Gel Network\nEgg white, Tofu, Yogurt]
    C --> E[Coagulum\nPaneer, Cheese curd]
    C --> F[Foam Stabilization\nMeringue, Bread]
    C --> G[Emulsion Stabilization\nMayonnaise, Milk]
```

## 4.3 Analytical Decision Tree ‚ÄĒ Color Reactions for Protein Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Unknown Protein Sample] --> Q1{Peptide bonds\npresent?}
    Q1 -- Yes, >=2 --> Biuret[BIURET TEST\nCuSO4 + NaOH\nVIOLET color]
    Q1 -- No --> End[Stop: Free AA only]
    Start --> Q2{Free alpha-NH2\ngroups?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Nin[NINHYDRIN TEST\nRuhemann's PURPLE\n570 nm]
    Start --> Q3{Aromatic AAs\nTyr, Trp, Phe?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Xan[XANTHOPROTEIC\nConc HNO3\nYELLOW then ORANGE]
    Start --> Q4{Tyrosine\npresent?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Mil[MILLON'S TEST\nHg NO3 2\nRED precipitate]
    Start --> Q5{Tryptophan\npresent?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Hop[HOPKINS-COLE\nGlyoxalic acid\nVIOLET ring]
    Start --> Q6{Arginine\npresent?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Sak[SAKAGUCHI\nalpha-Naphthol + NaOBr\nRED color]
    Start --> Q7{Cysteine\npresent?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Nit[NITROPRUSSIDE\nRed-Purple with NH3]
```

## 4.4 Solubility Behavior as a Function of pH (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWpH[pH less than pI]
        L1[Net POSITIVE charge]
        L2[High solubility]
    end
    subgraph ATpI[pH equals pI]
        M1[Net ZERO charge]
        M2[MINIMUM solubility\nISOELECTRIC PRECIPITATION]
    end
    subgraph HIGHpH[pH greater than pI]
        H1[Net NEGATIVE charge]
        H2[High solubility]
    end
    LOWpH --> ATpI
    ATpI --> HIGHpH
    M2 -->|e.g., Casein pH 4.6| APP[Application: Cheese, Paneer, Tofu]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (2 √ó 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define denaturation of proteins. List four factors that cause denaturation and state two physicochemical changes that accompany it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Denaturation is the disruption of the native secondary, tertiary and/or quaternary structure of a protein by physical or chemical agents, **without cleavage of peptide bonds**. The primary structure (AA sequence) remains intact.

**Four causative factors (1 mark, 0.25 each):**
1. Heat (> 60 ¬įC for most globular proteins)
2. Extreme pH (strong acid or alkali)
3. Heavy-metal ions (Hg¬≤‚Āļ, Pb¬≤‚Āļ, Ag‚Āļ)
4. Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone, isopropanol)
*(Acceptable alternates: urea, guanidine HCl, detergents, radiation, mechanical shear.)*

**Two physicochemical changes (1 mark, 0.5 each):**
1. **Loss of solubility** (e.g., egg-white coagulation) due to exposure of hydrophobic patches and aggregation.
2. **Loss of biological activity** (e.g., enzyme inactivation) due to disruption of active-site 3-D geometry.
*(Other accepted: increased viscosity, gelation, change in optical rotation, altered UV absorbance.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023]`
**Explain the amphoteric nature of proteins and the significance of the isoelectric point (pI) in food processing. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Amphoteric nature (1 mark):** Proteins contain both acidic (‚ÄďCOOH, ‚ÄďSH, phenolic ‚ÄďOH) and basic (‚ÄďNH‚āā, imidazole, guanidino) side chains. They can therefore **donate or accept protons** and behave as both an acid and a base depending on the surrounding pH. This makes them excellent biological buffers.

**Significance of pI (1.5 marks):**
- The pI is the pH at which a protein carries **zero net charge**.
- At pI, electrostatic repulsion between protein molecules is minimum ‚Üí **minimum solubility, maximum tendency to precipitate**.
- In food processing, controlling pH around the pI is used to **isolate and purify** proteins (e.g., casein from milk at pH 4.6, gluten from wheat dough).

**Example (0.5 mark):** **Casein** (pI ‚Čą 4.6) precipitates when milk is acidified (curdling for cheese/paneer); **Soy protein** (pI ‚Čą 4.5) is precipitated in tofu manufacture by adding MgCl‚āā or by acidification.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 √ó 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Read Before Attempting**
> - Do **not** confuse *denaturation* (non-covalent disruption) with *hydrolysis* (peptide bond cleavage). Examiners deduct 1‚Äď2 marks for this mix-up.
> - In color-reaction tables, **write the specific AA detected**, not just "amino acids". Eg: Biuret ‚Üí peptide bonds, Xanthoproteic ‚Üí **aromatic** AAs, Millon's ‚Üí **tyrosine** phenolic OH, Hopkins‚ÄďCole ‚Üí **tryptophan** indole.
> - For pI calculations, **state the two flanking pKa values clearly**. Skipping this step loses 1 mark.
> - Always include **units** in numerical answers (g/L, %, pH units).

---

### ūüďė Question A ‚ÄĒ `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

#### (a) Discuss in detail the physical properties of proteins that determine their functional behavior in food systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The functional behavior of proteins in foods ‚ÄĒ their ability to thicken, gel, foam, emulsify, and hold water ‚ÄĒ is governed by their physicochemical properties.

**1. Solubility (1 mark)**
Solubility depends on the balance of hydrophilic and hydrophobic residues and on pH, ionic strength, and temperature. Globular proteins (albumin, ő≤-lactoglobulin) are water-soluble due to surface-exposed polar groups. Fibrous proteins (collagen, keratin) are insoluble because hydrophobic residues dominate. Solubility is **minimum at the isoelectric point (pI)** because of zero net charge and absence of electrostatic repulsion, leading to aggregation. This principle is exploited in precipitating casein (pI 4.6) during cheese-making.

**2. Hydration / Water-holding capacity (0.5 mark)**
Polar and charged side chains bind water molecules by hydrogen bonds and ion-dipole interactions. Expressed as g water per g protein. Critical in comminuted meat products, baked goods, and dehydrated foods where it influences juiciness and freshness.

**3. Viscosity (0.5 mark)**
A function of molecular size, shape, and hydration. The intrinsic viscosity [ő∑] is related to molecular mass $M$ by the **Mark‚ÄďHouwink equation** $[\eta] = K M^{a}$. Asymmetric or unfolded proteins (e.g., heated myosin) have higher viscosity than compact globular ones.

**4. Gelation (1 mark)**
Gelation proceeds in two stages: (i) partial unfolding of native protein on heating, exposing reactive groups; (ii) ordered aggregation through H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and disulfide cross-links to form a three-dimensional network that traps water. Examples: ovalbumin in egg-white gels at 62‚Äď65 ¬įC; gelatin (denatured collagen) forms thermo-reversible gels; myosin forms the heat-set gel of sausage meat.

**5. Foaming (1 mark)**
Proteins adsorb at the air‚Äďwater interface, denature, and form a viscoelastic film. **Foam Capacity (FC)** measures initial foam volume; **Foam Stability (FS)** measures persistence. Globular proteins with balanced hydrophobic/hydrophilic character (egg-white, whey) make good foaming agents.

**6. Emulsification (0.5 mark)**
Proteins adsorb at oil‚Äďwater interfaces, with hydrophobic residues in the oil phase and hydrophilic residues in water, lowering interfacial tension. The **Emulsifying Activity Index (EAI)** and **Emulsifying Stability Index (ESI)** quantify this. Caseins are excellent emulsifiers in milk.

**7. Denaturation (1 mark)**
Heat, pH extremes, salts, organic solvents, and mechanical shear disrupt non-covalent interactions, leading to unfolding. In food systems, denaturation is usually **irreversible** and is often a prerequisite for gelation, foaming, and coagulation.

**8. Optical properties (0.5 mark)**
Proteins absorb UV light at **280 nm** (Tyr, Trp, Phe, Cys-SS) ‚ÄĒ basis of spectrophotometric quantification using the **Beer‚ÄďLambert Law**: $A = \varepsilon c \ell$. They are also **optically active** because of the chiral őĪ-carbon, rotating plane-polarized light.

**9. Sedimentation & Electrophoresis (1 mark)**
The **sedimentation coefficient** $s$ (in Svedbergs, 1 S = 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≥ s) is determined by analytical ultracentrifugation and reflects molecular mass and shape. **Electrophoresis** (SDS-PAGE, native PAGE, 2-D gel) separates proteins on the basis of charge and/or size; migration depends on the difference between the buffer pH and the protein's pI.

> *Diagrammatic representation of these properties and their inter-relationship in food systems earns extra credit (up to 0.5 mark).*

---

#### (b) Explain the principle and chemistry of any **four** important color reactions used to identify proteins and amino acids. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Each reaction is explained by its (i) reagent, (ii) chemistry, (iii) specific group detected, and (iv) observable change.

**1. Biuret Test (2 marks)**
- **Reagent:** CuSO‚āĄ in strong NaOH.
- **Principle:** In alkaline solution, Cu¬≤‚Āļ forms a violet coordination complex with the **nitrogen atoms of peptide bonds** (‚Č• 2 peptide bonds required). The reaction is named after the compound biuret (H‚āāN‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚ÄďCO‚ÄďNH‚āā).
- **Chemistry:** Four nitrogen atoms donate lone pairs to a single Cu¬≤‚Āļ ion, forming a square-planar complex.
- **Result:** Violet / purple color, measured at 540 nm for quantitative protein estimation.
- **Note:** Free amino acids (no peptide bond) do **not** give a positive test.

**2. Ninhydrin Test (2 marks)**
- **Reagent:** 0.2 % ninhydrin (indane-1,2,3-trione) in ethanol, heated.
- **Principle:** Ninhydrin reacts with **free őĪ-amino groups** (NH‚āā of free AAs, N-termini of peptides, and NH‚āÉ of ammonia) to give a reduced form of ninhydrin (hydrindantin) which condenses with another molecule of ninhydrin and ammonia to form **Ruhemann's Purple**.
- **Chemistry:** Oxidative deamination releases NH‚āÉ, CO‚āā, and an aldehyde; the NH‚āÉ condenses with ninhydrin to form the purple diketo-pyrrole dye.
- **Result:** Deep blue-purple color (őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď = 570 nm); Pro and Hyp give a **yellow** product (őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď = 440 nm) because their secondary amine forms a different chromophore.
- **Use:** Detection of free AAs, peptide mapping, forensic fingerprinting, quantitative AA analysis.

**3. Xanthoproteic Test (1 mark)**
- **Reagent:** Concentrated HNO‚āÉ, heat.
- **Principle:** Nitration of the **aromatic rings** of Tyrosine, Tryptophan, and (to a lesser extent) Phenylalanine.
- **Chemistry:** The ‚ÄďOH and ‚ÄďNH groups on the aromatic ring activate it towards electrophilic aromatic substitution; ‚ÄďNO‚āā is introduced to form yellow nitro-derivatives.
- **Result:** Yellow color (acidic) turning **orange** on addition of NH‚āÉ (because the deprotonated phenolate is more chromophoric).
- **Indicator:** Presence of aromatic AAs.

**4. Millon's Test (1 mark)**
- **Reagent:** Millon's reagent (Hg(NO‚āÉ)‚āā + HNO‚āÉ + trace HNO‚āā), heat.
- **Principle:** Mercury ions complex with the **phenolic hydroxyl group of Tyrosine** (and other phenols) to form a red mercury‚Äďphenoxide complex.
- **Result:** Brick-red precipitate / color.
- **Caveat:** Not specific to proteins ‚ÄĒ gives positive test with any phenolic compound.

**5. (Optional, brief) Hopkins‚ÄďCole Test (0.5 mark)**
- **Reagent:** Glyoxalic acid + concentrated H‚āāSO‚āĄ.
- **Principle:** Glyoxalic acid condenses with the **indole ring of Tryptophan** in the presence of a strong acid catalyst to give a violet-colored product at the acid‚Äďaqueous interface.
- **Result:** Violet ring at the junction of the two layers.

**6. (Optional, brief) Sakaguchi Test (0.5 mark)**
- **Reagent:** őĪ-Naphthol + sodium hypobromite (NaOBr) in alkaline medium.
- **Principle:** The **guanidino group of Arginine** reacts with őĪ-naphthol and an oxidant to form a red-colored naphthol‚Äďguanidine complex.

---

### ūüďó Question B ‚ÄĒ `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model Paper]` (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Describe the chemical properties of proteins with emphasis on amphoteric nature, isoelectric point, and hydrolysis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Amphoteric nature (2 marks)**
Proteins are polyampholytes because their constituent amino acids carry both acidic (‚ÄďCOOH, ‚ÄďSH, phenolic ‚ÄďOH of Tyr) and basic (őĶ-NH‚āā of Lys, imidazole of His, guanidino of Arg, őĪ-NH‚āā) groups. Depending on the pH of the medium, a protein can either donate or accept protons:
- In **acidic** solution (pH < pI): excess H‚Āļ ‚Üí carboxyls remain protonated (‚ÄďCOOH), amines become ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ ‚Üí net **positive** charge.
- In **alkaline** solution (pH > pI): OH‚ĀĽ deprotonates carboxyls to ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ and amines become ‚ÄďNH‚āā ‚Üí net **negative** charge.
- At the **pI**: the number of positive charges exactly equals the number of negative charges ‚Üí net charge = 0; molecule exists as a zwitterion.

This amphoteric behaviour makes proteins effective **biological buffers** and the basis of techniques like **ion-exchange chromatography** and **electrophoresis**.

**2. Isoelectric point (pI) (2 marks)**
The pI is the pH at which a protein carries **zero net charge** and shows **minimum solubility, minimum electrophoretic mobility, and maximum tendency to precipitate**.

For a neutral amino acid (no ionizable R group):
$$\mathrm{pI} = \frac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}$$

For acidic AAs (e.g., Asp, Glu) with an extra ‚ÄďCOOH in the side chain:
$$\mathrm{pI} = \frac{pK_{a1} + pK_{aR}}{2}$$
For basic AAs (e.g., Lys, Arg) with an extra ‚ÄďNH‚āā in the side chain:
$$\mathrm{pI} = \frac{pK_{a2} + pK_{aR}}{2}$$

**Examples:**
- Glycine: $(2.34 + 9.60)/2 = 5.97$
- Aspartic acid: $(1.88 + 3.65)/2 = 2.77$ (acidic)
- Lysine: $(8.95 + 10.53)/2 = 9.74$ (basic)

**Applications in food processing (0.5 mark):** Casein (pI 4.6) is precipitated from milk by acidification; soy protein (pI 4.5) is precipitated by Ca¬≤‚Āļ in tofu manufacture.

**3. Hydrolysis (2.5 marks)**
Hydrolysis is the cleavage of peptide bonds by the addition of water, releasing free amino acids or smaller peptides.

**(i) Acid hydrolysis (0.8 mark):**
- Conditions: 6 M HCl, 110 ¬įC, 22‚Äď24 h in a sealed tube.
- Produces complete hydrolysis to free L-amino acids.
- **Limitations:** Tryptophan is completely destroyed; cysteine, serine, threonine, and tyrosine are partially destroyed; asparagine and glutamine are converted to Asp and Glu with loss of the amide-N (cannot distinguish amides from acids).
- **Use:** Standard AA analysis (post-column ninhydrin detection).

**(ii) Alkaline hydrolysis (0.7 mark):**
- Conditions: 4 M NaOH, 110 ¬įC, 4 h.
- Preserves tryptophan (hence used for Trp estimation).
- **Limitations:** Cysteine, cystine, serine, threonine, arginine, and lysine are partially or fully destroyed; racemization may occur.

**(iii) Enzymatic hydrolysis (1 mark):**
- Proteases (pepsin at pH 1.5‚Äď2, trypsin at pH 7‚Äď9, chymotrypsin, papain, bromelain, alcalase) cleave specific peptide bonds.
- Produces peptides of defined chain length; **preserves all amino acids**.
- **Specificity:** Trypsin cleaves at the C-side of Lys and Arg; chymotrypsin at the C-side of aromatic AAs; pepsin at the C-side of Phe, Tyr, Trp.
- **Use:** Preparation of protein hydrolysates for hypoallergenic infant formula, sport nutrition, flavor generation (hydrolyzed vegetable protein, HVP).

> *A comparative table of the three hydrolysis methods (acid vs. alkaline vs. enzymatic) summarizing reagent, time, AA losses, and typical application earns an additional 0.5 mark.*

---

#### (b) Discuss the principle, procedure, and applications of **Kjeldahl method** for protein estimation. Mention its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle (2 marks)**
The Kjeldahl method estimates the crude protein content of a food by determining its total nitrogen content. The principle rests on the empirical observation that proteins contain on average **16 % nitrogen by mass**. The method involves three sequential steps:

(i) **Digestion:** Organic matter is oxidized by conc. H‚āāSO‚āĄ at ~350 ¬įC in the presence of a catalyst (CuSO‚āĄ or HgSO‚āĄ) and K‚āāSO‚āĄ (to raise the boiling point). All organic N is converted into ammonium sulfate:
$$\text{Protein} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, \text{cat.}} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{SO}_2 \uparrow$$

(ii) **Distillation:** The cooled digest is made alkaline with 40 % NaOH. NH‚āÉ is liberated and steam-distilled into a receiving flask containing a known excess of standard boric acid (H‚āÉBO‚āÉ, 4 %):
$$(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \uparrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{BO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
(Boronate‚Äďammonium complex fixes the NH‚āÉ in solution.)

(iii) **Titration:** The borate‚Äďammonium complex is titrated with a standard solution of HCl (or H‚āāSO‚āĄ) using a mixed indicator (methyl red + bromocresol green) until the green color changes to a faint pink at the endpoint:
$$\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{BO}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{H}_3\text{BO}_3$$

**2. Procedure (summary, 1.5 marks)**
1. Weigh 1‚Äď2 g of finely ground sample into a Kjeldahl flask.
2. Add 0.5 g CuSO‚āĄ, 10 g K‚āāSO‚āĄ, and 25 mL conc. H‚āāSO‚āĄ.
3. Digest in a fume hood until the solution is clear pale blue (‚Čą 2 h).
4. Cool, dilute carefully with distilled water, transfer to a distillation unit.
5. Add 40 % NaOH; distill NH‚āÉ into 50 mL of 4 % boric acid + 3‚Äď4 drops of indicator.
6. Titrate distillate with 0.1 N HCl. Record the titre value V.
7. Run a **blank** (reagents only) and record V_blank.

**3. Calculations (1.5 marks)**
$$\% N = \frac{(V - V_{\text{blank}}) \times N_{\text{HCl}} \times 1.4007}{\text{Weight of sample (g)}}$$

$$\% \text{ Crude Protein} = \%N \times 6.25$$

where 1.4007 = (14 g of N per equivalent √ó 100) / 1000 = atomic mass of N √ó 10 / 1000.

**Sample numerical:** A 1.5 g milk powder sample required 18.40 mL of 0.1 N HCl, blank 0.20 mL. Compute % protein.
- $\%N = \dfrac{(18.40 - 0.20) \times 0.1 \times 1.4007}{1.5} = \dfrac{18.20 \times 0.1 \times 1.4007}{1.5} = 1.699\%$
- $\% \text{ Protein (using factor 6.38 for milk)} = 1.699 \times 6.38 = 10.84\,\%$

**4. Applications (1 mark)**
- Routine protein estimation in dairy, cereal, meat, and feed industries.
- Official method of AOAC, FSSAI, BIS, and ISO for regulatory protein labelling.
- Used in nutritional labelling and quality control of flour, milk powder, infant formula, pet food, and forensic analysis of food adulteration.

**5. Limitations (1 mark)**
1. Does **not** measure protein directly ‚ÄĒ measures total N. Non-protein N (urea, free AAs, nucleotides, nitrates) inflates the value ‚Üí result is "**crude protein**", not true protein.
2. Hazardous reagents (conc. H‚āāSO‚āĄ, NaOH, Hg catalysts) require careful handling and disposal.
3. Time-consuming (digestion alone takes 1‚Äď2 h).
4. Cannot distinguish between different proteins or detect protein quality (AA profile).
5. Trp is destroyed during digestion ‚Üí underestimation in Trp-rich samples.
6. Nitrite- and nitrate-N are partially or fully lost during digestion.
7. Catalyst Hg must be precipitated before distillation (Na‚āāS or Zn) to avoid ammonia‚Äďmercury complexes.

> *Comparative note (0.5 mark optional):* Alternative methods ‚ÄĒ **Dumas** (combustion, faster, no hazardous chemicals) and **Bradford / Biuret / Lowry / BCA** (colorimetric, suited to soluble proteins). Each has its own merits and KTU students may be asked to compare.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Recap ‚ÄĒ Re-read the night before the exam.**

- ‚ėź Proteins = polypeptides of őĪ-amino acids linked by **peptide (amide) bonds**; average N content **16 %** ‚Üí factor **6.25**.
- ‚ėź **Denaturation ‚Č† hydrolysis**: denaturation disrupts 2¬į, 3¬į, 4¬į structure only; hydrolysis cleaves peptide bonds.
- ‚ėź **Amphoteric** = can act as both acid and base; carries both ‚ÄďNH‚āÉ‚Āļ and ‚ÄďCOO‚ĀĽ.
- ‚ėź **pI**: pH of zero net charge ‚Üí minimum solubility, maximum precipitation. For neutral AA: pI = (pK‚āź‚āĀ + pK‚āź‚āā) / 2.
- ‚ėź **At pH < pI ‚Üí positive charge** (migrates to cathode in electrophoresis); **pH > pI ‚Üí negative charge** (migrates to anode).
- ‚ėź **Biuret** ‚Üí peptide bonds (‚Č• 2); violet with Cu¬≤‚Āļ/NaOH.
- ‚ėź **Ninhydrin** ‚Üí free őĪ-amino groups; **Ruhemann's Purple** (570 nm); Pro ‚Üí **yellow** (440 nm).
- ‚ėź **Xanthoproteic** ‚Üí aromatic AAs (Tyr, Trp, Phe); yellow ‚Üí orange with NH‚āÉ.
- ‚ėź **Millon's** ‚Üí Tyr phenolic ‚ÄďOH; brick-red with Hg¬≤‚Āļ.
- ‚ėź **Hopkins‚ÄďCole** ‚Üí Trp indole ring; violet ring with glyoxalic acid/H‚āāSO‚āĄ.
- ‚ėź **Sakaguchi** ‚Üí Arg guanidino group; red with őĪ-naphthol/NaOBr.
- ‚ėź **Nitroprusside** ‚Üí Cys ‚ÄďSH group; red-purple with Na‚āā[Fe(CN)‚āÖNO]/NH‚āÉ.
- ‚ėź **UV absorbance at 280 nm** is due to Tyr, Trp, Phe and disulfide Cys; quantified by **Beer‚ÄďLambert**: $A = \varepsilon c \ell$.
- ‚ėź **Optical activity** of proteins arises from chiral őĪ-carbons.
- ‚ėź **Hydrolysis methods**: acid (6 M HCl, 110 ¬įC, 24 h ‚ÄĒ destroys Trp), alkaline (4 M NaOH ‚ÄĒ destroys Cys/Ser/Thr/Arg, preserves Trp), enzymatic (trypsin at Lys/Arg, chymotrypsin at aromatics, pepsin at Phe/Tyr/Trp ‚ÄĒ preserves all).
- ‚ėź **Kjeldahl steps**: Digestion (H‚āāSO‚āĄ + catalyst) ‚Üí Distillation (NH‚āÉ into H‚āÉBO‚āÉ) ‚Üí Titration (HCl) ‚Üí %N √ó 6.25 = % crude protein.
- ‚ėź **Salting-out** (high (NH‚āĄ)‚āāSO‚āĄ) precipitates proteins reversibly (e.g., globulins); **salting-in** (low salt) increases solubility.
- ‚ėź **Heavy metals (Hg¬≤‚Āļ, Pb¬≤‚Āļ, Ag‚Āļ)** denature proteins by binding ‚ÄďSH and ‚ÄďNH‚āā groups (clinical relevance: raw egg-white for Hg poisoning).
- ‚ėź **Sedimentation coefficient** $s$ is in **Svedbergs (1 S = 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≥ s)**; larger proteins have larger $s$ but the relationship with $M$ is non-linear due to shape.
- ‚ėź **Mark‚ÄďHouwink**: $[\eta] = K M^{a}$ ‚ÄĒ relates intrinsic viscosity to molecular mass; $a$ depends on conformation.
- ‚ėź **Foam Capacity %** = (V_foam / V_liquid) √ó 100; **Foam Stability** = foam volume retained after a fixed time (e.g., 30 min).
- ‚ėź **Gelation** requires partial unfolding ‚Üí ordered aggregation ‚Üí 3-D network trapping water; ovalbumin 62‚Äď65 ¬įC, gelatin below 30 ¬įC (thermo-reversible), myosin 40‚Äď60 ¬įC (irreversible).
- ‚ėź **Emulsifiers**: caseins (milk), egg-yolk lipoproteins, soy proteins ‚ÄĒ all adsorb at O/W interface.
- ‚ėź Memorize: 5.70 (rice), 5.83 (maize), 6.25 (general), 6.38 (milk) ‚ÄĒ the **Kjeldahl conversion factors**.
- ‚ėź **Mnemonic for color reactions**: "**B**ig **N**oise **X**plodes **M**aking **H**oles **S**lowly **N**ext" ‚Üí Biuret, Ninhydrin, Xanthoproteic, Millon's, Hopkins‚ÄďCole, Sakaguchi, Nitroprusside.
- ‚ėź Acidic AA pI uses $(pK_{a1} + pK_{aR})/2$; basic AA pI uses $(pK_{a2} + pK_{aR})/2$ ‚ÄĒ **do not blindly average all three pKa values.**
- ‚ėź **Urea and guanidine HCl** denature proteins by disrupting the hydrophobic core and forming competing H-bonds with peptide backbone.

> *End of Module 3 Notes ‚ÄĒ Physical and Chemical Properties of Proteins. Best of luck for the KTU examinations!* ūüéď
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Important Protein Sources - Milk, Meat, Fish, Egg and Cereal Proteins, Texturized Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Important Protein Sources - Milk, Meat, Fish, Egg and Cereal Proteins, Texturized Proteins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proteins** are high-molecular-weight biological polymers of $\alpha$-amino acids joined by **peptide bonds** ($-\text{CO}-\text{NH}-$). The **nutritional quality** of a dietary protein is determined by its **amino acid composition**, **digestibility**, **bioavailability**, and ability to support growth and maintenance of body tissues. Important protein sources are classified as **animal proteins** (milk, meat, fish, egg) and **plant proteins** (cereals, legumes, oilseeds), each differing markedly in amino acid profile, digestibility, and functional behaviour.

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies important protein sources into two broad families:

| Family | Sources | Key Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Animal Proteins** | Milk, Meat, Fish, Egg | *Complete* proteins (contain all 9 essential amino acids in adequate proportions) |
| **Plant Proteins** | Cereal grains (wheat, rice, maize) | *Incomplete* proteins (limited in **lysine**, sometimes threonine/tryptophan) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Think of proteins as Lego castles, and amino acids as individual Lego blocks."**
> - **Egg protein** = a perfectly designed Lego set with **all block shapes in exact right proportions** ‚Üí builds a flawless castle (REFERENCE PROTEIN).
> - **Milk/Meat/Fish** = excellent sets, missing maybe 1 piece, but you can borrow it from another set (high BV).
> - **Cereals** = a great castle kit, but the "studs" (lysine blocks) are missing, so the castle wall stays weak.
> - **Texturized Proteins** = *engineered* Lego kits ‚ÄĒ scientists re-shape plant proteins (like soy) into fibrous, meat-like structures to compensate for what nature left out.

## 1.3 Standard Quality Metrics (Highlighted)

The following are the **standard nutritional metrics** universally accepted by FAO/WHO and KTU examiners for evaluating protein quality:

- **BV (Biological Value)** = retained-N / absorbed-N √ó 100 ‚ÄĒ measures how efficiently the body utilises absorbed protein.
- **PER (Protein Efficiency Ratio)** = weight gain (g) / protein intake (g).
- **NPU (Net Protein Utilisation)** = retained-N / intake-N √ó 100.
- **Chemical Score** = (limiting AA in test protein / same AA in reference) √ó 100.
- **PDCAAS (Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score)** ‚ÄĒ modern global standard (max = 1.00).

> [!NOTE]
> **FAO/WHO Reference Pattern** uses whole-egg or human-milk amino acid composition as the benchmark, with **scoring values** of approximately: Lys = 5.8 g/100g, Met+Cys = 2.5, Thr = 3.4, Trp = 1.1 (g per 100 g protein).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Analysis: Classification of Major Food Proteins

### A. Milk Proteins (~3.3 g per 100 mL)

Milk proteins are divided into two principal fractions at **pH 4.6 (isoelectric point of casein)**:

**(i) Caseins (‚Čą 80 % of total milk protein)** ‚ÄĒ *phosphoproteins* that precipitate at pH 4.6.
- $\alpha_{s1}$-casein (‚Čą 38 %): Ca-sensitive, main curd former.
- $\alpha_{s2}$-casein (‚Čą 10 %): highly phosphorylated.
- $\beta$-casein (‚Čą 36 %): hydrophobic, forms micelles.
- $\kappa$-casein (‚Čą 13 %): contains *glycomacropeptide*; stabilises micelles; cleaved by **chymosin** (rennet) ‚Üí curd formation in cheese-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casein micelles** consist of sub-micelles (~ 20 nm) of $\alpha_{s}$ and $\beta$ caseins held together by **calcium phosphate bridges** and stabilised on the surface by $\kappa$-casein. Diameter = **30‚Äď300 nm**; average **~150 nm**.

**(ii) Whey Proteins (‚Čą 20 %)** ‚ÄĒ *remain soluble at pH 4.6*.
- $\beta$-lactoglobulin (‚Čą 50 % of whey) ‚ÄĒ binds retinol & fatty acids; heat-sensitive.
- $\alpha$-lactalbumin (‚Čą 20 %) ‚ÄĒ lactose synthesis cofactor; high in Trp.
- Immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM) ‚ÄĒ passive immunity.
- Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA).
- Lactoferrin ‚ÄĒ iron-binding, antimicrobial.
- Lysozyme, lactoperoxidase.

**Why milk is a "complete" protein:** It contains all 9 essential amino acids; **BV = 91**; **PDCAAS = 1.00**; **PER ‚Čą 3.1**.

---

### B. Meat Proteins (~20 g per 100 g edible portion)

Meat proteins are classified by their **solubility and location in muscle fibre**:

**(i) Myofibrillar proteins (50‚Äď55 %)** ‚ÄĒ salt-soluble; contractile machinery.
- **Myosin** (‚Čą 43 %): thick filament; ATPase activity; forms gel on heating.
- **Actin** (‚Čą 22 %): thin filament; binds myosin ‚Üí actomyosin complex (post-mortem).
- **Tropomyosin & Troponin**: regulatory proteins of contraction.

**(ii) Sarcoplasmic proteins (30‚Äď35 %)** ‚ÄĒ water-soluble; enzymes & pigments.
- **Myoglobin**: red pigment (Fe¬≤‚Āļ); gives *red* colour to beef/lamb. Converted to **metmyoglobin** (Fe¬≥‚Āļ) on oxidation ‚Üí brown colour.
- Glycolytic enzymes, creatine kinase.

**(iii) Connective-tissue proteins (‚Čą 10‚Äď15 %)** ‚ÄĒ insoluble.
- **Collagen**: triple helix of glycine‚Äďproline‚Äďhydroxyproline; converted to **gelatin** on moist heat (> 70 ¬įC).
- **Elastin**: elastic fibres; indigestible, contributes to meat toughness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHC (Water Holding Capacity)** of meat is governed by **myofibrillar proteins** at the **isoelectric point (pI ‚Čą 5.0‚Äď5.2)**. Adding salt shifts pH away from pI ‚Üí increases WHC and tenderness.

**Quality:** Meat has BV ‚Čą 80; limiting amino acid is often **methionine** in some cuts.

---

### C. Fish Proteins (~18‚Äď25 g per 100 g)

- Composition similar to meat but with **less connective tissue** and **softer collagen** (denatures at 25‚Äď30 ¬įC in fish vs. 70 ¬įC in mammals).
- Sarcoplasmic proteins dominate in some species; myofibrillar in others.
- Rich in **lysine, methionine, threonine** (excellent complement to cereals!).
- **Scombroid poisoning** arises from bacterial decarboxylation of histidine ‚Üí histamine in poorly stored dark-meat fish (tuna, mackerel).
- **PDCAAS ‚Čą 1.00**; high digestibility (~ 94‚Äď98 %).

---

### D. Egg Proteins (~12.5 g per 100 g whole egg; ~13 g in albumen)

The **reference standard** of protein nutrition. Key egg-white proteins (egg albumen):

| Protein | % of albumen protein | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Ovalbumin** | 54 | Storage phosphoprotein |
| **Ovotransferrin (Conalbumin)** | 12 | Iron-binding; antimicrobial |
| **Ovomucoid** | 11 | Trypsin inhibitor (heat-stable) |
| **Globulins (G2, G3)** | 8 | Lysozyme-like activity |
| **Lysozyme** | 3.5 | Hydrolyses bacterial cell walls |
| **Ovomucin** | 1.5 | Thick gel of thick white |
| **Avidin** | 0.05 | Binds biotin (raw egg "anti-nutrient") |

**Yolk proteins:** **lipovitellin**, **phosvitin** (phosphoprotein), **livetin** (globulins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whole egg**: PDCAAS = 1.00, BV = 94, PER = 3.9 ‚ÄĒ the *gold standard reference protein* per FAO/WHO 1991.

---

### E. Cereal Proteins (~7‚Äď15 %)

Cereal grains are the **world's largest protein source by mass** but are limited in **lysine** (and sometimes **threonine, tryptophan**). Classified by the **Osborne fractionation** scheme:

| Fraction | Solubility | Example | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Albumins** | Water | Minor | Enzymic |
| **Globulins** | Dilute salt | Minor | |
| **Prolamins** | 70 % EtOH | **Gliadin** (wheat), **Zein** (maize), **Hordein** (barley) | Hydrophobic; storage |
| **Glutelins** | Dilute acid/alkali | **Glutenin** (wheat), **Oryzenin** (rice) | Storage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wheat Gluten = Gliadin + Glutenin**. When hydrated and kneaded, glutenin provides **elasticity** (via disulfide cross-links) and gliadin provides **viscosity/ extensibility** ‚ÄĒ together they form the viscoelastic dough network responsible for bread structure. The *CO2-retention capacity* of gluten is what makes bread rise.

**Maize zein** is the most deficient ‚ÄĒ completely lacks **lysine and tryptophan**. **Rice oryzenin** has the best amino acid profile among cereals (lysine ~ 3.8 %).

---

### F. Texturized Proteins (Engineered Foods)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP)** is produced by thermoplastic extrusion of defatted soy flour/concentrate at **140‚Äď180 ¬įC, 20‚Äď40 % moisture, high shear**, causing the protein to *unfold, align, and re-cross-link* into fibrous, meat-like chunks. It is widely used as a **meat extender** (1:3 to 1:1 replacement) in sausages, patties, and ready-meals.

Other modern texturized products:

- **Single Cell Protein (SCP)** ‚ÄĒ microbial biomass from *Candida utilis*, *Methylophilus methylotrophus*, *Fusarium venenatum* (Quorn‚ĄĘ). Grown on petroleum fractions, methanol, molasses, whey, or agricultural waste.
- **Spun-fibre proteins** ‚ÄĒ wet spinning of soy/zein dope through spinneret into acid/salt bath ‚Üí isometric fibres assembled into bundles.
- **Structured plant proteins** ‚ÄĒ high-moisture extrusion mimics whole-muscle texture (chicken-like strips).
- **Insect protein** ‚ÄĒ *Tenebrio molitor* (yellow mealworm), *Acheta domesticus* (house cricket); ~ 50‚Äď65 % crude protein on dry weight.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Biological Value (BV)** | $\text{BV} = \dfrac{N_{\text{retained}}}{N_{\text{absorbed}}} \times 100$ | Egg 94, Cow milk 91, Fish 83, Beef 80, Rice 83 | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | **Net Protein Utilisation (NPU)** | $\text{NPU} = \dfrac{N_{\text{retained}}}{N_{\text{intake}}} \times 100$ | Egg 94, Milk 82, Wheat flour 35‚Äď50 | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | **Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER)** | $\text{PER} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{weight (g)}}{\text{protein intake (g)}}$ | Casein 2.5, Egg 3.9, Wheat 1.5 | ratio |
| 4 | **Chemical (Amino Acid) Score** | $\text{CS} = \dfrac{\text{mg limiting AA in 1 g test protein}}{\text{mg same AA in 1 g reference protein}} \times 100$ | ‚Č§ 100 | % |
| 5 | **Digestibility (D)** | $D = \dfrac{N_{\text{intake}} - N_{\text{feces}}}{N_{\text{intake}}} \times 100$ | Egg 97, Milk 95, Wheat 86 | % |
| 6 | **PDCAAS** | $\text{PDCAAS} = \text{Chemical Score} \times \text{Digestibility}$ (capped at 1.00) | Egg 1.00, Soy 0.91, Wheat 0.42 | 0‚Äď1 |
| 7 | **Nitrogen-to-Protein (N‚ÜíP)** | $\text{Protein \%} = N \% \times F$ where $F \approx 6.25$ (general); **5.70 for wheat**, **5.95 for rice**, **6.38 for milk** | specific factors | factor |
| 8 | **Mutual Complementation (proportion)** | $\text{Cereal : Pulse} \approx 4 : 1$ to $5 : 1$ for lysine‚Äďmethionine balance | mix-ratio | ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> Use **specific $N$-to-protein conversion factors** in numerical problems. $F = 6.38$ for milk/dairy, $6.25$ for meat/egg/soy, **5.70 for wheat**, **5.95 for rice**, **5.83 for maize**. Examiners frequently award 1 mark just for the correct factor.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Food Industry Utility

- **Cereal‚ÄďPulse complementation** (India's *Idli*, *Dosa* batter = rice + black gram) is a classical nutritional engineering solution to lysine limitation.
- **TVP extrusion** is a core food-engineering process with annual production > **1 million tonnes** globally ‚ÄĒ important for food security, low-cost protein delivery, and vegan markets.
- **Quorn‚ĄĘ (mycoprotein)** uses continuous-airlift fermenters with **Fusarium venenatum** ‚ÄĒ a textbook example of SCP in commercial food production.
- **Egg as reference** is used by every regulatory body (FDA, EFSA, FSSAI) to set the Daily Value (DV) for protein at **50 g/day** for an adult.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ‚ÄĒ Calculating BV, PER, and PDCAAS

**Problem (Model):** A rat-feeding trial yields the following data for a new protein source:
- N intake = 1.20 g, N in faeces = 0.18 g, N in urine = 0.42 g
- Weight gain = 24 g over 28 days; total protein eaten = 60 g
- Limiting amino acid (lysine) in test protein = 4.5 mg/g protein
- Lysine in reference (egg) = 7.2 mg/g protein

**Compute BV, PER, Chemical Score, Digestibility, and PDCAAS.**

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Digestibility (D)
$$
D \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{intake}} - N_{\text{feces}}}{N_{\text{intake}}} \times 100
$$
$$
D \;=\; \frac{1.20 - 0.18}{1.20} \times 100
\;=\; \frac{1.02}{1.20} \times 100
\;=\; 85.0\%
$$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Biological Value (BV)
First, **N absorbed** = N intake ‚ąí N faeces = 1.20 ‚ąí 0.18 = 1.02 g.
Then, **N retained** = N absorbed ‚ąí N urinary = 1.02 ‚ąí 0.42 = 0.60 g.
$$
\text{BV} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{retained}}}{N_{\text{absorbed}}} \times 100
\;=\; \frac{0.60}{1.02} \times 100
\;\approx\; 58.82\%
$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ PER
$$
\text{PER} \;=\; \frac{\Delta \text{weight}}{\text{protein intake}}
\;=\; \frac{24 \text{ g}}{60 \text{ g}}
\;=\; 0.40
$$

> **Interpretation:** A PER of 0.40 is *very low* ‚ÄĒ confirms a poor protein source (likely a cereal or a severely processed product).

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Chemical Score
$$
\text{CS} \;=\; \frac{\text{lysine in test}}{\text{lysine in reference}} \times 100
\;=\; \frac{4.5}{7.2} \times 100
\;=\; 62.5
$$

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ PDCAAS
$$
\text{PDCAAS} \;=\; \text{CS} \times D
\;=\; 0.625 \times 0.85
\;=\; 0.531
$$

> **Rounded answer:** PDCAAS = **0.53** ‚Üí indicates a *moderate* protein, useful as a supplement but not a sole source.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ‚ÄĒ Cereal‚ÄďPulse Complementation Calculation

**Problem:** A blend of rice and chickpea is to be formulated. Rice protein contains **3.8 % lysine**; chickpea contains **7.2 % lysine**. Reference (egg) lysine = **6.5 %**. Find the blend ratio (rice : chickpea) that achieves a Chemical Score = 100.

Let $x$ = fraction of rice protein in the blend, $(1-x)$ = fraction of chickpea protein.
$$
\text{Lys in blend} \;=\; 3.8x + 7.2(1-x) \;=\; 7.2 - 3.4x
$$

For CS = 100, blend lysine must equal reference lysine = 6.5 %:
$$
7.2 - 3.4x \;=\; 6.5
\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 3.4x \;=\; 0.7
\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x \;=\; 0.206
$$

So **rice ‚Čą 20.6 %** of total protein and **chickpea ‚Čą 79.4 %**. Equivalent to a **mass ratio rice : chickpea ‚Čą 1 : 1** (assuming similar protein contents), confirming the textbook recommendation of a **1:1 rice‚Äďdal** combination in Indian diets.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Comparison of Major Protein Sources

| Parameter | Whole Egg | Cow Milk | Beef Muscle | Fish (cod) | Wheat flour | Soybean |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Protein (%) | 12.5 | 3.3 | 20‚Äď25 | 18 | 10‚Äď14 | 36 |
| BV | **94** | 91 | 80 | 83 | 54‚Äď65 | 74 |
| PER | **3.9** | 3.1 | 2.9 | 3.5 | 1.5 | 2.2 |
| PDCAAS | **1.00** | 1.00 | 0.92 | 1.00 | 0.42 | 0.91 |
| Digestibility (%) | 97 | 95 | 94 | 98 | 86 | 95 |
| Limiting AA | None | Met/Cys (slight) | Met/Cys | None | **Lysine** | Met |
| Reference? | **YES (gold std.)** | YES | NO | YES | NO | NO |
| Key protein | Ovalbumin | Casein | Myosin | Myosin/Actin | Glutenin + Gliadin | Glycinin, $\beta$-conglycinin |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Reasoning: Why Gluten is Viscoelastic

1. **Hydration:** When flour is mixed with water, gluten proteins absorb ~ **2√ó their weight** in water.
2. **Disulfide bonds:** Glutenin subunits are linked by inter-chain **S‚ÄďS bridges** (cysteine residues).
3. **Shear unfolding:** Kneading aligns glutenin molecules ‚Üí exposes more ‚ÄďSH groups ‚Üí forms new S‚ÄďS cross-links.
4. **Gliadin role:** Gliadin (prolamin, single polypeptide) acts as a *plasticiser* between glutenin networks, contributing **viscosity and extensibility**.
5. **CO‚āā trapping:** During yeast fermentation, CO‚āā bubbles are trapped in the gluten mesh ‚Üí dough expansion ‚Üí porous bread crumb.
6. **Heat-set:** On baking (> 70 ¬įC), the network is fixed by thermal denaturation of gluten proteins ‚Üí permanent rigid foam.

> **Engineering insight:** This is the only naturally occurring *biopolymer foam* used in everyday food ‚ÄĒ and a marvel of food colloid / polymer engineering.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023]`
**"Why is whole egg considered the 'reference protein' for nutritional evaluation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Whole egg is taken as the reference standard (FAO/WHO 1991) because:
1. It contains **all 9 essential amino acids** in proportions that *exactly match* human requirement (Chemical Score ‚Čą 100).
2. It shows the **highest digestibility (~ 97 %)** and the **highest biological value (BV = 94, PER = 3.9, PDCAAS = 1.00)**.
3. It is easily available in pure form, stable, and well-characterised; therefore other proteins are *scored relative to it*.

*(3 √ó 1 mark distribution: amino acid balance / high BV-PER / regulatory standard.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]`
**"Mention any three functional properties of egg proteins in food systems."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Foaming/Whipping** ‚ÄĒ ovalbumin and globulin unfold at the air‚Äďwater interface, stabilising foam (meringue, cakes).
2. **Emulsification** ‚ÄĒ yolk lipoproteins (LDL) form stable oil-in-water emulsions (mayonnaise).
3. **Heat coagulation / Gelation** ‚ÄĒ ovalbumin coagulates at 80‚Äď85 ¬įC giving structural firmness to custards and cakes.
4. **Binding / Adhesion** ‚ÄĒ egg proteins bind crumbs in coating batters.

*(Any 3 √ó 1 mark each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (common loss-of-marks):**
> - Confusing **NPU with BV** (NPU uses *intake* in denominator; BV uses *absorbed*).
> - Using **$F = 6.25$** universally instead of the specific N‚ÜíP factor (e.g., 5.70 for wheat ‚ÄĒ loses 1 mark).
> - Writing "**meat has all amino acids**" without specifying *limiting AA* (methionine/cystine).
> - Skipping the **sub-part split** in 14-mark answers ‚ÄĒ examiner expects clearly demarcated (a) 7 + (b) 7.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**(a) Classify the major milk proteins with their approximate proportions and functions. Briefly explain how casein micelles are stabilised.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss the nutritional quality of cereal proteins. Why are they considered "incomplete"? Explain mutual supplementation with an example.** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification of Milk Proteins (7 marks):**

Milk proteins are separated into two fractions at **pH 4.6** (the pI of casein):

| Fraction | % of Total | Sub-fractions & Roles |
|---|---|---|
| **Caseins** | 80 % | $\alpha_{s1}$ (38 %) ‚Äď Ca¬≤‚Āļ sensitive, curd former; $\alpha_{s2}$ (10 %) ‚Äď highly phosphorylated; $\beta$ (36 %) ‚Äď hydrophobic, micelle core; $\kappa$ (13 %) ‚Äď **stabilises micelles** by forming hairy outer layer; chymosin cleaves it ‚Üí cheese curd. |
| **Whey proteins** | 20 % | $\beta$-lactoglobulin (10 % of total); $\alpha$-lactalbumin (4 %); immunoglobulins (passive immunity); BSA; lactoferrin (Fe-binding); lysozyme, lactoperoxidase. |

**Casein micelle architecture:**
1. Sub-micelles of $\alpha_{s1}$, $\alpha_{s2}$, $\beta$-casein are bridged by **colloidal calcium phosphate (CCP)** nanoclusters.
2. $\kappa$-casein projects its C-terminal **glycomacropeptide** ("hairy layer") outward, providing **steric + electrostatic repulsion** that prevents micelle aggregation.
3. Micelle diameter: **30‚Äď300 nm**, mean ~150 nm; carries a net negative charge at milk pH 6.6 ‚Üí remains colloidally dispersed.
4. Chymosin cleaves $\kappa$-casein between **Phe105‚ÄďMet106** ‚Üí loss of the hairy layer ‚Üí micelles aggregate ‚Üí curd (cheese-making principle).

*(Marks: classification 3, sub-fractions 2, micelle stabilisation 2.)*

**(b) Cereal Proteins & Mutual Supplementation (7 marks):**

Cereal proteins are **storage proteins** in the endosperm. By **Osborne fractionation**:
- **Prolamins** (alcohol-soluble): gliadin (wheat), zein (maize), hordein (barley), kafirin (sorghum).
- **Glutelins** (alkali-soluble): glutenin (wheat), oryzenin (rice).
- Small amounts of albumins and globulins.

**Why "incomplete"?**
- **Lysine** is the **first limiting amino acid** in wheat, rice, and maize because prolamins and glutelins have very low lysine content (rice is best with ~ 3.8 % Lys; maize zein is worst with almost zero Lys and Trp).
- Threonine and tryptophan may also be limiting.
- Hence **Chemical Score = 30‚Äď55** for most cereals; PDCAAS 0.4‚Äď0.6.

**Mutual Supplementation (Complementation):**
Combining cereals with **legumes** (which are *rich in lysine* but *low in methionine*) restores the balance of all essential amino acids.
- Cereals supply: Met, Cys, Trp.
- Pulses supply: Lys, Thr.
- **Examples:** Rice + dal (1:1); Idli/Dosa = rice + black gram (4:1); Khichdi; bread with peanut butter; wheat-soy blends (e.g., *Biscuits containing 10 % soy flour*).

*Mathematical demonstration* (mentioned in class):
If a blend of rice (3.8 % Lys) + chickpea (7.2 % Lys) is made ~ 20:80, blend lysine ‚Čą 6.5 % ‚Üí CS = 100 (per Example 3.2 above).

*(Marks: Osborne fractions 2, "incomplete" reason 2, complementation principle + example 3.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2022]`

**(a) Differentiate between myofibrillar, sarcoplasmic, and connective-tissue proteins of meat. Comment on the role of myoglobin in meat colour.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) What are texturized proteins? Describe the extrusion process for TVP. List four applications.** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Meat Proteins & Myoglobin (7 marks):**

| Class | % in muscle | Solubility | Examples & Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Myofibrillar** | 50‚Äď55 | Salt-soluble | **Myosin** (thick filament, ATPase, gel-forming), **Actin** (thin filament, contraction), tropomyosin, troponin. Convert to **actomyosin** post-mortem ‚Üí rigor mortis. |
| **Sarcoplasmic** | 30‚Äď35 | Water-soluble | **Myoglobin** (O‚āā storage, red colour), glycolytic enzymes, creatine kinase, hemoglobin (residual). |
| **Connective-tissue** | 10‚Äď15 | Insoluble | **Collagen** (triple helix of Gly-X-Y, converted to gelatin at > 70 ¬įC, < 25 ¬įC in fish), **elastin** (indigestible, tough). |

**Myoglobin and colour (3 marks):**
- Native myoglobin (**Mb**, Fe¬≤‚Āļ + H‚āāO) ‚Üí **purplish-red** (fresh cut).
- On exposure to O‚āā ‚Üí **oxymyoglobin (MbO‚āā, Fe¬≤‚Āļ + O‚āā)** ‚Üí **bright cherry red** (bloomed meat).
- Prolonged oxidation ‚Üí **metmyoglobin (MetMb, Fe¬≥‚Āļ)** ‚Üí **brown** (oxidised, undesirable in fresh display).
- Curing with **nitrite** produces **nitrosomyoglobin** ‚Üí pink colour of cured meats (ham, bacon).

**(b) Texturized Proteins & Extrusion (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Engineered protein products that mimic the *texture, appearance, and mouthfeel* of meat, derived from plant or microbial proteins. They are used as **meat extenders, analogues, and substitutes**.

**Types:** TVP (soy), spun-fibre proteins, SCP (yeast/bacteria), mycoprotein (Quorn‚ĄĘ), high-moisture extruded meat analogues.

**Extrusion Process Flow (4 marks):**
1. **Raw material** ‚ÄĒ defatted soy flour (52 % protein) or concentrate (70 %).
2. **Conditioning** ‚ÄĒ moisture adjusted to 20‚Äď40 %, pre-heated.
3. **Cooking extruder** ‚ÄĒ screwŤĹ¨ťÄü 200‚Äď500 rpm; barrel 140‚Äď180 ¬įC; pressure 20‚Äď40 bar. **High shear + heat** denatures protein ‚Üí unfolds polypeptide chains ‚Üí aligns along flow.
4. **Cooling die** ‚ÄĒ sudden pressure drop at die ‚Üí water flashes off ‚Üí **fibrous, anisotropic texture** set.
5. **Drying** ‚ÄĒ final moisture ~ 8 %; shelf-stable.
6. **Grading and packaging.**

**Key engineering principle:** The denatured proteins undergo **hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, and disulfide cross-links** that form a *continuous fibrous network* ‚ÄĒ meat-like chewiness.

**Applications (3 marks):**
1. **Meat extender** in sausages, burgers, kebabs (1:1 to 1:3 ratio with meat) ‚Üí cost reduction by 20‚Äď40 %.
2. **Vegetarian meat analogues** (chunks, strips, granules) for ready-to-eat meals.
3. **School mid-day meal programmes and space/military rations** ‚ÄĒ long shelf life, high protein.
4. **Pet food and aquaculture feed** ‚ÄĒ high palatability and digestibility.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-page revision:**
> - **Egg = reference (BV 94, PER 3.9, PDCAAS 1.00)**. *Always* the comparator.
> - **Milk = Casein (80 %, 4 types) + Whey (20 %, ő≤-LG major)**. $\kappa$-casein stabilises micelle; chymosin cleaves it.
> - **Meat = Myofibrillar (myosin/actin) + Sarcoplasmic (myoglobin) + Connective (collagen)**. Colour: Mb ‚Üí MbO‚āā ‚Üí MetMb (red ‚Üí cherry ‚Üí brown).
> - **Fish** = like meat but **softer collagen** (gelatinises at ~ 25 ¬įC), richer in lysine/methionine.
> - **Cereals** = deficient in **lysine** (and Trp in maize). **Gluten = Gliadin + Glutenin** (viscoelastic). **Osborne fractions**: albumins / globulins / prolamins / glutelins.
> - **Pulses** = deficient in methionine; *complement* cereals (rice-dal 1:1).
> - **TVP** = thermoplastic extrusion (140‚Äď180 ¬įC, 20‚Äď40 % moisture) of defatted soy ‚Üí fibrous chunks.
> - **SCP & Quorn** = microbial biomass for alternate protein.
> - **N‚ÜíP factors: milk 6.38, wheat 5.70, rice 5.95, maize 5.83, general 6.25** ‚ÄĒ *memorise the unusual ones*.
> - **BV vs NPU**: BV uses *absorbed*; NPU uses *intake* ‚Üí NPU = BV √ó Digestibility.
> - **PDCAAS capped at 1.00**; uses faecal + ileal digestibility.
> - **Anti-nutrients in raw egg**: avidin (binds biotin); ovomucoid (trypsin inhibitor) ‚ÄĒ both heat-labile except ovomucoid.
> - **Scombroidosis** ‚Üí histamine in dark-meat fish (tuna/mackerel); biogenic-amine poisoning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Enzymes - Food Sources, Functional Role and Uses in Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Food Enzymes - Food Sources, Functional Role and Uses in Foods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Enzymes** are **biocatalysts** ‚ÄĒ predominantly high-molecular-weight **globular proteins** (with a few catalytic RNA molecules called *ribozymes*) ‚ÄĒ that accelerate specific biochemical reactions in food systems without being consumed in the overall reaction. In the KTU 2024 Food Chemistry framework, **food enzymes** are defined as those enzymes that are either *endogenous* (naturally present in raw food materials), *exogenous* (added intentionally as processing aids), or *microbial* (produced by starter cultures and fermenting microorganisms). An enzyme lowers the **activation energy (Ea)** of a reaction by stabilizing the **transition state**, thereby increasing the reaction rate by factors of $10^{6}$ to $10^{12}$ compared to uncatalyzed reactions. The systematic suffix **-ase** (proposed by Duclaux in 1878) and the modern **EC (Enzyme Commission) numbering system** (introduced by the International Union of Biochemistry, 1961) classify enzymes into six major classes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course PCFTT205 explicitly categorizes food enzymes based on **(i) their source**, **(ii) their functional role in food processing**, and **(iii) their industrial applications**. This three-pronged classification is the foundation for all KTU exam questions from this module. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Molecular Lock-and-Key" Intuition Imagine a busy industrial kitchen where thousands of chemical transformations occur every second ‚ÄĒ proteins being broken down, starches being converted to sugars, fats being split into glycerol and fatty acids. Without enzymes, these reactions would proceed so slowly that food would spoil long before useful changes could occur. Think of an enzyme as a **highly specialized molecular worker holding a custom-shaped wrench**: - The **enzyme** is the worker ūüßĎ‚ÄćūüĒß - The **substrate** is the nut/bolt that needs turning ūüĒ© - The **active site** is the perfectly sized socket on the wrench that fits *only* that specific bolt - The **enzyme-substrate (ES) complex** is the moment the wrench engages the bolt - The **product** is the loosened bolt, and the **enzyme** walks away unchanged, ready to grip the next one This is the famous **"Lock-and-Key" model** (Fischer, 1894). A more refined version ‚ÄĒ the **"Induced-Fit" model** (Koshland, 1958) ‚ÄĒ suggests the active site actually *moulds* itself around the substrate, like a glove fitting a hand. > [!TIP] > **Real-world food example:** When you chew a piece of bread, the salivary enzyme **amylase** begins breaking down starch into maltose. The bread tastes slightly sweet after a minute ‚ÄĒ that sweetness is the *product* of enzymatic catalysis happening inside your mouth! ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Metric | Symbol / Value | Significance in Food Enzymology | |---|---|---| | Turnover Number | $k_{cat}$ | Molecules of substrate converted per enzyme molecule per second | | Standard Activation Energy Reduction | $\Delta E_{a} \approx 40\text{‚Äď}80\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | Energy barrier reduction by enzymatic catalysis | | Optimal pH range | **3.0 ‚Äď 9.0** | Most food enzymes operate in this window | | Optimal temperature | **30 ¬įC ‚Äď 70 ¬įC** | Activity peak; denaturation occurs above 80 ¬įC for most enzymes | | Q10 (temperature coefficient) | **2 ‚Äď 3** | Reaction rate doubles/triples per 10 ¬įC rise (within biological range) | | Michaelis Constant | $K_{m}$ (mol/L or M) | Substrate concentration at half-maximum velocity | | Standard pressure (for dissolved O‚āā enzymes) | $P^{\ominus} = 1\ \text{bar} = 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$ | Reference state for oxidoreductase kinetics | | Gas constant | $R = 8.314\ \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ | Used in Arrhenius equation for enzyme thermal behaviour | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Food Enzymes (KTU-Mandated Framework) #### A. Classification by **Source** (Biological Origin) 1. **Plant-derived enzymes (Phyt Enzymes)** - **Papain** ‚ÄĒ from *Carica papaya* latex (protease) - **Bromelain** ‚ÄĒ from *Ananas comosus* (pineapple) stem and fruit (protease) - **Ficin** ‚ÄĒ from *Ficus carica* (fig) latex (protease) - **Malt diastase (Amylase)** ‚ÄĒ from germinating barley - **Lipoxygenase** ‚ÄĒ from soybeans - **Polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** ‚ÄĒ from apples, potatoes, mushrooms 2. **Animal-derived enzymes (Zoological Enzymes)** - **Rennet (Chymosin)** ‚ÄĒ from calf stomach lining (milk coagulation) - **Trypsin & Chymotrypsin** ‚ÄĒ from bovine/porcine pancreas (proteases) - **Pepsin** ‚ÄĒ from pig stomach - **Lysozyme** ‚ÄĒ from hen egg-white (antibacterial) - **Catalase** ‚ÄĒ from bovine liver (decomposes H‚āāO‚āā) - **Lactoperoxidase** ‚ÄĒ from milk (antimicrobial system) 3. **Microbial-derived enzymes (Most Industrially Important)** - **Bacterial:** Subtilisin (Bacillus), Amylases (Bacillus subtilis), Lactase, Glucose isomerase - **Fungal:** Aspergillus oryzae (koji mould) ‚Üí amylase, protease, lipase - **Yeast:** Invertase (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) - **Mold:** Penicillium, Rhizopus ‚Üí pectinases, lipases - *Advantage:* Cheap, scalable, GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why microbial enzymes dominate industry:** They are produced by **fermentation technology** in large bioreactors, are often **more thermostable** than plant/animal counterparts, can be **genetically engineered** for specific pH/temperature optima, and avoid **BSE/Mad Cow concerns** associated with animal rennet (hence the rise of *fermentation-produced chymosin* ‚ÄĒ FPC). #### B. Classification by **Functional Role in Foods** (EC Numbering) | EC Class | Reaction Type | Food Examples | Specific Food Enzyme | |---|---|---|---| | EC 1 ‚ÄĒ **Oxidoreductases** | Redox (electron transfer) | Browning, fermentation | Polyphenol oxidase, Catalase, Peroxidase, Lipoxygenase | | EC 2 ‚ÄĒ **Transferases** | Group transfer | Sugar interconversions | Transglutaminase (TG), Hexokinase | | EC 3 ‚ÄĒ **Hydrolases** | Hydrolysis (add H/OH) | Digestion, tenderization, milk clotting | Amylase, Protease, Lipase, Pectinase, Lactase, Chymosin | | EC 4 ‚ÄĒ **Lyases** | Non-hydrolytic cleavage | Cheese ripening, juice clarification | Pectate lyase, Aldolase, Malate decarboxylase | | EC 5 ‚ÄĒ **Isomerases** | Structural rearrangement | HFCS production, lactose hydrolysis | Glucose isomerase, Xylose isomerase | | EC 6 ‚ÄĒ **Ligases** | Bond formation (ATP-dependent) | Rare in food; biosynthesis | Ubiquitin ligase (limited food relevance) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** The first three classes (EC 1, 2, 3) account for **~95% of all food-industry enzyme applications**. Hydrolases alone represent the largest share of the global food enzyme market. #### C. Classification by **Application in Food Industry** - **Starch-processing enzymes:** Amylases (liquefaction), Glucoamylases (saccharification), Pullulanase (debranching) - **Dairy enzymes:** Chymosin, Lactase, Lipases (cheese flavour) - **Fruit-juice enzymes:** Pectinases (clarification) - **Baking enzymes:** Amylase (bread softness), Protease (gluten modification), Lipase (dough stability), Xylanase - **Meat-tenderizing enzymes:** Papain, Bromelain, Ficin - **Brewing enzymes:** ő≤-amylase, Protease, Glucanase - **Protein hydrolysate production:** Alcalase, Flavourzyme ### 2.2 Why and How: Functional Role of Enzymes in Foods - **Texture modification:** Proteases break down myofibrillar proteins ‚Üí meat tenderization - **Flavour development:** Lipases release short-chain fatty acids ‚Üí cheese aroma - **Colour changes:** PPO catalyses browning; conversely, controlled PPO inactivation prevents enzymatic browning in processed fruits - **Preservation/extension of shelf-life:** Lysozyme inhibits Gram-positive bacteria; lactoperoxidase system kills pathogens - **Nutritional improvement:** Phytase liberates bound phosphorus from phytic acid in cereals - **Detoxification:** Nitroreductase, glucose oxidase removes residual O‚āā in beverages > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the six EC classes (for KTU exams):** **"Old T ransgressors H ate L unchtime I nspection"** ‚Üí **O**xidoreductases, **T**ransferases, **H**ydrolases, **L**yases, **I**somerases, **L**igases. The last class (Ligases) is rarely tested in food chemistry. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Warning:** When writing equations in KTU answer sheets, always state **units** explicitly.
