## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 ‚ÄĒ Module 4 Choice 1]`

**Question:** (a) Classify lipids in detail with a suitable classification chart, giving at least two examples of each class. (7 marks)

(b) What are Essential Fatty Acids? Discuss ŌČ-3 and ŌČ-6 fatty acids in detail with examples and their food sources. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part A(a) (7 Marks)

**Classification of Lipids (Bloor's Classification, 1943):**

Lipids are classified into **three major classes** based on their chemical structure:

**(1) Simple Lipids (3 marks):** Esters of fatty acids with various alcohols.

- **a) Acylglycerols (Glycerides):** Esters of glycerol with fatty acids.
  - *Monoacylglycerol (MAG):* 1 FA + glycerol (emulsifier in bread)
  - *Diacylglycerol (DAG):* 2 FAs + glycerol (DAG cooking oils)
  - *Triacylglycerol (TAG):* 3 FAs + glycerol (e.g., tripalmitin in palm oil)
- **b) Waxes:** Esters of FA with long-chain monohydric alcohols (e.g., cetyl palmitate, beeswax).

**(2) Compound (Conjugate) Lipids (2 marks):** Simple lipids + additional groups.

- **a) Phospholipids:** Glycerol + FAs + phosphate + alcohol (e.g., lecithin, cephalin)
- **b) Glycolipids (Sphingolipids/Cerebrosides):** Contain a carbohydrate moiety (e.g., brain cerebrosides)
- **c) Lipoproteins:** Lipid-protein complexes (e.g., LDL, HDL)

**(3) Derived Lipids (2 marks):** Products of hydrolysis of simple/compound lipids, or lipids not containing fatty acids.

- **a) Fatty Acids:** Saturated (palmitic, stearic), Unsaturated (oleic, linoleic)
- **b) Sterols:** Cholesterol, sitosterol, ergosterol
- **c) Fat-soluble vitamins:** Vitamins A, D, E, K

> [!TIP]
> Examiner tip: Always draw a **tree/flowchart** for classification to gain 1-2 extra marks. **Bloor's classification** should be explicitly named.

---

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part A(b) (7 Marks)

**Essential Fatty Acids (EFA):**

Essential Fatty Acids are fatty acids that **cannot be synthesized by the human body de novo** (specifically, humans lack **őĒ-12 and őĒ-15 desaturase enzymes** required to introduce double bonds at positions beyond C-9) and must therefore be obtained from the **diet**.

There are **two families** of EFAs, classified by the position of the **first double bond from the methyl (CH‚āÉ) end**:

**(1) ŌČ-3 (Omega-3) Family (3.5 marks):**

- The first C=C double bond is at the **3rd carbon from the methyl end**.
- **Parent FA:** őĪ-Linolenic acid (ALA) ‚ÄĒ **C18:3 (n-3)**, all-cis-9,12,15-octadecatrienoic acid.
- **Derived longer-chain ŌČ-3 FAs:**
  - **EPA (Eicosapentaenoic acid):** C20:5 (n-3) ‚ÄĒ 5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid
  - **DHA (Docosahexaenoic acid):** C22:6 (n-3) ‚ÄĒ 4,7,10,13,16,19-docosahexaenoic acid
- **Food sources:** Flaxseed (linseed) oil, walnut, chia seeds, **fatty fish** (salmon, mackerel, sardines), fish oil capsules.
- **Health benefits:** Anti-inflammatory, cardio-protective, brain/retinal development (DHA critical for infant brain).

**(2) ŌČ-6 (Omega-6) Family (3.5 marks):**

- The first C=C double bond is at the **6th carbon from the methyl end**.
- **Parent FA:** Linoleic acid (LA) ‚ÄĒ **C18:2 (n-6)**, cis,cis-9,12-octadecadienoic acid.
- **Derived longer-chain ŌČ-6 FAs:**
  - **ő≥-Linolenic acid (GLA):** C18:3 (n-6)
  - **Arachidonic acid (AA):** C20:4 (n-6) ‚ÄĒ 5,8,11,14-eicosatetraenoic acid
- **Food sources:** Sunflower oil, safflower oil, corn oil, soybean oil, **evening primrose oil** (GLA), egg yolk, liver (AA).
- **Health benefits:** Pro-inflammatory eicosanoids, skin integrity, growth, reproduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended ratio (ŌČ-6 : ŌČ-3) in the diet:** Approximately **4:1 to 10:1**. Modern Western diets often have a ratio of **20:1**, contributing to chronic inflammatory diseases.

---

### Question B (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 ‚ÄĒ Module 4 Choice 2]`

**Question:** (a) What is Saponification Value? How is it determined experimentally? Mention its significance in food chemistry. (7 marks)

(b) Discuss the structure and functions of phospholipids and sterols in food systems. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part B(a) (7 Marks)

**Saponification Value (SV):**

The saponification value is defined as the **number of milligrams of potassium hydroxide (KOH) required to completely saponify 1 gram of fat or oil**. It is a measure of the **average molecular weight of the fatty acids** present in the lipid.

**Chemical reaction:**

$$\text{Triacylglycerol} + 3\,\text{KOH} \longrightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{Potassium salts of FA (soaps)}$$

**Experimental determination (4 marks):**

1. **Weigh** accurately about **1‚Äď2 g** of the oil/fat into a **250 mL conical flask**.
2. Add **25 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH** solution.
3. Attach a **reflux condenser** and boil gently on a **water bath for 30‚Äď60 minutes** until saponification is complete (the solution becomes clear and homogeneous).
4. Add **2‚Äď3 drops of phenolphthalein indicator** (the solution turns pink).
5. **Titrate the excess (unreacted) KOH** against **0.5 N standard HCl** until the pink color disappears.
6. Run a **blank titration** (without oil) under identical conditions.
7. **Calculate:**

$$\text{SV} = \dfrac{56.1 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N}{W}$$

Where:
- $V_b$ = volume of HCl for blank (mL)
- $V_s$ = volume of HCl for sample (mL)
- $N$ = normality of HCl
- $W$ = weight of oil/fat (g)
- 56.1 = molecular weight of KOH

**Significance in food chemistry (3 marks):**

- **Average chain length:** Higher SV indicates **shorter-chain FAs** (coconut oil SV ~260, butter ~230, olive oil ~190, linseed oil ~190).
- **Detection of adulteration:** Used to detect paraffin oil or mineral oil adulteration in edible fats (paraffin does not saponify ‚Üí very low SV).
- **Quality control:** Coconut oil is distinguished from palm oil using SV.
- **Mean molecular weight of fatty acids** can be calculated: $\overline{M}_{FA} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$

---

#### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Part B(b) (7 Marks)

**Phospholipids ‚ÄĒ Structure and Functions (3.5 marks):**

Phospholipids are **amphipathic** lipids containing a **glycerol backbone**, **two fatty acid chains** (at sn-1 and sn-2 positions), a **phosphate group** (at sn-3), and a **polar head group** (choline, ethanolamine, serine, or inositol).

**Structure of phosphatidylcholine (lecithin):**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{CH}_2\text{-O-CO-R}_1 \\
&\vert \\
&\text{CH-O-CO-R}_2 \\
&\vert \\
&\text{CH}_2\text{-O-PO}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-N}^+\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_3
\end{aligned}$$

**Functions:**
- **Emulsification** in food (e.g., egg yolk lecithin in mayonnaise, soy lecithin in chocolates).
- **Biological membrane structure** in all living cells.
- **Lipid transport** in blood (as part of lipoproteins).
- **Surfactant activity** ‚ÄĒ the hydrophilic head faces water while the hydrophobic tail faces oil.

**Sterols ‚ÄĒ Structure and Functions (3.5 marks):**

Sterols are derivatives of a **cyclopentanoperhydrophenanthrene ring system** (3 fused cyclohexane rings A, B, C + 1 cyclopentane ring D) with an aliphatic side chain and a **hydroxyl (-OH) group at C-3**.

**Cholesterol structure (C‚āā‚āáH‚āĄ‚āÜO):**
- Four fused rings (A, B, C, D) with a 3ő≤-hydroxyl group and an 8-carbon iso-octyl side chain at C-17.
- The -OH makes it slightly **amphipathic**.

**Functions in food:**
- **Membrane fluidity regulator** in animal cells.
- **Precursor** of vitamin D, bile acids, and steroid hormones.
- **Emulsifier** in butter, egg yolk, and processed foods.
- In plants, **phytosterols** (ő≤-sitosterol, stigmasterol) have similar structures and reduce cholesterol absorption in the human gut.
- **Plasma cholesterol:** Normal < 200 mg/dL; LDL ("bad") and HDL ("good") balance determines cardiovascular risk.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "lipid" with "fat":** Lipid is the broader class; fat is a specific type (TAG) of lipid. Writing "fats and oils" as synonymous with "lipids" loses 1-2 marks. Always start: *"Lipids are a broader class that includes fats, oils, waxes, phospholipids, and sterols."*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the solubility criterion in the definition:** The single line *"soluble in non-polar solvents, insoluble in water"* carries 1 full mark. Many students write only the energy value.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Delta (őĒ) and Omega (ŌČ) nomenclature:** Delta counts from the **carboxyl (-COOH) end**; Omega counts from the **methyl (CH‚āÉ) end**. Writing these in reverse is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Not writing the chemical equation for TAG formation:** Always include the **condensation reaction with 3 H‚āāO released** for full marks in structure-based questions.
>
> 5. **Omitting the constants 56.1 (KOH MW) and 9 kcal/g (energy):** These are **must-remember numerical values** in every saponification and energy-based question.
>
> 6. **Writing "phospholipids are soluble in water":** They are **amphipathic**, not water-soluble. The head is hydrophilic, but the molecule as a whole forms emulsions/micelles rather than true solutions.
>
> 7. **In the Saponification Value experiment:** Students forget the **blank titration** is mandatory. Without it, the calculation is incomplete and 1-2 marks are deducted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam ‚ÄĒ Lipids Module 4:**

### ūüďĆ Definition
- Lipids = organic compounds **soluble in non-polar solvents**, **insoluble in water** (Bloor, 1943).
- Energy density = **9 kcal/g = 37 kJ/g** (highest among macronutrients).
- Building blocks: **Fatty acids** + **Isoprene units**.

### ūüďĆ Structure
- **Fatty acid formula:** $\text{CH}_3\text{-(CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{-COOH}$, n = 2‚Äď24.
- **Most natural FAs have even-numbered C** (acetyl-CoA biosynthesis).
- **Saturated FA:** no C=C (e.g., C16:0 palmitic, C18:0 stearic).
- **Unsaturated FA:** 1+ C=C (e.g., C18:1 oleic, C18:2 linoleic).
- **TAG:** glycerol + 3 FAs + 3 H‚āāO released (esterification).
- **Phospholipid:** glycerol + 2 FAs + phosphate + alcohol head group.
- **Sterol:** 4 fused rings (A, B, C, D) + -OH at C-3.

### ūüďĆ Classification (Bloor, 1943)
1. **Simple Lipids:** Acylglycerols (TAG, DAG, MAG), Waxes.
2. **Compound Lipids:** Phospholipids, Glycolipids, Lipoproteins.
3. **Derived Lipids:** Fatty Acids, Sterols, Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

### ūüďĆ Nomenclature
- **Shorthand:** Cx:y (x = carbons, y = double bonds). E.g., C18:2 = linoleic.
- **Delta (őĒ) system:** Position of C=C from -COOH end.
- **Omega (ŌČ) system:** Position of first C=C from -CH‚āÉ end.
- **ŌČ-3 (n-3):** ALA, EPA, DHA ‚Üí flaxseed, fatty fish.
- **ŌČ-6 (n-6):** Linoleic, arachidonic ‚Üí sunflower, soybean oil.

### ūüďĆ Essential Fatty Acids
- Cannot be synthesized by humans (no őĒ-12, őĒ-15 desaturases).
- Two families: **ŌČ-3** (őĪ-linolenic) and **ŌČ-6** (linoleic).
- Recommended dietary ratio: **ŌČ-6 : ŌČ-3 ‚Čą 4:1 to 10:1**.

### ūüďĆ Cis vs Trans
- **Cis:** Hydrogens on same side ‚Üí bent chain ‚Üí lower m.p. (natural).
- **Trans:** Hydrogens on opposite sides ‚Üí straight chain ‚Üí higher m.p. (industrial).

### ūüďĆ Key Formulas (Constants to Memorize)
- **IV** = (g I‚āā absorbed / g lipid) √ó 100.
- **SV** = (3 √ó 56.1 √ó 1000) / $M_{TAG}$.
- **$\overline{M}_{FA}$** = (3 √ó 56.1 √ó 1000) / SV.
- **Acid Value** = (V √ó N √ó 56) / W.
- KOH MW = **56.1**; Energy of lipid = **9 kcal/g**.

> ‚úÖ **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Solubility in non-polar solvents, 9 kcal per gram, glycerol + fatty acids, Bloor 1943, omega-3 and omega-6 essential, even-chain fatty acids, ester bonds release 3 waters."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Fats and Oils - Crystal Formation, Polymorphism, Melting Points, Plasticity and Isomerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fats and Oils - Crystal Formation, Polymorphism, Melting Points, Plasticity and Isomerisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fats and Oils ‚ÄĒ The Foundation

**Fats and oils** are triacylglycerols (triglycerides) ‚ÄĒ esters formed by the condensation of one molecule of glycerol (a trihydric alcohol) with three molecules of fatty acids. The physical state distinguishes them: **fats are solid at room temperature (~20 ¬įC)** while **oils are liquid under the same conditions**. The distinction is governed by the degree of unsaturation and the chain length of the constituent fatty acids.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** Properties of Fats and Oils ‚ÄĒ Crystal Formation, Polymorphism, Melting Points, Plasticity and Isomerisation. These are *physical* and *structural* properties, distinct from the chemical properties covered in Module 3.

### 1.2 Crystal Formation ‚ÄĒ Definition

**Crystal formation** (also termed *nucleation* and *crystal growth*) in fats is the process by which disordered liquid triacylglycerol molecules arrange themselves into an ordered, repeating three-dimensional lattice when the temperature drops below their melting point. This transition from a disordered liquid to an ordered crystalline solid is termed **lipid crystallisation** and is the foundational physical event that governs texture, appearance, mouthfeel, and functional performance of fat-rich foods (chocolate, butter, margarine, shortening, ice cream).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a crowded bus stop at peak hour. Initially, people (fatty acid chains) mill about in random directions (liquid oil). When the temperature drops, it's like a traffic policeman stepping in ‚ÄĒ the molecules start aligning shoulder-to-shoulder into neat parallel rows (crystal lattice). Once the first row forms, it acts as a template, and the next row stacks neatly behind it, just like how the first domino in a chain makes the entire line fall in a precise pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystal formation in fats is NEVER 100% complete.** Commercial fats always retain a fraction of liquid oil trapped within or between crystal networks. This co-existence of solid and liquid phases is termed the **solid fat content (SFC)** and is the single most important quality parameter measured by pulsed-NMR in industry.

### 1.3 Polymorphism ‚ÄĒ Definition

**Polymorphism** (Greek: *poly* = many, *morphe* = form) is the ability of a single chemical compound to crystallise into **more than one distinct crystal structure**, each possessing a different three-dimensional arrangement of molecules, a different melting point, and a different stability. In triacylglycerols, three principal polymorphic forms exist, named after their characteristic X-ray short spacings:

| Polymorph | X-ray Short Spacing (nm) | Stability |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **őĪ (alpha)** | ~0.415 | Least stable (metastable) |
| **ő≤‚Ä≤ (beta-prime)** | ~0.420 and ~0.380 | Intermediate stability |
| **ő≤ (beta)** | ~0.460 | Most stable |

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you have a set of identical toy bricks. You can build a wobbly house (őĪ ‚ÄĒ unstable, falls apart easily), a sturdy apartment block (ő≤‚Ä≤ ‚ÄĒ moderately strong), or a skyscraper with a steel frame (ő≤ ‚ÄĒ strongest, most rigid). The bricks are the same, but the *way you stack them* changes the structure's strength, melting point, and texture.

### 1.4 Melting Point ‚ÄĒ Definition

The **melting point** of a fat is the temperature at which the crystalline solid transitions to a clear liquid. Because natural fats are **mixtures of multiple triacylglycerols**, they do not exhibit a sharp melting point like a pure compound; instead, they display a **melting range** spanning several degrees Celsius. The melting point is governed by:
- **Chain length** of the fatty acids (longer chain = higher MP)
- **Degree of unsaturation** (more double bonds = lower MP)
- **Saturation state** (saturated > monounsaturated > polyunsaturated MP)
- **Polymorphic form** (őĪ < ő≤‚Ä≤ < ő≤ melting point)

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Pure water melts sharply at **0 ¬įC** (273.15 K), but butter melts gradually between ~32 ¬įC and ~35 ¬įC. This is because butter is a *mixture* of many different triglycerides, each with its own melting temperature ‚ÄĒ like a relay team where runners cross the finish line one by one, not all at once.

### 1.5 Plasticity ‚ÄĒ Definition

**Plasticity** is the property of a fat that allows it to be deformed permanently under mechanical stress (kneading, spreading, moulding) **without cracking, breaking, or losing its cohesive structure**. A plastic fat behaves as a **two-phase system**: a continuous three-dimensional crystal network (the "sponge") holding liquid oil within its pores (the "water in the sponge"). This crystal network must be strong enough to hold shape, yet weak enough to yield under tongue pressure during eating.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a wet sponge ‚ÄĒ you can squeeze it, fold it, deform it, and it holds together. The sponge skeleton is the solid fat crystals, and the water held inside is the liquid oil. A *brittle* fat (like fully hardened coconut oil at low temperature) is like a dry cracker ‚ÄĒ it shatters under stress. A *plastic* fat (like good margarine) is like modelling clay ‚ÄĒ it yields smoothly.

### 1.6 Isomerisation ‚ÄĒ Definition

**Isomerisation** in fats and oils refers to the rearrangement of atoms within fatty acid molecules to form isomers ‚ÄĒ compounds with the *same molecular formula* but *different structural arrangements*. The two principal types relevant to KTU Module 4 are:

1. **Geometric (cis-trans) isomerisation** ‚ÄĒ conversion of *cis* double bonds to *trans* double bonds, typically via partial hydrogenation.
2. **Positional isomerisation** ‚ÄĒ migration of the double bond along the carbon chain during partial hydrogenation.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Consider a paperclip bent into a "U" shape (cis ‚ÄĒ same side) versus an "S" shape (trans ‚ÄĒ opposite sides). They are made of the *same wire*, but the kink is in a different place. The U-shape is the natural form in vegetable oils (cis), and the S-shape is what is created in artificial trans fats via industrial hydrogenation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** Isomerisation of unsaturated fatty acids from *cis* to *trans* configuration is a high-priority topic because *trans* fats are directly linked to cardiovascular disease (raises LDL, lowers HDL). WHO recommends that *trans* fat intake be limited to **less than 1% of total daily energy intake** (‚Čą 2.2 g/day on a 2000 kcal diet).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crystal Formation ‚ÄĒ Mechanism in Three Stages

Crystallisation of triacylglycerols proceeds in **three kinetically and thermodynamically distinct stages**:

**Stage 1 ‚ÄĒ Supercooling (Undercooling):**
The liquid fat must be cooled *below* its thermodynamic equilibrium melting point ($T_m$) to a crystallisation temperature ($T_c$). The difference $\Delta T = T_m - T_c$ is the **degree of supercooling**. The system is now in a **metastable** state ‚ÄĒ thermodynamically unstable but kinetically inhibited from crystallising because the molecules lack the energy/alignment to overcome the nucleation energy barrier.

**Stage 2 ‚ÄĒ Nucleation:**
Molecules overcome the free-energy barrier ($\Delta G^*$) and form a critical-sized cluster (nucleus) ‚ÄĒ the smallest thermodynamically stable assembly. Nucleation is of two types:
- **Primary (homogeneous) nucleation** ‚ÄĒ rare, occurs in highly purified systems.
- **Secondary (heterogeneous) nucleation** ‚ÄĒ dominant in foods; induced by foreign surfaces (dust, container walls, seed crystals, emulsifier micelles).

**Stage 3 ‚ÄĒ Crystal Growth:**
Once nuclei form, additional molecules attach to the crystal faces, propagating the lattice. Growth proceeds at a rate determined by **mass transfer** of molecules to the crystal surface and **heat transfer** (removal of latent heat of crystallisation, $\Delta H_{cryst}$).

**Why & How ‚ÄĒ The Thermodynamic Driver:**
Crystallisation is exothermic; it releases the **latent heat of fusion** ($\Delta H_f$) because molecules lose kinetic energy and settle into lower-energy lattice positions. The driving force is the **Gibbs free energy change**:

$$\Delta G_{cryst} = \Delta H_{cryst} - T \cdot \Delta S_{cryst}$$

For $T < T_m$, $\Delta G_{cryst} < 0$, making crystallisation **spontaneous**. The critical radius $r^*$ of a stable nucleus is given by the **Gibbs-Thomson equation**:

$$r^* = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma_{sl} \cdot V_m}{\Delta H_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_m}{T}\right)}$$

where:
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid-liquid interfacial tension (**J/m¬≤**)
- $V_m$ = molar volume of the crystallising species (**m¬≥/mol**)
- $\Delta H_f$ = enthalpy of fusion (**J/mol**)
- $T_m, T$ = melting temperature and crystallisation temperature respectively (**K**)

### 2.2 Polymorphism ‚ÄĒ Detailed Analysis

Triacylglycerols exhibit **monotropic polymorphism** ‚ÄĒ only the least stable form (őĪ) crystallises first upon rapid cooling; the other forms are obtained only via solid-state transitions. The forms transform as:

$$\alpha \xrightarrow{\text{irreversible}} \beta' \xrightarrow{\text{irreversible}} \beta$$

**Key Structural Features:**

- **őĪ-form:** Hexagonal subcell packing; chains perpendicular to lamellar plane; rapid crystallisation; least stable; lowest melting point.
- **ő≤‚Ä≤-form:** Orthorhombic subcell packing; chains tilted; intermediate stability; provides **fine, needle-like crystals** ideal for margarine and shortening texture.
- **ő≤-form:** Triclinic subcell packing; chains fully extended; most stable; produces **large, coarse, grainy crystals** ‚ÄĒ undesirable in margarine (sandiness) but desirable in chocolate (snap and gloss).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Selection Criterion:** The ő≤‚Ä≤-form is preferred in margarine, shortening, and butter because its small needle-shaped crystals entrap a large amount of liquid oil, yielding a smooth, plastic texture. Cocoa butter is unique in that it transitions through **six** polymorphic forms (Forms I‚ÄďVI), with Form V (ő≤‚āā) being the glossy, snap-producing form required for premium chocolate.

### 2.3 Melting Points ‚ÄĒ Quantitative Analysis

For a **pure triacylglycerol** with saturated fatty acids of identical chain length $n$ carbons, the melting point follows an empirical relationship:

$$T_m(n) = T_{m,\infty} - \frac{k}{n}$$

where $T_{m,\infty}$ is the asymptotic melting point for an infinitely long chain (‚Čą **102 ¬įC** for triacylglycerols), $k$ is a constant, and $n$ is the carbon number.

**For mixed saturated/unsaturated triacylglycerols**, the **melting point depression** due to unsaturation is given approximately by:

$$\Delta T_{unsat} = 2.5 \times N_{C=C} \text{ [¬įC]}$$

where $N_{C=C}$ is the number of *cis* double bonds per molecule.

**For polymorphism**, the melting point hierarchy is:
$$T_m(\alpha) < T_m(\beta') < T_m(\beta)$$

with typical separations of 2‚Äď5 ¬įC between consecutive forms.

### 2.4 Plasticity ‚ÄĒ Quantitative Analysis

The **plastic range** of a fat is the temperature interval over which it remains plastic (workable). It is bounded below by the **solid fat content (SFC) at lower limit** (~15% SFC ‚ÄĒ fat becomes too hard) and above by the **upper limit SFC** (~5% SFC ‚ÄĒ fat loses structure and becomes oily).

The **plasticity index** (Pi) is defined as:

$$P_i = T_{upper} - T_{lower} \; [^\circ \text{C}]$$

The **yield value** (stress required to initiate flow) of a plastic fat follows the **Bingham plastic model**:

$$\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (**Pa**)
- $\tau_y$ = yield stress (**Pa**)
- $\eta_p$ = plastic viscosity (**Pa¬∑s**)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (**s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**)

### 2.5 Isomerisation ‚ÄĒ Reaction Kinetics

**Cis-Trans Isomerisation** during partial hydrogenation follows first-order kinetics with respect to the cis-isomer:

$$\frac{d[C_{cis}]}{dt} = -k_{iso} \cdot [C_{cis}]$$

Integrated form:

$$[C_{cis}]_t = [C_{cis}]_0 \cdot e^{-k_{iso} \cdot t}$$

The trans-isomer formation rate is:
$$\frac{d[C_{trans}]}{dt} = k_{iso} \cdot [C_{cis}]_0 \cdot e^{-k_{iso} \cdot t}$$

The **isomerisation selectivity ratio** ($S_i$) compares the rate of cis-trans conversion to the rate of saturation:

$$S_i = \frac{\text{Rate of geometric isomerisation}}{\text{Rate of hydrogenation (saturation)}}$$

**Positional isomerisation** involves double bond migration along the chain. For oleic acid (9c-18:1), positional isomers like 8t-, 10t-, and 11t-18:1 are commonly produced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Partial hydrogenation is the *only* commercial process that produces significant *trans* isomers. Fully hydrogenated fats (zero trans) and interesterified fats (no trans, no saturation shift) are the modern industrial alternatives to meet WHO 2023 *trans*-fat elimination targets.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Property / Equation** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Application** |
|------|------------------------|-------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Gibbs free energy of crystallisation | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | $\Delta G$ (J/mol), $T$ (K) | Spontaneity of crystal formation |
| 2 | Critical nucleus radius | $r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{sl}V_m}{\Delta H_f \ln(T_m/T)}$ | $r^*$ (m), $\gamma_{sl}$ (J/m¬≤) | Nucleation thermodynamics |
| 3 | Melting point vs chain length | $T_m(n) = T_{m,\infty} - k/n$ | $T_m$ (¬įC or K), $n$ (carbon number) | Predicting MP of TAGs |
| 4 | Unsaturation melting depression | $\Delta T \approx 2.5 \times N_{C=C}$ | $\Delta T$ (¬įC), $N_{C=C}$ (count) | Effect of double bonds |
| 5 | First-order isomerisation | $C_t = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$ | $C$ (mol/L), $k$ (s‚ĀĽ¬Ļ), $t$ (s) | Trans-fat formation rate |
| 6 | Solid Fat Content (SFC) | $\text{SFC}(\%) = \frac{m_{solid}}{m_{solid}+m_{liquid}} \times 100$ | $m$ (kg or g) | Quality control by p-NMR |
| 7 | Bingham plastic flow | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ (Pa), $\tau_y$ (Pa), $\eta_p$ (Pa¬∑s) | Rheology of plastic fats |
| 8 | Plasticity index | $P_i = T_{upper} - T_{lower}$ | $P_i$ (¬įC) | Spreading range of margarines |
| 9 | Polymorphic MP hierarchy | $T_m(\alpha) < T_m(\beta') < T_m(\beta)$ | $T_m$ (¬įC) | Predicting crystal stability |
| 10 | SVI (Solid Volume Index) | $\text{SVI} = \int_{T_{min}}^{T_{max}} \text{SFC}(T)\, dT$ | SVI (¬įC¬∑% solid) | Overall firmness parameter |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

| **Domain** | **Property Applied** | **Industrial Use** |
|------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Chocolate manufacture | Polymorphism (Form V) | Tempering to prevent *fat bloom* (white surface coating) |
| Margarine / Shortening | ő≤‚Ä≤-polymorph | Smooth texture, sandiness prevention |
| Bakery fats | Plasticity range | Laminating dough layers (croissant, puff pastry) |
| Ice cream | Partial crystallisation | Destabilisation of emulsion, controlled meltdown |
| Health policy | Isomerisation control | *Trans*-fat regulation, mandatory labelling |
| Interesterification | MP modification | Production of *zero-trans* plastic fats |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Nucleus Radius (Gibbs-Thomson)

We derive the critical radius of a stable crystalline nucleus in a supercooled fat melt.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Free Energy Balance:**
The total Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G_{total}$ for forming a spherical nucleus of radius $r$ has two contributions:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{bulk} + \Delta G_{surface} = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \cdot \Delta G_v + 4\pi r^2 \cdot \gamma_{sl}$$

where:
- $\Delta G_v$ = bulk free-energy change per unit volume (**J/m¬≥**)
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid-liquid interfacial energy (**J/m¬≤**)
- The negative sign on the bulk term indicates a *favourable* (released) free energy upon crystallisation.
- The positive surface term represents the *unfavourable* energy cost of creating a new solid-liquid interface.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Maximisation of $\Delta G_{total}$:**
The critical radius is found by setting $\frac{d(\Delta G_{total})}{dr} = 0$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G_{total})}{dr} = -4\pi r^2 \cdot \Delta G_v + 8\pi r \cdot \gamma_{sl} = 0$$

Solving for $r$:

$$8\pi r \cdot \gamma_{sl} = 4\pi r^2 \cdot \Delta G_v$$

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{sl}}{\Delta G_v}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitution of $\Delta G_v$:**
For a crystallisation process, $\Delta G_v$ is related to the latent heat of fusion $\Delta H_f$ and the degree of supercooling by:

$$\Delta G_v = \frac{\Delta H_f \cdot \Delta T}{T_m} = \frac{\Delta H_f}{T_m} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_m}{T}\right)$$

(The logarithmic form arises from the integration of $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ assuming $\Delta S = \Delta H_f / T_m$.)

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Final Critical Radius Expression:**

$$r^* = \frac{2\gamma_{sl} \cdot V_m}{\Delta H_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_m}{T}\right)}$$

where the molar volume $V_m$ is introduced to convert from per-mole to per-volume basis. This is the **Gibbs-Thomson equation** used throughout fat crystallisation studies.

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Physical Interpretation:**
- As supercooling increases ($T$ decreases, $\ln(T_m/T)$ increases), $r^*$ **decreases** ‚Üí nucleation is easier.
- Higher interfacial tension $\gamma_{sl}$ ‚Üí larger $r^*$ ‚Üí harder to nucleate.
- Higher latent heat $\Delta H_f$ ‚Üí smaller $r^*$ ‚Üí easier to nucleate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Cooling fats *faster* and *deeper* below $T_m$ produces many small nuclei (fine texture). Cooling *slowly* produces fewer, larger crystals (coarse, sandy texture) ‚ÄĒ this is why chocolate tempering protocols precisely control cooling rates.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Polymorphic Transition Temperature (Monotropic Relationship)

For a monotropic system like triacylglycerols, the transition temperature $T_{tr}$ from polymorph I (less stable) to polymorph II (more stable) lies **below** the melting point of the more stable form.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Clapeyron Equation for Solid-Solid Transition:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{tr}}{T_{tr} \cdot \Delta V_{tr}}$$

where $\Delta H_{tr}$ is the transition enthalpy and $\Delta V_{tr}$ the molar volume change.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Approximation at Constant Pressure:**
For lipid polymorphic transitions, $\Delta V_{tr}$ is small (typically < 1%), so we approximate:

$$T_{tr} \approx \frac{\Delta H_{tr}}{\Delta S_{tr}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Thermodynamic Stability Criterion:**
The more stable polymorph has a **lower chemical potential** $\mu$ at any temperature $T$:

$$\mu_\beta(T) < \mu_{\beta'}(T) < \mu_\alpha(T)$$

The difference in chemical potential drives the irreversible solid-state transition:

$$\Delta \mu_{\alpha \to \beta} = \Delta H_{\alpha \to \beta} - T \cdot \Delta S_{\alpha \to \beta} < 0 \quad \forall \, T$$

This confirms that the **ő≤-form is unconditionally the most stable** at all temperatures below the melting point.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Isomerisation Half-Life

For first-order cis ‚Üí trans isomerisation:

$$-\frac{d[C_{cis}]}{dt} = k \cdot [C_{cis}]$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Separation of Variables:**

$$\frac{d[C_{cis}]}{[C_{cis}]} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Integration from $t = 0$ to $t$:**

$$\int_{[C_{cis}]_0}^{[C_{cis}]_t} \frac{d[C_{cis}]}{[C_{cis}]} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{[C_{cis}]_t}{[C_{cis}]_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Exponential Form:**

$$[C_{cis}]_t = [C_{cis}]_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Half-Life Definition** (when 50% has isomerised):

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln(2) = k \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Numerical Example:**
At typical partial hydrogenation conditions (200 ¬įC, Ni catalyst, 1 atm H‚āā), $k_{iso} \approx 5 \times 10^{-4} \; s^{-1}$ for linoleic acid. Therefore:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1386 \; s \approx 23 \; \text{minutes}$$

This means within ~23 minutes under industrial conditions, **half of the cis-linoleic acid is geometrically isomerised to its trans form** ‚ÄĒ a sobering estimate of why partial hydrogenation produces so much *trans* fat.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚ÄĒ The Three Polymorphs

| **Parameter** | **őĪ (Alpha)** | **ő≤‚Ä≤ (Beta-prime)** | **ő≤ (Beta)** |
|---------------|---------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Subcell packing | Hexagonal | Orthorhombic | Triclinic |
| Chain conformation | Disordered | Intermediate | Fully extended (all-*trans*) |
| X-ray short spacing (nm) | 0.415 | 0.420 and 0.380 | 0.460 |
| Long spacing (3√ó chain) | Yes | Yes | Yes (doubled) |
| Density (g/cm¬≥) | Lowest | Intermediate | Highest |
| Melting point | Lowest | Intermediate | Highest |
| Heat of fusion (kJ/mol) | Smallest | Intermediate | Largest |
| Crystal habit | Platelets, fine | Fine needles | Large coarse needles/platelets |
| Texture in margarine | Undesirable (grainy) | **Ideal ‚ÄĒ smooth** | Undesirable (sandy/gritty) |
| Texture in chocolate | Undesirable (soft) | Intermediate | **Ideal ‚ÄĒ Form V, glossy** |
| Stability at $T < T_m$ | Metastable, transforms quickly | Metastable, moderate lifetime | Stable, indefinite |
| Industrial preference | Rarely (transient) | **Margarines, shortenings** | Chocolate (cocoa butter) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example ‚ÄĒ Solid Fat Content vs Temperature

A commercial margarine has the following SFC profile measured by pulsed-NMR:

| Temperature (¬įC) | SFC (%) |
|------------------|---------|
| 5 | 58 |
| 10 | 45 |
| 20 | 28 |
| 25 | 18 |
| 30 | 9 |
| 35 | 3 |

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify Plastic Range:**
- Lower plastic limit (15% SFC) is reached at approximately **22 ¬įC** (interpolated between 20 ¬įC ‚Üí 28% and 25 ¬įC ‚Üí 18%).
- Upper plastic limit (5% SFC) is reached at approximately **33 ¬įC** (interpolated between 30 ¬įC ‚Üí 9% and 35 ¬įC ‚Üí 3%).

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute Plasticity Index:**

$$P_i = 33 - 22 = 11 \; ¬į\text{C}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute Solid Volume Index (SVI) by Trapezoidal Rule:**

$$\text{SVI} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \frac{(\text{SFC}_{i} + \text{SFC}_{i+1})}{2} \times (T_{i+1} - T_i)$$

$$\text{SVI} = \frac{58+45}{2}(10-5) + \frac{45+28}{2}(20-10) + \frac{28+18}{2}(25-20) + \frac{18+9}{2}(30-25) + \frac{9+3}{2}(35-30)$$

$$\text{SVI} = 257.5 + 365 + 115 + 67.5 + 30 = 835 \; ¬į\text{C} \cdot \%\text{ solid}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Interpretation:**
A plasticity index of **11 ¬įC** and SVI of **835 ¬įC¬∑%** indicate a **moderately plastic fat**, suitable for table margarine applications. A wider $P_i$ (e.g., 20‚Äď25 ¬įC) would indicate a fat suitable for tropical climates; a narrower $P_i$ (e.g., 5‚Äď8 ¬įC) would be ideal for bakery uses requiring narrow temperature tolerance.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model]**
**Define polymorphism in fats. List the three polymorphic forms of triacylglycerols in order of increasing stability and indicate the X-ray short spacing of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Polymorphism is the ability of a chemically pure substance to exist in more than one crystalline form, each having a different three-dimensional arrangement of molecules in the crystal lattice. Triacylglycerols exhibit three principal polymorphic forms, listed below in order of increasing stability:

| **Polymorph** | **Stability** | **X-ray Short Spacing (nm)** |
|---------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| őĪ (alpha) | Least stable (metastable) | 0.415 |
| ő≤‚Ä≤ (beta-prime) | Intermediate stability | 0.420 and 0.380 |
| ő≤ (beta) | Most stable | 0.460 |

The forms are **monotropic** ‚ÄĒ they transform irreversibly: $\alpha \to \beta' \to \beta$ upon heating or long-term storage. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is plasticity in the context of fats? Why is the ő≤‚Ä≤-polymorph preferred over the ő≤-polymorph in margarines and shortenings?**

**Model Answer:**

**Plasticity** is the ability of a fat to be deformed permanently under mechanical stress (spreading, kneading) without cracking or breaking. It arises from the two-phase structure: a continuous crystal network holding trapped liquid oil.

The **ő≤‚Ä≤-polymorph** is preferred in margarines and shortenings because:
1. It forms **fine, needle-shaped crystals** that create a smooth texture and entrap a large amount of liquid oil.
2. The ő≤-polymorph produces **large, coarse crystals** that cause **sandiness or grittiness**, a major quality defect.
3. ő≤‚Ä≤ provides a **wider plastic range** and stable aeration properties for baking applications. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of **crystal formation** in fats. Discuss **primary and secondary nucleation** with neat diagrams, and describe the role of supercooling in the crystallisation process. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the phenomenon of **polymorphism in triacylglycerols**. Compare the **őĪ, ő≤‚Ä≤ and ő≤** forms with respect to their subcell packing, X-ray short spacings, melting points, crystal habit, and industrial applications. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Definition and Three-Stage Mechanism:**
Crystal formation in fats is the process by which disordered liquid triacylglycerol molecules transition into an ordered three-dimensional crystalline lattice. It occurs in three sequential stages:

- **Stage 1 ‚ÄĒ Supercooling:** The melt is cooled to a temperature $T_c$ below the equilibrium melting point $T_m$. The difference $\Delta T = T_m - T_c$ is the degree of supercooling. The system is metastable.
- **Stage 2 ‚ÄĒ Nucleation:** Molecules overcome the free-energy barrier $\Delta G^*$ to form a critical-sized stable cluster (nucleus) of radius $r^* = 2\gamma_{sl}V_m / [\Delta H_f \ln(T_m/T)]$.
- **Stage 3 ‚ÄĒ Growth:** Molecules attach to the nucleus, releasing the latent heat of fusion $\Delta H_f$.

**2. Primary Nucleation:**

- **Homogeneous (primary):** Occurs spontaneously in the bulk liquid without foreign surfaces; requires a high degree of supercooling (10‚Äď15 ¬įC).
- **Heterogeneous (primary):** Occurs at foreign interfaces (dust, container walls, emulsifier micelles); requires a much lower supercooling (2‚Äď5 ¬įC); dominant in foods.

**3. Secondary Nucleation:**
Pre-existing crystals of the same species (e.g., seed crystals added in chocolate tempering) induce nucleation at very low supercooling, because the seed eliminates the need to form a new interface.

**4. Role of Supercooling:**
- Higher $\Delta T$ ‚Üí smaller $r^*$ ‚Üí more nuclei ‚Üí **finer crystal network** (smooth texture).
- Lower $\Delta T$ ‚Üí larger $r^*$ ‚Üí fewer nuclei ‚Üí **coarser crystals** (grainy texture).

**Diagram:** A schematic of the free-energy barrier vs. nucleus size (to be drawn in exam; it shows $\Delta G^*$ at $r^*$). **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**1. Definition:**
Polymorphism is the existence of a single chemical compound in multiple crystal forms. Triacylglycerols show **monotropic** polymorphism with three forms: $\alpha, \beta', \beta$.

**2. Comparative Table:**

| **Property** | **őĪ** | **ő≤‚Ä≤** | **ő≤** |
|--------------|-------|--------|-------|
| Subcell | Hexagonal | Orthorhombic | Triclinic |
| Short spacing (nm) | 0.415 | 0.420, 0.380 | 0.460 |
| Chain tilt | Perpendicular | Tilted | Extended |
| Melting point | Lowest | Intermediate | Highest |
| Crystal habit | Platelets | Fine needles | Coarse platelets |
| Stability | Least | Intermediate | Most |
| Industrial use | Rare | Margarine, shortening | Cocoa butter Form V |

**3. Transition Behaviour:**
On heating, $\alpha \to \beta' \to \beta$ irreversibly with $\Delta H_{tr}$ released. The ő≤-form has the **lowest chemical potential** at all temperatures below $T_m$ and is therefore the thermodynamically stable end-state.

**4. Industrial Relevance:**
- ő≤‚Ä≤ is preferred in margarine for smooth texture.
- ő≤ (Form V) is desired in tempered chocolate for snap and gloss.
- ő≤ in margarine is a defect (sandiness). **[7 marks]**

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper, 14 Marks ‚Äď ALTERNATIVE]**

**(a)** Define **melting point** of fats. Why do natural fats not exhibit a sharp melting point? Discuss the effect of **chain length, degree of unsaturation, and polymorphism** on the melting point. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Define **isomerisation** in the context of fats and oils. Explain **cis-trans (geometric) isomerisation** and **positional isomerisation**. Discuss the health implications of *trans* fatty acids and the modern industrial alternatives to partial hydrogenation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Definition:**
The melting point of a fat is the temperature at which the crystalline solid transitions to a clear liquid. For a pure compound, it is sharp; for natural fats, which are mixtures of many triacylglycerols, it is a **melting range** spanning several degrees Celsius.

**2. Why No Sharp Melting Point:**
Natural fats (e.g., butter, lard, soybean oil) contain 50‚Äď100+ different triacylglycerol molecular species, each with its own characteristic $T_m$. As temperature rises, the lowest-melting species melt first, followed progressively by higher-melting species, producing a smooth DSC melting curve with a **melting range** of 5‚Äď40 ¬įC.

**3. Effect of Chain Length:**
For saturated triacylglycerols of uniform chain length $n$:
$$T_m(n) = T_{m,\infty} - \frac{k}{n}$$
Longer chains ‚Üí higher $T_m$ (e.g., trilaurin MP ~ 46 ¬įC; tristearin MP ~ 72 ¬įC).

**4. Effect of Unsaturation:**
Each *cis* double bond depresses $T_m$ by approximately 2.5 ¬įC (due to chain-kinking disrupting crystal packing). Example: triolein (unsaturated) MP ~ 5 ¬įC vs. tristearin MP ~ 72 ¬įC.

**5. Effect of Polymorphism:**
$T_m(\alpha) < T_m(\beta') < T_m(\beta)$, with 2‚Äď5 ¬įC between consecutive forms. The ő≤-form is the most stable and has the highest MP. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**1. Definition:**
Isomerisation in fats is the rearrangement of atoms within fatty acid molecules to form isomers with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements. The two principal types are geometric (cis-trans) and positional isomerisation.

**2. Cis-Trans (Geometric) Isomerisation:**
During partial hydrogenation, the *cis* double bond of an unsaturated fatty acid (e.g., oleic acid, 9c-18:1) can be converted to a *trans* double bond (elaidic acid, 9t-18:1). The reaction follows first-order kinetics:
$$[C_{cis}]_t = [C_{cis}]_0 \cdot e^{-k_{iso} t}$$
The half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k_{iso}$.

**3. Positional Isomerisation:**
The double bond migrates along the carbon chain during partial hydrogenation. For oleic acid (9c-18:1), positional isomers 8t-, 10t-, and 11t-18:1 are produced. This occurs because the half-hydrogenated intermediate allows rotation before re-elimination of hydrogen.

**4. Health Implications of *trans* Fats:**
- Raise LDL ("bad") cholesterol and lower HDL ("good") cholesterol.
- Increase risk of coronary heart disease by ~21% per 2% of energy from trans fat (WHO data).
- WHO recommends **< 1% of total energy** from trans fats.

**5. Modern Industrial Alternatives:**
- **Full hydrogenation** (followed by blending with liquid oil) ‚ÄĒ zero *trans* but produces hard stocks.
- **Interesterification** (chemical or enzymatic) ‚ÄĒ rearranges fatty acids on the glycerol backbone without producing *trans*; creates plastic fats with customised MP and SFC.
- **Natural high-oleic oils** (sunflower, canola) ‚ÄĒ replace partially hydrogenated oils directly. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing *cis-trans* with positional isomerisation:** Students often state that "double bond shift" alone is *trans* isomerisation. It is actually *positional* isomerisation. *Trans* means the geometry at the double bond changes; positional means the location shifts.
> 2. **Confusing the order of polymorphic stability:** The correct order of INCREASING stability is őĪ < ő≤‚Ä≤ < ő≤. Students frequently reverse this.
> 3. **Confusing melting point with smoke point:** The Module 4 "melting point" refers to the solid‚Üíliquid transition of crystalline fat. It is NOT the smoke point (~230 ¬įC) or flash point.
> 4. **Writing "fats melt at one temperature":** Always clarify that natural fats have a *melting range* because they are mixtures.
> 5. **Forgetting units in derivations:** Always specify K, ¬įC, J/mol, Pa, or Pa¬∑s with the numerical answer.
> 6. **Conflating SFC and plasticity:** SFC is a *measurement* (% solid by NMR); plasticity is a *property* (mechanical behaviour). Both are linked but not the same.
> 7. **In diagrams, omitting arrows or labels:** A Mermaid diagram or hand-drawn schematic without clear labels and arrows will lose 1‚Äď2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Fats vs. Oils:** Fats are solid at ~20 ¬įC; oils are liquid. Both are triacylglycerols; the difference is unsaturation and chain length.
- **Crystal formation** has three stages: **supercooling ‚Üí nucleation ‚Üí growth**. Faster cooling ‚Üí finer crystals; slower cooling ‚Üí coarser crystals.
- **Polymorphism** in triacylglycerols is **monotropic** with three forms: **őĪ (0.415 nm), ő≤‚Ä≤ (0.420/0.380 nm), ő≤ (0.460 nm)**.
- **Stability order:** őĪ < ő≤‚Ä≤ < ő≤. **MP order:** őĪ < ő≤‚Ä≤ < ő≤. They transform irreversibly: őĪ ‚Üí ő≤‚Ä≤ ‚Üí ő≤.
- **ő≤‚Ä≤** is preferred in margarine and shortening (smooth texture). **ő≤ (Form V)** is preferred in tempered chocolate (glossy snap).
- **Melting point** of natural fats is a *range*, not a sharp point, because fats are mixtures of triacylglycerols.
- **MP increases** with chain length; **MP decreases** with each *cis* double bond (by ~2.5 ¬įC each).
- **Plasticity** = the ability to deform permanently without cracking. It requires a **two-phase structure** (crystal network + liquid oil).
- **Plastic range** is bounded by SFC = 15% (lower) and SFC = 5% (upper).
- **Bingham plastic** behaviour: $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$.
- **Isomerisation** = same formula, different structure. **Cis-trans** = geometry change; **positional** = location change.
- **Trans fats** are formed during *partial* hydrogenation. They are harmful (raise LDL, lower HDL).
- **Modern alternatives** to partial hydrogenation: **full hydrogenation**, **interesterification**, **high-oleic oils**.
- **Solid Fat Content (SFC)** is measured by **pulsed-NMR** and is the primary industrial quality parameter.
- **Critical nucleus radius** $r^* = 2\gamma_{sl} V_m / [\Delta H_f \ln(T_m/T)]$.
- **Plasticity index** $P_i = T_{upper} - T_{lower}$ (¬įC).
- **Isomerisation half-life** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ for first-order kinetics.
- WHO limit on *trans* fats: **< 1% of total daily energy** (~ 2.2 g/day on a 2000 kcal diet).
- KTU frequently asks: compare őĪ, ő≤‚Ä≤, ő≤ polymorphs (4‚Äď5 marks); explain polymorphism in chocolate tempering (5‚Äď7 marks); cis-trans isomerisation health impact (3‚Äď5 marks); SFC and plastic range (5‚Äď7 marks).

---

topics.title: Modification of Fats - Hydrogenation (Cis and Trans Isomers), Inter-esterification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modification of Fats - Hydrogenation (Cis and Trans Isomers), Inter-esterification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Modification of Fats?

Modification of fats refers to a set of **deliberate physico-chemical processes** employed in the food industry to alter the **functional, physical, and nutritional properties** of edible oils and fats. The three principal industrial routes covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCFTT205, Module 4) are:

1. **Hydrogenation** ‚Äď saturation of double bonds using molecular hydrogen.
2. **Cis‚ÄďTrans Isomerization** ‚Äď geometric rearrangement during partial hydrogenation.
3. **Inter-esterification** ‚Äď redistribution of fatty acids on the glycerol backbone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Modification is necessary because natural oils rarely possess the exact melting point, plasticity, oxidative stability, or crystallization behaviour demanded by modern food formulations (margarine, shortenings, confectionery fats, etc.).

---

## 1.2 Hydrogenation ‚ÄĒ Formal Definition

**Hydrogenation** is a catalytic chemical reaction in which **molecular hydrogen (H‚āā)** is added across the **carbon‚Äďcarbon double bonds (C=C)** of unsaturated fatty acids, converting **cis-double bonds** into **saturated C‚ÄďC single bonds**, in the presence of a **heterogeneous transition-metal catalyst** (most commonly **nickel (Ni)**, also Pd, Pt, or Cu) at controlled **temperatures (140‚Äď225 ¬įC)**, **pressures (1‚Äď10 atm)**, and with vigorous agitation.

The generic reaction is:

$$
\text{Unsaturated fat (cis) } + \text{ H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni, }\Delta} \text{Saturated fat}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogenation ‚Č† Hydrogenolysis.** Hydrogenolysis severs C‚ÄďC or C‚ÄďO bonds; hydrogenation only adds H across double bonds without breaking the carbon skeleton.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) ūüßą

Imagine a **sponge soaked in water** (the natural unsaturated oil ‚ÄĒ soft, fluid, full of kinks/bends). Now imagine you take that sponge and **press it flat under a heavy book** (heat + pressure + catalyst). The kinks flatten out, the sponge becomes firm and compact. That "flattened sponge" is your **hydrogenated, saturated fat** ‚ÄĒ solid, dense, and resistant to flow. The double bonds in the fatty-acid chains act exactly like those kinks; hydrogen straightens them, producing a solid fat from a liquid oil (e.g., converting **liquid soy oil ‚Üí Vanaspati / margarine**).

---

## 1.3 Cis and Trans Isomers ‚ÄĒ Formal Definition

**Cis‚Äďtrans isomerism (geometric isomerism)** in fatty acids arises because the **C=C double bond is rigid** (no free rotation), and the two substituents (the rest of the carbon chain and the hydrogen) can lie either on the **same side (cis)** or on the **opposite side (trans)** of the double-bond plane.

- **Cis-isomer (Z):** The two hydrogens are on the **same side** of the double bond ‚Üí the molecule has a **30¬į "kink" or "bend"** ‚Üí **liquid at room temperature** (e.g., oleic acid in olive oil).
- **Trans-isomer (E):** The hydrogens are on **opposite sides** ‚Üí the molecule remains **straight**, like a saturated chain ‚Üí **semi-solid or solid at room temperature** (e.g., elaidic acid).

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) ūü§Ě

Hold your two hands together, palms facing each other, fingers bent at a 30¬į angle ‚ÄĒ that is the **cis** shape (natural, kinked, fluid). Now rotate one hand 180¬į so the fingers point in **opposite directions** and the structure straightens out ‚ÄĒ that is the **trans** shape. The trans molecule "looks like" a saturated fat to the human body, which is precisely why it packs tightly and solidifies ‚ÄĒ and why it causes the well-known cardiovascular concerns.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** During **partial hydrogenation**, not all double bonds are saturated. The double bonds that *remain* undergo **geometric (cis ‚Üí trans) isomerization** as a side reaction, producing **trans-fatty acids (TFAs)**. WHO recommends TFA intake below **1% of total energy** (‚Čą 2.2 g/day for a 2000 kcal diet).

---

## 1.4 Inter-esterification ‚ÄĒ Formal Definition

**Inter-esterification** (also called **intraesterification** or **transesterification**) is the process of **rearranging the fatty acid moieties** esterified to the glycerol backbone of a triacylglycerol (TAG), producing a **new triacylglycerol composition** with **modified physical properties** (melting point, crystallization, plasticity), **without altering the degree of unsaturation** of the fatty acids themselves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** Inter-esterification **does not change the iodine value (IV)** of the fat (no change in unsaturation), but it **does change the melting profile, solid fat content (SFC), and polymorphism** of the fat. This distinguishes it from hydrogenation, which changes the IV.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) ūüéī

Imagine three children ‚ÄĒ A, B, and C ‚ÄĒ each holding three different coloured balls (the three fatty acids) on a 3-step ladder (the glycerol). Originally A holds Red‚ÄďGreen‚ÄďBlue (top to bottom). Now you ask the children to **swap their balls** randomly. The **balls themselves are unchanged**, but the **distribution changes**. The "team performance" (the fat's melting behaviour) is now different. That is inter-esterification: the fatty-acid identities are preserved; only their **positional distribution** on glycerol changes.

---

## 1.5 Why Modify Fats? ‚ÄĒ Industry Motivation Summary

| Property | Natural Liquid Oil | Modified Fat (Goal) |
|---|---|---|
| Melting point | Low (~ ‚ąí10 to +10 ¬įC) | Higher, controllable |
| Oxidative stability | Poor (polyunsaturated) | Improved |
| Plasticity (spreadability) | Absent | Achieved (margarine) |
| Crystallization behaviour | Polymorphic issues | Tailored ő≤‚Ä≤/ő≤ forms |
| Nutritional profile | High PUFA | Controlled TFAs / lower saturates |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrogenation ‚ÄĒ Mechanism in Detail

Hydrogenation proceeds through **five sequential steps** at the catalyst surface (Horiuti‚ÄďPolanyi mechanism, 1934):

1. **Diffusion** of H‚āā from the bulk gas phase to the catalyst surface.
2. **Chemisorption** of H‚āā onto the metal surface ‚Üí H‚ÄďH bond breaks, forming two surface-adsorbed H atoms (**atomic hydrogen, H***).
3. **Diffusion and chemisorption** of the unsaturated fat (C=C) onto an adjacent catalyst site.
4. **Surface reaction (half-hydrogenation):** One H* adds to one carbon of the C=C ‚Üí a **free radical / ŌÄ-allyl intermediate** is formed. This intermediate is the **branch point** ‚ÄĒ it can either:
   - (a) Accept a second H* ‚Üí **fully saturated product** (irreversible).
   - (b) Lose H* and re-form the double bond ‚Üí **isomerized product** (cis ‚Üí trans, or positional shift of the double bond).
5. **Desorption** of the product from the catalyst surface back into the bulk oil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 4 is the key to TFA formation.** When conditions favour "desorption before second hydrogenation" (high temperature, low H‚āā pressure, low agitation, sulfur-poisoned catalyst), the half-hydrogenated intermediate **releases H* and re-forms a double bond in the trans configuration**, which is thermodynamically more stable than cis.

### 2.1.1 Selectivity Concept

**Selectivity** in hydrogenation refers to the preferential reduction of:
- **Linolenic (C18:3) ‚Üí linoleic (C18:2) ‚Üí oleic (C18:1) ‚Üí stearic (C18:0)** ‚ÄĒ a stepwise cascade.

Industrial hydrogenations are often described as:
- **Non-selective (or "once-through") hydrogenation** ‚ÄĒ all double bonds reduced roughly proportionally ‚Üí produces hard, stearic-rich fats.
- **Selective hydrogenation** ‚ÄĒ designed to reduce linolenic acid (which causes flavour reversion in soy oil) while preserving linoleic and oleic acids. Achieved by **high temperature, low pressure, and sulfur-poisoned Ni catalysts**.

### 2.1.2 Effect of Process Variables

| Variable | Effect on Saturation Rate | Effect on Trans-Isomer Formation |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature ‚ÜĎ** | Increases | **Increases sharply** (favours desorption of half-hydrogenated intermediate) |
| **H‚āā Pressure ‚ÜĎ** | Increases | **Decreases** (favours second H* addition) |
| **Agitation ‚ÜĎ** | Increases | Decreases slightly |
| **Catalyst Loading ‚ÜĎ** | Increases | Decreases (more sites for second H*) |
| **Sulfur Poisoning** | Decreases | **Increases** (selective hydrogenation) |

---

## 2.2 Cis‚ÄďTrans Isomerization ‚ÄĒ Thermodynamic & Kinetic Basis

- **Cis double bond:** methyl groups (chain continuations) on same side ‚Üí steric strain ‚Üí **higher internal energy**.
- **Trans double bond:** methyl groups on opposite sides ‚Üí no steric strain ‚Üí **lower internal energy (‚Čą 4‚Äď5 kJ/mol more stable)**.

Because of this, the equilibrium mixture at hydrogenation temperatures is heavily **trans-enriched** (~ 60‚Äď90 % trans depending on conditions).

### 2.2.1 Conjugated vs Non-conjugated Double Bonds

- **Non-conjugated PUFA** (e.g., linoleic acid: 9-cis, 12-cis) ‚Üí under hydrogenation can form **conjugated linoleic acid (CLA)** as an intermediate.
- **Conjugated linoleic acid (CLA, 9-cis,11-trans or 10-trans,12-cis)** has documented **anticarcinogenic and anti-atherogenic** properties in animal models.

---

## 2.3 Inter-esterification ‚ÄĒ Detailed Classification

### 2.3.1 Types of Inter-esterification

| Type | Mechanism | Random/Position-Specific? | Catalyst |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acidolysis** | FA + TAG ‚Üí new TAG | Random | Acid (H‚āāSO‚āĄ, p-toluene sulfonic acid) |
| **Alcoholysis** | Alcohol + TAG ‚Üí new ester | Random | Acid / Base |
| **Glycerolysis** | Glycerol + TAG ‚Üí MAG/DAG | Random | Acid / Base |
| **Transesterification (TAG‚ÜĒTAG)** | TAG‚āĀ + TAG‚āā ‚Üí new TAGs | Random or Directed | **Base (NaOMe, NaOEt)** at **60‚Äď80 ¬įC** |
| **Enzymatic Inter-esterification** | TAG rearrangement via lipase | **1,3-position specific** (sn-1,3 regioselective) | Lipase (Lipozyme TL IM, RM IM, Novozym 435) |

### 2.3.2 Modes of Inter-esterification

- **Random (chemical) Inter-esterification:** Sodium methoxide (**CH‚āÉONa, 0.1‚Äď0.3 % w/w**) at 60‚Äď80 ¬įC, vacuum, 30‚Äď60 min ‚Üí **Bernoulli random distribution** of fatty acids on all three positions.
- **Directed (1,3-specific) Inter-esterification:** Low temperature, with a **1,3-specific lipase** (e.g., *Aspergillus oryzae* lipase) ‚Üí only the **sn-1 and sn-3 positions** exchange, leaving sn-2 untouched.
- **Acid-catalyzed inter-esterification:** Used when the oil contains **high free fatty acids (FFA > 2 %)** or **moisture**, where alkali catalysts would form soaps.

### 2.3.3 Effect on Melting Profile

Consider a blend of **fully saturated tripalmitin (PPP, m.p. 66 ¬įC)** and **triolein (OOO, m.p. 5 ¬įC)**:

| Composition | m.p. of mixture | SFC at 25 ¬įC |
|---|---|---|
| 50 % PPP + 50 % OOO (unrandomized) | Sharp, ~ 30‚Äď35 ¬įC (eutectic) | Low |
| 50 % PPP + 50 % OOO (**randomized**) | Smooth, 20‚Äď25 ¬įC | Higher, smoother |
| 50 % PPP + 50 % OOO (**directed, 1,3-specific**) | Reduced (POP/OPO formation) | Tailored |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why inter-esterification matters industrially:** It produces **zero trans fat** and **zero change in saturation**, making it the **preferred "clean-label" alternative to partial hydrogenation** since the WHO/FSSAI push to eliminate industrially produced TFAs (FSSAI limit in India: **not more than 5 % trans fat in oils and fats**, target 2 % by 2025 as per FSSAI notification).

---

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{1.99 \times 1000 \times V_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3}}{M}$ | Iodine Value (g I‚āā absorbed per 100 g fat) | g I‚āā/100 g |
| 2 | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N}{W}$ | Saponification Value (mg KOH/g fat) | mg KOH/g |

> **Constants to remember:**

> - 1 mL of 1 N Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ ‚Č° **0.127 g I‚āā**
> - 1 mL of 0.1 N KOH ‚Č° **5.61 mg KOH**

### Real-World Utility
- **Hydrogenation** ‚Üí margarine, vanaspati, shortenings, industrial frying fats.
- **Inter-esterification** ‚Üí zero-trans plastic fats, infant formula lipids (OPO/PLO structured TAGs), cocoa-butter equivalents (CBEs), salad oils with cloud-point stability.
- **Trans isomer monitoring** ‚Üí mandatory under **FSSAI, US FDA, EU regulations** for nutritional labeling.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Hydrogenation on Iodine Value (IV)

The **iodine value (IV)** measures total unsaturation. During hydrogenation, one H‚āā molecule saturates one C=C, removing 1 mole of I‚āā equivalent per double bond reduced.

**Given:**
- A soy oil with **IV‚āÄ = 130 g I‚āā/100 g**
- After partial hydrogenation, **IV_f = 90 g I‚āā/100 g**
- Mass of oil = **100 g** (basis)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Initial moles of double bonds:**

$$
n_{\text{C=C,0}} = \dfrac{\text{IV}_0}{2 \times 126.9} \text{ per 100 g}
$$

(2 √ó 126.9 = 253.8, because 1 mol of C=C reacts with 1 mol of I‚āā, MW(I‚āā) = 253.8 g/mol)

$$
n_{\text{C=C,0}} = \dfrac{130}{253.8} = 0.5122 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Final moles of double bonds:**

$$
n_{\text{C=C,f}} = \dfrac{90}{253.8} = 0.3546 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Moles of H‚āā consumed (i.e., double bonds saturated):**

$$
n_{\text{H}_2} = n_{\text{C=C,0}} - n_{\text{C=C,f}} = 0.5122 - 0.3546 = 0.1576 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Mass of H‚āā consumed:**

$$
m_{\text{H}_2} = n_{\text{H}_2} \times 2.016 = 0.1576 \times 2.016 = 0.3177 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Volume of H‚āā at STP (22.414 L/mol):**

$$
V_{\text{H}_2,\text{STP}} = 0.1576 \times 22.414 = 3.533 \text{ L per 100 g oil}
$$

**Result:** To reduce soy oil's IV from **130 ‚Üí 90**, you need **‚Čą 3.53 L of H‚āā at STP per 100 g oil**, consuming **0.318 g of H‚āā**.

> [!NOTE]
> The % saturation achieved = $\dfrac{130 - 90}{130} \times 100 = 30.77\,\%$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Saponification Value (SV) and Average Molecular Weight of Fat

The **saponification value (SV)** is the mg KOH required to saponify 1 g of fat. It is inversely proportional to the **mean molecular weight (MWŐĄ)** of the fatty acids.

**Starting relationship:**

$$
1 \text{ mol fat (3 ester bonds)} \equiv 3 \text{ mol KOH}
$$

**Let** the mean molecular weight of the fat be **MW_fat (g/mol)**. Then 1 g of fat contains **1/MW_fat mol**. Each mole of fat consumes 3 mol KOH:

$$
\text{SV} \left(\dfrac{\text{mg KOH}}{\text{g}}\right) = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\overline{\text{MW}}_{\text{fat}}}
$$

**Solving for MW_fat:**

$$
\overline{\text{MW}}_{\text{fat}} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}} = \dfrac{168300}{\text{SV}}
$$

**Worked example ‚ÄĒ soy oil with SV = 192 mg KOH/g:**

$$
\overline{\text{MW}}_{\text{fat}} = \dfrac{168300}{192} = 876.6 \text{ g/mol}
$$

Since 1 mol TAG yields 3 mol FA + 1 mol glycerol (MW 92), the **mean MW of the fatty acids** is:

$$
\overline{\text{MW}}_{\text{FA}} = \dfrac{876.6 - 92}{3} + 1 = 263.5 \text{ g/mol}
$$

(Addition of 1 accounts for the H added to the carboxyl end during saponification, by convention.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cis vs Trans Isomer Properties

| Property | Cis Isomer (e.g., Oleic, 9c-C18:1) | Trans Isomer (e.g., Elaidic, 9t-C18:1) |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Bent (~ 30¬į kink) | Linear, straight chain |
| **Intermolecular packing** | Loose, disordered | Tight, crystalline |
| **Melting point of pure FA** | **13.4 ¬įC** (oleic) | **44.5 ¬įC** (elaidic) |
| **Physical state at RT** | Liquid | Solid / semi-solid |
| **Stability** | Less stable (őĒG¬į higher) | More stable (~ 4.7 kJ/mol) |
| **Membrane fluidity** | High | Low |
| **Health impact** | Neutral / beneficial | ‚ÜĎ LDL-C, ‚Üď HDL-C, **atherogenic** |
| **Origin** | Natural (plant oils) | Industrial (partial hydrogenation), also natural (rumen bacteria) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ruminant trans fats** (vaccenic acid, t11-C18:1, and conjugated linoleic acid, c9,t11-CLA) occur naturally in dairy and beef (~ 2‚Äď6 % of fat) and are metabolized differently from **industrial TFAs** ‚ÄĒ though FSSAI treats both together under the 5 % / 2 % limits.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Random Inter-esterification Distribution (Bernoulli)

For a blend of TAGs undergoing **random chemical inter-esterification**, the probability of any fatty acid **A** occupying a given position on glycerol is:

$$
P(A \text{ at position}) = x_A
$$

where $x_A$ is the **mole fraction** of A in the total fatty-acid pool.

The probability that the three positions are occupied by A, B, C in a specific arrangement (e.g., **A-B-C**) is:

$$
P(\text{ABC}) = x_A \cdot x_B \cdot x_C
$$

**Example ‚ÄĒ 50:50 blend of tripalmitin (PPP) and triolein (OOO):**

Mole fraction of palmitic acid, $x_P = 0.5$; of oleic acid, $x_O = 0.5$.

Possible new TAGs and their mole fractions (after random rearrangement, 8 possible species if $n_{FA} = 2$):

| New TAG | Probability | Mole % |
|---|---|---|
| PPP | $(0.5)^3$ | 12.5 |
| PPO | $3 \times (0.5)^2 \times 0.5$ | 37.5 |
| POO | $3 \times 0.5 \times (0.5)^2$ | 37.5 |
| OOO | $(0.5)^3$ | 12.5 |

**Total = 100 %** ‚úď

This **broadening of the TAG distribution** is precisely why random inter-esterification produces a **smoother melting curve** than the original eutectic mixture.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical ‚ÄĒ Trans Fat Content in Partially Hydrogenated Soy Oil

**Given:** Partially hydrogenated soy oil contains the following C18:1 isomer distribution (by GC):

- 9c (cis-oleic) = **18.0 %**
- 9t (trans-elaidic) = **22.0 %**
- 10t‚Äď12t (positional trans) = **8.0 %**
- Saturated (C16:0 + C18:0) = **15.0 %**
- Other (PUFA, etc.) = balance

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Total trans C18:1:**

$$
\text{TFA}_{\text{C18:1}} = 22.0 + 8.0 = 30.0\,\%
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ FSSAI trans-fat % (TFA expressed as % of total fatty acids):**

$$
\text{TFA}_{\%,\text{FSSAI}} = \dfrac{30.0}{100} \times 100 = \mathbf{30.0\,\%}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ WHO recommendation check:**

For a 2000 kcal/day diet, 1 % energy from TFA = $\dfrac{0.01 \times 2000}{9} = 2.22$ g/day.

If 1 tablespoon (‚Čą 14 g) of this oil is consumed: TFA intake = $14 \times 0.30 = 4.2$ g ‚Üí **exceeds WHO limit by ~ 89 %** for that one serving.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked "is this fat safe?", compute the **trans percentage of total fat, not the trans as % of unsaturated fraction.** FSSAI and CODEX define TFA per total fatty acids.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model]**
**Define hydrogenation of oils and fats. Mention any two industrial applications of hydrogenated fats.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hydrogenation is a **catalytic chemical process** in which molecular hydrogen (H‚āā) is added across the carbon‚Äďcarbon double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids in the presence of a metal catalyst (Ni, Pd, or Pt) at elevated temperature (140‚Äď225 ¬įC) and pressure, converting liquid unsaturated oils into semi-solid or solid fats. (2 marks)

**Industrial applications:** (1 mark for any two)
- Manufacture of **margarine** and **vanaspati** (e.g., Dalda).
- Production of **shortenings** for baking and confectionery.
