> **Mark-split:** Definition (2 marks) + 2 applications (¬Ĺ mark each).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model]**
**What are cis and trans isomers of fatty acids? Why are trans fatty acids considered harmful?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cis isomers** have the two hydrogen atoms of the C=C double bond on the **same side**, producing a **bent (30¬į) molecular geometry** ‚Üí liquid at room temperature (e.g., oleic acid). **Trans isomers** have the hydrogens on **opposite sides**, producing a **linear geometry** ‚Üí semi-solid at room temperature (e.g., elaidic acid). (1¬Ĺ marks)

**Harmful effects:** Trans fats **raise LDL-cholesterol** (atherogenic), **lower HDL-cholesterol**, promote **inflammation**, and are associated with a **~ 23 % higher risk of coronary heart disease** per 2 % energy increment. FSSAI limits industrially produced TFA to ‚Č§ 5 % of total fat. (1¬Ĺ marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Module 4, Set 1

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the mechanism of catalytic hydrogenation of oils with a neat labelled diagram. Explain the formation of trans isomers during partial hydrogenation.

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss the different types of inter-esterification reactions. Compare chemical and enzymatic inter-esterification in terms of products and applications.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Hydrogenation Mechanism (Horiuti‚ÄďPolanyi) ‚ÄĒ 4 marks**

The Horiuti‚ÄďPolanyi mechanism involves the following steps on the Ni surface:

1. **Hydrogen adsorption:** H‚āā molecules chemisorb on Ni(0) sites, dissociating into two H* atoms.
2. **Fatty-acid adsorption:** The C=C double bond of the unsaturated fatty acid (e.g., oleic acid) ŌÄ-bonds to an adjacent Ni site.
3. **Half-hydrogenation:** One H* transfers to one of the sp¬≤ carbons ‚Üí a **half-hydrogenated intermediate** (free radical-like) is formed, with the other carbon still bonded to Ni.
4. **Branch point:** This intermediate has two fates:
   - **(i)** Accepts a second H* ‚Üí fully saturated product (**stearic acid**).
   - **(ii)** Loses H* and re-forms the double bond ‚Üí **geometric isomerization (cis ‚Üí trans)** or **positional isomerization** (őĒ9 ‚Üí őĒ10, őĒ11).
5. **Desorption:** Final product desorbs from the Ni surface back to the oil phase.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Should illustrate the Ni surface with H* atoms, adsorbed C=C, half-hydrogenated intermediate, and the two product routes.

**Trans Isomer Formation (2 marks):** When process conditions favour **high temperature, low H‚āā pressure, low agitation, or sulfur-poisoned Ni catalyst**, the half-hydrogenated intermediate **desorbs before acquiring the second H***. Because the **trans configuration is thermodynamically more stable by ~ 4.7 kJ/mol** (less steric strain), the regenerated double bond preferentially adopts the trans geometry, giving **elaidic acid (9t-C18:1)**. The longer the intermediate resides on the surface, the more it isomerizes ‚Üí higher TFA content.

**Industrial process parameters (1 mark):** Typical hydrogenation is carried out at 140‚Äď225 ¬įC, 1‚Äď10 atm H‚āā, 0.05‚Äď0.2 % Ni catalyst (or 0.01‚Äď0.05 % Pd), with vigorous agitation. **Selective hydrogenation** uses sulfur-poisoned Ni at 180 ¬įC to reduce linolenic acid preferentially while preserving linoleic and oleic acids.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Where students lose marks:**
> - **Skipping the half-hydrogenated intermediate** (it's the *most critical* mechanistic step; omitting it = lose 1 mark).
> - Confusing **trans isomerization** with **positional isomerization** ‚ÄĒ these are two different side reactions.
> - Failing to mention **sulfur poisoning** when discussing selective hydrogenation.
> - Drawing catalyst surface diagrams with H‚āā and fat on *different* sides instead of on the *same* Ni surface.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Types of Inter-esterification (3 marks):**
- **Acidolysis** ‚ÄĒ fatty acid + TAG ‚Üí new TAG.
- **Alcoholysis** ‚ÄĒ alcohol + TAG ‚Üí new ester + glycerol.
- **Glycerolysis** ‚ÄĒ glycerol + TAG ‚Üí mono- and diacylglycerols.
- **Transesterification (TAG ‚ÜĒ TAG)** ‚ÄĒ most common in fat modification.

**Chemical Inter-esterification (2 marks):**
- Uses **sodium methoxide (CH‚āÉONa) or sodium ethoxide (NaOEt)** at 60‚Äď80 ¬įC under vacuum for 30‚Äď60 min.
- Catalyst loading: 0.1‚Äď0.3 % w/w.
- Produces a **random (Bernoulli) distribution** of fatty acids on all three sn-positions.
- **Products:** Smooth-melting plastic fats (zero trans, IV unchanged), used in margarine, shortenings, and confectionery fats (CBE manufacture).
- **Limitations:** Random product, requires dry oil (< 0.01 % moisture), cannot produce structured TAGs.

**Enzymatic Inter-esterification (2 marks):**
- Uses **1,3-specific lipases** (e.g., *Rhizomucor miehei* lipase, *Aspergillus oryzae* lipase) immobilized on silica (Lipozyme RM IM, TL IM).
- Operates at 40‚Äď70 ¬įC, 4‚Äď8 h, mild pH, no soap formation.
- **Selectively exchanges fatty acids at sn-1 and sn-3 positions, preserving sn-2** ‚Üí produces **structured TAGs** (e.g., OPO for infant formula, MLM-type lipids).
- **Applications:** Infant formula (Betapol‚ĄĘ, OPO), cocoa-butter equivalents (CBE), structured lipids with nutritional benefits (e.g., medium-chain FA at sn-1,3 for quick energy).

**Comparison Table (additional clarity):**

| Parameter | Chemical | Enzymatic |
|---|---|---|
| Specificity | Random, all positions | 1,3-specific (sn-2 preserved) |
| Temperature | 60‚Äď80 ¬įC | 40‚Äď70 ¬įC |
| Time | 30‚Äď60 min | 4‚Äď8 h |
| Catalyst cost | Low | High |
| Product | Broad melting curve | Tailored, structured |
| Downstream | Wash needed | None |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Part (b):**
> - Confusing **acidolysis** with **alcoholysis** (acidolysis uses free FA; alcoholysis uses free alcohol).
> - Saying enzymatic inter-esterification is "random" ‚ÄĒ it is **1,3-specific, not random**.
> - Forgetting to mention **cocoa-butter equivalent (CBE)** manufacture, a classic KTU-asked application.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Module 4, Set 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat flowchart, explain the industrial process of hydrogenation. Discuss the effect of temperature, pressure, and catalyst on the rate of hydrogenation and trans-isomer formation.

**(b) (7 marks)** A soy oil sample (IV = 130) is partially hydrogenated to an IV of 90. Calculate: (i) the % reduction in unsaturation, (ii) the moles of H‚āā consumed per 100 g oil, (iii) the volume of H‚āā at STP consumed, and (iv) the mass of saturated product formed (assume complete saturation of the reduced double bonds to stearic).

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Industrial Hydrogenation Flowchart (2 marks):** Cover the sequence: Crude oil ‚Üí Degumming ‚Üí Refining ‚Üí Bleaching ‚Üí Pre-heated reactor (140‚Äď225 ¬įC) ‚Üí H‚āā sparged + Ni catalyst ‚Üí Agitated converter ‚Üí Filter ‚Üí Deodorization ‚Üí Finished hydrogenated fat.

**Effect of variables ‚ÄĒ Rate vs Trans-isomer formation (5 marks):**

| Variable | Effect on Rate | Effect on TFA |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature ‚ÜĎ** | ‚ÜĎ (Arrhenius) | **‚ÜĎ‚ÜĎ Marked increase** (favours desorption of half-hydrogenated intermediate ‚Üí trans re-formation) |
| **H‚āā Pressure ‚ÜĎ** | ‚ÜĎ‚ÜĎ (high [H*] on catalyst) | **‚Üď‚Üď Decreased** (high H* flux ensures rapid 2nd H* addition ‚Üí saturation, no isomerization) |
| **Agitation ‚ÜĎ** | ‚ÜĎ (better gas‚Äďliquid‚Äďsolid mass transfer) | ‚Üď (less residence time of intermediate) |
| **Catalyst (Ni) loading ‚ÜĎ** | ‚ÜĎ (more active sites) | ‚Üď (faster second hydrogenation) |
| **Sulfur poisoning of Ni** | ‚Üď (blocks active sites) | **‚ÜĎ‚ÜĎ** (forces selective conditions ‚Üí high TFA) |
| **Pd catalyst (vs Ni)** | Much faster | Lower TFA at same conversion |
| **Cu catalyst (vs Ni)** | Slower, more selective for linolenic | Lower TFA |

**Conclusion (additional):** To **minimize TFA** while achieving desired saturation, use **lower temperature (~ 140 ¬įC), higher H‚āā pressure (~ 5‚Äď10 atm), and high-quality unpoisoned Ni or Pd catalyst**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Part (a):**
> - Drawing flowchart with missing unit operations (e.g., omitting **deodorization**).
> - Stating "higher temperature reduces TFA" ‚ÄĒ this is **WRONG**; higher temperature **increases** TFA.
> - Confusing "**selective**" hydrogenation (preserve PUFA) with "**non-selective**" (saturate everything).

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** IV‚āÄ = 130 g I‚āā/100 g, IV_f = 90 g I‚āā/100 g, mass of oil = 100 g.

**(i) % reduction in unsaturation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \dfrac{130 - 90}{130} \times 100 = \dfrac{40}{130} \times 100 = \mathbf{30.77\,\%}
$$

**(ii) Moles of H‚āā consumed per 100 g oil (2 marks):**

Initial moles of C=C = $\dfrac{130}{253.8} = 0.5122$ mol

Final moles of C=C = $\dfrac{90}{253.8} = 0.3546$ mol

Moles of C=C reduced = $0.5122 - 0.3546 = 0.1576$ mol

Since 1 mol C=C requires 1 mol H‚āā:

$$
n_{\text{H}_2} = \mathbf{0.1576 \text{ mol}}
$$

**(iii) Volume of H‚āā at STP (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_{\text{H}_2,\text{STP}} = 0.1576 \times 22.414 = \mathbf{3.533 \text{ L}}
$$

**(iv) Mass of saturated product formed (2 marks):**

Each double bond reduced converts one **C=C (MW ‚Čą 28)** into a **C‚ÄďC** with addition of 2 H atoms (2 √ó 1.008 = 2.016 g/mol).

Mass of H added to 100 g oil = $0.1576 \times 2.016 = 0.3177$ g (already shown above).

Mass of saturated product (stearic acid equivalent) formed:

If we approximate that the reduced double bonds now form **stearic acid (C18:0)**:

$$
\text{Mass of stearic formed} = 0.1576 \times 284.48 = 44.83 \text{ g}
$$

(where 284.48 g/mol = MW of stearic acid)

**Final composition (qualitative check):** 100 g oil + 0.318 g H‚āā ‚Üí **100.318 g hydrogenated product**, of which ~ 44.8 g is now stearic-rich.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Part (b):**
> - Using molecular weight of I‚āā as **127** (single atom) instead of **253.8** (I‚āā molecule).
> - Forgetting that 1 mol of I‚āā reacts with **1 mol of C=C** (so MW(I‚āā) = 253.8 must be used in denominator).
> - Conflating "% reduction in IV" with "% saturation achieved" ‚ÄĒ they are mathematically identical here, but students should *show* the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Hydrogenation
- ‚ėź Catalytic addition of H‚āā to C=C bonds; Ni is the dominant industrial catalyst.
- ‚ėź Converts **liquid oils ‚Üí solid/semi-solid fats** (vanaspati, margarine, shortening).
- ‚ėź **Higher temperature** = faster but **more trans isomers**.
- ‚ėź **Higher H‚āā pressure** = faster and **fewer trans isomers**.
- ‚ėź **Sulfur-poisoned Ni** = selective hydrogenation (target linolenic, preserve linoleic/oleic).
- ‚ėź Horiuti‚ÄďPolanyi mechanism: **half-hydrogenated intermediate is the key to TFA formation**.

### Cis‚ÄďTrans Isomerism
- ‚ėź **Cis** = bent (kinked), liquid, e.g., oleic (m.p. 13.4 ¬įC).
- ‚ėź **Trans** = linear, solid, e.g., elaidic (m.p. 44.5 ¬įC).
- ‚ėź Trans is **~ 4.7 kJ/mol more stable** than cis.
- ‚ėź **Trans fats ‚ÜĎ LDL-C, ‚Üď HDL-C** ‚Üí WHO recommends < 1 % energy intake.
- ‚ėź **FSSAI limit (India):** ‚Č§ 5 % TFA in oils/fats; target ‚Č§ 2 % by 2025.
- ‚ėź Partial hydrogenation is the main industrial route to TFA.

### Inter-esterification
- ‚ėź Rearranges fatty acids on glycerol; **does not change IV**.
- ‚ėź **Chemical (NaOMe):** random, fast, cheap; produces broad-melting plastic fat.
- ‚ėź **Enzymatic (1,3-specific lipase):** preserves sn-2; produces **structured TAGs** (OPO, MLM).
- ‚ėź Applications: zero-trans margarine, **cocoa-butter equivalents**, **infant formula** (Betapol‚ĄĘ).
- ‚ėź **Acid-catalyzed** inter-esterification used when oil has high FFA / moisture.

### Numerical Constants (Memorize!)
- ‚ėź **MW(I‚āā) = 253.8 g/mol**
- ‚ėź **MW(KOH) = 56.1 g/mol**
- ‚ėź **Molar volume (STP) = 22.414 L/mol**
- ‚ėź $\overline{\text{MW}}_{\text{fat}} = \dfrac{168300}{\text{SV}}$

### Key Industrial Distinctions (Frequently Asked)
- ‚ėź Hydrogenation **changes IV**; inter-esterification **does not**.
- ‚ėź Hydrogenation can form **TFAs**; inter-esterification **never forms TFAs**.
- ‚ėź Inter-esterification can produce **zero-trans plastic fats** ‚ÄĒ the modern alternative to partial hydrogenation.

### Regulatory Quick-Fire
- ‚ėź **FSSAI** (India): TFA ‚Č§ 5 % of total fat in oils.
- ‚ėź **WHO global target:** TFA elimination by 2023.
- ‚ėź **US FDA:** TFA no longer "GRAS" (since 2018) for use in foods.
- ‚ėź **Codex Alimentarius:** Inter-esterified fats are recognized as a separate modification class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> When a question combines "**modification**" + "**trans fat**" + "**inter-esterification**," the most common 14-mark question pattern is: (a) mechanism with diagram (7) + (b) comparison/numerical (7). Always draw a **labelled catalyst-surface diagram** in (a) ‚ÄĒ it is the **single highest-scoring visual** in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rancidity and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT205 - FOOD CHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rancidity and Its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Rancidity** is the oxidative, hydrolytic, or microbial deterioration of fats, oils, and lipid-containing foods, resulting in the development of off-flavors, off-odors, and nutritionally compromised products. The American Oil Chemists' Society (AOCS) formally defines it as "the development of any objectionable flavour and odour in fats and oils, or in foods containing them, attributable to the chemical alteration of the lipid fraction."

In food chemistry, rancidity is classified as a **lipid deterioration phenomenon** triggered by three fundamental chemical pathways:
1. Reaction with **molecular oxygen (O‚āā)** ‚ÄĒ *Oxidative Rancidity*
2. Reaction with **water (H‚āāO)** ‚ÄĒ *Hydrolytic Rancidity*
3. Action of **microbial/enzymatic activity** ‚ÄĒ *Microbial & Ketonic Rancidity*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 places special emphasis on the **mechanism of lipid oxidation**, the **types of rancidity**, the **measurement of oxidative deterioration** (Peroxide Value, TBA value, Kries test), and **methods of prevention** (antioxidants, packaging).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine leaving a slice of **fresh butter** on the kitchen counter for a week. When you come back, it smells **paints-like, metallic, or even like old crayons**. That disagreeable change is rancidity at work.

Think of unsaturated fats as **delicate glass ornaments**:

- Each **double bond (C=C)** in the fatty acid chain is a **fragile joint** that is vulnerable to attack.
- **Oxygen from air** is like a **small hammer** that, once it strikes, propagates a chain of damage.
- **Heat and light** are accelerants ‚ÄĒ like throwing the ornament onto a hot stove.
- **Antioxidants** are the **protective packaging foam** that absorbs the blows before they crack the glass.

In essence, *rancidity = fat + time + oxygen + warmth + (sometimes microbes) ‚Üí "stale, unpleasant" product*.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Chemical Metrics Used

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Peroxide Value (PV)** | **meq O‚āā per kg of fat** | Measures primary oxidation products (hydroperoxides) |
| **Thiobarbituric Acid (TBA) Number** | **mg malonaldehyde per kg sample** | Measures secondary oxidation (aldehydes) |
| **Kreis Test** | Qualitative (pink color) | Detects epihydrinaldehyde (early oxidation) |
| **Acid Value (AV)** | **mg KOH per g fat** | Detects free fatty acids (hydrolytic rancidity) |
| **Iodine Value (IV)** | **g I‚āā per 100 g fat** | Indicates degree of unsaturation |
| **p-Anisidine Value (p-AV)** | Dimensionless | Measures secondary non-volatile carbonyls |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bohm peroxide test** and **Wheeler's test** are other classical qualitative methods. KTU 2024 frequently tests the **Peroxide Value titration principle** in numerical problems.

## 1.4 Why Rancidity Matters in Food Industry

- **Economic Loss:** Globally, ~25% of edible oils and fat-rich foods are lost annually to oxidation.
- **Nutritional Degradation:** Loss of essential fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic) and fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
- **Toxicological Risk:** Secondary products like **malonaldehyde (MDA)** and **4-hydroxynonenal (4-HNE)** are **suspected carcinogens** and **mutagens**.
- **Sensory Rejection:** Off-flavors cause consumer rejection even if the food is technically safe.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Rancidity ‚ÄĒ The Master Framework

Rancidity is broadly classified into the following six principal types. Each arises from a different chemical trigger.

```
                       RANCIDITY
                           |
        ___________________|____________________
        |              |            |          |
   OXIDATIVE      HYDROLYTIC    KETONIC    MICROBIAL
   (Auto-oxidation) (Lipolysis)  (Mold)   (Bacterial)
        |              |            |
   Photochemical  Enzymatic     Aspergillus,
   Thermal        Chemical      Penicillium
```

### 2.1.1 Oxidative Rancidity (Auto-oxidation) ‚ÄĒ The Most Common

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Oxidative rancidity is the deterioration of lipids caused by the reaction of unsaturated fatty acids with molecular oxygen, proceeding through a **free-radical chain mechanism**. It is the single most commercially important form of rancidity in vegetable oils, fried foods, and nuts.

#### **Chemistry of Auto-oxidation ‚ÄĒ The Three Stages**

**Stage 1 ‚ÄĒ Initiation (Induction Period)**

A hydrogen atom is abstracted from the **őĪ-methylene group** (the carbon adjacent to a C=C double bond) of an unsaturated fatty acid, producing an **alkyl free radical (R‚ÄĘ)**. This step has high activation energy (~75‚Äď150 kJ/mol) and is the rate-limiting initiation.

$$\text{RH} \xrightarrow{\text{initiator (heat, light, metal)}} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$

**Stage 2 ‚ÄĒ Propagation (Chain Branching)**

The alkyl radical rapidly reacts with triplet oxygen (¬≥O‚āā) to form a **peroxyl radical (ROO‚ÄĘ)**, which abstracts another hydrogen from a neighboring fatty acid, producing a **hydroperoxide (ROOH)** and a new alkyl radical. This continues the chain.

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

**Stage 3 ‚ÄĒ Termination (Radical Quenching)**

Two free radicals combine to form non-radical, stable products, ending the chain.

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R-R}$$

$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOR}$$

$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{ROO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **hydroperoxides (ROOH) are tasteless and odorless** ‚ÄĒ they are *primary* oxidation products. They decompose (especially in the presence of metals) into **secondary** products: aldehydes, ketones, alcohols, hydrocarbons ‚ÄĒ these are the actual carriers of rancid odor/flavor.

#### **Secondary Decomposition of Hydroperoxides**

$$\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Cu}^{+}} \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{OH}^{-} + \text{Fe}^{3+}/\text{Cu}^{2+}$$

$$\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Fe}^{2+}} \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} + \text{Fe}^{3+}$$

The **alkoxyl radical (RO‚ÄĘ)** then undergoes **ő≤-scission**, breaking the C‚ÄďC bond and releasing:
- **Aldehydes** (e.g., hexanal, malonaldehyde) ‚ÄĒ grassy, beany odors
- **Ketones** ‚ÄĒ metallic odors
- **Hydrocarbons** ‚ÄĒ pentane, ethane

### 2.1.2 Hydrolytic Rancidity (Lipolysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Hydrolytic rancidity is the enzymatic or chemical hydrolysis of triglycerides by water, releasing **free fatty acids (FFA)**. When short-chain fatty acids (C4‚ÄďC10) are released, they produce **soapy, bitter, or "rancid butter"** off-flavors.

**Reaction:**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{lipase}} \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{Free Fatty Acids}$$

**Key Catalysts:**
- **Lipases** (endogenous ‚ÄĒ present in oilseeds, milk, adipose tissue)
- **Microbial lipases** (from *Pseudomonas*, *Staphylococcus*, *Geotrichum*)
- **Heat + Moisture** (chemical hydrolysis, slower)

**Commonly Affected Foods:** Butter, ghee, coconut oil, palm oil, rice bran, dairy products.

**Quantitative Indicator:** The **Acid Value (AV)** is used to measure hydrolytic rancidity.

### 2.1.3 Ketonic Rancidity

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A specialized microbial rancidity in which certain molds (especially *Aspergillus niger*, *Penicillium glaucum*) convert saturated fatty acids into **methyl ketones** (alkan-2-ones) via ő≤-oxidation followed by decarboxylation.

**General Reaction:**

$$\text{Fatty Acid} \xrightarrow{\text{mold enzyme}} \beta\text{-keto acid} \xrightarrow{-\text{CO}_2} \text{Methyl Ketone}$$

**Example:** Lauric acid (C12) ‚Üí 2-undecanone (methyl nonyl ketone) ‚ÄĒ produces a **"paraffin-like" or "pineapple-like"** off-odor.

**Commonly Affected Foods:** Coconut oil, palm kernel oil, copra, stored corn.

### 2.1.4 Microbial Rancidity

Caused by **bacteria, yeasts, and molds** that produce **lipolytic enzymes (lipases and phospholipases)**. Often occurs in:
- Contaminated oilseeds (especially copra, palm fruits)
- Improperly stored dairy
- Fermented foods going beyond intended fermentation

This type overlaps with hydrolytic and ketonic rancidity since microbial enzymes drive both.

### 2.1.5 Photochemical / Light-Induced Rancidity

Light (especially **UV and blue light, 350‚Äď500 nm**) catalyzes the formation of **singlet oxygen (¬ĻO‚āā)** from triplet oxygen via **sensitizers** like:
- Chlorophyll
- Riboflavin (Vitamin B‚āā)
- Myoglobin
- Synthetic dyes

Singlet oxygen adds directly across the double bond (ene reaction) at rates **~1500√ó faster** than triplet oxygen.

$$\text{Sensitizer} + h\nu \rightarrow {}^{1}\text{S}^{*}$$
$${}^{1}\text{S}^{*} \rightarrow {}^{3}\text{S}^{*} \quad (\text{intersystem crossing})$$
$${}^{3}\text{S}^{*} + {}^{3}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow {}^{1}\text{S} + {}^{1}\text{O}_2$$
$$\text{RH} + {}^{1}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROOH (hydroperoxide)}$$

### 2.1.6 Thermal Rancidity (Heat-Induced Deterioration)

At frying temperatures (160‚Äď200 ¬įC), fats undergo:
- **Polymerization** ‚Üí viscous, foaming oil
- **Cyclization** ‚Üí cyclic monomers
- **Scission** ‚Üí short-chain volatiles
- **Trans-isomerization** ‚Üí trans-fatty acids (health hazard)

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Oxidative Rancidity

| Factor | Effect on Oxidation Rate |
|---|---|
| **Degree of Unsaturation** | Higher IV (more double bonds) ‚Üí exponentially higher oxidation (linolenic oxidizes ~25√ó faster than oleic) |
| **Oxygen Partial Pressure** | Above ~100 mmHg, rate is O‚āā-concentration dependent |
| **Temperature** | Rate roughly doubles for every **10 ¬įC** rise (Q‚āĀ‚āÄ ‚Čą 2) |
| **Light (UV/visible)** | Accelerates via singlet oxygen formation |
| **Metal Ions (Cu¬≤‚Āļ, Fe¬≤‚Āļ)** | Powerful pro-oxidants (Cu¬≤‚Āļ effective at ppb levels) |
| **pH** | Acidic pH stabilizes hydroperoxides; alkaline pH accelerates breakdown |
| **Water Activity (aŠĶ•)** | Low aŠĶ• (<0.2) accelerates; intermediate aŠĶ• (~0.3‚Äď0.7) slows it |
| **Antioxidants** | Phenolic compounds (BHA, BHT, TBHQ, tocopherols) retard oxidation |

## 2.3 Prevention & Control of Rancidity

### 2.3.1 Physical Methods
- **Vacuum packaging** / **Nitrogen flushing** (oxygen replacement)
- **Opaque packaging** (light exclusion)
- **Refrigeration / freezing**
- **Hydrogenation** (converts unsaturated ‚Üí saturated bonds)

### 2.3.2 Chemical Methods ‚ÄĒ Antioxidants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Antioxidants** are substances that, when present at low concentrations compared to the oxidizable substrate, **delay or prevent oxidation**. They are classified as:

**(a) Primary (Chain-Breaking) Antioxidants:**
- Phenolic compounds: **BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole), BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene), TBHQ (Tert-Butyl Hydroquinone), PG (Propyl Gallate)**
- They donate H‚ÄĘ to ROO‚ÄĘ, terminating the chain.

$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{A}^{\bullet}$$

The antioxidant radical (A‚ÄĘ) is stabilized by resonance and does **not** propagate the chain.

**(b) Secondary (Preventive / Synergistic) Antioxidants:**
- **Chelators:** EDTA, citric acid ‚ÄĒ sequester pro-oxidant metals.
- **Oxygen scavengers:** Ascorbic acid ‚ÄĒ reduces O‚āā to H‚āāO‚āā.
- **Synergists:** Lecithin, phospholipids.

### 2.3.3 Permitted Antioxidant Limits (FSSAI / Codex)

| Antioxidant | Max Permitted Level (in fats/oils) |
|---|---|
| BHA | **200 mg/kg** |
| BHT | **200 mg/kg** |
| TBHQ | **200 mg/kg** |
| PG | **100 mg/kg** |
| Total (BHA+BHT+PG+TBHQ combined) | **200 mg/kg** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars in formulas are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 1000}{W}$ | **Peroxide Value** ‚ÄĒ measures primary oxidation (hydroperoxides) | meq O‚āā per kg fat |
| 2 | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | **Acid Value** ‚ÄĒ measures hydrolytic rancidity (FFA) | mg KOH per g fat |
| 3 | $\text{FFA (\%)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times M}{W \times 10}$ | Free fatty acids as oleic / palmitic acid | % (w/w) |
| 4 | $\text{TBA Number} = \dfrac{A_{532} \times 9.48}{W}$ | Secondary oxidation (malonaldehyde); 9.48 = constant for TBA-MDA chromogen | mg MDA per kg |
| 5 | $\text{p-AV} = \dfrac{A \times V \times 1.2}{W}$ | *p*-Anisidine Value ‚ÄĒ non-volatile aldehydes | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Totox Value} = 2 \times \text{PV} + \text{p-AV}$ | Total oxidation value (combined primary + secondary) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_a \approx 75-150 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Activation energy of lipid auto-oxidation initiation | kJ/mol |
| 8 | $\text{Chain length} = \dfrac{\text{rate of O}_2 \text{ uptake}}{\text{rate of initiation}}$ | Kinetic chain length of propagation | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Oxidative Stability Index (OSI)} = t_{i}$ at fixed temp (e.g., 110 ¬įC) | Rancimat / OSI induction time | hours |
| 10 | $\text{Rate} \propto 2^{(T-25)/10}$ | Approximate temperature effect (Q‚āĀ‚āÄ ‚Čą 2) | ‚ÄĒ |

**Where in the formulas:**
- $V$ = titrant volume (mL) of Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ (for PV) or KOH (for AV)
- $N$ = normality of titrant
- $W$ = weight of fat sample (g)
- $M$ = molecular weight of the chosen fatty acid (282 for oleic)
- $A_{532}$ = absorbance at 532 nm
- $A$ = absorbance of sample in p-anisidine test
- $56.1$ = equivalent weight of KOH

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Edible oil refining** | Understanding PV guides deodorization & bleaching process design |
| **Shelf-life prediction** | OSI/Rancimat data extrapolated via Arrhenius to predict storage life |
| **Packaging design** | Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) of packaging material is selected based on oxidation kinetics |
| **Frying oil quality** | Total Polar Materials (TPM) and PV are used in HACCP monitoring of fryers |
| **Margarine / shortening** | Hydrogenation + antioxidant use requires balancing oxidative stability vs. trans-fat formation |
| **Cosmetics / Pharma** | Same chemistry applies to mineral oils, paraffin, and lipid-based drug carriers |

# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Models & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Peroxide Value (PV) Formula

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks for the **derivation of the PV formula** and a sample numerical problem. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation and a worked-out problem.

### 3.1.1 Chemical Principle

The peroxide value is determined by **iodometric titration**. Hydroperoxides (ROOH) present in the oxidized fat react with **potassium iodide (KI)** in the presence of **acetic acid / chloroform** solvent, liberating **iodine (I‚āā)**.

The liberated I‚āā is then titrated with **standard sodium thiosulfate (Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ)** using **starch indicator** (blue ‚Üí colorless endpoint).

### 3.1.2 The Reaction Steps

**Step A ‚ÄĒ Liberation of iodine by hydroperoxides:**

$$\text{ROOH} + 2\,\text{KI} + 2\,\text{H}^{+} \rightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{I}_2 + 2\,\text{K}^{+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step B ‚ÄĒ Titration of liberated iodine with thiosulfate:**

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

### 3.1.3 Stoichiometric Equivalence

From the balanced equations:
- **1 mole** of ROOH liberates **1 mole** of I‚āā
- **1 mole** of I‚āā reacts with **2 moles** of Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ
- Therefore: **1 mole ROOH ‚Č° 2 moles Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ**

The equivalent weight of oxygen is **8** (since O = 16, but one O accepts 2 electrons ‚áí eq. wt. = 16/2 = 8).

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the PV Formula

The number of **milli-equivalents of oxygen per kg of fat** is calculated as follows:

$$\text{Meq of O}_2 = \text{Meq of Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 = V \times N$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ used (mL)
- $N$ = normality of Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ (eq/L)

To convert this to **per kg of fat** (since the sample is in grams):

$$\text{PV} = \frac{V \times N \times 1000}{W} \;\; \text{meq O}_2 \text{ per kg fat}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PV} = \frac{V \times N \times 1000}{W}}$$

where $W$ = mass of fat sample (in grams), and the factor 1000 converts g to kg.

### 3.1.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** A 5.00 g sample of refined sunflower oil is analyzed for peroxide value. The liberated iodine required **28.50 mL** of **0.01 N Na‚āāS‚āāO‚āÉ** for titration. Calculate the PV and interpret the result.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the values.
- $V = 28.50$ mL
- $N = 0.01$ N
- $W = 5.00$ g

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.

$$\text{PV} = \frac{V \times N \times 1000}{W} = \frac{28.50 \times 0.01 \times 1000}{5.00}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator.

$$28.50 \times 0.01 \times 1000 = 28.50 \times 10 = 285.0$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $W$.

$$\text{PV} = \frac{285.0}{5.00} = 57.0 \;\; \text{meq O}_2 \text{ per kg fat}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard PV Limits for Edible Oils (FSSAI / Codex):**
> - **PV < 10 meq/kg** ‚Üí Fresh, good quality oil
> - **PV = 10‚Äď20 meq/kg** ‚Üí Acceptable, but monitor
> - **PV = 20‚Äď40 meq/kg** ‚Üí Borderline, slight rancidity
> - **PV > 40 meq/kg** ‚Üí Rancid, **rejected**

Since PV = 57.0 meq/kg **>> 40**, this sunflower oil is **highly rancid** and **not fit for consumption**.

### 3.1.6 Second Numerical Problem ‚ÄĒ Acid Value

> **Question:** 2.0 g of ghee is titrated with 0.1 N KOH. The titre value is **5.6 mL**. Calculate the Acid Value. Is the ghee acceptable? (FSSAI limit for ghee AV ‚Č§ 2.5 mg KOH/g)

**Solution:**

$$\text{AV} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} = \frac{5.6 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{2.0}$$

$$\text{AV} = \frac{5.6 \times 5.61}{2.0} = \frac{31.416}{2.0} = 15.708 \;\; \text{mg KOH/g}$$

$$\text{AV} = 15.71 \;\; \text{mg KOH/g}$$

Since **15.71 >> 2.5**, the ghee has undergone **extensive hydrolytic rancidity** and is **rejected**.

## 3.2 Derivation of TBA Number (Secondary Oxidation)

### 3.2.1 Principle

**Malonaldehyde (MDA)** ‚ÄĒ a 3-carbon dicarbonyl secondary oxidation product ‚ÄĒ reacts with **2-thiobarbituric acid (TBA)** under acidic conditions to form a **pink-red chromogen** (1 MDA : 2 TBA adduct) with maximum absorbance at **532 nm**.

### 3.2.2 Calculation

$$\text{TBA Number} = \frac{A_{532} \times 9.48}{W} \;\; \text{mg MDA per kg sample}$$

The constant **9.48** incorporates the molar extinction coefficient of the TBA-MDA adduct, the dilution factor, and the path length of the cuvette (typically 1 cm).

## 3.3 Derivation of Totox (Total Oxidation) Value

Totox combines **primary (PV)** and **secondary (p-AV)** oxidation data, since:
- PV captures **active** peroxides (transient).
- p-AV captures **decomposed** carbonyls (stable).

Empirically, primary oxidation is weighted twice because each peroxide eventually yields one carbonyl.

$$\boxed{\text{Totox} = 2 \times \text{PV} + \text{p-AV}}$$

> **Example:** If PV = 12 and p-AV = 8, then Totox = 2(12) + 8 = **32**.

## 3.4 Oxidative Stability ‚ÄĒ Arrhenius-Based Shelf-Life Model

The rate constant of oxidation follows the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

The **Oxidative Stability Index (OSI)** or **Rancimat induction time ($t_i$)** at temperature $T$ is inversely proportional to $k$:

$$t_i \propto \frac{1}{k} \propto e^{+E_a / RT}$$

Plotting $\ln t_i$ vs. $1/T$ gives a straight line (**Arrhenius plot**), used to **extrapolate shelf life at room temperature** from accelerated high-temperature data.

$$\ln t_i = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \text{constant}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Comparison of Rancidity Types

| Property | Oxidative | Hydrolytic | Ketonic | Microbial | Photochemical | Thermal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | O‚āā + unsaturation | H‚āāO + lipase | Mold metabolism | Bacteria/yeast | Light + sensitizer | Heat |
| **Primary Product** | Hydroperoxides | Free fatty acids | Methyl ketones | FFA + aldehydes | Hydroperoxides (via ¬ĻO‚āā) | Polymers, trans isomers |
| **Off-odor** | Paint, varnish, fishy | Soapy, bitter, "rancid butter" | Paraffin, fruity | Putrid, sour | Oxidized, tallowy | Burnt, acrid |
| **Indicator** | PV, TBA | AV (Acid Value) | Sensory + GC-MS | Microbial count | PV, bleaching test | TPC, TPM |
| **Affected Foods** | Vegetable oils, nuts, fried snacks | Butter, ghee, coconut oil | Copra, palm oil | Spoiled dairy, oilseeds | Milk, beer, oils in clear bottles | Frying oils |
| **Prevention** | Antioxidants, vacuum pack | Dry storage, low aŠĶ• | Mold control | Hygiene, refrigeration | Opaque packaging, riboflavin removal | Avoid overheating |

# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]`
**Define oxidative rancidity. List the three stages of the free-radical chain mechanism with one example reaction for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Oxidative rancidity is the deterioration of lipids caused by reaction of unsaturated fatty acids with atmospheric oxygen via a **free-radical chain mechanism**, producing hydroperoxides and secondary volatile compounds (aldehydes, ketones) responsible for off-flavors.

**Three stages:**

1. **Initiation:** $\text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$ (catalyzed by heat, light, or metal ions)
2. **Propagation:** $\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet}$; then $\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$
3. **Termination:** $\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R-R}$ (or analogous radical couplings)

**Diagram (3-stage chain)** would be drawn by the student to fetch full 3 marks. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between hydrolytic rancidity and oxidative rancidity in 6 points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Oxidative Rancidity | Hydrolytic Rancidity |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Trigger | Reaction with O‚āā | Reaction with H‚āāO |
| 2. Catalyst | Heat, light, metals, free radicals | Lipase enzyme (microbial or endogenous) |
| 3. Primary product | Hydroperoxides (ROOH) | Free fatty acids (FFA) |
| 4. Off-flavor | Paint-like, fishy, tallowy | Soapy, bitter (for short-chain FFA) |
| 5. Quantitative test | Peroxide Value (PV) | Acid Value (AV) |
| 6. Affected foods | Vegetable oils, nuts, fried foods | Butter, ghee, coconut oil, dairy |

**(3 marks)** ‚ÄĒ full credit for any 6 distinguishing points in tabular or bullet form.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Rancidity Questions):**
> 1. Many students write the auto-oxidation steps **without balancing** ‚ÄĒ half-mark deduction per unbalanced equation.
> 2. In PV numericals, forgetting the **factor 1000** (g‚Üíkg conversion) is the most common error, costing 2 marks.
> 3. Conflating **PV (primary)** with **TBA (secondary)** is a frequent conceptual error ‚ÄĒ examiners deduct 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 4. Failing to state **units** (meq/kg, mg KOH/g) in numerical answers leads to mark loss.
> 5. Skipping the **interpretation** of PV against FSSAI/Codex limits in numerical problems loses the final 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ Choice Option 1

`[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **mechanism of oxidative rancidity (auto-oxidation)** in fats and oils with suitable equations. Explain the role of **metal catalysts** and **antioxidants** in this mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4.0 g sample of stored vanaspati is tested for peroxide value. The titration consumed **24.0 mL of 0.005 N sodium thiosulfate**. Compute the PV and state whether the sample is fit for consumption (FSSAI limit: PV < 10 meq/kg for fresh oil). List **four physical methods** to prevent rancidity in stored oils.

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Oxidative rancidity is the deterioration of unsaturated lipids by reaction with atmospheric oxygen through a free-radical chain mechanism, producing hydroperoxides and secondary volatile off-flavor compounds.

**Initiation Stage (1 mark):**
$$\text{RH} \xrightarrow{h\nu, \Delta, \text{M}^{n+}} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$

**Propagation Stage (2 marks):**
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

**Termination Stage (1 mark):**
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R-R}$$
$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{ROO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2$$

**Secondary Decomposition (1 mark):**
$$\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Fe}^{2+}} \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{OH}^{-} + \text{Fe}^{3+}$$

This produces aldehydes (hexanal, malonaldehyde) ‚ÄĒ responsible for **rancid odor**.

**Role of Metal Catalysts (0.5 marks):**
Metal ions like Cu‚Āļ/Cu¬≤‚Āļ and Fe¬≤‚Āļ/Fe¬≥‚Āļ catalyze the **decomposition of hydroperoxides**, accelerating chain branching. Even at **ppm levels**, they drastically shorten the induction period.

**Role of Antioxidants (0.5 marks):**
Primary antioxidants (BHA, BHT, tocopherols) donate a hydrogen atom to ROO‚ÄĘ, **terminating the chain**:
$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{A}^{\bullet}$$
The antioxidant radical A‚ÄĘ is **resonance-stabilized** and does not propagate the chain.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Numerical Solution (5 marks):**

Given:
- $W = 4.0$ g
- $V = 24.0$ mL
- $N = 0.005$ N

Applying the formula:

$$\text{PV} = \frac{V \times N \times 1000}{W} = \frac{24.0 \times 0.005 \times 1000}{4.0}$$

$$\text{PV} = \frac{120.0}{4.0} = 30.0 \;\; \text{meq O}_2 \text{ per kg fat}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
PV = 30.0 meq/kg, which is **above 10 meq/kg** (FSSAI fresh-oil limit) and falls in the **20‚Äď40 meq/kg "borderline" range**. The vanaspati is **not fit for long-term consumption** and should be reprocessed or rejected.

**Four Physical Methods of Prevention (1 mark ‚ÄĒ 0.25 each):**
1. **Vacuum packaging** / nitrogen flushing (oxygen removal)
2. **Opaque / amber packaging** (light exclusion)
3. **Refrigerated / cold storage** (rate reduction, $Q_{10} \approx 2$)
4. **Hydrogenation** (reduces unsaturation)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ Choice Option 2

`[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alternate Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following with examples: **(i)** Hydrolytic rancidity, **(ii)** Ketonic rancidity, and **(iii)** Microbial rancidity. State the **quantitative test** used to detect each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **classification of antioxidants** used in food. Compare the **peroxide value (PV)** and **TBA value** as indices of lipid oxidation. A 3.0 g oil sample has AV of 8.0. Calculate the % FFA as oleic acid (mol. wt. 282).

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**(i) Hydrolytic Rancidity (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Enzymatic breakdown of triglycerides by lipase + water to release free fatty acids (FFA).
- **Reaction:** Triglyceride + 3H‚āāO ‚Üí Glycerol + 3 FFA
- **Off-flavor:** Soapy/bitter (from butyric, caproic, caprylic acids in butter & ghee).
- **Affected foods:** Butter, ghee, coconut oil, dairy.
- **Test:** **Acid Value (AV)** ‚ÄĒ titration with KOH.

**(ii) Ketonic Rancidity (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Mold-mediated conversion of saturated fatty acids to methyl ketones via ő≤-oxidation and decarboxylation.
- **Example:** Lauric acid (C12) ‚Üí 2-undecanone (off-odor in copra).
- **Affected foods:** Copra, palm kernel oil, stored coconut products.
- **Causative molds:** *Aspergillus niger*, *Penicillium glaucum*.
- **Test:** **GC-MS** for methyl ketones; sensory panel for "paraffin" odor.

**(iii) Microbial Rancidity (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Lipolytic enzymes (lipases, phospholipases) from bacteria, yeasts, and molds degrade lipids.
- **Causative organisms:** *Pseudomonas*, *Staphylococcus*, *Geotrichum*, *Clostridium*.
- **Overlap:** Often co-occurs with hydrolytic and ketonic rancidity.
- **Test:** **Microbial count (CFU/g)** + **Acid Value** + **sensory evaluation**.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Classification of Antioxidants (3 marks):**

**(A) Primary (Chain-Breaking) Antioxidants:** Donate H‚ÄĘ to radicals.
- Phenolic: BHA, BHT, TBHQ, Propyl Gallate, natural tocopherols (Vitamin E).
- Example reaction: ROO‚ÄĘ + AH ‚Üí ROOH + A‚ÄĘ

**(B) Secondary (Preventive / Synergistic) Antioxidants:** Reduce pro-oxidants or absorb O‚āā.
- Chelators: EDTA, citric acid (bind Fe¬≥‚Āļ/Cu¬≤‚Āļ)
- Oxygen scavengers: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C)
- Reducing agents: Sulfites
- Synergists: Lecithin, phospholipids

**(C) Natural vs Synthetic:**
- **Natural:** Tocopherols, rosemary extract, ascorbic acid, carotenoids.
- **Synthetic:** BHA, BHT, TBHQ, PG.

**Comparison of PV vs TBA (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Peroxide Value (PV) | TBA Value |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidation stage | Primary (hydroperoxides) | Secondary (aldehydes, MDA) |
| Principle | Iodometric titration | Spectrophotometry at 532 nm |
| Sample | Fat extracted in solvent | Distillate or direct extract |
| Units | meq O‚āā/kg fat | mg MDA/kg sample |
| Limitation | PV peaks and then falls (transient) | TBA keeps rising (cumulative) |
| Best use | Early oxidation detection | Advanced oxidation detection |

**Numerical ‚ÄĒ % FFA Calculation (2 marks):**

Given:
- $W = 3.0$ g
- AV = 8.0 mg KOH/g
- Mol. wt. of oleic acid = 282

The % FFA as oleic acid formula:

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \frac{\text{AV} \times M}{561}$$

(Since AV is in mg KOH/g, and 1 mL of 1 N KOH = 56.1 mg KOH, the factor 561 = 56.1 √ó 10)

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \frac{8.0 \times 282}{561} = \frac{2256}{561} \approx 4.02\%$$

**Alternative derivation using direct formula:**

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \frac{V \times N \times M}{W \times 10}$$

We can back-calculate $V \times N$ from AV: $\text{AV} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} \Rightarrow V \times N = \frac{\text{AV} \times W}{56.1} = \frac{8.0 \times 3.0}{56.1} = 0.428$

$$\text{FFA (\%)} = \frac{0.428 \times 282}{3.0 \times 10} = \frac{120.7}{30} = 4.02\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{FFA} = 4.02\% \text{ as oleic acid}}$$

This is a **high FFA value** for a refined oil (typical: < 0.1% for refined; < 5% for crude oils). Indicates the oil is partially hydrolyzed and of **inferior quality**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Box ‚ÄĒ Read Twice Before Exam**

- **Rancidity** = deterioration of lipids producing off-flavor, off-odor, and nutritional loss.
- **Six types:** Oxidative, Hydrolytic, Ketonic, Microbial, Photochemical, Thermal.
- **Oxidative rancidity** follows **Initiation ‚Üí Propagation ‚Üí Termination** (free-radical chain).
- **Hydroperoxides (ROOH)** = primary products; **Aldehydes (hexanal, MDA)** = secondary odoriferous products.
- **Initiation equation:** RH ‚Üí R‚ÄĘ + H‚ÄĘ (requires heat, light, or metal catalyst).
- **Propagation equations:** R‚ÄĘ + O‚āā ‚Üí ROO‚ÄĘ; ROO‚ÄĘ + RH ‚Üí ROOH + R‚ÄĘ.
- **Termination:** R‚ÄĘ + R‚ÄĘ ‚Üí R-R; ROO‚ÄĘ + ROO‚ÄĘ ‚Üí ROOR + O‚āā.
- **Peroxide Value (PV)** = (V √ó N √ó 1000)/W, expressed in **meq O‚āā/kg fat**.
- **Acid Value (AV)** = (V √ó N √ó 56.1)/W, expressed in **mg KOH/g fat**.
- **% FFA** = (V √ó N √ó M)/(W √ó 10), where M = mol. wt. of chosen fatty acid (oleic = 282).
- **TBA Number** = (A‚āÖ‚āÉ‚āā √ó 9.48)/W, in **mg MDA/kg sample** ‚ÄĒ measures secondary oxidation.
- **Totox** = 2 √ó PV + p-AV ‚ÄĒ combined oxidation indicator.
- **FSSAI PV limit:** < 10 meq/kg (fresh), 10‚Äď20 (acceptable), 20‚Äď40 (borderline), > 40 (rejected).
- **Antioxidants:** Primary (BHA, BHT, TBHQ, PG, tocopherols) + Secondary (EDTA, citric acid, ascorbic acid).
- **FSSAI antioxidant limit:** BHA 200, BHT 200, TBHQ 200, PG 100 mg/kg; combined ‚Č§ 200 mg/kg.
- **Singlet oxygen (¬ĻO‚āā)** formation by **chlorophyll + light** causes photochemical rancidity ‚ÄĒ ~1500√ó faster than triplet O‚āā.
- **Ketonic rancidity** = mold (*Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*) ‚Üí methyl ketones; common in **copra**.
- **Hydrolytic rancidity** common in **butter, ghee, coconut oil**; produces soapy taste from short-chain FFA.
- **Activation energy of lipid oxidation** ‚Čą 75‚Äď150 kJ/mol.
- **Q‚āĀ‚āÄ** (rate doubling per 10 ¬įC) ‚Čą 2 for lipid oxidation.
- **Hot-point for numerical:** Always carry units and apply the **√ó1000 g-to-kg** factor in PV calculations.
- **Hot-point for theory:** Differentiate PV (primary) vs TBA (secondary); oxidative vs hydrolytic vs ketonic.
- **Industrial prevention:** Vacuum/N‚āā packaging + antioxidants (BHA/BHT/TBHQ) + refrigeration + opaque containers.

---

**End of Module 4 ‚ÄĒ Rancidity and Its Types | PCFTT205 ‚ÄĒ Food Chemistry | KTU 2024 Scheme**
